The expected MACE rate for the predicate device is $p_0 = 0.10$, and the sponsor hypothesizes their new stent achieves $p_1 = 0.06$. Calculate the sample size per arm for $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided) and power $= 80\%$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the critical Z-values from the standard normal table.

$$Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96 \quad \text{(two-sided test)}$$

$$Z_{\beta} = Z_{0.20} = 0.842 \quad \text{(for 80\% power)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the proportions.

$$p_0 = 0.10, \quad p_1 = 0.06$$

**Step 3:** Compute the average proportion $p$ used in the pooled variance.

$$p = \frac{p_0 + p_1}{2} = \frac{0.10 + 0.06}{2} = 0.08$$

**Step 4:** Compute the effect size (clinically meaningful difference).

$$p_1 - p_0 = 0.06 - 0.10 = -0.04$$

We take the absolute difference: $\vert p_1 - p_0 \vert = 0.04$.

**Step 5:** Compute the pooled variance term.

$$2 \cdot p(1-p) = 2 \times 0.08 \times (1 - 0.08) = 2 \times 0.08 \times 0.92 = 0.1472$$

**Step 6:** Compute the numerator.

$$(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 = (1.96 + 0.842)^2 = (2.802)^2 = 7.8512$$

**Step 7:** Compute the denominator.

$$(p_1 - p_0)^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$$

**Step 8:** Assemble the formula.

$$n = \frac{7.8512 \times 0.1472}{0.0016}$$

**Step 9:** Multiply the numerator.

$$7.8512 \times 0.1472 = 1.1557$$

**Step 10:** Divide to obtain sample size per arm.

$$n = \frac{1.1557}{0.0016} = 722.31$$

**Step 11:** Round up to the next integer and inflate for dropout (typically 10–15%).

$$n_{\text{inflated}} = 723 \times 1.10 = 795.3 \approx 796 \text{ per arm}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{n \approx 796 \text{ subjects per arm}, \quad N \approx 1592 \text{ total}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cardiac stent trials are expensive — each enrolled patient can cost **$20,000–$50,000 USD**. A 1000-patient trial can exceed **$30 million USD**. This is why sample size justification is both a statistical AND a financial decision.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Risk-Benefit Determination (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A manufacturer is investigating a novel implantable neurostimulator for epilepsy. Known risks: 2% infection rate, 1% lead migration, 0.5% device failure. Anticipated benefit: 60% seizure reduction in refractory patients. Determine whether the clinical investigation is ethically and regulatorily justified.

### Step-by-Step Risk-Benefit Analysis

**Step 1:** Quantify the **Probability × Severity** of each risk.

| Risk | Probability | Severity (1–5) | Risk Score (P×S) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infection | 0.02 | 4 (serious, hospitalization) | 0.08 |
| Lead migration | 0.01 | 3 (re-surgery required) | 0.03 |
| Device failure | 0.005 | 5 (life-threatening in epileptics) | 0.025 |

**Step 2:** Sum the aggregate risk.

$$R_{total} = 0.08 + 0.03 + 0.025 = 0.135$$

**Step 3:** Quantify the benefit using Number Needed to Treat (NNT).

For 60% seizure reduction, assume 30% success in standard care (control). Absolute Risk Reduction:

$$ARR = 0.60 - 0.30 = 0.30$$

$$NNT = \frac{1}{ARR} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33$$

This means **for every 3–4 patients treated, one additional patient achieves 60% seizure reduction** compared to standard care.

**Step 4:** Apply the Regulatory Test.

Under **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 5.5** and **EU MDR Annex I Section 8**, the clinical investigation is **justified** IF:

$$\text{Benefit (NNT)} \ll \text{Risk Severity} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{No alternative exists for refractory patients}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

$$\boxed{\text{Risk-Benefit is FAVOURABLE. Clinical investigation is ethically and regulatorily justified.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** The answer must explicitly cite ISO 14155 Clause 5.5 (anticipated clinical benefits must justify the risks) and mention that for **refractory epilepsy** (treatment-resistant), the high unmet medical need strengthens the justification.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Informed Consent Process Flow

The **informed consent** is a continuous process, not a one-time signature. The sequence required by ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6.6:

1. Investigator identifies potential subject
2. Subject is provided **all information** (objectives, risks, benefits, alternatives, compensation, confidentiality)
3. Subject is given **adequate time** (no coercion)
4. Subject voluntarily signs the **Informed Consent Form (ICF)**
5. Investigator countersigns
6. **Subject receives a signed copy**
7. Consent is **re-affirmed** if new risks emerge during the study
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clinical Investigation Lifecycle — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept and Planning] --> B[Risk Benefit Assessment]
    B --> C[Develop Clinical Investigation Plan CIP]
    C --> D[Prepare Investigator Brochure IB]
    D --> E[Ethics Committee Submission]
    E --> F[Competent Authority Notification]
    F --> G[Site Initiation Visit SIV]
    G --> H[Subject Recruitment and Informed Consent]
    H --> I[Clinical Investigation Execution]
    I --> J[Data Monitoring and Safety Reporting]
    J --> K[Database Lock and Statistical Analysis]
    K --> L[Close Out Visit COV]
    L --> M[Clinical Investigation Report CIR]
    M --> N[Regulatory Submission for CE Mark or 510k]
    N --> O[Post Market Clinical Follow Up PMCF]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Stakeholder & Documentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG[Regulators]
        NB[Notified Body EU]
        FDA[FDA USA]
        CDSCO[CDSCO India]
    end

    subgraph SP[Sponsor Manufacturer]
        SP1[Sponsor Representative]
        SP2[CRO Contract Research Organization]
        SP3[Biostatistician]
    end

    subgraph CLIN[Clinical Site]
        PI[Principal Investigator]
        SC[Study Coordinator]
        SUB[Human Subject]
    end

    subgraph DOC[Key Documents]
        CIP[Clinical Investigation Plan]
        IB[Investigator Brochure]
        ICF[Informed Consent Form]
        CRF[Case Report Form]
        CIR[Clinical Investigation Report]
    end

    SP1 --> CIP
    SP1 --> IB
    PI --> ICF
    SUB --> ICF
    PI --> CRF
    CRF --> CIR
    CIR --> NB
    CIR --> FDA
    CIR --> CDSCO

    style REG fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#900
    style SP fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#005
    style CLIN fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#060
    style DOC fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#a80
```

## 4.3 Clinical Evaluation vs. Clinical Investigation — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CE[Clinical Evaluation Continuous Lifecycle Process]
    CE --> CD1[Pre Market Clinical Data]
    CE --> CD2[Post Market Clinical Data]
    CE --> CD3[Literature Review]
    CE --> CD4[Clinical Investigation Data]
    CD4 --> CI[Clinical Investigation Single Bounded Study]
    CI --> CIP[CIP Execution]
    CI --> SAE[SAE Reporting]
    CI --> CIR[Clinical Investigation Report]
    CIR --> CER[Clinical Evaluation Report CER]
    CD1 --> CER
    CD2 --> CER
    CD3 --> CER

    style CE fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CI fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CER fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Risk-Benefit Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Medical Device Submission] --> Q1{Is device Class III or Implantable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Mand[Clinical Investigation MANDATORY]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does an equivalent device exist?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Equiv[Equivalence route may be sufficient]
    Q2 -->|No| Mand
    Mand --> RBA[Perform Risk Benefit Analysis]
    RBA --> RBAQ{Benefit outweighs Risk?}
    RBAQ -->|Yes| EC[Ethics Committee Approval]
    RBAQ -->|No| Reject[Investigation Rejected or Redesigned]
    EC --> CA[Competent Authority Notification]
    CA --> Conduct[Conduct Clinical Investigation]
    Conduct --> Report[Generate Clinical Investigation Report]

    style Mand fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EC fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Reject fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define Clinical Investigation of a medical device. State the international standard that governs Good Clinical Practice for medical device investigations.**

**Model Answer:**

Clinical Investigation of a medical device is a systematic, scientifically designed study conducted in human subjects to evaluate the **safety, performance, and effectiveness** of a medical device for its intended purpose, in compliance with applicable regulatory and ethical requirements.

The international standard governing Good Clinical Practice (GCP) for medical device investigations is **ISO 14155:2020** — *"Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice."*

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming ISO 14155, 1 mark for full name or its purpose.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between Clinical Evaluation and Clinical Investigation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Clinical Evaluation | Clinical Investigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Continuous lifecycle process | Specific bounded study |
| **Scope** | All clinical data (literature, PMS, investigations) | Only the human-subject study data |
| **Output** | Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) | Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) |
| **Subjects** | Not mandatory | Mandatory human subjects |
| **Regulatory Reference** | EU MDR Article 61 | EU MDR Article 62, Annex XIV |

*(2 marks for tabular differentiation, 1 mark for regulatory citation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model QP based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**a)** Discuss in detail the structure and contents of a **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** as required by **ISO 14155:2020**. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Explain the **risk-benefit determination** process for justifying a clinical investigation. Illustrate with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is the master protocol document that governs the conduct of a clinical investigation of a medical device, as mandated by **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6**.

A complete CIP must contain the following 12 sections:

1. **General Information** — Title, identification number, version, date, sponsor name and address, investigator details, and monitoring organization.

2. **Device Identification and Description** — Name, model, manufacturer, batch/lot numbers, intended purpose, classification under applicable regulations, and a summary of prior preclinical and clinical data.

3. **Justification for the Investigation Design** — Scientific rationale and explanation of why the chosen design is appropriate to address the clinical question.

4. **Objectives and Hypothesis** — Clearly stated **primary objective** and **secondary objectives**, along with null and alternative hypotheses.

5. **Design of the Clinical Investigation** — Type of study (randomized, blinded, multi-centre, comparative, single-arm), blinding method, and control selection rationale.

6. **Subject Selection Criteria** — **Inclusion criteria**, **exclusion criteria**, and subject withdrawal/discontinuation criteria.

7. **Variables and Endpoints** — Primary endpoint, secondary endpoints, safety variables, and efficacy variables with their measurement definitions.

8. **Follow-up Schedule** — Visit schedule, examination procedures, and duration of follow-up.

9. **Statistical Considerations** — Sample size justification, statistical analysis plan (SAP), significance level, power, and handling of missing data.

10. **Monitoring Plan** — Sponsor's monitoring strategy, frequency, and data verification procedures.

11. **Adverse Event Reporting** — Definitions (AE, SAE, SADe, USADE), reporting timelines, and responsible parties.

12. **Deviations and Amendments** — Procedures for protocol deviations, amendments, and EC/CA notification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for each correctly named and explained CIP section. Listing 10 out of 12 sections with brief descriptions typically scores **6–7 marks**.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **risk-benefit determination** is a continuous, iterative process required by **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 5.5** and **EU MDR Annex I, Section 8**.

**Step 1 — Identify Risks:** Document all known and foreseeable risks from preclinical data, literature, and prior investigations. Quantify them by **probability and severity**.

**Step 2 — Estimate Benefits:** Quantify the expected clinical benefits using **objective measures** (e.g., MACE rate, survival improvement, quality-of-life scores, NNT).

**Step 3 — Apply the Risk-Benefit Test:** The investigation is **justified** if and only if:

$$\sum \text{(Probability of Risk} \times \text{Severity)} \ll \text{Clinical Benefit (with NNT)}$$

**Step 4 — Consider Mitigation:** Document risk-mitigation measures (e.g., training, contraindications, device labelling) that reduce residual risk.

**Step 5 — Ethical Review:** Submit the risk-benefit analysis to the **Ethics Committee (EC)** and **Competent Authority (CA)** for independent review.

### Worked Example — Implantable Cardiac Defibrillator (ICD)

| Risk | Probability | Severity | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infection at implant site | 0.015 | 4 | 0.060 |
| Lead dislodgement | 0.020 | 3 | 0.060 |
| Inappropriate shock | 0.050 | 4 | 0.200 |
| Device malfunction | 0.010 | 5 | 0.050 |
| **Total Risk** | | | **0.370** |

- **Aggregate Risk Score:** 0.370
- **Benefit:** 35% reduction in sudden cardiac death; NNT = 5 (one life saved per 5 patients over 3 years)
- **Conclusion:** Benefit vastly outweighs risk → **Investigation is justified.**

$$\boxed{\text{Conclusion: Risk-benefit ratio is favourable; clinical investigation ethically and regulatorily justified.}}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model QP based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**a)** Explain the **purpose, scope, and key requirements of ISO 14155:2020** for medical device clinical investigations. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Describe the **informed consent process** and the **role of the Ethics Committee** in a clinical investigation. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Purpose of ISO 14155:2020:**
ISO 14155:2020 establishes the **Good Clinical Practice (GCP) standard** for the design, conduct, recording, and reporting of clinical investigations of medical devices involving human subjects. Its primary purpose is to:

- Protect the **rights, safety, and well-being** of human subjects
- Ensure **scientific credibility** of clinical data submitted to regulators
- Provide a globally harmonized GCP framework specific to **medical devices** (as opposed to drugs)

**Scope of ISO 14155:2020:**
The standard applies to **all clinical investigations of medical devices** carried out to assess safety and/or performance. It does **not apply to in-vitro diagnostic devices** (covered by ISO 20916).

**Key Requirements (7 Elements):**

1. **Sponsor Responsibilities** — Protocol design, study oversight, regulatory submissions, pharmacovigilance, data integrity.
2. **Investigator Qualifications and Responsibilities** — Qualified medical practitioner, trained in GCP, with documented experience.
3. **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** — Mandatory master protocol document.
4. **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** — Compilation of all device-related data.
5. **Ethics Committee (EC) / IRB Review** — Independent ethics review before initiation.
6. **Informed Consent** — Voluntary, documented, comprehensible subject agreement.
7. **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)** — Comprehensive final report compliant with regulatory standards.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Informed Consent Process (per ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6.6):**

The informed consent is a **continuous process** comprising 7 steps:

1. Subject identification by investigator
2. Provision of **complete information** — purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, alternatives, confidentiality, compensation, contact details
3. Use of **comprehensible language** (layperson level, native language)
4. Provision of **adequate time** for the subject to consider participation
5. **Voluntary signature** of the Informed Consent Form (ICF) by subject and investigator
6. **Subject receives a signed copy** of the ICF
7. **Re-consent** required if new safety information emerges during the study

> The informed consent is **never a one-time event** — it is an ongoing dialogue.

**Role of the Ethics Committee (EC) / Institutional Review Board (IRB):**

The EC is an **independent body** constituted of medical, scientific, and non-medical members. Its statutory roles include:

1. **Independent Review** of the CIP, IB, ICF, and risk-benefit analysis **before** study initiation.
2. **Continuing Review** — at least once per year, monitoring ongoing investigations.
3. **Review of Amendments** — approval required for any protocol modification.
4. **Review of Adverse Events** — assessment of SAE reports.
5. **Subject Protection** — ensures vulnerable populations (children, pregnant women, prisoners) receive extra safeguards.
6. **Authority to Suspend or Terminate** — EC can stop an investigation if subject safety is compromised.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "informed consent is just a signature" — it is a **process**, not a form.
> 2. ❌ Confusing the role of **EC/IRB** (ethics review) with **Competent Authority** (regulatory approval). They are **two separate bodies** with different mandates.
> 3. ❌ Omitting **re-consent** when new safety information arises.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting that EC has authority to **suspend or terminate** an investigation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Clinical Investigation** is a systematic study in human subjects to assess **safety, performance, and risk-benefit** of a medical device.
- ✅ The governing international standard is **ISO 14155:2020** (GCP for medical devices).
- ✅ **Clinical Evaluation** is a continuous lifecycle process; **Clinical Investigation** is a single bounded study — they are **not synonymous**.
- ✅ Class III, implantable, and novel devices require a **mandatory clinical investigation** under EU MDR.
- ✅ The **CIP** is the master protocol document; the **IB** is the device manual for the investigator; the **ICF** is the subject's signed permission.
- ✅ The **Informed Consent Process** has 7 steps and is **continuous**, not a single event.
- ✅ **Ethics Committee (EC/IRB)** provides independent ethics review — it is **separate** from the Competent Authority (regulator).
- ✅ The **risk-benefit analysis** must be **favourable** for the investigation to be approved; cite **ISO 14155 Clause 5.5**.
- ✅ **Sample size** is calculated using the standard normal formula based on significance level (α), power (1−β), and effect size.
- ✅ **SAE reporting** is mandatory — typically within 24 hours to the sponsor, 7–10 days to the EC and CA.
- ✅ **PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up)** continues clinical data collection **after** market launch under EU MDR Article 83.
- ✅ Key documents in the regulatory dossier: **CIP → IB → ICF → CRF → CIR → CER**.
- ✅ The two largest regulatory frameworks are **EU MDR 2017/745** (Europe) and **21 CFR Part 812** (USA).
- ✅ Always cite the **specific clause number** (e.g., ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6.6) — KTU examiners reward clause-level precision.
- ✅ Differentiate **AE**, **SAE**, **SADE** (Serious Adverse Device Effect), and **USADE** (Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, structure answers in **headings + sub-points + tables** to maximize readability marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clinical Investigation Plan for Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clinical Investigation Plan for Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is a comprehensive, structured, and scientifically rigorous document that outlines the rationale, objectives, design, methodology, statistical considerations, and organizational framework of a clinical investigation involving a medical device. The CIP is the operational blueprint that governs the conduct of a clinical study to evaluate the safety, performance, and clinical benefit of a medical device in human subjects, in accordance with the requirements specified in **ISO 14155:2020 (Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice)**, the **EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)**, and the **U.S. FDA 21 CFR Part 812** framework.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEEBT862 syllabus**, the CIP serves as the legally binding protocol that ensures:
- Scientific validity of the investigation
- Protection of rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects
- Compliance with Good Clinical Practice (GCP) principles
- Traceability and reproducibility of clinical data submitted for regulatory conformity assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Clinical Investigation Plan is mandated under **Annex XV of EU MDR 2017/745**, **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6**, and **21 CFR 812.25 (FDA IDE)**. For Class III and implantable devices, a fully approved CIP is a *prerequisite* for CE marking or FDA approval.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The CIP as a Movie Director's Script"**
>
> Imagine you are directing a high-budget Hollywood movie. Before any camera rolls, you create a **Production Script** that specifies every scene, every actor's movement, every camera angle, the budget, the safety protocols, and the contingency plans. No actor improvises, and no scene is left unplanned.
>
> The **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is exactly that — but for a medical device study in human patients. It is the **master script** that the **Sponsor**, **Principal Investigator (PI)**, **Clinical Research Associate (CRA)**, **Ethics Committee (EC)**, and **Regulatory Authorities** all follow to the letter. Without the CIP, a clinical investigation would be like an unscripted movie — chaotic, non-reproducible, and legally indefensible.

### 1.3 The Regulatory Triangle of the CIP

The CIP sits at the intersection of three regulatory pillars:

| Pillar | Standard / Regulation | Issuing Body | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **International GCP** | **ISO 14155:2020** | ISO | Global GCP for medical device investigations |
| **European Union** | **MDR 2017/745, Annex XV** | European Commission | Mandatory CIP for CE marking |
| **United States** | **21 CFR Part 812 (IDE)** | FDA | Investigational Device Exemption requirements |
| **India** | **MDR-2017 (Amended), Schedule Y** | CDSCO | Indian regulatory CIP requirements |

### 1.4 Why is the CIP Important? (The "Why" from KTU Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Purposes of a Clinical Investigation Plan:**
> 1. **Patient Safety** — Ensures risks are minimized and benefits justified
> 2. **Scientific Rigor** — Defines hypothesis, endpoints, and statistical methodology
> 3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Satisfies MDR, ISO 14155, and FDA submission requirements
> 4. **Data Integrity** — Guarantees ALCOA+ principles (Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate)
> 5. **Auditability** — Provides a single reference document during regulatory inspections and audits
> 6. **Ethical Compliance** — Aligns with the **Declaration of Helsinki (2013)** and **ICH-GCP E6(R2)** ethical principles

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics & Definitions (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Clinical Investigation** — Any systematic investigation in one or more human subjects, undertaken to assess the safety or performance of a medical device. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.6)*
- **Sponsor** — The individual or organization taking responsibility and liability for the initiation or implementation of a clinical investigation. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.30)*
- **Principal Investigator (PI)** — A qualified person responsible for conducting the clinical investigation at an investigation site. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.23)*
- **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** — A document that states the rationale, objectives, design, and proposed analysis, methodology, monitoring, conduct, and record-keeping of the clinical investigation. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.13)*
- **Investigational Device** — A medical device being assessed for safety or performance in a clinical investigation. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.15)*
- **Adverse Event (AE)** — Any untoward medical occurrence, unintended disease or injury, or any untoward clinical sign in subjects, users, or other persons. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.1)*
- **Serious Adverse Event (SAE)** — An adverse event that led to death, serious deterioration in health, or required intervention. *(ISO 14155:2020, Clause 3.32)*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of a Clinical Investigation Plan

The CIP is structured in a modular, hierarchical format to enable systematic review by ethics committees, regulatory authorities, and investigators. The structure prescribed by **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6** and **EU MDR Annex XV** is as follows:

### Module A: Administrative & Identification Information
1. **Cover Page** — Title, protocol code, version, date, sponsor details
2. **Table of Contents**
3. **Sponsor Information** — Name, address, contact, legal representative
4. **Investigator Information** — Name, qualification, site address, CV references
5. **Investigational Device Identification** — Model, lot/batch number, expiry, manufacturer
6. **Reference to Risk Management Documentation** — Link to **ISO 14971:2019** Risk Management File
7. **Reference to Instructions for Use (IFU)** and **Investigator's Brochure (IB)**

### Module B: Scientific & Methodological Framework
1. **Rationale and Background** — Device description, preclinical data, prior clinical evidence
2. **Aim and Objectives** — Primary, secondary, and exploratory objectives
3. **Hypothesis Statement** — Null hypothesis (H₀) and alternative hypothesis (H₁)
4. **Design Type** — Randomized controlled, non-randomized, single-arm, crossover, multi-arm
5. **Endpoints** — Primary endpoint, secondary endpoints, safety endpoints
6. **Study Population** — Inclusion criteria, exclusion criteria, withdrawal criteria
7. **Sample Size Justification** — Statistical power calculation
8. **Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP)** — Descriptive and inferential methods, handling of missing data

### Module C: Operational & Procedural Framework
1. **Subject Recruitment & Screening Procedures**
2. **Investigational Device Handling & Accountability**
3. **Concomitant Treatments & Medications**
4. **Data Collection Methods** — CRFs (Case Report Forms), eCRFs
5. **Monitoring Plan** — On-site, central, risk-based monitoring
6. **Source Data Verification (SDV)**
7. **Deviation Management & Reporting**

### Module D: Risk, Safety, and Ethics
1. **Risk-Benefit Analysis** — Anticipated benefits vs. residual risks
2. **Adverse Event Reporting Procedures** — AE, SAE, SADEs, USADEs
3. **Serious Adverse Device Effect (SADE)** — Causality assessment
4. **Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect (USADE)** — Reporting timelines
5. **Vulnerable Population Protections** — Minors, pregnant women, cognitively impaired
6. **Informed Consent Process**
7. **Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB)** or **Clinical Events Committee (CEC)**

### Module E: Administrative Closure & Reporting
1. **End-of-Investigation Definition**
2. **Subject Follow-up Procedures**
3. **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) Preparation**
4. **Archival Duration** — Minimum **15 years** (MDR) or **10 years** post-study completion
5. **Publication Policy & Data Sharing**

## 2.2 The Hypothesis Framework — Formulating the Clinical Question

The CIP must articulate a **PICOT-formatted** clinical question:

| Element | Definition | Example (Cardiac Stent Trial) |
|---|---|---|
| **P**opulation | Target patient group | Patients with single-vessel coronary artery disease |
| **I**ntervention | The medical device under investigation | Drug-eluting stent (DES) |
| **C**omparator | Reference treatment | Bare-metal stent (BMS) |
| **O**utcome | Measurable endpoint | Major Adverse Cardiac Events (MACE) at 12 months |
| **T**ime | Follow-up duration | 12 months post-implantation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Standards Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **critical formulas, statistical calculations, and regulatory thresholds** used in clinical investigation planning. These are **high-yield** for KTU University ESE and Internal Assessment.

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Standard Reference | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample Size (Two-Arm Comparison)** | $n = \dfrac{2 \cdot (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}$ | ISO 14155 Annex A | Calculated per study |
| **Sample Size (Proportion)** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2})^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{d^2}$ | ISO 14155 Annex A | Calculated per study |
| **Statistical Power (1 − β)** | $P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ | ISO 14155 / ICH E9 | Typically **0.80 (80%)** |
| **Significance Level (α)** | Probability of Type I error | ICH E9 | Typically **0.05 (5%)** |
| **Confidence Interval (95%)** | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | ISO 14155 | **95% CI** standard |
| **Risk-Benefit Ratio (RBR)** | $\text{RBR} = \dfrac{\text{Anticipated Clinical Benefit}}{\text{Residual Risk}}$ | ISO 14971 / MDR Annex I | $\text{RBR} \geq 1$ acceptable |
| **SADE Reporting Timeline (EU)** | Report to CA within **15 days** | EU MDR 2017/745 Article 87 | **≤ 15 days** |
| **USADE Reporting Timeline (FDA)** | Report to FDA within **10 working days** | 21 CFR 812.150(b)(1) | **≤ 10 days** |
| **CIR Submission Deadline (EU)** | Within **1 year** of end of investigation | EU MDR Article 77(5) | **≤ 365 days** |
| **CIP Deviation Classification** | Major vs. Minor | ISO 14155 Clause 8.3.4 | Categorical |
| **Data Retention Period (EU)** | **15 years** minimum | EU MDR Article 10(8) | **≥ 15 years** |
| **Data Retention Period (US)** | **2 years post-510(k) clearance** | 21 CFR 820.180 | **≥ 2 years** |

## 2.4 Risk-Benefit Determination — The Heart of the CIP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The risk-benefit analysis is the **single most scrutinized section** of the CIP by ethics committees and notified bodies.

The risk-benefit determination follows a **three-tier assessment** as defined in **ISO 14971:2019** and **MDR Annex I, Section 8**:

1. **Risk Identification** — Identify all foreseeable hazards from the Risk Management File
2. **Risk Estimation** — Calculate severity × probability for each hazard
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against anticipated clinical benefit
4. **Risk Control** — Inherent safe design, protective measures, information for safety
5. **Residual Risk Evaluation** — Final determination that residual risk is acceptable

The overall determination statement must explicitly conclude that:

> *"The anticipated clinical benefits outweigh the residual risks, and the investigation will be conducted with appropriate risk minimization measures, subject monitoring, and data safety oversight."*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the CIP

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** The CIP is not a regulatory formality — it is an **engineering control** that bridges the gap between bench testing (ISO 10993 biocompatibility, IEC 60601 electrical safety) and market access. Without a properly designed CIP, even a technically superior device cannot achieve **CE marking under MDR Class III** or **FDA Premarket Approval (PMA)**.

In the medical device industry, the CIP integrates with:
- **Design History File (DHF)** under **21 CFR 820.30**
- **Risk Management File (RMF)** under **ISO 14971:2019**
- **Usability Engineering File (UEF)** under **IEC 62366-1:2015**
- **Post-Market Surveillance Plan (PMS)** under **MDR Article 84**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sample Size Calculation for Continuous Outcome (Two-Arm Parallel Study)

This derivation follows the statistical framework of **ICH E9** and **ISO 14155:2020 Annex A**. It is **highly examinable** in KTU ESE Part B questions.

**Step 1: Define the Clinical Question**

We want to detect a difference in mean efficacy scores between the investigational device group and the control group. Let:
- $\mu_T$ = Mean outcome in treatment (device) arm
- $\mu_C$ = Mean outcome in control arm
- $\sigma$ = Common standard deviation (assumed equal in both arms)
- $\Delta = \mu_T - \mu_C$ = Clinically meaningful difference to detect

**Step 2: State the Hypotheses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 : \mu_T - \mu_C &= 0 \quad \text{(no difference between device and control)} \\
H_1 : \mu_T - \mu_C &= \Delta \neq 0 \quad \text{(clinically meaningful difference exists)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Specify Error Rates**

We choose the significance level (Type I error) and the desired power (1 − Type II error):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96 \\
1 - \beta &= 0.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{\beta} = Z_{0.20} = 0.842
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Set Up the Test Statistic**

For a two-sample t-test with equal variances, the standardized effect size is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{(\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C) - 0}{\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{n}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $n$ is the sample size per arm. We want this Z statistic to reach the critical value under H₁.

**Step 5: Solve for the Required Sample Size**

Setting the expected value of Z under H₁ equal to $Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta}{\sigma \cdot \sqrt{2/n}} &= Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta} \\
\sqrt{\frac{2}{n}} &= \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}}{\Delta / \sigma} \\
\frac{2}{n} &= \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2}{\Delta^2 / \sigma^2} \\
n &= \frac{2 \cdot (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Formula:**

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{2 \cdot (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{(\mu_T - \mu_C)^2}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**

> **Problem:** A sponsor plans a two-arm study comparing a new blood glucose monitor (treatment) against the standard device (control). Based on prior data, the common standard deviation is **σ = 12 mg/dL**, and the clinically meaningful difference to detect is **Δ = 8 mg/dL**. Using α = 0.05 (two-sided) and power = 80%, calculate the required sample size per arm.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{2 \cdot (1.96 + 0.842)^2 \cdot 12^2}{8^2} \\
  &= \frac{2 \cdot (2.802)^2 \cdot 144}{64} \\
  &= \frac{2 \cdot 7.851 \cdot 144}{64} \\
  &= \frac{2260.99}{64} \\
  &= 35.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rounded up: n = 36 per arm. Total enrollment = 72 subjects.**

**Step 6: Adjust for Dropouts (Inflated Sample Size)**

If we anticipate a **15% dropout rate**, the adjusted sample size per arm is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{adj}} &= \frac{n}{(1 - d)^2} = \frac{36}{(1 - 0.15)^2} = \frac{36}{0.7225} = 49.83
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rounded up: n_adj = 50 per arm. Total enrollment = 100 subjects.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state the unrounded value, the rounding rule (round *up* conservatively), and the dropout adjustment separately. This is a **3-step presentation** that KTU examiners reward.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Confidence Interval for a Single Proportion (Endpoint Estimation)

**Step 1: Define the Parameter**

Let $p$ be the true proportion of subjects achieving the primary endpoint (e.g., successful device implantation). The sample estimate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{p} &= \frac{x}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $x$ is the number of successes and $n$ is the sample size.

**Step 2: Standard Error of Proportion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE}(\hat{p}) &= \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Construct the 95% Confidence Interval**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{95\% CI} &= \hat{p} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}} \\
              &= \hat{p} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** In a CIP for a novel orthopedic implant, 87 of 100 subjects achieved the primary success endpoint. Calculate the 95% confidence interval for the true success rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{p} &= \frac{87}{100} = 0.87 \\
\text{SE} &= \sqrt{\frac{0.87 \times 0.13}{100}} = \sqrt{0.001131} = 0.0336 \\
\text{95\% CI} &= 0.87 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0336 \\
              &= 0.87 \pm 0.0659 \\
              &= (0.8041, \, 0.9359)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true device success rate lies between **80.41% and 93.59%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Clinical Investigation Design

| Design Type | When to Use | Advantages | Disadvantages | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)** | Gold standard for efficacy | Minimizes bias, supports strong claims | Expensive, ethical constraints | Stent vs. surgery |
| **Single-Arm Study** | Rare diseases, ethical constraints | Feasible, smaller sample | No concurrent control, bias risk | Pediatric orphan device |
| **Crossover Design** | Chronic stable conditions | Each subject serves as own control | Carryover effects | Asthma inhaler trial |
| **Adaptive Design** | Uncertain sample size/dose | Flexible, efficient | Complex statistical methods | COVID-19 device trials |
| **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** | After CE marking | Real-world data | Confounding, recall bias | Hip implant surveillance |

## 3.4 Causality Assessment for Adverse Events (Per ISO 14155 Annex F)

Every AE in a clinical investigation must be classified using a **5-point causality scale**:

| Relationship | Description | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Not related** | No temporal/plausible relationship | Report in CRF only |
| **Unlikely related** | Reasonable alternative explanation exists | Report in CRF only |
| **Possibly related** | Temporal relationship but could be alternative cause | Report as AE |
| **Probably related** | Strong temporal relationship, unlikely alternative | Report as AE, consider SADE |
| **Causally related** | Plausible temporal relationship, no alternative explanation | Report as AE, escalate to SADE/USADE |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Q: Define a Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) as per ISO 14155:2020. List any four essential elements that must be included in a CIP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution):**

A **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is a document that states the rationale, objectives, design, proposed analysis, methodology, monitoring, conduct, and record-keeping of a clinical investigation of a medical device in human subjects, prepared in accordance with **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6**.

**Four essential elements of a CIP (½ mark each):**

1. **Rationale and objectives** of the clinical investigation, including the clinical question and hypothesis
2. **Study design and methodology**, including the type of investigation (RCT, single-arm, crossover)
3. **Inclusion and exclusion criteria** for subject selection
4. **Statistical analysis plan (SAP)** with sample size justification and significance criteria

*(Valid alternatives: Risk-benefit analysis, AE reporting procedures, monitoring plan, informed consent process, device accountability procedures)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **Q: Differentiate between a SADE and a USADE with suitable examples. Mention the reporting timeline for USADE under U.S. FDA regulations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | SADE (Serious Adverse Device Effect) | USADE (Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A serious adverse event that is related to the investigational device | A serious adverse device effect that is **unanticipated** in nature, severity, or incidence |
| **Anticipation** | Could have been anticipated based on the risk analysis or IB | Was **not anticipated** based on the current risk management documentation |
| **Example** | Known bleeding complication from a coronary stent | Unexpected device migration to a non-target organ |
| **Reporting (FDA)** | Per 21 CFR 812.150(b)(2) — within **10 working days** | Per 21 CFR 812.150(b)(1) — within **10 working days** to FDA, EC, and all reviewing IRBs |

*(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for example distinction, 1 mark for timeline)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks) — Preferred for ESE Module 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / June 2024 Style]**

> **Q: A medical device company is planning a clinical investigation of a novel Class III implantable cardiac defibrillator (ICD) for CE marking under EU MDR 2017/745.**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Prepare a detailed structure of the Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) that the sponsor must submit. Cover at least **eight (8) mandatory components** as per ISO 14155:2020 and MDR Annex XV.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **risk-benefit determination process** that must be included in the CIP. Discuss how it integrates with **ISO 14971:2019** risk management and justify the role of the **Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB)** in ongoing risk assessment.

#### Model Solution for Choice A

##### Part (a) — Detailed CIP Structure (7 Marks)

The Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) for the novel implantable cardiac defibrillator shall contain the following eight mandatory components (½ mark each + 1 mark for structure, 1 mark for integration logic):

**1. Administrative Information (½ mark)**
- Sponsor name, address, contact details, legal representative
- Principal Investigator (PI) name, qualifications, site details
- CIP version, date, document control number

**2. Investigational Device Description (½ mark)**
- Device name, model number, batch/lot number
- Manufacturer details, intended purpose, classification (Class III, active implantable)
- Reference to CE technical documentation, ISO 14708 series compliance

**3. Rationale and Background (½ mark)**
- Summary of preclinical data (biocompatibility per ISO 10993, electrical safety per IEC 60601)
- Summary of prior clinical evidence (literature review, prior studies)
- Justification for conducting the clinical investigation

**4. Aim and Objectives (½ mark)**
- **Primary objective:** Demonstrate safety and performance of the novel ICD at 12 months
- **Secondary objectives:** Quality of life improvement, device longevity, inappropriate shock rate

**5. Hypothesis Statement (½ mark)**
- H₀: There is no difference in MACE-free survival between novel ICD and standard ICD
- H₁: Novel ICD provides superior MACE-free survival at 12 months

**6. Study Design (½ mark)**
- Type: Prospective, multi-center, randomized (2:1), controlled, parallel-arm, non-blinded
- Sample size: n = 200 (133 novel ICD : 67 control ICD) with 15% dropout inflation
- Stratification: By site, age group, and LVEF

**7. Endpoints (½ mark)**
- Primary: MACE-free survival at 12 months post-implantation
- Secondary: All-cause mortality, inappropriate shock rate, device-related complications
- Safety: Lead dislodgement rate, infection rate, generator malfunction

**8. Statistical Analysis Plan (½ mark)**
- Primary analysis: Cox proportional hazards regression, two-sided α = 0.05, 80% power
- Interim analysis at 50% enrollment by DSMB
- Missing data: Multiple imputation method
- Subgroup analyses: Pre-specified by age, sex, and baseline LVEF

**Additional Mandatory Components (Bonus marks):**

**9. Risk-Benefit Analysis (½ mark)**
- Anticipated benefits: Reduced sudden cardiac death, improved QoL
- Residual risks: Infection, lead fracture, inappropriate shocks
- Overall determination: Benefits outweigh residual risks with mitigation

**10. Adverse Event Reporting (½ mark)**
- All AEs recorded in CRF; SAEs reported to EC within 24 hours
- USADEs reported to CA within 10 working days
- DSMB quarterly safety review

**Integration logic (1 mark):** The CIP integrates with the Risk Management File (ISO 14971), the Investigator's Brochure (IB), the Instructions for Use (IFU), and the Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) plan to form a complete regulatory submission package under MDR Annex XV.

---

##### Part (b) — Risk-Benefit Determination & DSMB Role (7 Marks)

**Risk-Benefit Determination Process (4 Marks):**

The risk-benefit determination follows the **5-step framework of ISO 14971:2019** integrated with **MDR Annex I, Section 8** and **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6.5**:

**Step 1: Risk Identification (½ mark)**
All foreseeable hazards are identified from the Risk Management File. For an ICD, these include: lead dislodgement, infection, inappropriate shocks, generator failure, electromagnetic interference.

**Step 2: Risk Estimation (½ mark)**
Each hazard is categorized by:
- Severity (Negligible → Minor → Serious → Critical → Catastrophic per ISO 14971 Annex C)
- Probability of occurrence (Rare → Unlikely → Possible → Likely → Frequent)

**Step 3: Risk Evaluation (½ mark)**
Each estimated risk is compared against the **Risk Acceptability Matrix** defined in the Risk Management Plan. Risks above the acceptability threshold require risk control.

**Step 4: Risk Control (½ mark)**
Risk control measures are applied in the following hierarchy (ISO 14971 Clause 7):
- Inherent safe design (e.g., bipolar leads to reduce EMI)
- Protective measures (e.g., far-field sensing algorithms)
- Information for safety (e.g., MRI conditional labeling)

**Step 5: Overall Residual Risk Evaluation (½ mark)**
The sponsor must conclude that the **overall residual risk is acceptable** when weighed against the **anticipated clinical benefits** (e.g., 30–50% reduction in sudden cardiac death in at-risk patients).

**Determination Statement (½ mark):**
> *"The sponsor concludes that the anticipated clinical benefits of the novel ICD outweigh the residual risks, and the clinical investigation is justified on the basis of this risk-benefit profile."*

**Role of the Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) — 3 Marks:**

1. **Independent Safety Oversight (½ mark):** The DSMB is an independent panel of clinicians, biostatisticians, and ethicists who conduct ongoing safety reviews of accumulating trial data, free from sponsor influence.

2. **Interim Analysis Review (½ mark):** The DSMB reviews pre-planned interim analyses (e.g., at 50% enrollment) to assess whether the device shows clear benefit, harm, or futility, using **predefined stopping rules** (O'Brien-Fleming or Haybittle-Peto boundaries).

3. **Risk-Benefit Reassessment (½ mark):** Based on emerging AE patterns, the DSMB may recommend CIP amendments, additional subject monitoring, suspension, or termination of the investigation. They communicate directly with the EC and regulatory authorities if the risk-benefit profile becomes unfavorable.

4. **Vulnerable Population Protection (½ mark):** For implantable devices like ICDs used in elderly or critically ill populations, the DSMB provides additional scrutiny of subgroup-specific safety signals.

5. **Documentation and Recommendations (½ mark):** The DSMB issues written minutes after each meeting, which are filed in the Trial Master File (TMF) and made available during regulatory inspections.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / June 2023 Style]**

> **Q: A medical device manufacturer intends to conduct a multi-center clinical investigation of a new blood glucose monitoring system for self-testing in diabetic patients.**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **statistical methodology** that must be incorporated in the CIP. Include sample size calculation, randomization, blinding, and handling of protocol deviations.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **informed consent process**, **data monitoring plan**, and **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)** requirements as per ISO 14155:2020 and EU MDR 2017/745.

#### Model Solution for Choice B

##### Part (a) — Statistical Methodology in the CIP (7 Marks)

**1. Sample Size Calculation (2 marks)**

For a two-arm non-inferiority trial comparing the new device to a reference device:
- Primary endpoint: Mean Absolute Relative Difference (MARD) from a reference laboratory method
- Non-inferiority margin: Δ = 5%
- Common standard deviation: σ = 8%
- α = 0.025 (one-sided), Power = 80%, Z_β = 0.842

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{(Z_{\alpha} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{\Delta^2} \\
  &= \frac{(1.96 + 0.842)^2 \cdot 8^2}{5^2} \\
  &= \frac{7.851 \cdot 64}{25} \\
  &= 20.1 \approx 21 \text{ per arm}
\end{aligned}
$$

With 20% dropout inflation: n_adj = 21 / 0.64 = 32.8 ≈ **33 per arm** (Total = 66).

**2. Randomization (2 marks)**
- Method: Centralized, computer-generated block randomization (block size = 4 or 6)
- Stratification: By site, diabetes type (Type 1 vs. Type 2), and HbA1c level (<7% vs. ≥7%)
- Allocation concealment: Sequential sealed envelope or interactive web response system (IWRS)
- Documentation: Randomization log maintained in the Trial Master File

**3. Blinding (1½ marks)**
- Open-label design justified due to device visibility (different physical form factors)
- **Blinded outcome assessment:** Centralized laboratory analysis of blood samples by personnel blinded to device assignment
- **Blinded statistical analysis:** Statistician analyzes data with group labels masked (A vs. B) until database lock

**4. Handling of Protocol Deviations (1½ marks)**
- **Major deviation:** Any deviation that affects subject rights, safety, or data integrity (e.g., inclusion of ineligible subject)
- **Minor deviation:** Deviation that does not affect data validity (e.g., missed visit window by 1 day)
- All deviations recorded in the Deviation Log within 5 working days
- Major deviations require EC notification within 7 days per ISO 14155
- **Per-protocol (PP)** and **intention-to-treat (ITT)** populations pre-specified

---

##### Part (b) — Informed Consent, Monitoring, and CIR (7 Marks)

**1. Informed Consent Process (2½ marks)**

Per **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 7.3** and **MDR Article 63**, the informed consent must include:
- **Written consent form** signed by subject or legally authorized representative
- **Lay-language explanation** of the investigation purpose, procedures, risks, and benefits
- **Voluntariness statement:** Right to withdraw at any time without penalty
- **Data privacy disclosure:** GDPR (if EU), HIPAA (if US), DPDP Act 2023 (if India) compliance
- **Compensation details:** Treatment and compensation for trial-related injury
- **Contact information:** PI, EC, sponsor contact for questions or complaints
- **Re-consent** required if new safety information emerges or CIP is substantially amended

**2. Data Monitoring Plan (2½ marks)**

Per **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 8.2**:
- **Risk-based monitoring:** Frequency and intensity proportional to device risk class
- **Source Data Verification (SDV):** Comparison of CRF entries with original medical records
- **Centralized monitoring:** Statistical outlier detection, site performance metrics
- **On-site monitoring visits:** At initiation, during conduct, and at close-out
- **Clinical Research Associate (CRA)** responsibilities: Verify consent forms, AE reporting, device accountability, regulatory binder
- **Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB)** for Class III devices: Independent safety review at pre-defined intervals

**3. Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) Requirements (2 marks)**

Per **EU MDR Article 77(5)** and **ISO 14155:2020 Clause 9.4**:
- **Submission deadline:** Within **1 year** of end-of-investigation
- **Structure (per ISO 14155 Annex E):**
  - Cover page, synopsis, table of contents
  - Introduction and objectives
  - Methods (design, population, endpoints, statistical methods)
  - Results (subject disposition, demographics, efficacy, safety)
  - Discussion and conclusions
  - Appendices (CRF, IB, statistical analysis output)
- **Regulatory submission:** Filed with the Notified Body (EU), FDA (US), or CDSCO (India) as part of the conformity assessment
- **Public disclosure:** Summary results posted on **ClinicalTrials.gov**, **EudraCT**, or **CTRI (India)** within 12 months of study completion

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating CIP with Investigator's Brochure (IB):** The CIP is the *plan* (the recipe), while the IB is the *product dossier* (the ingredient data sheet). Students frequently mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Missing SADE vs. USADE distinction:** Always specify *anticipation* as the differentiator. SADE is anticipated serious harm; USADE is *unanticipated*. Many students write only "serious" without "unanticipated" and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Reporting timelines must match the regulation cited:** FDA USADE = 10 working days; EU SADE = 15 days; India CDSCO = 10 calendar days. Mixing these loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Sample size formulas:** Always state whether the outcome is continuous (use the σ-based formula) or binary (use the proportion-based formula). Wrong formula = 0 marks even if the arithmetic is correct.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the rounding-up rule:** Sample size must be **rounded UP** (conservative). A student who writes n = 35.33 and concludes 35 instead of 36 loses ½ mark.
>
> 6. **Risk-Benefit analysis is a mandatory section, not optional:** Any CIP answer without it loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Don't forget the PMCF link:** Post-Market Clinical Follow-up is the *post-approval* continuation of the CIP. KTU examiners reward students who mention this transition.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Key Things to Remember — Clinical Investigation Plan for Medical Devices**

### 📌 Core Definitions (Must Recall Verbatim)
- ✅ **CIP** — Document stating rationale, objectives, design, methodology, monitoring, and record-keeping of a clinical investigation
- ✅ **Sponsor** — Individual/organization taking responsibility for initiation/implementation of the CIP
- ✅ **PI (Principal Investigator)** — Qualified person responsible for conducting the CIP at an investigation site
- ✅ **AE** — Any untoward medical occurrence in subjects, users, or other persons
- ✅ **SADE** — Serious adverse device effect (serious + device-related)
- ✅ **USADE** — Unanticipated serious adverse device effect (serious + device-related + **unanticipated**)
- ✅ **PMCF** — Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (post-approval clinical study)

### 📌 Regulatory Framework
- ✅ **ISO 14155:2020** — Global GCP for medical device clinical investigations
- ✅ **EU MDR 2017/745** — Annex XV (CIP requirements), Article 77 (CIR)
- ✅ **21 CFR Part 812 (FDA)** — Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) regulations
- ✅ **21 CFR 812.25** — Specific CIP content requirements (US)
- ✅ **ISO 14971:2019** — Risk Management File integration with CIP
- ✅ **Declaration of Helsinki (2013)** — Ethical principles for human subject research

### 📌 Mandatory CIP Components (The 10 Pillars)
1. Administrative information (sponsor, PI, device)
2. Rationale and background
3. Aim and objectives (primary, secondary)
4. Hypothesis statement (H₀ and H₁)
5. Study design (RCT, single-arm, crossover, adaptive)
6. Endpoints (primary, secondary, safety)
7. Subject selection (inclusion, exclusion, withdrawal criteria)
8. Sample size justification (power calculation)
9. Statistical analysis plan (SAP)
10. Risk-benefit analysis + AE/SADE/USADE reporting procedures

### 📌 Critical Numerical Thresholds (Board Exam Favorites)
- ✅ **α = 0.05** (two-sided) — Standard significance level
- ✅ **Power = 80%** — Standard statistical power (1 − β = 0.80)
- ✅ **Z_0.025 = 1.96** — 95% CI critical value
- ✅ **Z_0.20 = 0.842** — 80% power critical value
- ✅ **15 days** — EU SADE reporting deadline
- ✅ **10 working days** — FDA USADE reporting deadline
- ✅ **1 year** — CIR submission deadline post end-of-investigation
- ✅ **15 years** — Data retention period (EU)
- ✅ **10 years** — Data retention period (FDA)

### 📌 Common Statistical Formulas
- ✅ Sample size (continuous, two-arm): $n = \dfrac{2(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$
- ✅ Sample size (proportion, single-arm): $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{d^2}$
- ✅ 95% CI for proportion: $\hat{p} \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$
- ✅ Risk-Benefit Ratio: $\text{RBR} = \dfrac{\text{Anticipated Benefit}}{\text{Residual Risk}}$

### 📌 Document Linkage (Integration Map)
- ✅ **CIP** ← links to → **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)**
- ✅ **CIP** ← links to → **Investigator's Brochure (IB)**
- ✅ **CIP** ← links to → **Instructions for Use (IFU)**
- ✅ **CIP** ← executes to → **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)**
- ✅ **CIR** ← feeds into → **CE Mark / PMA / 510(k)** submission
- ✅ **CE Mark/PMA** ← transitions to → **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) + PMCF**

### 📌 Top 5 Examiner Buzzwords (Use These to Score High)
- **"ALCOA+ data integrity"** — Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate
- **"Risk-based monitoring"** — Intensity of monitoring proportional to device risk
- **"Pre-specified subgroup analysis"** — Avoid post-hoc bias
- **"Independent DSMB oversight"** — Critical for Class III and implantable devices
- **"Unanticipated serious adverse device effect"** — The key differentiator from SADE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked about the CIP, always begin your answer with the **formal ISO 14155:2020 definition**, then structure your answer using the **5-Module framework** (Administrative → Scientific → Operational → Risk/Ethics → Closure). This guarantees 80%+ marks on a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Good Clinical Practice for Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices (ISO 14155:2011)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 14155:2011 — Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices for Human Subjects — Good Clinical Practice (GCP)** is an international standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that specifies the requirements for the **design, conduct, recording, and reporting of clinical investigations** carried out in human subjects to assess the **clinical performance, effectiveness, and safety** of medical devices.

The standard establishes the **general principles of Good Clinical Practice (GCP)** that protect the **rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects**, while ensuring the **scientific credibility of clinical data** submitted to regulatory authorities for **conformity assessment, CE marking (Europe), and post-market surveillance** of medical devices.

### Scope of ISO 14155:2011
The standard applies to:
- **Pre-market clinical investigations** of medical devices
- **Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF)** studies
- All medical device classifications (Class I, II, III — risk-based)
- **Sponsor**, **Principal Investigator (PI)**, **Investigators**, and **Ethics Committee (EC/IRB)** responsibilities

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ISO 14155 Matters in KTU Context:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme (PEEBT862), this topic is essential because medical device companies must demonstrate **clinical evidence** before commercialization. ISO 14155 is the bridge between **ISO 14971 (Risk Management)** and **ISO 13485 (QMS)** for clinical evaluation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to launch a **new airplane model** for commercial passenger travel. Before flying real passengers, aviation authorities (like DGCA or FAA) require you to:

1. **Test the aircraft systematically** (design a flight test plan)
2. **Get ethical clearance** (approval from safety review boards)
3. **Inform test pilots of all risks** (informed consent)
4. **Monitor and record every anomaly** (adverse event reporting)
5. **Generate a scientific report** demonstrating the aircraft is safe

**ISO 14155:2011 works exactly the same way for medical devices.** Before a **new pacemaker**, **stent**, or **MRI machine** is used on patients, this standard mandates a structured, ethical, and scientifically valid clinical investigation process. The "patients" are the "test pilots," the "Ethics Committee" is the "safety review board," and the "Clinical Investigation Report" is the "final safety certification."

### Key Stakeholders (The "Aircraft Test Team")
| Stakeholder | Real-World Analogy | Role in ISO 14155 |
|---|---|---|
| **Sponsor** | Aircraft manufacturer (Boeing, Airbus) | Initiates, manages, finances the investigation |
| **Principal Investigator** | Chief test pilot | Leads the clinical site, ensures protocol compliance |
| **Ethics Committee (EC)** | Aviation safety board (DGCA/FAA) | Approves the study, protects subject rights |
| **Regulatory Authority** | International aviation body (ICAO) | Reviews data, grants marketing approval |
| **Human Subject** | Passenger (test pilot) | The patient participating under informed consent |

---

## 1.3 Core Definitions (As per ISO 14155:2011)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Definitions You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**

**1. Clinical Investigation (CI):**
Any systematic investigation in one or more human subjects, undertaken to assess the **clinical performance, effectiveness, or safety** of a medical device.

**2. Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP):**
A document that states the **rationale, objectives, design, methodology, statistical considerations, and organization** of a clinical investigation. (Equivalent to a "Protocol" in drug trials).

**3. Sponsor:**
An individual, company, institution, or organization that **takes responsibility for the initiation, management, and/or financing** of a clinical investigation.

**4. Principal Investigator (PI):**
A qualified person **responsible for the conduct of the clinical investigation** at a specific investigation site.

**5. Ethics Committee (EC) / Institutional Review Board (IRB):**
An **independent body** constituted of medical, scientific, and non-scientific members, whose responsibility is to ensure the **protection of the rights, safety, and well-being** of human subjects.

**6. Adverse Event (AE):**
Any untoward medical occurrence, unintended disease or injury, or any untoward clinical sign (including abnormal laboratory findings) in subjects, users, or other persons, whether or not related to the investigational medical device.

**7. Serious Adverse Event (SAE) / Serious Adverse Device Effect (SADE):**
An adverse event that:
- Led to **death**
- Led to **serious deterioration in health** (life-threatening, hospitalization, permanent disability)
- Led to **fetal distress, death, or congenital abnormality**

**8. Investigational Medical Device (IMD):**
A medical device being **assessed for clinical performance, effectiveness, or safety** in a clinical investigation.

**9. Informed Consent:**
A process by which a subject **voluntarily confirms his or her willingness** to participate in a particular investigation, after having been informed of all aspects of the investigation that are relevant to the decision to participate.

**10. Case Report Form (CRF):**
A printed, optical, or electronic document designed to **record all protocol-required information** to be reported to the sponsor on each trial subject.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Two-Part Architecture of ISO 14155:2011

ISO 14155:2011 is structured into **two principal parts** with multiple sub-clauses. Understanding this architecture is critical for KTU exam questions on "Describe the structure of ISO 14155."

### PART 1: General Requirements
Covers the **broad principles** applicable to all clinical investigations:
- **Ethical considerations** (Declaration of Helsinki compliance)
- **Risk-benefit analysis** framework
- **Roles and responsibilities** of sponsor, PI, EC
- **Informed consent** procedures
- **Clinical investigation plan (CIP)** content
- **Clinical investigation report (CIR)** content
- **Records, documentation, and archiving**

### PART 2: Special Considerations for Medical Devices
Addresses **device-specific** aspects:
- **Device accountability** (traceability, lot numbers)
- **Device-related adverse events** (SADE, USADE)
- **Risk-based classification** of investigations
- **Quality management integration** with ISO 13485
- **Usability and human factors** considerations
- **Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF)**

---

## 2.2 The Clinical Investigation Lifecycle (Theoretical Framework)

The ISO 14155 process can be broken down into **7 sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Pre-Investigation Planning
**Why it matters:** A poorly planned study will be rejected by Ethics Committees and regulatory bodies.

**Key activities:**
1. Define **clinical question** and **hypothesis**
2. Conduct **risk analysis** (per ISO 14971)
3. Develop the **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)**
4. Prepare **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** or **Instructions for Use (IFU)**
5. Design **Case Report Forms (CRFs)**
6. Identify **investigation sites** and **Principal Investigators**

### Phase 2: Ethics Committee Approval
**Why it matters:** No clinical investigation can begin **without prior EC/IRB approval**.

**Required submissions to EC:**
- Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)
- Investigator's Brochure
- Informed Consent Form (ICF) — in local language
- Subject information sheet
- Risk-benefit analysis report
- Investigator CVs and qualifications
- Insurance/indemnity documentation
- Device description and labeling

### Phase 3: Regulatory Approval (Country-Specific)
- **India:** CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation) approval under **MDR 2017**
- **Europe:** Competent Authority notification under **EU MDR 2017/745**
- **USA:** FDA **IDE (Investigational Device Exemption)** for significant risk devices

### Phase 4: Investigation Conduct
- **Informed consent** obtained from every subject before any procedure
- **Screening & enrollment** of subjects per inclusion/exclusion criteria
- **Device implantation/use** per protocol
- **Data collection** in CRFs
- **Adverse event monitoring** and reporting
- **Source document verification**

### Phase 5: Monitoring & Quality Assurance
- **Site monitoring visits** by sponsor's CRA (Clinical Research Associate)
- **Source data verification (SDV)**
- **Protocol deviation tracking**
- **Annual safety reports** to EC and regulators
- **Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB)** review for high-risk devices

### Phase 6: Investigation Closure
- **End-of-investigation notification** to EC and regulators
- **Final device accountability** (all used/unused devices accounted for)
- **Database lock** and statistical analysis
- **Subject follow-up** completion
- **Archiving** of essential documents

### Phase 7: Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)
- **Statistical analysis** per pre-defined plan
- **Safety and performance results**
- **Discussion and conclusions**
- **Deviations from protocol** disclosed
- Submitted to **regulatory authority and Ethics Committee**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam cheat sheet for "List" type questions worth 3-5 marks.**

| Element | ISO 14155:2011 Requirement | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ethical Foundation** | Declaration of Helsinki (1964, last revised 2013) | Nuremberg Code for medical research |
| **Risk-Benefit Justification** | Anticipated benefits must justify foreseeable risks | New cardiac stent: risk of thrombosis vs. life-saving benefit |
| **Informed Consent** | Written, voluntary, revocable, in subject's language | Patient signs consent before hip implant trial |
| **Sponsor Responsibilities** | QA, monitoring, regulatory submissions, financing | Medtronic sponsoring a pacemaker trial |
| **PI Qualifications** | Qualified by education, training, experience | Cardiac surgeon for heart valve trial |
| **EC Composition** | Minimum 5 members, both scientific & non-scientific, both genders | Hospital IRB with doctors, lawyers, laypersons |
| **EC Voting** | Majority vote required; member with conflict of interest excluded | EC member with stock in company cannot vote |
| **SADE Reporting** | Within 10 days to EC and regulators (in EU MDR) | Patient dies from implanted device → immediate report |
| **USADE Reporting** | Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect → expedited reporting | Unexpected device failure causing harm |
| **CIR Submission** | Within 1 year of investigation completion (typical) | Final report to CDSCO after device trial ends |
| **Record Retention** | Minimum 10 years (or per local regulation) | Patient data archived for 10+ years post-study |
| **Data Confidentiality** | Subject identity protected per applicable data protection laws | GDPR compliance for European subjects |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For KTU B.Tech students entering the **medical device industry**, mastering ISO 14155 is essential for the following career roles:

- **Clinical Research Associate (CRA):** Monitors sites, verifies data integrity
- **Regulatory Affairs Specialist:** Submits clinical evaluation reports (CER) to regulators
- **Quality Engineer (ISO 13485):** Integrates GCP with QMS processes
- **Biomedical R&D Engineer:** Designs devices with clinical evidence requirements in mind
- **Clinical Evaluation Expert:** Authors the Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) per EU MDR Article 61

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** In 2024-2026, the EU MDR 2017/745 has made **clinical evidence** a strict requirement. ISO 14155-compliant data is now the **gold standard** for any medical device manufacturer seeking global market access.

---

## 2.5 Relationship Between ISO 14155 and Other Standards

ISO 14155 does not exist in isolation. It is part of a **regulatory ecosystem**:

```
ISO 14971 (Risk Management)
        ↓
ISO 13485 (Quality Management System)
        ↓
ISO 14155 (Good Clinical Practice)  ←  THIS TOPIC
        ↓
ISO 14708 (Active Implantable Medical Devices)
        ↓
EU MDR 2017/745 / US FDA 21 CFR Part 812 / India MDR 2017
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "Explain the role of ISO 14155 in the regulatory framework," always position it as the **clinical evidence bridge** between QMS (ISO 13485) and regulatory approval.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Risk-Benefit Analysis Framework (Step-by-Step Logical Derivation)

Since this topic is **regulatory/standards-based** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" here is the **logical decision-tree** used to justify proceeding with a clinical investigation. The KTU examiner often awards marks for **structured logical reasoning**.

### Step 1: Define the Clinical Question
**Logic:** Before any investigation, the sponsor must identify the **unmet medical need** and the **specific clinical question** the device will address.

**Example derivation:**

$$
\text{Clinical Question} = \text{Can Device X reduce mortality in patients with Y by } \geq 20\% \text{ compared to current standard of care?}
$$

### Step 2: Risk Identification (Per ISO 14971)
Identify all **foreseeable risks** associated with the device using ISO 14971's risk management process.

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability of Harm})
$$

| Harm Category | Severity Level | Probability | Risk Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Device malfunction | Moderate | 0.05 | Medium |
| Infection at implant site | Serious | 0.02 | Medium |
| Death from device failure | Catastrophic | 0.001 | Acceptable if mitigated |

### Step 3: Anticipated Benefit Quantification
Estimate the **probable clinical benefit** based on preclinical data, bench testing, and similar device history.

$$
\text{Benefit} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Clinical Outcome}_i \times \text{Patient Population}_i)
$$

### Step 4: Risk-Benefit Comparison
The investigation can proceed **only if**:

$$
\text{Anticipated Clinical Benefit} > \text{Residual Risk (after mitigation)}
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
D = \begin{cases} 
\text{Proceed} & \text{if } \frac{B}{R} \geq 1.0 \\
\text{Reject/Redesign} & \text{if } \frac{B}{R} < 1.0
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Decision outcome
- $B$ = Quantified anticipated benefit
- $R$ = Residual risk after risk control measures

### Step 5: Documentation in the Clinical Investigation Plan
The risk-benefit conclusion must be **explicitly documented** in the CIP and submitted to the Ethics Committee for review.

### Step 6: Ongoing Risk-Benefit Reassessment
Risk-benefit is **not a one-time check**. The sponsor and PI must **continuously monitor** safety data throughout the investigation.

$$
\text{Risk-Benefit Profile}(t) = \text{Re-evaluated at each SAE report and at periodic safety reviews}
$$

---

## 3.2 Informed Consent Process (10-Step Derivation)

The informed consent process in ISO 14155 follows a **strictly sequential 10-step derivation**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** "Explain the informed consent process as per ISO 14155:2011" — This is a guaranteed 7-14 mark question.

| Step | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify potential subject** | Eligibility per inclusion/exclusion criteria |
| 2 | **Provide subject information sheet** | Plain language, in local language, no technical jargon |
| 3 | **Allow adequate time for decision** | Subject must not be rushed; consider vulnerable populations |
| 4 | **Explain study purpose, procedures, duration** | Transparency on what participation entails |
| 5 | **Explain reasonably foreseeable risks and discomforts** | Honesty about device-related risks |
| 6 | **Explain anticipated benefits to subject or others** | Realistic benefit expectations (no over-promising) |
| 7 | **Explain alternative treatments available** | Subject's right to know options |
| 8 | **Explain confidentiality measures** | Data privacy and anonymization |
| 9 | **Explain voluntary nature and right to withdraw** | No coercion; withdrawal at any time without penalty |
| 10 | **Obtain written, dated, signed consent** | Documentation; subject + investigator signatures |

**Logical derivation of consent validity:**

$$
C_{valid} = \text{Disclosed Info} \cap \text{Comprehension} \cap \text{Voluntariness} \cap \text{Capacity} \cap \text{Documentation}
$$

A consent is valid **only if ALL five elements are present**. The absence of any one element renders the consent invalid, potentially invalidating the entire clinical investigation.

---

## 3.3 Adverse Event Classification Matrix

The classification of adverse events in ISO 14155 follows a **decision matrix logic**. The KTU examiner often asks students to classify events. Let us derive the decision tree:

$$
\text{Adverse Event (AE)} \rightarrow \begin{cases} 
\text{Serious?} \begin{cases} \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{SAE} \begin{cases} \text{Device-related?} \begin{cases} \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{SADE} \begin{cases} \text{Anticipated?} \begin{cases} \text{No} \rightarrow \text{USADE} \\ \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{SADE} \end{cases} \\ \text{No} \rightarrow \text{SAE (non-device)} \end{cases} \\ \text{No} \rightarrow \text{AE (non-serious)} \end{cases} \\ \text{No} \rightarrow \text{AE} \end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

**Key definitions in plain language:**

- **AE (Adverse Event):** Any bad thing that happens during the study
- **SAE (Serious Adverse Event):** A bad thing that is serious (death, hospitalization, disability)
- **SADE (Serious Adverse Device Effect):** A serious bad thing **caused by the device**
- **USADE (Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect):** A serious bad thing caused by the device that was **not expected** based on the risk analysis

**Reporting timelines (typical under EU MDR 2017/745, referenced in ISO 14155:2011):**

$$
\text{Reporting Timeline} = \begin{cases} 
\text{USADE} & \rightarrow \text{10 calendar days (expedited)} \\
\text{SADE} & \rightarrow \text{10 calendar days} \\
\text{AE in CIR} & \rightarrow \text{In Clinical Investigation Report (final)} \\
\text{Device deficiency with harm potential} & \rightarrow \text{Expedited (varies by jurisdiction)}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.4 Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) — Required Content Derivation

The CIP is a **regulatory document** with mandatory content sections. For KTU exams, remember the acronym **"RISKS-PAD"**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic: RISKS-PAD** — The 7 mandatory sections of a CIP per ISO 14155:2011

| Letter | Section | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **R** | **Rationale** | Clinical question, unmet need, literature review |
| **I** | **Investigation Objectives** | Primary and secondary endpoints |
| **S** | **Subject Selection** | Inclusion/exclusion criteria, sample size |
| **K** | **Key Procedures** | Study design, device use, follow-up schedule |
| **S** | **Statistical Plan** | Sample size justification, analysis methods |
| **P** | **Procedures for Adverse Event Reporting** | Safety monitoring plan |
| **A** | **Amendments to CIP** | Process for protocol modifications |
| **D** | **Data Management & Record Keeping** | CRF design, data verification, archiving |

---

## 3.5 Sample Size Justification (Statistical Derivation)

Although not a heavy math topic, ISO 14155 requires **scientific justification** of the sample size. The logic is:

$$
n = \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \times 2\sigma^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = Sample size per group
- $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = Z-score for significance level (typically 1.96 for $\alpha = 0.05$)
- $Z_{\beta}$ = Z-score for power (typically 0.84 for 80% power)
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of primary endpoint
- $(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$ = Clinically meaningful difference to detect

**Logic derivation:**
1. Define the **primary endpoint** (e.g., reduction in blood pressure)
2. Determine the **clinically meaningful difference** $\Delta = \mu_1 - \mu_2$
3. Estimate the **variability** $\sigma$ from pilot data
4. Set **significance level** $\alpha$ (typically 0.05) and **power** $1 - \beta$ (typically 0.80)
5. Calculate $n$
6. Add **dropout buffer** (typically 10-20%): $n_{final} = n \times 1.15$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You will NOT be asked to compute $n$ in detail. But if a question asks "Justify sample size in a clinical investigation," mention the **four parameters**: difference to detect, variability, alpha, and power.
