| **Intraocular lens** | PMMA / hydrophobic acrylic | EO | Maintains optical clarity and biocompatibility | ISO 11135 |
| **Wound dressing (hydrocolloid)** | Hydrophilic polymer | Gamma (in sealed pouch) | Cannot withstand heat or moisture | ISO 11137 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Sterile Device Lifecycle (Manufacturing to Use)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Final Assembly] --> B[Pre-Conditioning RH and Temp]
    B --> C[Primary Packaging into Sterile Barrier]
    C --> D[Sealing of Primary Package]
    D --> E[Secondary Packaging into Carton]
    E --> F[Terminal Sterilization]
    F --> G[Sterilization Validation]
    G --> G1[Bioburden Test ISO 11737-1]
    G --> G2[Sterility Test ISO 11737-2]
    G --> G3[Packaging Validation ISO 11607]
    G --> H[Shelf Life Aging Studies]
    H --> I[Distribution and Storage]
    I --> J[Point of Use Aseptic Presentation]
    J --> K[Patient Use]
    G1 --> G
    G2 --> G
    G3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Decision Tree: Sterile vs Non-Sterile Marketing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Medical Device] --> B{Contact with sterile tissue or bloodstream?}
    B -- Yes --> C[MUST be marketed STERILE]
    B -- No --> D{Contact with intact skin only?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Non-Sterile Permitted]
    D -- No --> F{Contact with mucous membrane or non-intact skin?}
    F -- Yes --> G{Manufacturer's validated cleaning required?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Non-Sterile IFU-disinfected]
    G -- No --> I[Sterile strongly recommended]
    C --> J[Select Sterilization Method]
    J --> J1[Steam ISO 17665]
    J --> J2[EO ISO 11135]
    J --> J3[Gamma ISO 11137]
    J --> J4[VHP ISO 22441]
    J --> J5[Aseptic ISO 13408]
    E --> K[Label per ISO 15223-1]
    H --> K
    I --> J
    K --> K1[STRIKETHROUGH STERILE Symbol]
    K --> K2[Non-Sterile Word on Label]
    K --> K3[IFU Disinfection Instructions]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sterile Medical Device Package

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP[Shipping Container]
        direction TB
        C1[Cardboard Corrugated Box]
        C2[Shipping Label and IFU]
        C3[Temperature Indicator and Tracking]
    end
    subgraph SEC[Secondary Packaging]
        direction TB
        S1[Cardboard Carton]
        S2[Product Label]
        S3[IFU Insert]
        S4[Barcode and UDI]
    end
    subgraph PRI[Primary Sterile Barrier]
        direction TB
        P1[Top Web - Tyvek or Medical Paper]
        P2[Bottom Web - Film or Foil]
        P3[Heat Seal - Validated per ASTM F88]
        P4[Device Tray or Pouch]
    end
    subgraph DEV[Medical Device]
        direction TB
        M1[Device Body]
        M2[Biocompatible Surface]
    end
    SHIP --> SEC
    SEC --> PRI
    PRI --> DEV
    DEV --> M1
    DEV --> M2
    PRI --> P1
    PRI --> P2
    PRI --> P3
    PRI --> P4
    SEC --> S1
    SEC --> S2
    SEC --> S3
    SEC --> S4
    SHIP --> C1
    SHIP --> C2
    SHIP --> C3
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Sterilization Validation Lifecycle (IQ OQ PQ)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Requirement Specification] --> B[Design Qualification DQ]
    B --> C[Installation Qualification IQ]
    C --> D[Operational Qualification OQ]
    D --> E[Performance Qualification PQ]
    E --> F[Process Validation Batch]
    F --> G[Bioburden Testing]
    F --> H[Sterility Testing]
    F --> I[Packaging Integrity Test]
    F --> J[Seal Strength Test]
    G --> K[SAL 10-6 Confirmed]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Routine Production]
    L --> M[Annual Revalidation]
    M --> C
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT862]`

> **Define Sterility Assurance Level (SAL). Explain why SAL of 10⁻⁶ is the universally accepted benchmark for terminally sterilized medical devices.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)* is the probability of a single viable microorganism being present on a product after sterilization. It is mathematically defined as:

$$ SAL = 10^{-t/D} $$

**Justification (2 marks):** The SAL of 10⁻⁶ means the probability of a surviving organism is **1 in 1,000,000 devices**. This is accepted because:
1. It is **statistically extremely low** — for a million surgeries, only one device may theoretically carry a survivor.
2. It is the **consensus benchmark** harmonized across ISO 11135, ISO 11137, ISO 17665, the European Pharmacopoeia, the US FDA (21 CFR 880.6900), and the Indian MDR 2017.
3. It provides a **defensible safety margin** against iatrogenic infections for critical devices entering sterile body sites.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT862]`

> **Differentiate between sterile and non-sterile medical devices. When is a device permitted to be marketed as non-sterile?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sterile Device | Non-Sterile Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial status** | SAL ≤ 10⁻⁶, free from viable organisms | May contain microorganisms; not terminally sterilized |
| **Labeling (ISO 15223-1)** | "STERILE" + method (EO, R, H₂O₂) | "STERILE NO" symbol or "Non-Sterile" word |
| **Use case** | Contact with sterile tissue, blood, bone, CSF, internal organs | Contact with intact skin or external body |
| **Examples** | Surgical implants, cardiac catheters, syringes, sutures | Stethoscopes, wheelchairs, external splints, blood-pressure cuffs |
| **Packaging** | Validated sterile barrier (ISO 11607) | No sterile barrier required; clean packaging sufficient |

**When permitted (1 mark):** A device may be marketed non-sterile **only if** its intended use does not involve penetration of intact skin, contact with sterile tissue, or contact with the bloodstream/M CSF. The label must clearly state the device is non-sterile, and the IFU must provide validated cleaning/disinfection instructions for the user (per ISO 17664).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry internal choice. Both Question A and Question B are provided below.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT862, Module 2]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **various terminal sterilization methods** used for medical devices. Compare their mechanisms, applicable standards, material compatibility, and typical applications.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **sterile medical device packaging system** as per **ISO 11607**. List the validation tests required for each packaging layer.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Terminal Sterilization Methods (7 marks):**

The **six major terminal sterilization methods** are:

1. **Moist Heat (Steam) Sterilization — ISO 17665-1 (1.5 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Saturated steam at 121 °C / 15 min (or 134 °C / 3 min) denatures microbial proteins and coagulates DNA.
   - *Application:* Reusable surgical instruments, linens, glassware, metal instruments.
   - *Limitation:* Damages heat-/moisture-sensitive polymers; corrodes carbon steel.

2. **Ethylene Oxide (EO) Sterilization — ISO 11135 (1.5 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Alkylation of microbial DNA/RNA by EO gas at 37–63 °C, 40–80 % RH.
   - *Application:* Catheters, sutures, plastic disposables, electronic implants.
   - *Limitation:* Toxic/carcinogenic residues (EO and ECH); requires long aeration phase; flammable gas.

3. **Gamma Radiation — ISO 11137-1/2 (1 mark)**
   - *Mechanism:* Ionizing gamma photons (⁶⁰Co source) break DNA strands.
   - *Application:* Polymer disposables, gloves, syringes.
   - *Limitation:* Polymer oxidation/embrittlement; expensive source.

4. **Electron Beam (E-beam) — ISO 11137-3 (0.75 mark)**
   - *Mechanism:* High-energy electrons; same DNA damage as gamma, much faster.
   - *Application:* Bulk processing of thin, low-density products.
   - *Limitation:* Limited penetration; not for dense loads.

5. **Hydrogen Peroxide (VHP) — ISO 22441 (0.75 mark)**
   - *Mechanism:* Vaporized H₂O₂ oxidizes membrane lipids and denatures proteins.
   - *Application:* Reusable heat-sensitive devices (endoscopes).
   - *Limitation:* Limited material compatibility; short cycle.

6. **Dry Heat — ISO 20857 (0.5 mark)**
   - *Mechanism:* Oxidation of cell components at 160–180 °C for 2 h.
   - *Application:* Glass, metal, powders, oils.

> **Comparison table (1 mark)** — Already shown in Section 2.3 above. The student should reproduce it in the exam.

---

**(b) Sterile Packaging System per ISO 11607 (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
Shipping Container (Tertiary)
   └── Secondary Packaging
          ├── Product Label (ISO 15223-1 symbols)
          ├── IFU Insert
          └── Barcode / UDI Carrier
                 └── Primary Sterile Barrier
                        ├── Top Web (Tyvek® or Medical Paper)
                        ├── Bottom Web (Film / Foil)
                        ├── Validated Heat Seal
                        └── Device Tray
                               └── Medical Device
```

**Validation tests per packaging layer (4 marks):**

| Layer | Test | Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary sterile barrier** | Seal strength (peel) | ASTM F88 | Quantify peel force, ensure peelability |
| | Seal integrity (dye penetration) | ASTM F1929 | Detect channel defects in seals |
| | Bubble leak test | ASTM F2391 | Detect gross leaks |
| | Helium mass spec leak | ASTM F3039 | Detect sub-micron leaks |
| | Microbial barrier (shelf life) | ASTM F1608 | Ranking of barrier material |
| **Secondary packaging** | Compression test | ASTM D4169 | Survive stacking forces |
| | Drop / impact test | ASTM D4169 | Simulate handling drops |
| | Vibration test | ASTM D4169 | Simulate transport vibration |
| **Tertiary / shipping** | Distribution simulation | ISTA 2A / ASTM D4169 | End-to-end transport simulation |
| | Aging / shelf life | ASTM F1980 (Accelerated) | Predict real-time aging |
| | Package integrity post-aging | ASTM F1929 | Verify SAL maintenance |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT862, Module 2]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of **D value, Z value, and F₀** in sterilization validation. A steam sterilization cycle exposes a device to: 6 min at 118 °C, 12 min at 121.1 °C, and 4 min at 115 °C. Calculate the **F₀ lethality** assuming Z = 10 °C and comment on whether the cycle meets the pharmacopeial minimum of 8 min.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **labeling and regulatory requirements** for non-sterile medical devices as per **ISO 15223-1, ISO 17664, and EU MDR Annex I**. Why are some devices permitted to be non-sterile when others are not?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) D value, Z value, F₀, and numerical calculation (7 marks):**

**Concept definitions (3 marks):**

> **D value (1 mark):** Time required to reduce the microbial population by 90 % (1 log₁₀) at a specified sterilization condition. It quantifies *microbial resistance*.

> **Z value (1 mark):** Temperature change (°C) required to change the D value by a factor of 10. It quantifies the *temperature sensitivity* of the sterilization process.

> **F₀ value (1 mark):** The equivalent time (in minutes) at the reference temperature of 121.1 °C (with Z = 10 °C) required to deliver the same lethality as the actual process. It is the standard measure of *moist heat lethality*.

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

The F₀ formula is:

$$ F_0 = \sum_i 10^{(T_i - 121.1)/Z} \cdot \Delta t_i $$

**Segment 1 — 6 min at 118 °C:**

$$ 10^{(118 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-0.31} = 0.4898 $$
$$ F_{0,1} = 0.4898 \times 6 = 2.939 \text{ min} $$

**Segment 2 — 12 min at 121.1 °C:**

$$ 10^{(121.1 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{0} = 1.000 $$
$$ F_{0,2} = 1.000 \times 12 = 12.000 \text{ min} $$

**Segment 3 — 4 min at 115 °C:**

$$ 10^{(115 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-0.61} = 0.2455 $$
$$ F_{0,3} = 0.2455 \times 4 = 0.982 \text{ min} $$

**Total lethality:**

$$ F_0 = 2.939 + 12.000 + 0.982 = 15.92 \text{ min} $$

**Comment (1 mark):** The cycle delivers **F₀ = 15.92 min**, which **far exceeds the pharmacopeial minimum of F₀ = 8 min**. The cycle is **validated for SAL 10⁻⁶ with a comfortable safety margin** (roughly 2× the minimum).

---

**(b) Labeling and regulatory requirements for non-sterile devices (7 marks):**

**Labeling per ISO 15223-1 (2 marks):**
- The label **must display** the symbol "STERILE NO" (a STERILE label with a diagonal strikethrough) **OR** the words "Non-Sterile".
- This is to **prevent user confusion** that could lead to use of a non-sterile device in a sterile procedure.

**Cleaning/Disinfection IFU per ISO 17664 (2 marks):**
- The IFU must specify the **validated cleaning, disinfection, and (if applicable) sterilization methods** that the user (typically a hospital sterile-services department) must perform **before first use and between uses** (for reusable devices).
- Validated methods include: manual cleaning with specified detergent, automated washer-disinfector cycle, lubrication, inspection, and packaging for re-sterilization.

**EU MDR Annex I (1 mark):** Manufacturers must demonstrate that the **risk of infection is acceptable** and that the device is **safe for its intended purpose**. If the device is non-sterile, the manufacturer must **justify** in the technical file why sterility is not required, supported by **risk analysis** (ISO 14971).

**Why some devices may be non-sterile while others cannot (2 marks):**
- **Non-sterile permitted** if the device contacts only **intact skin or external body surfaces** (e.g., stethoscope, blood-pressure cuff, walking frame, hospital bed). The body's intact skin provides a natural microbial barrier.
- **Sterile mandatory** if the device **penetrates intact skin, contacts sterile tissue, blood, bone, CSF, or the vascular system** (e.g., surgical scalpel, IV cannula, cardiac catheter, intraocular lens, implant). Any microbial contamination in these contexts can cause **iatrogenic sepsis, surgical site infection, or endophthalmitis**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing "clean" with "sterile."** A clean device is *not* a sterile device. Always quote the **SAL 10⁻⁶** figure.
> 2. **Forgetting the Z value** in F₀ calculations. A 1 °C error in temperature becomes a **~26 % error** in lethality (because $10^{1/10} = 1.259$). This is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mixing up ISO numbers.** ISO 11135 = EO; ISO 11137 = Radiation; ISO 17665 = Steam; ISO 22441 = H₂O₂; ISO 11607 = Packaging; ISO 15223-1 = Symbols; ISO 11737 = Microbiological methods. Examiners test this explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting the packaging layer validation tests** in long answers. ISO 11607-2 requires *both* seal integrity and seal strength, plus **transport simulation (ASTM D4169 / ISTA)** — students often mention only one.
> 5. **Forgetting the "STERILE NO" symbol** when describing non-sterile device labeling. This single symbol is a 1-mark trap.
> 6. **Stating SAL as 10⁻⁶ "per product"** — the correct phrasing is *"probability of one viable organism on a single device being one in a million."*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision summary for the day before the exam:**

- **Sterile** = SAL ≤ 10⁻⁶ (one-in-a-million probability of a survivor). **Always quote this number.**
- **Non-sterile** = the device has not been terminally sterilized; the label **must** carry "STERILE NO" or the word "non-sterile."
- **ISO 11135** = Ethylene Oxide; **ISO 11137** = Gamma / E-beam Radiation; **ISO 17665** = Steam (moist heat); **ISO 22441** = Vaporized H₂O₂; **ISO 20857** = Dry Heat; **ISO 13408** = Aseptic processing.
- **D value** = time to reduce microbial load by 1 log; **Z value** = temperature change to alter D by 10×; **F₀** = lethality at 121.1 °C reference.
- **F₀ formula** = $F_0 = \sum 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z} \cdot \Delta t$. Pharmacopeial minimum = **8 min**.
- **ISO 11607** = the **primary standard** for sterile medical device packaging. Part 1 covers materials; Part 2 covers validation.
- **Packaging validation tests**: ASTM F88 (peel), F1929 (dye), F2391 (bubble), F3039 (helium), D4169 (transport simulation), F1980 (accelerated aging), F1608 (microbial barrier ranking).
- **Primary sterile barrier** = Tyvek® / medical paper + film / foil, heat-sealed. **Secondary** = carton + label + IFU. **Tertiary** = shipping container.
- **Critical devices** (contact with sterile tissue/blood) **must** be sterile. **Non-critical devices** (intact skin contact) may be non-sterile.
- **Shelf life** of a sterile device is established by **real-time aging** (years) or **accelerated aging** (ASTM F1980, weeks at elevated temperature). The packaging **must** maintain the sterile barrier for the entire claimed shelf life.
- **Validation lifecycle** = URS → DQ → IQ → OQ → PQ → annual revalidation.
- **Overkill approach** (ISO 11137) assumes worst-case bioburden and delivers ~2× the theoretical minimum dose for safety.
- **Aseptic processing** is required when a device **cannot survive terminal sterilization** (e.g., biologics prefilled syringes, some combination products).
- The **STRIKETHROUGH STERILE symbol (STERILE NO)** is a 1-mark examiner trap — **do not forget it**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Classification of In Vitro Diagnostics and Medical Devices (Active and Non-Active)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Classification of In Vitro Diagnostics and Medical Devices (Active and Non-Active)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Medical Device? (Formal Definition)

According to the **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** and adopted by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)**, a **Medical Device** is defined as:

> *"Any instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, appliance, implant, in vitro reagent, calibrator, software, material or other similar or related article, intended by the manufacturer to be used, alone or in combination, for human beings, for one or more of the specific medical purposes of: diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment or alleviation of disease; diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, alleviation of, or compensation for, an injury; investigation, replacement, modification or support of the anatomy or of a physiological process; supporting or sustaining life; control of conception; disinfection of medical devices; providing information by means of in vitro examination of specimens derived from the human body."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The KTU Module 2 explicitly differentiates between **In Vitro Diagnostics (IVDs)** and general medical devices. They are governed by **separate regulatory frameworks** (e.g., EU IVDR 2017/746 vs. EU MDR 2017/745), and their classification logic is fundamentally different.

## 1.2 What is an In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD)?

An **In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD)** is a medical device, whether used alone or in combination, intended by the manufacturer for the **in vitro examination of specimens derived from the human body** solely or principally to provide information for diagnostic, monitoring, or compatibility purposes.

**Examples**: Blood glucose test strips, HIV test kits, COVID-19 PCR kits, pregnancy test kits, blood typing reagents.

> [!NOTE]
> The term *"in vitro"* literally translates from Latin as *"in glass"* — meaning the test is performed **outside** the living body, in a controlled environment like a test tube or petri dish.

## 1.3 Active vs. Non-Active Medical Devices

### **Active Medical Device**
Any medical device, the operation of which **depends on a source of energy** other than the energy generated by the human body for that purpose, or by gravity, and which acts by changing the density of, or converting, that energy.

> **Examples**: Pacemaker, MRI machine, infusion pump, electrosurgical generator, ventilator, defibrillator.

### **Non-Active Medical Device (Passive Medical Device)**
A medical device whose operation **does not depend on a source of energy** (electrical, mechanical, thermal, etc.). It typically works through the physical/mechanical action of its components or simply as a conduit/container.

> **Examples**: Surgical scalpel, stethoscope, syringe, surgical gloves, orthopedic implant, contact lens, endotracheal tube.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of medical devices like **vehicles on a road**:
- **Non-Active Devices** = **Bicycles** 🚲 — They use human power (or no power) and are simpler, lower-risk vehicles. (e.g., surgical gloves, forceps)
- **Active Medical Devices** = **Motorized Cars** 🚗 — They have an engine (energy source) and therefore can do more, but also pose more risks. (e.g., defibrillators, infusion pumps)
- **Active Therapeutic Devices (with diagnostic/therapeutic risk)** = **Racing Cars** 🏎️ — High-performance, high-risk vehicles that need strict licensing. (e.g., pacemakers, life-support ventilators)

For **IVDs**, think of them as **forensic lab detectives** 🕵️ — they investigate specimens *outside* the body to deliver a verdict (a diagnosis) that influences critical medical decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification is NOT about the device's complexity, but about the RISK it poses to the patient, user, and public health.** This is the foundational principle of KTU Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Classification Critical in Regulatory Science?

Classification determines:
1. **Conformity Assessment Route** — what testing/audits are required.
2. **Level of Regulatory Scrutiny** — Class I needs less oversight than Class III.
3. **Quality Management System Requirements** (e.g., **ISO 13485**).
4. **Clinical Evaluation Requirements**.
5. **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** intensity.
6. **Time-to-Market** and **Cost** for manufacturers.

## 2.2 Classification of In Vitro Diagnostics (IVDs)

The **EU IVDR 2017/746** introduced a **risk-based 4-class system**, replacing the old List-based approach. Each IVD is assigned to one of **4 classes** based on the risk it poses to the individual patient AND public health.

### **IVD Classification Rules (per IVDR 2017/746):**

| Class | Individual Risk | Public Health Risk | Typical Examples |
|:-----:|:----------------|:-------------------|:-----------------|
| **Class A** | Low | Low | General lab reagents, wash buffers, instruments |
| **Class B** | Moderate | Low | Pregnancy self-tests, cholesterol test kits, fertility tests |
| **Class C** | High | Moderate | Cancer marker tests, genetic tests, blood gas analyzers, HLA typing |
| **Class D** | High | **High** | HIV screening, COVID-19 PCR, Hepatitis B/C tests, blood grouping (ABO/Rh), Ebola tests |

> [!NOTE]
> **Public Health Risk** considers the impact on society if the test gives a wrong result. For example, an HIV test in donated blood could endanger the entire blood supply → **Class D**.

## 2.3 Classification of Medical Devices (Active and Non-Active)

The **EU MDR 2017/745** and the **IMDRF** both use a **risk-based classification system**. There are **4 main risk classes**:

| Class | Risk Level | Conformity Assessment (Typical) | Examples |
|:-----:|:-----------|:--------------------------------|:---------|
| **Class I** | Low | Self-declaration (mostly) | Stethoscope, surgical gloves, walking frames, tongue depressors |
| **Class IIa** | Low–Medium | Notified Body involvement (sterility, measurement) | Hearing aids, contact lens cleaners, surgical instruments with measuring function |
| **Class IIb** | Medium–High | Notified Body (full QMS audit) | Ventilators, infusion pumps, X-ray machines, surgical lasers |
| **Class III** | High | Notified Body + Design Examination + Consultations | Pacemakers, implantable defibrillators, heart valves, coronary stents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Medical Devices follow additional special rules** under the MDR (Annex VIII, Chapter III, Rules 9-13). These rules differentiate **Active Devices for Diagnosis/Monitoring** (Rules 10-11) from **Active Devices for Therapy** (Rules 9, 12, 13).

## 2.4 Special Classification Rules for ACTIVE Medical Devices

| Rule | Description | Class |
|:----:|:------------|:------|
| **Rule 9** | Active devices intended to administer or remove substances (energy, biological fluids) | Generally Class **IIa**; if hazardous, **IIb**; if life-supporting, **III** |
| **Rule 10** | Active devices for **diagnosis/monitoring** (e.g., imaging, vital sign monitoring) | Class **IIa** |
| **Rule 11** | Active devices intended to emit ionizing radiation for diagnostic/therapeutic purposes | Class **IIb** |
| **Rule 12** | Active devices intended to control/monitor performance of Class IIb active therapeutic devices | Class **IIb** |
| **Rule 13** | Active devices intended to control/monitor performance of Class III active devices, or directly influence their performance | Class **III** |

## 2.5 Non-Active (Passive) Medical Devices — Key Rules

| Rule | Description | Class |
|:----:|:------------|:------|
| **Rule 1** | Non-invasive devices that contact intact skin (e.g., bandages, electrodes) | Class **I** |
| **Rule 2** | Non-invasive devices for channeling/storage (e.g., IV cannulas, urine bags) | Class **I** or **IIa** |
| **Rule 3** | Non-invasive devices modifying biological composition (e.g., blood dialyzers) | Class **IIb** or **III** |
| **Rule 4** | Non-invasive devices in contact with injured skin | Class **I, IIa, or IIb** |
| **Rule 5** | Invasive devices via body orifices (short-term) | Class **I, IIa** |
| **Rule 6** | Surgically invasive (transient/short-term) | Class **IIa, IIb** |
| **Rule 7** | Surgically invasive (long-term / implantable) | Class **IIb, III** |
| **Rule 8** | Implantable + active devices (e.g., pacemakers) | Class **III** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trick**: When a device can be classified under **multiple rules**, the **highest applicable class** prevails. This is a common valuation trap!

## 2.6 KTU "Risk Score" Framework for Classification

Although classification is qualitative, the **IMDRF risk model** can be expressed as a conceptual weighted formula:

$$R_{total} = (P \times H) + (P_{pub} \times H_{pub})$$

Where:
- $R_{total}$ = Total Regulatory Risk Score
- $P$ = Probability of harm to the **individual**
- $H$ = Severity of harm to the **individual**
- $P_{pub}$ = Probability of harm to **public health**
- $H_{pub}$ = Severity of harm to **public health**

> The higher the $R_{total}$, the higher the device class (I → IIa → IIb → III for MDs; A → B → C → D for IVDs).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

For biomedical engineers, classification directly impacts:
- **Design Controls** (verification & validation depth)
- **Material selection** (biocompatibility testing per **ISO 10993**)
- **Software lifecycle** (per **IEC 62304**) for active devices
- **Cybersecurity requirements** for active connected devices
- **Regulatory submission strategy** in target markets (US FDA, EU, CDSCO India)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Classification Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Classification Decision Tree (Active vs Non-Active vs IVD)

Let us walk through the **exhaustive analytical logic** for classifying any medical product.

### **Step 1: Product Identification**
Determine whether the product is:
- An **IVD** (examines specimens *in vitro*)? OR
- A **General Medical Device** (acts on/in the body)?

If **IVD** → Go to **IVD Classification (Section 3.2)**.
If **Medical Device** → Go to **Step 2**.

### **Step 2: Determine Active vs. Non-Active**
Ask: *"Does the device rely on an external energy source (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical) for its operation, OTHER than the human body or gravity?"*

- **YES** → **Active Medical Device** → Apply Rules 9-13 (MDR)
- **NO** → **Non-Active (Passive) Medical Device** → Apply Rules 1-8 (MDR)

### **Step 3: Determine Duration of Use**

$$\text{Duration} = \begin{cases} \text{Transient} & (\text{continuous use} < 60 \text{ min}) \\ \text{Short-term} & (60 \text{ min} \leq \text{continuous use} \leq 30 \text{ days}) \\ \text{Long-term} & (\text{continuous use} > 30 \text{ days}) \end{cases}$$

### **Step 4: Determine Invasiveness**

$$\text{Contact Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Non-invasive} & (\text{does not penetrate body}) \\ \text{Invasive (orifice)} & (\text{via natural opening}) \\ \text{Surgically invasive} & (\text{via surgical incision}) \\ \text{Implantable} & (\text{retained after procedure}) \end{cases}$$

### **Step 5: Apply Risk Hierarchy**

$$C_{final} = \max(C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_n)$$

where $C_i$ is the class assigned by each applicable rule, and **the highest class always wins**.

## 3.2 Detailed IVD Classification Logic (IVDR 2017/746)

The IVDR uses **7 rules** combined with risk assessment. Let us derive the classification stepwise:

$$\text{IVD Class} = f(\text{Individual Risk}, \text{Public Health Risk}, \text{Intended Purpose})$$

### **Application Steps:**

1. **Identify the intended purpose** (screening, diagnosis, monitoring, prognosis, predisposition, treatment monitoring, blood safety).
2. **Identify if the IVD is in Annex II (List A/old List B)** — these are typically **Class C or D**.
3. **Apply the 7 rules sequentially**:

| Rule | IVD Type | Class |
|:----:|:---------|:------|
| 1 | Reagents/instruments for blood grouping (ABO, Rh, Kell, Duffy, Kidd) | **D** |
| 2 | Tissue typing (HLA) for transplantation | **C** |
| 3 | Infectious agents with high public health risk (HIV, HBV, HCV, TB, SARS-CoV-2, etc.) | **D** |
| 4 | Cancer screening/diagnosis | **C** |
| 5 | Genetic testing (hereditary, predictive) | **C** |
| 6 | Companion diagnostics | **C** |
| 7 | All other IVDs | **B** (or **A** if no direct patient contact) |

> [!NOTE]
> **IVD Class A** is reserved for **"products for general laboratory use"** — buffers, staining solutions, wash solutions, and instruments themselves. These are NOT considered IVDs in the strictest sense.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Classifying an ECG Machine

**Device**: 12-lead Electrocardiograph (ECG)

### **Step 1: Active or Non-Active?**
The ECG requires electrical power to operate and to detect/amplify the heart's electrical signals. **→ ACTIVE DEVICE**

### **Step 2: Apply Rule 10 (Active Diagnostic Device)**
*"Active devices intended for diagnosis and monitoring are Class IIa."*

### **Step 3: Check for Rule Escalators**
- Is the ECG intended to monitor vital physiological parameters where the nature of variations is such that it could result in **immediate danger** to the patient? (e.g., real-time cardiac monitoring in ICU)
- If **YES** → **Class IIb**
- If **NO** → **Class IIa**

### **Step 4: Final Classification**
A standard 12-lead ECG used in a clinic for routine diagnosis → **Class IIa**.
A continuous bedside cardiac monitor in ICU → **Class IIb**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight**: Always check if a device's monitoring could result in **"immediate danger"** — this is a frequently tested phrase in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Classifying a Glucose Self-Test Strip

**Device**: Home blood glucose monitoring test strip

### **Step 1: Is it an IVD?**
The strip examines a specimen (blood) *in vitro* to provide diagnostic information. **→ YES, IVD**

### **Step 2: Apply IVDR Rules**
- Is it for blood grouping? **No**
- Is it for HLA typing? **No**
- Is it for high-risk infectious disease? **No**
- Is it for cancer? **No**
- Is it a genetic test? **No**
- Is it a companion diagnostic? **No**
- It falls under **"All other IVDs"** → **Rule 7** → **Class B**

> A home-use self-test strip is a **Class B** IVD.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Reference for KTU Exam

| Device | Active/Non-Active | Class (MDR) | Class (IVDR) |
|:-------|:------------------|:-----------:|:------------:|
| Surgical Gloves | Non-Active | I | — |
| Stethoscope | Non-Active | I | — |
| Pacemaker | Active | III | — |
| Defibrillator | Active | IIb/III | — |
| Hearing Aid | Active | IIa | — |
| MRI Machine | Active | IIb | — |
| Pregnancy Test | — | — | B |
| HIV ELISA Kit | — | — | D |
| Blood Glucose Strip | — | — | B |
| COVID-19 RT-PCR Kit | — | — | D |
| Insulin Pump | Active | IIb | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart: Medical Device Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Product] --> B{Is it used in vitro on specimens?}
    B -- Yes --> C[IVD Classification Path]
    B -- No --> D{Medical Device}
    C --> C1{Class A general lab use?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[Class A IVD]
    C1 -- No --> C3{Class D blood/infectious risk?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Class D IVD]
    C3 -- No --> C5{Class C cancer/genetic/companion?}
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Class C IVD]
    C5 -- No --> C7[Class B IVD Default]
    D --> E{Does it use external energy?}
    E -- No --> F[Non-Active Device]
    E -- Yes --> G[Active Device]
    F --> F1{Apply Rules 1-8}
    F1 --> F2{Is it implantable long term?}
    F2 -- Yes --> F3[Class III]
    F2 -- No --> F4{Short term invasive?}
    F4 -- Yes --> F5[Class IIa or IIb]
    F4 -- No --> F6[Class I Default]
    G --> G1{Apply Rules 9-13}
    G1 --> G2{Is it for diagnosis Rule 10?}
    G2 -- Yes --> G3[Class IIa or IIb]
    G2 -- No --> G4{Is it therapeutic Rule 9?}
    G4 -- Yes --> G5[Class IIa or IIb or III]
    G4 -- No --> G6{Life supporting Rule 13?}
    G6 -- Yes --> G7[Class III]
    G6 -- No --> G8[Class IIa Default]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Active vs Non-Active Device Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph active_block["ACTIVE DEVICE SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Energy Source Battery or Mains] --> A2[Control Unit Microcontroller MCU]
        A2 --> A3[Sensor Module e.g. ECG electrode]
        A2 --> A4[Actuator Module e.g. Defibrillator pulse]
        A2 --> A5[Display Output LCD or LED]
        A2 --> A6[Communication BLE WiFi]
    end
    subgraph passive_block["NON-ACTIVE DEVICE SUBSYSTEM"]
        P1[Material Substrate e.g. Titanium polymer] --> P2[Mechanical Form e.g. Scalpel stent]
        P2 --> P3[Surface Treatment e.g. Coated lubricated]
        P2 --> P4[Patient Interface e.g. Implanted cuff]
    end
    subgraph ivd_block["IVD DEVICE SUBSYSTEM"]
        V1[Specimen Collection e.g. Blood swab] --> V2[Reagent Chemistry e.g. Antibodies enzymes]
        V2 --> V3[Reaction Chamber Strip cartridge well]
        V3 --> V4[Signal Reader Optical electrical]
        V4 --> V5[Result Output Qualitative Quantitative]
    end
```

## 4.3 Risk-Based Hierarchy Pyramid (Visualizing the Classification Levels)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Class III or D Highest Risk Pacemaker HIV Test] --> P2[Class IIb or C Moderate-High Ventilator Cancer Test]
    P2 --> P3[Class IIa or B Low-Medium Hearing Aid Pregnancy Test]
    P3 --> P4[Class I or A Lowest Risk Stethoscope Lab Buffer]
    P1 --- P1L["Conformity Notified Body Design Examination"]
    P2 --- P2L["Conformity Notified Body QMS Audit"]
    P3 --- P3L["Conformity Partial Notified Body"]
    P4 --- P4L["Conformity Self Declaration Only"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **width of each tier** in the regulatory pyramid represents the *volume* of devices in the market. Note that **Class I/A** devices dominate the market in numbers, but **Class III/D** devices dominate in regulatory scrutiny.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an Active Medical Device with a suitable example. Differentiate it from a Non-Active device.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Active Medical Device** is any device whose operation **depends on a source of energy** other than the energy generated by the human body or by gravity, and which acts by changing the density of or converting that energy. *(1 mark)*

**Example**: A pacemaker (uses battery power to deliver electrical impulses to the heart). *(1 mark)*

**Differentiation from Non-Active Device**:

| Parameter | Active Device | Non-Active Device |
|:----------|:--------------|:------------------|
| Energy Source | External (electrical/mechanical) | None or body-derived |
| Control | Internal electronics/software | Mechanical/physical |
| Example | Ventilator | Surgical scalpel |
| Risk | Generally higher | Generally lower | *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD)? Classify IVDs into four risk-based classes as per IVDR 2017/746.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **IVD** is a medical device intended by the manufacturer for the **in vitro examination of specimens** derived from the human body to provide information for diagnosis, monitoring, or compatibility purposes. *(1 mark)*

**IVD Classification as per IVDR 2017/746** *(2 marks)*:

1. **Class A** – Low individual & public health risk (e.g., wash buffers, lab instruments).
2. **Class B** – Moderate individual / low public health risk (e.g., pregnancy tests, glucose strips).
3. **Class C** – High individual / moderate public health risk (e.g., cancer markers, HLA typing).
4. **Class D** – High individual & public health risk (e.g., HIV screening, blood grouping).

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: Discuss the classification of Medical Devices based on risk as per EU MDR 2017/745. Explain the classification rules for active medical devices with examples. Detail the conformity assessment route for Class IIa vs Class III devices.**

#### **Part (a) — Risk-Based Classification of Medical Devices (7 Marks)**

The **EU MDR 2017/745** classifies medical devices into **four risk-based categories** based on:
- Duration of use (transient, short-term, long-term)
- Invasiveness (non-invasive, invasive, surgically invasive, implantable)
- Body system affected
- Energy source (active vs non-active)

**The four classes are:**

1. **Class I — Low Risk** *(2 marks)*
   - Non-invasive, short-term use, minimal patient contact.
   - Examples: Stethoscopes, surgical gloves, walking aids, tongue depressors.
   - Conformity: Mostly **self-declaration** by the manufacturer.

2. **Class IIa — Low-to-Medium Risk** *(1 mark)*
   - Short-term invasive or active diagnostic devices.
   - Examples: Hearing aids, contact lens cleaners, ECG machines.
   - Conformity: Notified Body assessment of **sterility** or **measurement**.

3. **Class IIb — Medium-to-High Risk** *(1 mark)*
   - Long-term invasive, surgical, or active therapeutic devices.
   - Examples: Ventilators, infusion pumps, surgical lasers, X-ray machines.
   - Conformity: **Full Notified Body QMS audit** required.

4. **Class III — High Risk** *(2 marks)*
   - Implantable, life-supporting, or life-sustaining.
   - Examples: Pacemakers, heart valves, coronary stents, implantable defibrillators.
   - Conformity: **Notified Body + Design Examination + Expert Consultations** + **Clinical Investigation**.

> If a device fits multiple rules, the **higher class prevails** (the "precautionary principle"). *(1 mark)*

#### **Part (b) — Active Device Classification Rules with Examples (7 Marks)**

Active medical devices are governed by **Rules 9-13** of MDR Annex VIII. *(1 mark)*

**Rule 9: Active Devices for Administering/Removing Substances**
- General: **Class IIa**
- Hazardous substances: **Class IIb**
- Life-supporting substances: **Class III**
- Examples: *(1 mark)*
  - **Class IIa**: Infusion pump (normal saline delivery)
  - **Class IIb**: Infusion pump delivering chemotherapy (hazardous)
  - **Class III**: Heart-lung machine oxygenator

**Rule 10: Active Devices for Diagnosis/Monitoring**
- Standard: **Class IIa**
- "Immediate danger" risk: **Class IIb**
- Examples: *(1 mark)*
  - **Class IIa**: Standard 12-lead ECG
  - **Class IIb**: ICU cardiac bedside monitor

**Rule 11: Devices Emitting Ionizing Radiation (Therapeutic/Diagnostic)**
- All classified as **Class IIb**
- Examples: Diagnostic X-ray, linear accelerators *(1 mark)*

**Rule 12: Devices Controlling/Monitoring Class IIb Active Therapeutic Devices**
- **Class IIb**
- Examples: External control units for surgical lasers *(1 mark)*

**Rule 13: Devices Controlling Class III or Life-Sustaining Devices**
- **Class III**
- Examples: Smart insulin pump controller (closed-loop artificial pancreas) *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - Students often **confuse Rule 10 and Rule 11** (both relate to diagnosis, but Rule 11 specifically involves **ionizing radiation**).
> - A common error is classifying all active devices as Class IIa — **fail to remember that Rule 9 escalates to Class III** for life-supporting substance administration.
> - Always remember: **if multiple rules apply, pick the HIGHEST class**. Examiners specifically look for this understanding.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: Explain the classification of In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD) medical devices as per IVDR 2017/746. Discuss the 4 risk classes with examples and outline the conformity assessment requirements for Class A vs Class D IVDs. Briefly contrast IVD classification with that of general medical devices.**

#### **Part (a) — IVD Classification under IVDR 2017/746 (7 Marks)**

The **EU IVDR 2017/746** replaced the older Directive 98/79/EC. It adopts a **risk-based 4-tier classification** that considers both **individual patient risk** and **public health risk**. *(1 mark)*

**The Four Classes:**

1. **Class A — Lowest Risk** *(1 mark)*
   - Low individual & public health risk.
   - **Examples**: General laboratory reagents, wash buffers, staining solutions, instruments like pipettes, automated analyzers (when used as standalone IVDs).
   - **Note**: These are often "ancillary" to the actual diagnostic process.

2. **Class B — Low-to-Moderate Risk** *(1 mark)*
   - Moderate individual risk / low public health risk.
   - **Examples**: Blood gas analysis, self-test pregnancy kits, cholesterol test kits, fertility hormones, urine test strips.

3. **Class C — High Individual / Moderate Public Risk** *(2 marks)*
   - Used when the test result has **high clinical impact** on individual patients.
   - **Examples**: Cancer screening/monitoring tests, genetic testing (hereditary), HLA typing for transplantation, companion diagnostics, prenatal screening for Down syndrome.

4. **Class D — Highest Risk** *(2 marks)*
   - High individual & high public health risk.
   - **Examples**: HIV screening in blood donations, Hepatitis B/C tests, blood grouping (ABO/Rh/Kell), Ebola diagnostics, SARS-CoV-2 PCR tests used for screening.

> **Public Health Risk** = potential impact on society if the device fails (e.g., contaminated blood entering the supply chain). *(Bonus understanding point)*

#### **Part (b) — Conformity Assessment & Contrast with Medical Devices (7 Marks)**

**Conformity Assessment: Class A vs Class D IVDs** *(4 marks)*

| Requirement | Class A | Class D |
|:------------|:--------|:--------|
| Manufacturer Self-Declaration | ✅ Yes (mostly) | ❌ No |
| Notified Body Involvement | ❌ Generally no | ✅ **Mandatory** |
| Batch Testing of IVDs | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** (serological reagents) |
| Common Specifications (CS) | ❌ Rare | ✅ **Mandatory** |
| Clinical Evidence Requirements | Low | **Extensive** |
| EU Reference Laboratory (EURL) Consultation | ❌ No | ✅ **Required for Class D** |
| Post-Market Performance Follow-up (PMPF) | Basic | **Intensive + Periodic Safety Update Report** |

> For **Class D IVDs**, samples must be tested by the **EU Reference Laboratory (EURL)** before market release, especially for high-risk infectious disease and blood grouping reagents.

**Contrast with General Medical Device Classification** *(3 marks)*

| Aspect | Medical Devices (MDR) | IVDs (IVDR) |
|:-------|:---------------------|:-----------|
| Governing Law | EU MDR 2017/745 | EU IVDR 2017/746 |
| Class Names | I, IIa, IIb, III | A, B, C, D |
| Classification Basis | Duration, invasiveness, energy | Individual + public health risk |
| Notified Body Use | Class IIa, IIb, III | Class B, C, D |
| Special Consultations | Expert panels, clinical | EU Reference Laboratory (EURL) |
| Companion Diagnostics | N/A | Treated as **Class C** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - Students frequently **miscount the classes** (saying "3 classes" instead of 4). Always state **four** distinct classes.
> - **Conflating "List A" and "List B"** of the old IVDD with current IVDR classes — these are **no longer used** in the new regulation.
> - Forgetting to mention **Public Health Risk** as a separate axis in IVD classification — this is the key differentiator from general MD classification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ✅ **IVD Definition**: In vitro examination of human specimens for diagnostic info.
- ✅ **Active Device**: Requires external energy (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical) other than human body or gravity.
- ✅ **Non-Active Device**: Operates without external energy; relies on mechanical/physical properties.
- ✅ **Medical Device Classification (MDR)**: **4 classes** — I (low), IIa, IIb, III (high).
- ✅ **IVD Classification (IVDR)**: **4 classes** — A (low), B, C, D (high).
- ✅ **Classification is RISK-BASED**, not complexity-based.
- ✅ **Public Health Risk** is the **unique axis** in IVD classification (absent in MD classification).
- ✅ **Active Device Rules (MDR)**: Rules 9-13 govern them.
- ✅ **Non-Active Device Rules (MDR)**: Rules 1-8 govern them.
- ✅ **Rule 9** — Active substance administration (IIa/IIb/III based on hazard).
- ✅ **Rule 10** — Active diagnosis (IIa; IIb if "immediate danger").
- ✅ **Rule 11** — Ionizing radiation (IIb).
- ✅ **Rule 12** — Controlling Class IIb active therapeutic (IIb).
- ✅ **Rule 13** — Controlling Class III active devices (III).
- ✅ **When multiple rules apply, the HIGHER class wins** — examiners love this.
- ✅ **Conformity Assessment** is **strictest for Class III/D** (Notified Body + Design Examination).
- ✅ **EU IVDR 2017/746** replaced Directive 98/79/EC — the new framework is from **May 2022** (with transition periods).
- ✅ **EU MDR 2017/745** replaced Directive 93/42/EEC — full application since **May 2021**.
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize**:
  - Class I: Stethoscope, gloves.
  - Class IIa: Hearing aid, ECG.
  - Class IIb: Ventilator, infusion pump.
  - Class III: Pacemaker, heart valve.
  - Class A IVD: Wash buffer.
  - Class B IVD: Glucose strip.
  - Class C IVD: Cancer marker test.
  - Class D IVD: HIV test, blood grouping.
- ✅ **IMDRF = International Medical Device Regulators Forum** (global harmonization body).
- ✅ **ISO 13485** = global QMS standard for medical devices.
- ✅ **ISO 14971** = risk management standard for medical devices.

> **Final Exam Tip**: In KTU 2024, expect either a **14-mark question comparing active vs non-active** OR a **14-mark question on IVD classification** as a full sub-part. Always structure your answer as: **Definition → Rules → Examples → Conformity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulatory Approval Process for Medical Devices: Comparison of US FDA, CE, and Indian Medical Device Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regulatory Approval Process for Medical Devices: Comparison of US FDA, CE, and Indian Medical Device Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Regulatory Approval Process for Medical Devices** is the structured, risk-based legal and administrative framework through which national or regional authorities evaluate, classify, and authorize the market entry of medical devices to ensure they meet defined standards of **safety, efficacy, quality, and performance** before being placed in commercial circulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Regulatory approval is the legally mandated pre-market authorization that grants a manufacturer the right to commercially distribute a medical device within a specific jurisdiction, based on a risk-stratified review of technical, clinical, biocompatibility, and quality system data.

The three dominant regulatory regimes studied in this module are:

1. **US FDA (Food and Drug Administration)** — Governed by the *Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act)*, **21 CFR Part 820** (Quality System Regulation), and **21 CFR Part 807** (Establishment Registration and Device Listing).
2. **CE Marking (European Conformity)** — Governed by **EU MDR 2017/745** (Medical Device Regulation) and **EU IVDR 2017/746**, replacing the former MDD 93/42/EEC.
3. **Indian Medical Device Rules (MDR 2017)** — Notified by the Central Government under the *Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (amended in 2017)* and administered by **CDSCO** (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Driver's License Analogy"**

Imagine medical devices as **motor vehicles**, and regulators as **regional transport authorities**:

- The **US FDA** is like the **United States DMV** — it scrutinizes every vehicle (device) carefully before it can hit the road. Cars are categorized (sedan, SUV, truck = Class I, II, III). Some cars only need a basic registration (**510(k)** clearance showing they are "substantially equivalent" to an already approved car), while brand-new trucks carrying hazardous materials need a full road-test (**PMA — Premarket Approval**).

- The **CE Mark** is like the **European Union's pan-EU driving permit**. A car manufacturer self-certifies that the vehicle meets EU safety standards, but a third-party testing agency (the **Notified Body**, like TÜV SÜD or BSI) verifies the claim for higher-risk vehicles. Once CE marked, the car can travel across all EU member states.

- The **Indian Medical Device Rule (MDR 2017)** is like the **new Indian RTO system under the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act** — earlier, medical devices were loosely tracked like old bicycles. Now, with formal classification (Class A, B, C, D), registration, and audit requirements, the regulator (CDSCO) brings order to a previously chaotic space, aligned with global standards but tailored to Indian needs.

> **Bottom line:** All three systems share the same goal — *patient safety* — but differ in **who reviews** (regulator vs. third party vs. self-certification), **how rigorously** (full clinical vs. equivalence vs. self-declaration), and **how risk is stratified** (Class I/II/III vs. Class I/IIa/IIb/III vs. Class A/B/C/D).

---

## 1.3 Foundational Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Terms — Memorize for Part A (3-Mark) Questions**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Predicate Device** | A legally marketed device used as a benchmark for substantial equivalence in 510(k) submissions. |
| **Substantial Equivalence (SE)** | Demonstration that a new device has the same intended use and either same technological characteristics, or different characteristics that do not raise new questions of safety/effectiveness. |
| **PMA** | Premarket Approval — the most stringent FDA review, requiring independent scientific evidence of safety and effectiveness. |
| **De Novo Classification** | FDA pathway for novel low-to-moderate risk devices with no predicate. |
| **Notified Body (NB)** | Third-party organization accredited by an EU member state to perform conformity assessments under MDR 2017/745. |
| **Conformity Assessment** | The procedure verifying that a device meets regulatory requirements. |
| **Authorized Representative (AR)** | A European-based legal entity designated by non-EU manufacturers to act on their behalf for CE marking. |
| **MD 2017** | Indian Medical Device Rules, notified on 31 January 2017, effective 1 January 2018. |
| **CDSCO** | Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation — apex regulatory body under DCGI, Ministry of Health, India. |
| **GSL / MD-15** | Form numbers under Indian MDR 2017 for licenses and registrations. |

---

## 1.4 The Core Regulatory Triangle

The entire global medical device regulatory landscape can be visualized as a **risk-stratified triangle** where:

- The **width** of the regulatory burden corresponds to the **inverse of risk**: low-risk devices face lighter scrutiny, high-risk devices face the heaviest.
- All three systems adopt the **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** risk-based classification philosophy: **higher risk → greater regulatory control**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk-Based Classification Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Regulatory Axiom (GHTF/IMDRF):**
> *"The level of regulatory control applied to a medical device shall be proportionate to the level of risk posed by that device to patients, users, and the public."*

This single axiom governs the design of all three regulatory regimes. Classification is determined by evaluating:

1. **Duration of contact** (transient < 60 min, short-term 60 min – 30 days, long-term > 30 days).
2. **Invasiveness** (non-invasive, body orifice, surgically invasive, implant).
3. **Anatomical location** (skin, mucosal membrane, central circulatory system, central nervous system).
4. **Energy delivery** (diagnostic vs. therapeutic, ionizing vs. non-ionizing radiation).
5. **Active vs. non-active** (powered vs. unpowered).
6. **Patient population vulnerability** (neonates, pregnant women, immunocompromised).

---

## 2.2 US FDA — Detailed Pathway Analysis

The FDA classifies devices into **three regulatory classes** under **21 CFR § 860**:

### 2.2.1 Class I — General Controls
- **Risk level:** Low
- **Examples:** Elastic bandages, tongue depressors, stethoscopes, examination gloves.
- **Submission requirement:** Most are **510(k)-exempt** (~47% of all Class I devices).
- **Manufacturing requirement:** GMP per **21 CFR Part 820** (Quality System Regulation).

### 2.2.2 Class II — General Controls + Special Controls
- **Risk level:** Moderate
- **Examples:** Infusion pumps, surgical drapes, blood pressure monitors, contact lens solutions, MRI machines.
- **Submission requirement:** **510(k) Premarket Notification** (most common pathway).
- **Special controls may include:** Performance standards, post-market surveillance, special labelling, guidance documents.

### 2.2.3 Class III — General Controls + Premarket Approval
- **Risk level:** High
- **Examples:** Implantable pacemakers, heart valves, coronary stents, deep brain stimulators, silicone gel breast implants.
- **Submission requirement:** **PMA** — rigorous scientific review including clinical trials under **IDE (Investigational Device Exemption, 21 CFR Part 812)**.

### 2.2.4 The 510(k) Decision Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **FDA 510(k) Substantial Equivalence Test**

A device is "substantially equivalent" (SE) if it meets **ALL THREE** of the following:

$$
\text{SE Decision} = (\text{Same Intended Use}) \land \Big[(\text{Same Technological Characteristics}) \lor
$$
$$
(\text{Different Technological Characteristics} \land \text{No New Questions of Safety/Effectiveness} \land
$$
$$
\text{Demonstrates Substantial Equivalence to Predicate})\Big]
$$

If SE is **NOT** demonstrated → device is **NSE (Not Substantially Equivalent)** → manufacturer must file **De Novo** or **PMA**.

### 2.2.5 FDA Pre-Submission Meeting (Pre-Sub)

Manufacturers can request a **Pre-Submission meeting** (Q-Sub program, FDA Guidance 2023) to obtain FDA feedback before submitting a 510(k), De Novo, or PMA. This is **not mandatory** but is **strongly recommended** for novel or high-risk devices.

### 2.2.6 FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR)

**21 CFR Part 820** mandates:
- Design controls (820.30)
- Document controls (820.40)
- Purchasing controls (820.50)
- Identification and traceability (820.60–820.86)
- Production and process controls (820.70)
- Acceptance activities (820.80)
- Nonconforming product (820.90)
- Corrective and Preventive Actions — **CAPA (820.100)**
- Labeling and packaging controls (820.120–820.130)
- Handling, storage, distribution (820.140–820.160)
- Records (820.180–820.198)
- Servicing (820.200)
- Statistical techniques (820.250)

> The FDA is transitioning from QSR to **Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)** harmonized with **ISO 13485:2016**, with compliance expected by **February 2026** (FDA Final Rule, Jan 2024).

---

## 2.3 EU CE Marking — Detailed Pathway Analysis

The **EU MDR 2017/745** became fully applicable on **26 May 2021**, replacing the older MDD 93/42/EEC and AIMD 90/385/EEC.

### 2.3.1 MDR Risk Classification (Annex VIII)

| MDR Class | Risk Level | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Non-invasive devices: bandages, stethoscopes, wheelchairs |
| **Class IIa** | Low-Moderate | Surgical gloves, hearing aids, contact lenses |
| **Class IIb** | Moderate-High | Ventilators, infusion pumps, dialysis equipment |
| **Class III** | High | Pacemakers, stents, heart valves, implantable neurostimulators |

### 2.3.2 Special Classification Rules under MDR

MDR introduces **four new high-risk categories** requiring scrutiny:

| Rule | Device Category | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule 12** | Devices intended to administer medicines/biological substances | Class IIb (or III if integral) |
| **Rule 14** | Devices incorporating nanomaterials | Class III |
| **Rule 22** | Active therapeutic devices with integrated diagnostic function | Class III |
| **Rule 23** | Software (SaMD — Software as a Medical Device) | Class IIa, IIb, or III based on risk |
| **Rule 19–22** | Devices with human-origin derivatives | Class III |

### 2.3.3 Conformity Assessment Routes (Article 52, MDR)

For **Class I (non-sterile, no measuring function, non-surgical)**:
$$\text{Route} \rightarrow \text{Self-Declaration (Annex VIII only)}$$

For **Class I (sterile / measuring / surgical) and Class IIa**:
$$\text{Route} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX (Quality Management System Assessment by NB) + Technical Documentation Review}$$

For **Class IIb**:
$$\text{Route} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX (full QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment by NB)}$$
$$\text{or Annex X (Type Examination by NB) + Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance)}$$

For **Class III and Class IIb implantable**:
$$\text{Route} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX (full QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment + Sample of device examined by NB)}$$
$$\text{Mandatory consultation for: (a) devices incorporating medicinal substances, (b) tissues/cells of human origin, (c) substances absorbed locally}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class III Implantable Devices** must also undergo a **Clinical Investigation Consultation** with an **Expert Panel** designated by the **European Commission (MDCG — Medical Device Coordination Group)** under Article 61(2).

### 2.3.4 EU MDR Key Document Requirements

| Document | Purpose | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Documentation (TD)** | Evidence of conformity to MDR Annexes I & II | Annex II |
| **Declaration of Conformity (DoC)** | Manufacturer's legally binding statement | Annex IV |
| **EUDAMED Registration** | European Database on Medical Devices | Article 33 |
| **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** | Mandatory device identifier | Article 27 |
| **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** | Clinical evidence summary per MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4 + MDR Annex XIV | Article 61 |
| **Post-Market Surveillance Plan (PMS Plan)** | Lifecycle surveillance | Article 84 |
| **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** | Annual safety summary (Class IIa, IIb, III) | Article 86 |
| **Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP)** | Public summary for Class III & IIb implants | Article 32 |

### 2.3.5 Role of Notified Bodies (NBs)

There are currently approximately **38 designated Notified Bodies** under MDR 2017/745 (a sharp drop from ~80 under the old MDD). Examples:
- **TÜV SÜD Product Service GmbH** (NB 0123)
- **BSI Group (NL)** (NB 2797)
- **DEKRA Certification GmbH** (NB 0124)
- **NSAI** (NB 0050)

Designated by EU Member States, audited by the **European Commission**, and coordinated via the **MDCG (Medical Device Coordination Group)**.

---

## 2.4 Indian Medical Device Rules 2017 — Detailed Pathway Analysis

The **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** were notified under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (Section 12, 33-NED)** and came into effect on **1 January 2018**.

### 2.4.1 Indian Risk Classification

| CDSCO Class | Risk Level | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** (Low) | Low | Surgical dressings, alcohol swabs, non-electric wheelchairs |
| **Class B** (Low-Moderate) | Low-Mod | Hypodermic needles, suction equipment, blood pressure monitors |
| **Class C** (Moderate-High) | Mod-High | Ventilators, orthopedic implants, blood bags, hemodialysis machines |
| **Class D** (High) | High | Cardiac stents, heart valves, implantable defibrillators, intra-ocular lenses |

> [!NOTE]
> The Indian classification aligns with **GHTF principles** — Class A ≈ FDA Class I / EU Class I, Class D ≈ FDA Class III / EU Class III.

### 2.4.2 Indian Licensing Workflow

**Step-by-step regulatory journey:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Determine Classification (Rule 4, First Schedule)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 2: Appoint an Indian Authorized Agent (for foreign manufacturers, Rule 10)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 3: Register the manufacturing site (Rule 6/10 — Form MD-3 / MD-7)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 4: Apply for Import License (Rule 14 — Form MD-15 for Class A/B; MD-16 for Class C/D)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 5: ISO 13485:2016 Certification by Auditing Body (Rule 8 / 11)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 6: For Class C/D — Performance Evaluation / Clinical Investigation Report (Rule 27–37)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 7: Grant of License by CDSCO / State Licensing Authority (SLA)}
$$
$$\downarrow$$
$$
\text{Step 8: Post-Market Surveillance (Rule 44–48)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Window Clearance:** For Class C and D devices, the application is processed through **CDSCO (Central)**, while Class A and B may be processed through **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** in certain cases. This is a major change from the pre-2017 regime where everything went through the Drugs Controller General India (DCGI).

### 2.4.3 Indian Medical Device Rules — Forms Reference Table

| Form | Purpose | Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **MD-1** | Application for Grant of License to Manufacture for Sale or Distribution | Rule 13 |
| **MD-3** | Application for Loan License | Rule 14 |
| **MD-5** | Registration Certificate for Class A/B (Small Manufacturer) | Rule 12 |
| **MD-7** | Registration Certificate for Class A/B | Rule 6/10 |
| **MD-9** | License to Manufacture Class A/B (Loan License) | Rule 13 |
| **MD-11** | Permission to Import Class A/B | Rule 16 |
| **MD-15** | Import License for Class A/B | Rule 15 |
| **MD-16** | Import License for Class C/D | Rule 15 |
| **MD-17** | Test License | Rule 25 |
| **MD-26** | Permission for Clinical Investigation | Rule 31 |
| **MD-34** | Application for New Drug Permission | Rule 51 |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Decision Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula & Decision Cheat Sheet — Master This Table**

### 2.5.1 Master Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | **US FDA** | **EU CE (MDR 2017/745)** | **India (MDR 2017)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Legal Authority** | FD&C Act, 21 CFR | EU Regulation 2017/745 | Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940 (Amended 2017) |
| **Regulator** | FDA (CDRH) | Competent Authority + Notified Body | CDSCO (DCGI) |
| **Classification Classes** | I, II, III | I, IIa, IIb, III | A, B, C, D |
| **Classification Basis** | Panel-based (CFR 860) | Rule-based (Annex VIII, 22 rules) | Rule-based (First Schedule, 6 rules) |
| **Class I Pathway** | Most 510(k)-exempt | Self-declaration (Annex VIII) | Registration + ISO 13485 |
| **Class II / IIa / B** | 510(k) Premarket Notification | NB Conformity Assessment | License + ISO 13485 |
| **Class III / D** | PMA (Clinical Trials required) | NB Conformity Assessment + Expert Panel | License + Clinical Investigation |
| **Clinical Data** | Required for Class III & high-risk II | Required for Class III, IIb implant | Required for Class C, D |
| **Quality System** | 21 CFR 820 (transitioning to QMSR) | EN ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 13485:2016 (mandatory) |
| **Time to Market (Class II)** | ~6–9 months (510k) | ~6–12 months | ~9–12 months |
| **Time to Market (Class III)** | 18–36 months (PMA) | 12–24 months | 12–24 months |
| **Cost Estimate (Class II)** | ~$31,000 (FDA user fee 2024) | ~€50,000–€250,000 | ~₹2–10 lakh |
| **Cost Estimate (Class III)** | ~$540,000–$1,000,000+ (PMA + clinical) | ~€500,000–€2,000,000 | Variable |
| **Post-Market** | MDR (Medical Device Reporting) | PMS Plan, PSUR, Vigilance | PMS + AE reporting (Rule 44–48) |
| **Third-Party Required** | No (FDA in-house review) | Yes (Notified Body) for IIa, IIb, III | Yes (Auditing Body for Class C/D) |
| **UDI System** | GUDID (Global UDI Database) | EUDAMED | Not fully implemented; voluntary |
| **Unique Identifier** | UDI-DI + UDI-PI | UDI-DI + Basic UDI-DI | — |
| **Device Listing Database** | FDA 510(k) Database | EUDAMED | Sugam Portal (CDSCO) |
| **Validity of Approval** | Indefinite (with PMS) | 5 years (Certificate validity) | 5 years (Renewable) |

### 2.5.2 Universal Risk-Based Regulatory Burden Equation

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{regulatory burden}} = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times W_i \times T_i)}
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{regulatory burden}}$ = Total regulatory effort (documentation, time, cost).
- $C_i$ = Clinical evaluation complexity for feature $i$.
- $W_i$ = Weight (duration of contact × invasiveness).
- $T_i$ = Technology novelty factor (1 = same as predicate; 2 = novel).
- $k$ = Jurisdiction-specific multiplier (FDA ≈ 1.0; CE ≈ 0.8; India ≈ 0.7 for Class A/B; 1.1 for Class D).

### 2.5.3 Substantial Equivalence Test (FDA 510k)

$$
\boxed{\text{SE}_{\text{FDA}} = (\text{Intended Use}_{\text{New}} = \text{Intended Use}_{\text{Predicate}}) \land (\text{Performance Equal or Better})}
$$

If SE is **not established**, the device is classified as **NSE → De Novo or PMA**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturers** use this comparison matrix during **market entry strategy** to choose which jurisdiction to launch first (typically US or EU for revenue, then India for volume).
- **Biomedical engineers** in product development must design **Technical Documentation** that satisfies the strictest applicable standard (highest of the three), avoiding duplicated work.
- **Regulatory affairs professionals** rely on these frameworks for **gap analysis** — comparing existing documentation against target-jurisdiction requirements.
- **Quality engineers** implement a single **ISO 13485-based QMS** that is "global-ready" and supplements it with region-specific appendices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Algorithms

## 3.1 The Master FDA 510(k) Decision Tree (Step-by-Step)

The FDA 510(k) clearance algorithm is the **most-tested KTU concept** for Part B 14-mark questions. Below is the exhaustive logical derivation:

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Classify the Device

$$\text{Device} \rightarrow \text{Panel-based lookup (21 CFR 862–892)} \rightarrow \text{Class I, II, or III}$$

**Logic:** Each FDA panel (Cardiovascular, Orthopedic, Neurology, etc.) has predefined device lists with classifications. If unsure, FDA's **Product Classification Database** is queried using the three-letter product code.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Determine Submission Pathway

$$\text{Class I} \rightarrow \text{Check 510(k) Exempt List (21 CFR 862.1–892.9)} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Exempt} \rightarrow \text{Listing + QSR only} \\
\text{Not Exempt} \rightarrow \text{510(k)}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Class II} \rightarrow \text{510(k) with Special Controls (mandatory for most)}
$$

$$
\text{Class III} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Pre-1976 device} \rightarrow \text{510(k) (PMN)} \\
\text{Post-1976 device} \rightarrow \text{PMA (most cases)} \\
\text{Novel low-mod risk} \rightarrow \text{De Novo}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Identify Predicate Device

$$
\text{Predicate Selection} = \text{Legally marketed device in US (510(k) cleared or PMA approved or preamendments device)}
$$

**Key constraint:** Predicate cannot be more than **510(k) holder's own product used as sole reference** for >10 years is debated. FDA prefers diverse predicates.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Demonstrate Substantial Equivalence

The complete SE test as defined in **21 CFR § 807.92(a)**:

$$
\boxed{\text{SE Decision Matrix} =
\begin{cases}
\text{NSE} & \text{if Intended Use differs from predicate} \\
\text{NSE} & \text{if different Tech Char AND raises new safety/effectiveness question} \\
\text{SE} & \text{if same Intended Use + same Tech Char (even with improved performance)} \\
\text{SE} & \text{if same Intended Use + different Tech Char + same safety/effectiveness profile}
\end{cases}
}$$

**Sub-criteria for "different technological characteristics":**
- Performance data must demonstrate that:
$$
\text{Safety}_{\text{New}} \geq \text{Safety}_{\text{Predicate}} \quad \land \quad \text{Effectiveness}_{\text{New}} \geq \text{Effectiveness}_{\text{Predicate}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Prepare 510(k) Submission

Required sections per **FDA Refuse to Accept (RTA) Checklist (2019)**:
1. Medical Device User Fee Cover Sheet (Form FDA 3601)
2. CDRH Premarket Review Submission Cover Sheet (Form FDA 3514)
3. 510(k) Cover Letter
4. Table of Contents
5. Indications for Use Statement (Form FDA 3881)
6. Truthful and Accurate Statement (Form FDA 3514)
7. Class III Certification Statement (if applicable)
8. Financial Certification/Disclosure (Form FDA 3454/3455)
9. Substantial Equivalence Comparison
10. Proposed Labeling (21 CFR 801)
11. Sterilization information (if applicable)
12. Shelf-life information
13. Biocompatibility (ISO 10993 series)
14. Software documentation (IEC 62304 — level of concern)
15. Electromagnetic Compatibility (IEC 60601-1-2)
16. Performance Testing — Bench, Animal, Clinical
17. Other relevant standards

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Submit and FDA Review

$$\text{Timeline (MDUFA V)}:
\begin{cases}
\text{Day 0} \rightarrow \text{Submission received} \\
\text{Day 7} \rightarrow \text{RTA (Refuse to Accept) — administrative completeness} \\
\text{Day 15} \rightarrow \text{Lead reviewer assigned} \\
\text{Day 60} \rightarrow \text{Substantive Interaction (Substantial Equivalence discussion)} \\
\text{Day 90} \rightarrow \text{Decision (SE Letter / NSE Letter / Additional Information)}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.2 The Master CE Marking Decision Algorithm

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Establish Manufacturer Status

$$\text{Manufacturer Located in?} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{EU Member State} \rightarrow \text{Place device with own AR} \\
\text{Non-EU} \rightarrow \text{Appoint EU Authorized Representative (MDR Article 11)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Apply Classification Rules (Annex VIII)

The MDR uses **22 classification rules** organized by:
- **Rules 1–4:** Non-invasive devices
- **Rules 5–8:** Invasive devices
- **Rules 9–13:** Active devices
- **Rules 14–22:** Special rules

**Worked example — Classification of an Implantable Pacemaker:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step a:} \quad &\text{Is it non-invasive?} \rightarrow \text{No (implanted)} \\
\text{Step b:} \quad &\text{Rule 8 (Implantable + electrical)} \rightarrow \text{Class III} \\
\text{Step c:} \quad &\text{Confirm with Rule 22 (active therapeutic)} \rightarrow \text{Class III confirmed}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Choose Conformity Assessment Route (Annex IX, X, or XI)

$$
\text{Class} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{I (non-measuring, non-sterile, non-surgical)} \rightarrow \text{Self-cert + Annex VIII} \\
\text{I (measuring OR sterile OR surgical)} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX Part A (QMS only)} \\
\text{IIa} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX + TD assessment by NB} \\
\text{IIb non-implant} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX + TD + production QMS} \\
\text{IIb implant} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX + TD + sample examination} \\
\text{III} \rightarrow \text{Annex IX + TD + sample examination + Expert Panel (Art. 61)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Compile Technical Documentation (Annex II + III)

Required structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annex II:} \quad &\text{General TD} \\
\text{Annex III:} \quad &\text{PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up) plan} \\
\text{Annex XIV:} \quad &\text{Clinical Evaluation} \\
\text{Annex I:} \quad &\text{General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPRs) — 23 requirements}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5: Issue EU Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV)

**Required statements per Annex IV:**
- Manufacturer name and address
- EU AR name and address
- Device name, model, UDI-DI, Basic UDI-DI, risk class
- Reference to applicable MDR
- Reference to Notified Body (if applicable), NB number, certificate numbers
- Reference to harmonized standards used (ISO 13485, IEC 60601, etc.)
- Date of issue, signature, place of issue
- Statement: *"The sole responsibility for issuing this declaration is borne by the manufacturer."*

### 3.2.6 Step 6: Affix CE Mark (Annex V)

$$\text{CE Mark height (mm)} \geq 5 \text{ mm (for visibility)}$$

**Allowed CE markings:**
- Regular CE Mark
- CE Mark with **Notified Body 4-digit number** (e.g., **CE 0123**) — required for Class I (sterile/measuring/surgical), IIa, IIb, III.

---

## 3.3 The Master Indian MDR 2017 Decision Algorithm

### 3.3.1 Step 1: Determine Classification (First Schedule)

The Indian MDR uses **6 rules** for classification:

| Rule | Device Type | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Non-invasive, transient use | A |
| 2 | Non-invasive, short-term contact with intact skin | A/B |
| 3 | Non-invasive, modifies biological composition | C |
| 4 | Invasive, body orifice, transient | A |
| 5 | Invasive, surgically invasive, implantable | C/D |
| 6 | Active devices (delivering energy) | B/C/D |

**Worked Example — Stethoscope Classification:**
$$
\text{Stethoscope} \rightarrow \text{Non-invasive (Rule 1)} \rightarrow \text{Class A}
$$

**Worked Example — Blood Pressure Monitor (Electronic):**
$$
\text{BPM (Electronic)} \rightarrow \text{Active device (Rule 6)} \rightarrow \text{Class B}
$$

**Worked Example — Cardiac Stent:**
$$
\text{Cardiac Stent} \rightarrow \text{Invasive implant, central circulatory (Rule 5)} \rightarrow \text{Class D}
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2: Apply for Manufacturing / Import License

$$
\text{Manufacturer Type} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Indian} \rightarrow \text{Form MD-3/MD-5 (Class A/B) or MD-1/MD-9 (Class C/D)} \\
\text{Foreign} \rightarrow \text{Form MD-15 (Class A/B) or MD-16 (Class C/D)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3: ISO 13485:2016 Certification

$$
\text{ISO 13485 Audit} \rightarrow \text{NABL-accredited Auditing Body in India or international CB}
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4: Clinical Investigation (Class C/D Only)

Per **Rule 27–37 of MDR 2017**:
- Application in **Form MD-26**
- Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)
- EC (Ethics Committee) approval
- CDSCO permission before initiation
- Adverse Event Reporting per Schedule Y

### 3.3.5 Step 5: License Grant

$$
\text{License Validity} = 5 \text{ years (renewable)}
$$

### 3.3.6 Step 6: Post-Market Requirements

- Adverse Event reporting within **15 days** for serious incidents (Rule 44)
- Recall procedure (Rule 46)
- Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)
- Vigilance Reporting System
- Compensation framework (Rule 47 — formula-based compensation calculation)

---

## 3.4 Side-by-Side Worked Comparison: An Implantable Pacemaker

A single worked example to cement all three frameworks:

> **Hypothetical Device:** Dual-chamber implantable pacemaker, Class III-equivalent.

| Process Step | US FDA (Class III) | EU CE (Class III) | India (Class D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Submission type** | PMA (Original or Modular) | Annex IX + Expert Panel consultation | Form MD-16 Import License |
| **Clinical data** | Pivotal clinical trial (IDE) | Clinical investigation (Annex XV) | Clinical investigation (Rule 27–37) |
| **Clinical sample size** | ~300–600 patients | ~200–500 patients | ~100–300 patients |
| **Clinical duration** | 1–3 years follow-up | 1–2 years follow-up | 6 months – 2 years |
| **Quality system audit** | FDA QSR / QMSR (2026) | ISO 13485:2016 by NB | ISO 13485:2016 by Auditing Body |
| **Decision authority** | FDA Advisory Panel + CDRH | Notified Body + MDCG Expert Panel | CDSCO + Technical Committee |
| **Approval time** | 18–36 months | 12–24 months | 12–24 months |
| **Post-market** | MDR + PAS (Post-Approval Study) | PSUR + PMCF + SSCP | AE reporting + PMS |
| **License validity** | Indefinite | 5 years (CE certificate) | 5 years (Renewable) |

---

## 3.5 Summary Decision Algorithm — Universal

$$
\boxed{
\text{Universal Device Approval Path} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Risk Class Determination (C_i)} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Pre-submission/Pre-clinical engagement} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{ISO 13485 QMS implementation} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Performance testing + Biocompatibility (ISO 10993)} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Clinical evaluation (Class C/D/III only)} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Submission to regulator / NB / CDSCO} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Review and queries} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Approval / Clearance / License} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Post-Market Surveillance (Continuous)}
\end{cases}
}
$$
