# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Regulatory Comparison — Flow Architecture

The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow** maps the regulatory approval process across all three jurisdictions, illustrating the parallel pathways and key decision points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DeviceIn["Medical Device Manufacturer<br/>New Product Concept"] --> Classify["Step 1: Risk-Based Classification<br/>FDA: I / II / III<br/>EU: I / IIa / IIb / III<br/>India: A / B / C / D"]

    Classify -->|Low Risk Class I / A| LowPath["Simplified Pathway<br/>FDA: Most 510(k)-exempt<br/>EU: Self-Declaration<br/>India: Registration only"]
    Classify -->|Moderate Class II / IIa / B| MidPath["Standard Pathway<br/>FDA: 510(k) Premarket Notification<br/>EU: NB Conformity Assessment<br/>India: License + ISO 13485"]
    Classify -->|High Class III / D| HighPath["Rigorous Pathway<br/>FDA: PMA or De Novo<br/>EU: NB + Expert Panel<br/>India: License + Clinical Investigation"]

    LowPath --> QMS["Step 2: ISO 13485 QMS Implementation<br/>Design Controls, CAPA, Document Controls"]
    MidPath --> QMS
    HighPath --> QMS

    QMS --> Testing["Step 3: Performance Testing<br/>Bench Testing per ISO standards<br/>Biocompatibility ISO 10993<br/>EMC per IEC 60601-1-2<br/>Software per IEC 62304"]

    Testing --> Clinical{"Step 4: Clinical Evaluation<br/>Required for High-Risk?"}
    Clinical -->|No| Submit["Step 5: Submission"]
    Clinical -->|Yes| ClinicalStudy["Conduct Clinical Trial<br/>FDA: IDE Application<br/>EU: Clinical Investigation per MDR Annex XV<br/>India: Clinical Investigation per Rule 27-37"]
    ClinicalStudy --> Submit

    Submit --> FDA["US FDA Pathway<br/>21 CFR 807-820<br/>CDRH Review<br/>510k/PMA/De Novo"]
    Submit --> EU["EU CE Pathway<br/>MDR 2017/745<br/>Notified Body Assessment<br/>Conformity Assessment"]
    Submit --> India["India MDR Pathway<br/>MDR 2017<br/>CDSCO Review<br/>Form MD-15 or MD-16"]

    FDA --> FDAReview["FDA Review<br/>MDUFA V Timeline<br/>RTA → Substantive Interaction<br/>SE Letter or PMA Approval"]
    EU --> EUReview["NB Review + EUDAMED Registration<br/>Annex IX / X / XI Assessment<br/>CE Certificate Issued<br/>Validity 5 Years"]
    India --> IndiaReview["CDSCO / SLA Review<br/>Class A/B: SLA<br/>Class C/D: CDSCO<br/>License Validity 5 Years"]

    FDAReview --> Approve{"Approval Status"}
    EUReview --> Approve
    IndiaReview --> Approve

    Approve -->|Approved| Market["Commercial Market Entry<br/>+ Post-Market Surveillance<br/>FDA: MDR Reporting<br/>EU: PSUR, PMS Plan, PMCF<br/>India: AE Reporting per Rule 44-48"]
    Approve -->|Rejected / Additional Info| Revision["Address Deficiencies<br/>Additional Studies Required<br/>Resubmit"]

    Revision --> Submit
```

## 4.2 FDA 510(k) vs PMA vs De Novo — Pathway Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Novel Medical Device<br/>to be marketed in US"] --> Class{"FDA Risk<br/>Classification"}

    Class -->|"Class I<br/>(Most Exempt)"| Exempt["510(k)-Exempt<br/>Listing + QSR Only<br/>No Premarket Submission"]
    Class -->|"Class II<br/>(Most Common)"| K510["510(k) Pathway<br/>Predicate Comparison<br/>Substantial Equivalence Test"]
    Class -->|"Class III<br/>(High Risk)"| C3path{"Has Valid<br/>Predicate?"}

    K510 --> SETest{"Substantial<br/>Equivalence<br/>Established?"}
    SETest -->|"Yes - SE"| SEAppr["510(k) Cleared<br/>FDA Letter<br/>Class I/II Clearance"]
    SETest -->|"No - NSE"| DeNovo["De Novo Request<br/>Establish New Classification<br/>Class I or II"]

    C3path -->|"Yes"| K510
    C3path -->|"No"| PMA["PMA Pathway<br/>Most Stringent<br/>Independent Safety + Efficacy<br/>Clinical Trials via IDE"]

    DeNovo --> DAppr{"FDA Grants<br/>New<br/>Classification?"}
    DAppr -->|"Yes"| DGrant["Class I or II<br/>De Novo Granted<br/>Marketing Authorization"]
    DAppr -->|"No - High Risk"| PMA

    PMA --> IDE["IDE Application<br/>21 CFR Part 812<br/>Clinical Trial Approval"]
    IDE --> Trial["Pivotal Clinical Trial<br/>Statistical Analysis<br/>Effectiveness + Safety"]
    Trial --> Panel["FDA Advisory Panel<br/>Recommendation"]
    Panel -->|"Approve"| PMAAppr["PMA Approval<br/>Class III Cleared<br/>Most Restrictive Approval"]
    Panel -->|"Reject"| Reject["FDA Not Approved<br/>Appeal or Resubmit"]

    Exempt --> Market["Commercial Market Entry<br/>+ Post-Market Surveillance"]
    SEAppr --> Market
    DGrant --> Market
    PMAAppr --> Market
```

---

## 4.3 EU CE Conformity Assessment Routes — Visual Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L["Class I"]
        L1["Non-sterile<br/>Non-measuring<br/>Non-surgical"]
        L2["Sterile OR<br/>Measuring OR<br/>Surgical"]
    end

    subgraph M["Class IIa"]
        M1["Annex IX<br/>QMS Audit by NB"]
    end

    subgraph N["Class IIb"]
        N1["Non-Implantable<br/>Annex IX or X+XI"]
        N2["Implantable<br/>Annex IX Full + Sample Exam"]
    end

    subgraph O["Class III"]
        O1["Annex IX Full<br/>QMS + TD + Sample<br/>+ Expert Panel<br/>(Art. 61(2))"]
    end

    L1 --> SelfDec["Self-Declaration<br/>Manufacturer's Responsibility<br/>Annex VIII Only"]
    L2 --> L2NB["Annex IX Part A<br/>NB Audits QMS Only<br/>(Sterile / Meas / Surgical)"]

    M1 --> MIDNB["Annex IX + TD Review<br/>NB Assesses QMS<br/>+ Technical Documentation"]

    N1 --> NINB["Annex IX OR<br/>Annex X + XI<br/>NB Assessment"]
    N2 --> NINB2["Annex IX Full<br/>+ Sample Examination<br/>Class IIb Implantable"]

    O1 --> ONB["Annex IX Full +<br/>Sample + Expert Panel<br/>Highest Regulatory Burden"]

    SelfDec --> CEMark["CE Marking Affixed<br/>Manufacturer Issues DoC"]
    L2NB --> CENB["CE + NB 4-digit Number<br/>e.g., CE 0123"]
    MIDNB --> CENB
    NINB --> CENB
    NINB2 --> CENB
    ONB --> CENB

    CEMark --> MarketEU["EU Market Entry<br/>EUDAMED Registration<br/>Post-Market Vigilance"]
    CENB --> MarketEU
```

---

## 4.4 Indian MDR 2017 — Licensing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Device["Medical Device<br/>to be marketed in India"] --> Class{"Risk Classification<br/>First Schedule<br/>Rule 1-6"}

    Class -->|"Class A<br/>Low Risk"| APath["State Licensing Authority<br/>Form MD-3 / MD-5<br/>Registration + ISO 13485"]
    Class -->|"Class B<br/>Low-Moderate"| BPath["State Licensing Authority<br/>Form MD-3 / MD-9<br/>License + ISO 13485"]
    Class -->|"Class C<br/>Moderate-High"| CPath["CDSCO Central<br/>Form MD-1 / MD-7<br/>License + ISO 13485<br/>+ Performance Evaluation"]
    Class -->|"Class D<br/>High Risk"| DPath["CDSCO Central<br/>Form MD-1 / MD-7<br/>License + ISO 13485<br/>+ Clinical Investigation<br/>Form MD-26"]

    APath --> AppQA["Application Review<br/>by SLA"]
    BPath --> AppQA
    CPath --> AppQA2["Application Review<br/>by CDSCO<br/>DCGI Approval"]
    DPath --> AppQA2

    AppQA -->|Approved| LicenseA["License Granted<br/>5-Year Validity"]
    AppQA2 -->|Approved| LicenseCD["License Granted<br/>5-Year Validity"]

    LicenseA --> Mkt["Market Entry<br/>Sugam Portal Listing<br/>Post-Market AE Reporting<br/>Rule 44-48"]
    LicenseCD --> Mkt

    AppQA -->|Query| QResp["Query Response<br/>+ Additional Data"]
    AppQA2 -->|Query| QResp
    QResp --> AppQA
    QResp --> AppQA2
```

## 4.5 Classification Alignment Map — GHTF-Based Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph US["US FDA"]
        USI["Class I<br/>General Controls"]
        USII["Class II<br/>General + Special"]
        USIII["Class III<br/>PMA Required"]
    end

    subgraph EU["EU MDR 2017/745"]
        EUI["Class I<br/>Low Risk"]

    subgraph IN["India MDR 2017"]

    USI -.->|"Equivalent"| EUI
    USI -.->|"Equivalent"| INA

    USII -.->|"Approximately"| EUIIa
    USII -.->|"Approximately"| INB

    USII -.->|"High-Risk Class II"| EUIIb

    USIII -.->|"Equivalent"| EUIII

```

> [!NOTE]
> The alignment is **approximate** — actual device classification depends on specific characteristics and panel assignments. The diagram above represents the **most common crosswalks**, but edge cases (e.g., combination products, devices incorporating medicinal substances) require careful case-by-case analysis.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Substantial Equivalence (SE) in the context of US FDA 510(k) Premarket Notification. List the two essential criteria that must be satisfied to establish SE.**

#### Model Answer:
**Substantial Equivalence (SE)** is a regulatory determination, defined under **21 CFR § 807.92(a)**, that a new medical device has the same intended use as a legally marketed predicate device and either has the same technological characteristics, or has different technological characteristics that do not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness.

The **two essential criteria** to establish SE are:

1. **Same Intended Use:** The new device must be used for the same diagnostic or therapeutic purpose as the predicate device.

2. **Same Technological Characteristics OR Different but Equivalent Technology:** Either the technological features (materials, design, energy source, etc.) are identical to the predicate, OR they differ but do not raise new safety/effectiveness concerns, AND the device is at least as safe and effective as the predicate.

If both criteria are met, FDA issues a **510(k) Clearance Letter**; if not, the device is classified as **Not Substantially Equivalent (NSE)** and must pursue the **De Novo** or **PMA** pathway.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two criteria]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Notified Body under EU MDR 2017/745? Why are Notified Bodies mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices but not for non-sterile, non-measuring Class I devices?**

#### Model Answer:
A **Notified Body (NB)** is a third-party conformity assessment organization, designated by an EU Member State and audited by the European Commission, that is accredited to assess whether medical devices comply with the requirements of **EU MDR 2017/745**. Examples include TÜV SÜD, BSI, and DEKRA.

Notified Bodies are **mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices** because the technical complexity and risk of these devices require independent expert verification beyond what the manufacturer can self-validate. The CE mark with the NB's four-digit identification number (e.g., **CE 0123**) confirms NB oversight.

In contrast, **non-sterile, non-measuring, non-surgical Class I devices** are considered **low risk** (e.g., elastic bandages, non-powered wheelchairs), where general safety and performance requirements can be verified through the manufacturer's own internal controls. Hence, the manufacturer issues a **Self-Declaration of Conformity** (Annex VIII) without NB involvement, reducing regulatory burden and time to market.

**[3 Marks: 1 for NB definition + 1 for mandatory NB classes + 1 for Class I exemption logic]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### KTU Module Internal Choice — Choose Either Question A OR Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) Explain in detail the US FDA medical device classification system with the three classes and the corresponding pre-market submission pathways. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compare and contrast the regulatory frameworks of US FDA, EU CE Marking, and Indian MDR 2017 for medical device approval. Use a comparison matrix covering classification, regulatory authority, conformity assessment, time-to-market, and post-market surveillance. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — FDA Classification System

The US FDA classifies medical devices into **three regulatory classes** under **21 CFR § 860**, based on the level of risk and the regulatory controls necessary to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness.

**Class I — General Controls (Low Risk):**
- Risk Level: Minimal, where general controls are sufficient to ensure safety and effectiveness.
- Examples: Elastic bandages, stethoscopes, examination gloves, manual toothbrushes (with medical claim).
- Regulatory requirement: Most Class I devices are **510(k)-exempt** (~47%) and require only Establishment Registration (21 CFR 807), Device Listing, QSR compliance (21 CFR 820), and proper labeling (21 CFR 801).
- Examples requiring 510(k) despite Class I status: surgical masks, certain sterile devices.

**Class II — General Controls + Special Controls (Moderate Risk):**
- Risk Level: Moderate, requiring additional controls beyond Class I to ensure safety and effectiveness.
- Examples: Infusion pumps, blood pressure monitors, contact lens solutions, surgical drapes, MRI machines.
- Regulatory requirement: **510(k) Premarket Notification** is mandatory (with few exemptions). Special controls may include: mandatory performance standards, special labeling requirements, post-market surveillance, and FDA guidance documents.
- Submission: 21 CFR 807 Subpart E + applicable special controls.

**Class III — General Controls + Premarket Approval (High Risk):**
- Risk Level: High, typically life-sustaining, life-supporting, or implantable, where general and special controls are insufficient.
- Examples: Implantable pacemakers, coronary stents, heart valves, deep brain stimulators, intra-ocular lenses.
- Regulatory requirement: **Premarket Approval (PMA)** under 21 CFR Part 814 — the most stringent pathway requiring independent scientific evidence of safety and effectiveness, including results of clinical investigations conducted under **IDE (Investigational Device Exemption, 21 CFR Part 812)**.
- Statistical rigor: Hypothesis testing for primary endpoints with pre-defined success criteria.

**Summary of Pre-market Pathways:**

| FDA Class | Primary Pathway | Stringency | Typical Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class I | 510(k)-exempt / 510(k) | Low | 1–3 months (exempt); 3–6 months (510k) |
| Class II | 510(k) with Special Controls | Moderate | 6–9 months (MDUFA V) |
| Class III | PMA / De Novo | High | 18–36 months (PMA); 9–15 months (De Novo) |

**[7 Marks: 1 each for Class I/II/III description + 2 marks for pathway comparison summary table]**

---

#### Part (b) — Comprehensive Three-Way Comparison

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Parameter | **US FDA** | **EU CE Marking (MDR 2017/745)** | **Indian MDR 2017** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Legal Authority** | FD&C Act; 21 CFR | EU Regulation 2017/745 | Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940 (Amended 2017) |
| **Regulator** | FDA (Center for Devices and Radiological Health – CDRH) | EU Member State Competent Authority + Notified Body | CDSCO (DCGI); SLA for low-risk |
| **Classification** | Class I, II, III (Panel-based, 21 CFR 860–892) | Class I, IIa, IIb, III (Rule-based, Annex VIII, 22 rules) | Class A, B, C, D (Rule-based, First Schedule, 6 rules) |
| **Conformity Assessment** | FDA in-house review (CDRH) | Third-party Notified Body (for IIa, IIb, III); self-declaration for I | State Licensing Authority (A/B) or CDSCO (C/D); ISO 13485 audit by Auditing Body |
| **Quality System** | 21 CFR Part 820 → transitioning to QMSR (ISO 13485-aligned, Feb 2026) | EN ISO 13485:2016 (mandatory) | ISO 13485:2016 (mandatory) |
| **Clinical Data** | Required for PMA & some 510(k) | Mandatory for Class III, IIb implantables | Required for Class C and D |
| **Time to Market (Class II/IIa/B)** | 6–9 months (510k) | 6–12 months | 9–12 months |
| **Time to Market (Class III/D)** | 18–36 months (PMA) | 12–24 months | 12–24 months |
| **Approval Cost** | $31K (510k, FY24 fee); $540K+ (PMA + clinical) | €50K–€500K (depending on class) | Variable; significantly lower for Class A/B |
| **License Validity** | Indefinite (subject to PMS) | 5 years (CE certificate renewal) | 5 years (renewable) |
| **UDI System** | GUDID (FDA UDI rule, 2013) | EUDAMED (under phased implementation) | Not mandatory yet; voluntary on Sugam portal |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | MDR (Medical Device Reporting, 21 CFR 803); Post-Approval Studies (PAS) | PMS Plan, PSUR (Class IIa, IIb, III), SSCP (Class III & implantables) | AE reporting (Rule 44); FSCA (Rule 46); compensation formula (Rule 47) |
| **Third-Party Audit** | Not required (FDA in-house) | Mandatory Notified Body for IIa, IIb, III | Mandatory Auditing Body (ISO 13485) for all |
| **Key Database** | FDA 510(k) Database, MAUDE, TPLC | EUDAMED, Eudamed Vigilance Module | Sugam Portal (CDSCO) |
| **Recognition of Foreign Standards** | Limited (FDA-accepted consensus standards) | Harmonized EN ISO standards | BIS adoption of ISO standards; gradual alignment |

**Critical Differences:**

1. **Self-certification vs. Third-party Certification:** EU relies on Notified Bodies; FDA performs in-house review; India requires state/central licensing with ISO 13485 audit.

2. **Risk Philosophy:** All three follow GHTF/IMDRF risk-based philosophy but differ in rule structure (Panel-based FDA vs. Rule-based EU/India).

3. **Clinical Data Requirements:** EU MDR has expanded clinical evidence requirements (Article 61) significantly, narrowing the historical gap with FDA.

4. **Time-to-market advantage:** For Class II devices, the **510(k) pathway is often the fastest**; for novel Class III devices, the **EU MDR Expert Panel process may extend timelines** beyond PMA review.

5. **Cost asymmetry:** US PMA costs remain the highest globally; India offers significant cost advantage but with evolving regulatory maturity.

**[7 Marks: 3 marks for matrix; 4 marks for critical differences and analytical comparison]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a) Describe in detail the conformity assessment routes available under EU MDR 2017/745. Explain how the choice of route depends on the device classification. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the Indian Medical Device Rules 2017, including classification, licensing workflow, and the role of CDSCO and State Licensing Authority. Discuss the importance of ISO 13485:2016 certification in the Indian regulatory process. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — EU MDR Conformity Assessment Routes

The EU MDR 2017/745 (effective 26 May 2021, replacing MDD 93/42/EEC) defines **multiple conformity assessment routes** under **Annexes IX, X, and XI**, with the choice depending on the device classification.

**Annex IX — Quality Management System and Technical Documentation Assessment:**
This is the most comprehensive route and is the default for most devices. It consists of two parts:
- **Part A (QMS Audit):** The Notified Body audits the manufacturer's QMS for compliance with **EN ISO 13485:2016**, including design controls, document controls, CAPA, purchasing controls, and post-market surveillance.
- **Part B (Technical Documentation Assessment):** The NB reviews the Technical Documentation for representative samples of each device category (Class IIa), per family or generic group (Class IIb), or every individual device (Class III).

**Annex X — Type Examination:**
This route is used as an alternative to Annex IX for Class IIb devices. The NB examines a representative sample (the "type") of the device to verify its conformity with MDR requirements. It must be combined with Annex XI for production phase conformity.

**Annex XI — Production Quality Assurance:**
Used together with Annex X (Type Examination) to ensure ongoing production conformity through QMS audit focused on production and post-production phases.

**Route Selection Based on Classification:**

| Class | Allowed Routes | NB Involvement |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I (non-sterile, non-measuring, non-surgical)** | Self-declaration + Annex VIII only | None (Manufacturer self-certifies) |
| **Class I (sterile / measuring / surgical)** | Annex IX Part A only (QMS audit by NB) | Limited (QMS only, no TD review) |
| **Class IIa** | Annex IX (QMS + TD per category) | Yes — QMS + TD sampling |
| **Class IIb (non-implantable)** | Annex IX (QMS + TD per family) OR Annex X + Annex XI | Yes — full assessment |
| **Class IIb (implantable)** | Annex IX (QMS + TD + sample examination) | Yes — full + sample |
| **Class III** | Annex IX (QMS + TD + sample) + Expert Panel consultation (Article 61(2)) + EU reference laboratory consultation (for certain devices) | Yes — maximum scrutiny |

**Special Considerations:**
- **Devices incorporating medicinal substances** (Article 117): Require consultation with **EMA (European Medicines Agency)** or national competent authority for the medicinal component.
- **Devices incorporating human tissues/cells:** Require consultation with the relevant competent authority.
- **Class III implantable devices** must undergo **Clinical Investigation Consultation Procedure (CIC)** with an MDCG Expert Panel.
- **Annex VIII Classification Rules:** 22 rules determine the class — manufacturers may consult Notified Bodies or Competent Authorities when classification is unclear.

**Conformity Assessment Outcome:**
- NB issues **EU Technical Documentation Assessment Certificate** + **EU QMS Certificate** (for Annex IX route) → Manufacturer issues **EU Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV)** → Affixes **CE Marking with NB 4-digit number** → Registers in **EUDAMED**.

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for Annex IX/X/XI explanation + 3 marks for classification-based route selection table + 2 marks for special considerations]**

---

#### Part (b) — Indian MDR 2017 Detailed Analysis

**Historical Context and Significance:**
The **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** were notified on **31 January 2017** and became effective from **1 January 2018**, representing the first comprehensive regulatory framework for medical devices in India. Prior to 2017, medical devices were loosely regulated under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** with only ~15 notified categories. The 2017 Rules expanded regulation to **all medical devices** based on risk classification.

**Classification System (First Schedule, Rule 4):**

The Indian MDR uses **6 classification rules** based on the GHTF framework:

| Class | Risk Level | Examples | Regulatory Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Low | Surgical dressings, alcohol swabs, non-electric wheelchairs | State Licensing Authority (SLA) |
| **B** | Low-Moderate | Hypodermic needles, suction equipment, blood pressure monitors | SLA |
| **C** | Moderate-High | Ventilators, orthopedic implants, blood bags, hemodialysis machines | CDSCO (Central) |
| **D** | High | Cardiac stents, heart valves, implantable defibrillators, intra-ocular lenses | CDSCO (Central) |

**Licensing Workflow:**

For **Indian Manufacturers:**
1. Determine classification (Rule 4, First Schedule).
2. Establish manufacturing facility compliant with **Schedule V (Quality Management System)** — equivalent to **ISO 13485:2016**.
3. Apply in **Form MD-1** for Class A/B Loan License; **Form MD-3** for Class A/B Manufacturing License; **Form MD-5** for Class C/D License to Manufacture.
4. Submit application to **CDSCO** (Class C/D) or **SLA** (Class A/B).
5. Site inspection by regulatory inspectors.
6. Grant of license (validity: **5 years, renewable**).

For **Foreign Manufacturers:**
1. Appoint an **Indian Authorized Agent** (Rule 10).
2. Apply for **Import License** in **Form MD-15** (Class A/B) or **Form MD-16** (Class C/D).
3. Submit ISO 13485:2016 certificate issued by a recognized Certifying Body.
4. For Class C/D — submit **Performance Evaluation Report** (Rule 27–37) and/or **Clinical Investigation Report**.
5. Grant of import license by CDSCO.

**Role of CDSCO vs. SLA:**

- **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation):** Apex regulatory body headed by the **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)**. Processes all **Class C and D** device applications, oversees clinical investigations, issues import licenses for high-risk devices, and manages the **Sugam Portal** (online licensing platform).

- **State Licensing Authority (SLA):** Each Indian state has an SLA that processes **Class A and B** device manufacturing licenses within the state. SLA conducts site inspections and grants manufacturing licenses for low-risk devices.

This **dual structure** distributes regulatory burden and accelerates approval for low-risk devices while ensuring central oversight for high-risk ones.

**Importance of ISO 13485:2016 Certification:**

The **ISO 13485:2016** standard ("Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes") is the **de facto global QMS standard** for medical device manufacturers.

In the Indian MDR 2017 framework, ISO 13485:2016 certification is **mandatory** for all license applications (Rule 8 for manufacturing, Rule 11 for import). Its significance includes:

1. **Harmonization:** ISO 13485 is recognized globally, allowing Indian manufacturers to leverage the same QMS for FDA, CE, and Indian submissions.

2. **Risk Management Integration:** The 2016 version mandates **risk-based approach** throughout the QMS (Clauses 4.1.2, 8.2), aligning with **ISO 14971:2019** (Application of risk management to medical devices).

3. **Design Controls:** Clause 7.3 requires systematic design and development, including design input/output, verification, validation, design transfer, and design changes — essential for safety-critical devices.

4. **Post-Market Surveillance:** Clauses 8.2.1, 8.2.2, and 8.2.3 require feedback collection, complaint handling, AE reporting, and FSCA — aligned with Rule 44–48 of Indian MDR.

5. **Supplier Controls:** Clause 7.4 mandates purchasing controls and supplier qualification — critical for outsourced manufacturing.

6. **Document and Record Controls:** Clauses 4.2.4 and 4.2.5 establish rigorous documentation — essential for regulatory audit trails.

7. **Regulatory Audit Readiness:** ISO 13485 certification prepares manufacturers for **MDSAP (Medical Device Single Audit Program)** audits accepted in US, Canada, Japan, Australia, and Brazil, enabling global market access.

The Indian **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** has adopted ISO 13485 as **IS 13485**, and **NABL-accredited Auditing Bodies** (like TÜV SÜD India, BSI India, DNV) issue certifications accepted by CDSCO.

**Recent Developments:**
- **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI)** — adverse event reporting system for medical devices, under IPC (Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission).
- **2020 Amendment to MDR 2017** — introduced **risk-based classification** refinements, mandatory **bar coding** for top 14 device categories, and **unique device identification (UDI)** pilot.
- **Medical Device Parks** — Government of India initiative to set up **4 medical device parks** (in Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Uttar Pradesh) to reduce manufacturing costs and import dependence.

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for classification + 2 marks for licensing workflow + 2 marks for CDSCO/SLA roles + 1 mark for ISO 13485 importance]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**

1. **Confusing Class II with Class IIa/IIb:** Students often forget that the EU splits Class II into **IIa and IIb**, which have **different conformity assessment routes**. EU Class IIa = Annex IX with limited TD review; EU Class IIb = Annex IX with full TD review.

2. **Mixing US and EU terminology:** Saying "FDA Class IIb" is wrong. FDA has only **Class I, II, III** (no sub-classes). Sub-classes (IIa, IIb) are an EU MDR concept.

3. **Forgetting the Predicate Device concept:** Many students describe 510(k) without explaining what a **predicate device** is. The **Predicate device** is the cornerstone of the entire 510(k) pathway.

4. **Overlooking ISO 13485's mandatory status in India:** While 21 CFR Part 820 is being phased out for QMSR (ISO 13485-aligned), in India, ISO 13485:2016 has been **mandatory since 2018**, not a future requirement.

5. **Confusing CE Mark for a quality mark:** CE Marking is **not a quality mark**; it is a **conformity declaration** that the device complies with EU regulations. CE Mark stands for "**Conformité Européenne**."

6. **Not mentioning EUDAMED:** EU MDR's central database for device registration is **EUDAMED**, not EUDAMED-2 or ECDC. Spelling and name accuracy matter.

7. **Missing the role of Clinical Investigation:** For EU MDR Class III and IIb implantables, the **Clinical Investigation Consultation Procedure** with **Expert Panels** is mandatory — students often skip this advanced requirement.

8. **Not mentioning compensation framework in India:** Rule 47 of Indian MDR 2017 introduces a **formula-based compensation calculation** for clinical trial-related deaths/injuries — a unique Indian feature often missed.

9. **Incorrect timeline estimates:** Time-to-market varies widely by class and pathway; stating generic "1–2 years" without specifying pathway demonstrates weak understanding.

10. **Ignoring the **MDSAP** program:** A global regulatory trend — single audit accepted by multiple countries — is increasingly important for medical device manufacturers and may earn bonus marks if mentioned.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Exam**

- ✅ All three regulatory regimes (US FDA, EU CE, Indian MDR) follow the **GHTF/IMDRF risk-based classification** philosophy.

- ✅ FDA classifications: **I, II, III**. EU: **I, IIa, IIb, III**. India: **A, B, C, D**.

- ✅ **US FDA Class III ≈ EU Class III ≈ India Class D** (highest risk, most scrutiny).

- ✅ The **510(k) pathway** is the most common FDA submission (~3,000 submissions/year) — based on **Substantial Equivalence** to a **predicate device**.

- ✅ **PMA** is the **most rigorous** FDA pathway — required for ~10% of devices but for the highest-risk ones (pacemakers, stents).

- ✅ **De Novo** is for **novel low-to-moderate risk** devices with no predicate — establishes a new classification.

- ✅ The **CE Mark** is **NOT a quality mark** — it is a regulatory conformity mark, with a **Notified Body 4-digit number** for higher-risk devices.

- ✅ **EU MDR 2017/745** became applicable on **26 May 2021**, replacing the old MDD 93/42/EEC.

- ✅ **Notified Bodies (NBs)** are **third-party** organizations designated by EU Member States — currently ~38 NBs under MDR.

- ✅ **Indian MDR 2017** became effective on **1 January 2018**, governed by **CDSCO (DCGI)** for Class C/D and **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** for Class A/B.

- ✅ **CDSCO** = Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation — apex Indian regulatory body.

- ✅ **ISO 13485:2016** is **mandatory** in India for all license applications.

- ✅ **Sugam Portal** is the online CDSCO portal for medical device licensing in India.

- ✅ The **PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up)** and **PSUR (Periodic Safety Update Report)** are essential EU MDR post-market requirements.

- ✅ **EUDAMED** is the EU's central medical device database (UDI, registration, vigilance, certificates).

- ✅ **GUDID** is the FDA's Global Unique Device Identification Database.

- ✅ All three jurisdictions mandate **adverse event / incident reporting** post-market.

- ✅ The Indian **Materiovigilance Programme (MvPI)** supports post-market surveillance.

- ✅ The **MDSAP** (Medical Device Single Audit Program) enables a single audit accepted in **US, Canada, Japan, Australia, Brazil** — increasingly important for global market access.

- ✅ **Clinical Investigation** is mandatory for Class III devices under FDA, EU MDR, and Indian Class C/D devices.

- ✅ **Substantial Equivalence Test:** Same Intended Use + Same (or equivalent) Technological Characteristics + Demonstrated Safety/Effectiveness.

- ✅ License validity: **5 years (renewable)** in EU and India; **Indefinite** in US (subject to PMS).

- ✅ **Materiovigilance** in India + **MDR reporting** in US + **Vigilance reporting** in EU = post-market surveillance triangle.

- ✅ **MDR 2017/745** has **22 classification rules** in Annex VIII; Indian MDR has **6 rules** in First Schedule.

- ✅ **FDA is transitioning** from 21 CFR Part 820 (QSR) to **QMSR** (harmonized with ISO 13485:2016), compliance expected by **February 2026**.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES**
