topics.title: Device Risk Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Device Risk Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Device Risk Classification** is the systematic categorization of medical devices into discrete regulatory classes based on the level of risk they pose to the patient, user, and the environment, considering factors such as the duration of use, invasiveness, the part of the body contacted, the energy source applied, and the potential consequences of failure. This classification is the cornerstone of risk-based regulatory frameworks adopted by major jurisdictions such as the **US FDA**, the **EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)**, the **IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum)**, and India's **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)**.

Under the harmonized GHTF/IMDRF philosophy, risk is fundamentally defined as:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity of Harm}, \text{Probability of Occurrence})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Classification is *not* a measure of device quality. A low-risk device (e.g., a tongue depressor) is not "inferior" to a high-risk device (e.g., a pacemaker). Classification is purely a regulatory tool that determines the **conformity assessment route**, **clinical evidence requirements**, and **post-market surveillance intensity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of medical device risk classification like the **security clearance system at an airport**:

- A **Class I device** is like walking through a public terminal — minimal screening, low consequences if something goes wrong. (e.g., bandage, stethoscope).
- A **Class II device** is like a domestic flight check-in — you need a boarding pass (510(k) clearance) and basic ID (special controls).
- A **Class III device** is like accessing a high-security military hangar — you need exhaustive background checks (Premarket Approval), continuous monitoring, and full audit trails.

The higher the risk, the more "layers of regulatory scrutiny" are wrapped around the device to protect public health.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — Risk-Based Classification Philosophy**
> Every regulatory body uses a **risk-based approach** because the same quality system (e.g., ISO 13485) cannot equally apply to a cotton swab and an implantable defibrillator. The classification tier *scales* the regulatory burden proportionally to the potential harm.

### 1.3 Global Classification Snapshot

| Regulatory Body | Classification Schema | Number of Tiers |
|---|---|---|
| **US FDA** | Class I, II, III | 3 |
| **EU MDR (2017/745)** | Class I, IIa, IIb, III | 4 |
| **IMDRF / GHTF** | Class A, B, C, D | 4 |
| **CDSCO (India)** | Class A, B, C, D | 4 |
| **Health Canada** | Class I, II, III, IV | 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Risk Equation

At the heart of every classification system is the qualitative risk equation:

$$R = S \times P \times D$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (qualitative or quantitative)
- $S$ = **Severity** of harm (negligible → catastrophic)
- $P$ = **Probability** of occurrence (remote → frequent)
- $D$ = **Detectability** (used in FMEA-style approaches, ISO 14971)

> [!NOTE]
> **Detectability (D)** is sometimes added in the third dimension. If a hazard is *unseen* until it causes harm, the effective risk is amplified because no intervention can occur mid-event. This is why software bugs in infusion pumps (Class III) require extremely high detection rigor.

### 2.2 The Four Determinative Classification Parameters

Regulators across the globe converge on **four interrogative parameters** to determine device class:

1. **Duration of Contact**
   - Transient (< 60 minutes)
   - Short-term (60 min – 30 days)
   - Long-term (> 30 days)

2. **Invasiveness**
   - Non-invasive
   - Invasive (body orifice / surgical)
   - Implantable

3. **Body System Impact**
   - Does it affect the **central circulatory system** or **central nervous system**?
   - Is it a **life-sustaining / life-supporting** device?

4. **Energy Source**
   - Active vs. Non-active
   - Therapeutic vs. Diagnostic
   - Whether the device *supplies* energy or merely *measures* it

### 2.3 The 13 EU MDR Classification Rules (Decision Flow)

The **EU MDR 2017/745** uses a logic-tree of **22 rules** (often summarized as 13 thematic rules for teaching). The decision cascades from least risky to most risky:

| Rule Category | Rule Theme | Resulting Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule 1** | Non-invasive devices (transient use) | Class I |
| **Rule 2** | Non-invasive devices (channeling/storage) | Class I (or IIa if blood) |
| **Rule 3** | Non-invasive devices modifying biological composition | Class IIb / III |
| **Rule 4** | Non-invasive devices in contact with injured skin | Class I / IIa / IIb |
| **Rule 5** | Invasive via body orifice (transient) | Class I |
| **Rule 6** | Invasive via body orifice (surgically / short-term) | Class IIa |
| **Rule 7** | Invasive via body orifice (long-term) | Class IIb |
| **Rule 8** | Surgical invasive (transient/short-term) | Class IIa |
| **Rule 9** | Surgical invasive (long-term / implantable) | Class IIb / III |
| **Rule 10** | Active diagnostic devices (energy supply) | Class IIa |
| **Rule 11** | Active monitoring devices (vital physiological parameters) | Class IIa |
| **Rule 12** | Active therapeutic devices (administering energy) | Class IIa / IIb / III |
| **Rule 13** | Devices incorporating medicinal substances / biological origin | Class III |

### 2.4 US FDA Classification: Product Code Driven

Unlike the EU's rule-based system, the FDA uses a **product-code database** with three regulatory tiers:

| FDA Class | Regulatory Control | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I — General Controls** | Registration, listing, GMP, labeling | Tongue depressor, elastic bandage, enema tip |
| **Class II — General + Special Controls** | 510(k) Premarket Notification, performance standards | Blood pressure cuff, infusion pump, surgical gloves |
| **Class III — General + Premarket Approval (PMA)** | Full clinical evidence, bench testing | Heart valve, implantable pacemaker, coronary stent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PMA vs. 510(k) Distinction:** A 510(k) demonstrates *substantial equivalence* to a predicate device. A PMA requires *independent demonstration of safety and effectiveness*. This is the single most important regulatory bifurcation in US device law.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Risk | Output metric |
| $S$ | Severity (1–5 scale in FMEA) | Hazard analysis |
| $P$ | Probability (1–5 scale) | ISO 14971 risk matrix |
| $D$ | Detectability (1–5, where 5 = undetectable) | FMEA |
| $RPN$ | Risk Priority Number $= S \times P \times D$ | Failure Mode analysis |
| $\rho_{harm}$ | Harm density per use-cycle | Post-market statistics |
| $N_{class}$ | Device class number (1, 2, 3 or I, IIa, IIb, III) | Regulatory labeling |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering practice, knowing the device's risk class drives:
- **Quality Management System depth** (ISO 13485 clauses activated)
- **Clinical evaluation report (CER) rigor**
- **Post-market surveillance (PMS) reporting frequency**
- **Notified Body involvement** (mandatory from Class IIa upward in EU)
- **Unique Device Identification (UDI) carrier requirements**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Classification Decision Logic

The classification of a device is fundamentally a **multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** problem. We can formalize the EU MDR cascade as a Boolean logic expression.

Let $C$ = assigned class, $D$ = duration, $I$ = invasiveness, $B$ = body system, $E$ = energy supplied, $M$ = medicinal integration.

**Step 1 — Define base classification vector**

$$C_0 = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I = \text{non-invasive AND } D < 60\text{ min AND } E = \text{none} \\ 2a & \text{if conditions demand moderate controls} \\ 2b & \text{if conditions demand high controls} \\ 3 & \text{if life-sustaining, implantable in CNS, or integrates medicinal substance} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Apply rule chain (transitive closure)**

For an invasive long-term surgical device:
$$C = \text{Rule 9} \Rightarrow C \geq IIb$$
For an active therapeutic device administering exchangeable energy to a vital organ:
$$C = \text{Rule 12} \Rightarrow C \geq IIb$$
If central circulatory or central nervous system contact:
$$C \rightarrow III$$

**Step 3 — Combine with OR logic (highest class wins)**

$$C_{final} = \max(C_{rule1}, C_{rule2}, C_{rule3}, \ldots, C_{rule22})$$

This **"highest class wins"** principle is critical. If a device triggers multiple rules, it is assigned the *most stringent* applicable class.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Insulin Pump Classification

**Step 1 — Identify device characteristics:**
- $I$ = Invasive (via subcutaneous cannula)
- $D$ = Long-term (continuous wear)
- $E$ = Active (electrical pump with software)
- $B$ = Delivers a medicinal substance (insulin)
- $M$ = Yes, integrates a medicinal substance

**Step 2 — Apply EU MDR rules:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 8 (Surgical invasive, short-term)} &: C \to IIa \\
\text{Rule 9 (Surgical invasive, long-term)} &: C \to IIb \\
\text{Rule 12 (Active therapeutic, energy administration)} &: C \to IIb \\
\text{Rule 13 (Medicinal substance integration)} &: C \to III \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply max() operator:**

$$C_{final} = \max(IIa, IIb, IIb, III) = III$$

**Step 4 — FDA equivalence check (US):**
The FDA similarly classifies insulin pumps as **Class III** because life-sustaining active therapeutic devices require Premarket Approval.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Major Risk Frameworks

| Parameter | US FDA | EU MDR 2017/745 | IMDRF | CDSCO (India) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total classes | 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| Driving mechanism | Product code database | Rule-based (22 rules) | Rule-based (13 rules) | Rule-based (13 rules) |
| Conformity assessment | 510(k) or PMA | Notified Body + Annex VIII | Country-specific | CDSCO audit |
| Class III equivalent | Life-sustaining / implant | Rule 6, 7, 8 + Rule 13 trigger | Class D (high risk) | Class D |
| Software classification | Based on functionality | Rule 11 (MDSW) | IMDRF SaMD framework | MD Rule 11 |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Surgical Glove Classification

**Step 1 — Characteristics:**
- $I$ = Invasive (skin barrier breach if punctured)
- $D$ = Transient to short-term
- $E$ = Non-active
- $B$ = Skin contact, not CNS

**Step 2 — EU MDR evaluation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 1 (Non-invasive, transient)} &: C \to I \\
\text{Rule 5 (Invasive via orifice, transient)} &: C \to I \\
\text{Rule 7 (long-term invasive)} &: \text{Not applicable} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final class:**

$$C_{final} = I \text{ (with sterilization making it Class Is — sterile Class I)}$$

**Step 4 — FDA classification:**
Surgical gloves are **Class I** (general controls) in the US as well, but require a 510(k) only if they incorporate novel materials (in which case they shift to **Class II**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Device Risk Classification Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Medical Device Evaluation Start] --> nodeB{Is device invasive?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC{Duration of contact?}
    nodeC -- Transient --> nodeD{Modifies body fluids?}
    nodeC -- Short or Long --> nodeE{Contacts injured skin?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[Class I General Controls]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeG[Class IIb or III]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeF
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeH[Class I or IIa]

    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeI{Entry route?}
    nodeI -- Body orifice --> nodeJ{Duration?}
    nodeJ -- Transient --> nodeK[Class I]
    nodeJ -- Short term --> nodeL[Class IIa]
    nodeJ -- Long term --> nodeM[Class IIb]

    nodeI -- Surgical --> nodeN{Is it implantable or long-term surgical?}
    nodeN -- No --> nodeO[Class IIa]
    nodeN -- Yes --> nodeP{CNS or CCS contact?}
    nodeP -- No --> nodeQ[Class IIb]
    nodeP -- Yes --> nodeR[Class III PMA Required]

    nodeB -- Active device --> nodeS{Function type?}
    nodeS -- Diagnostic --> nodeT[Class IIa]
    nodeS -- Therapeutic Energy --> nodeU[Class IIa IIb or III]
    nodeS -- Monitoring Vital --> nodeV[Class IIa or IIb]
    nodeS -- Medicinal integration --> nodeR

    nodeF --> nodeZ[Conformity Assessment Route]
    nodeH --> nodeZ
    nodeK --> nodeZ
    nodeL --> nodeZ
    nodeM --> nodeZ
    nodeO --> nodeZ
    nodeQ --> nodeZ
    nodeR --> nodeZ
    nodeT --> nodeZ
    nodeU --> nodeZ
    nodeV --> nodeZ
    nodeG --> nodeZ

    nodeZ --> nodeAA{Choose Pathway}
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[US FDA 510k]
    nodeAA --> nodeAC[US FDA PMA]
    nodeAA --> nodeAD[EU MDR Annex VIII]
    nodeAA --> nodeAE[CDSCO Audit]
```

### 4.2 Risk Severity vs. Probability Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SeverityAxis [Severity Scale]
        sev1[S1 Negligible]
        sev2[S2 Minor]
        sev3[S3 Serious]
        sev4[S4 Critical]
        sev5[S5 Catastrophic]
    end

    subgraph ProbabilityAxis [Probability Scale]
        prob1[P1 Improbable]
        prob2[P2 Remote]
        prob3[P3 Occasional]
        prob4[P4 Probable]
        prob5[P5 Frequent]
    end

    subgraph RiskOutputs [Risk Classification Output]
        r1[Class I Low Risk]
        r2[Class IIa Moderate]
        r3[Class IIb Elevated]
        r4[Class III High Risk]
    end

    sev1 --> r1
    sev2 --> r1
    prob1 --> r1
    prob2 --> r1

    sev3 --> r2
    prob3 --> r2

    sev4 --> r3
    prob4 --> r3

    sev5 --> r4
    prob5 --> r4
```

### 4.3 Conformity Assessment Routing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    devStart[Identified Medical Device] --> classAssign{Class Determination}
    classAssign -- Class I --> path1[Self Declaration by Manufacturer]
    classAssign -- Class IIa --> path2[Notified Body Assessment Required]
    classAssign -- Class IIb --> path3[Notified Body with Full Quality Audit]
    classAssign -- Class III --> path4[Notified Body plus Design Examination plus PMS Plan]

    path1 --> end1[Affix CE Mark Register with Competent Authority]
    path2 --> end2[Affix CE Mark Notified Body Issues Certificate]
    path3 --> end3[Affix CE Mark Full Technical Documentation Review]
    path4 --> end4[Affix CE Mark Clinical Evaluation plus Implant Registry]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Medical Device Risk Classification" and list the four key parameters used globally to determine the class of a medical device.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Medical device risk classification is the regulatory process of categorizing devices into discrete classes based on the level of inherent risk they pose to the patient and user, thereby determining the extent of regulatory controls required.
- **Four parameters (1.5 marks):**
  1. Duration of contact (transient / short-term / long-term)
  2. Degree of invasiveness (non-invasive / invasive / implantable)
  3. Body system affected (especially CNS or central circulatory system)
  4. Energy source (active vs. non-active; therapeutic vs. diagnostic)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between 510(k) Premarket Notification and Premarket Approval (PMA) as applied by the US FDA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | 510(k) | PMA |
|---|---|---|
| **Devices** | Class I and II (most cases) | Class III |
| **Evidence** | Substantial equivalence to a predicate | Independent safety and effectiveness data |
| **Review duration** | 90 days (typical) | 180+ days |
| **Clinical data** | Often not required | Almost always required |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model QP]**
*Classify the following devices under EU MDR 2017/745 and justify your answer using the classification rules. Show all decision steps explicitly.*
*(a) An implantable hip joint prosthesis with no medicinal component.* **(7 marks)**
*(b) An active, software-controlled infusion pump that delivers insulin.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hip Prosthesis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify characteristics (2 marks):**
- $I$ = Surgically invasive and implantable
- $D$ = Long-term (> 30 days)
- $B$ = Contacts musculoskeletal system (not CNS/CCS)
- $E$ = Non-active

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 8 (surgical invasive, transient/short-term):** Not applicable (it is long-term).
**Step 3 — Apply Rule 9 (long-term surgical invasive / implantable):** $C \to IIb$ (3 marks)
**Step 4 — Check Rule 12, 13:** Not active; no medicinal substance → no escalation.
**Step 5 — Final classification:** $C_{final} = IIb$ (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Insulin Pump (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify characteristics (2 marks):**
- $I$ = Invasive (subcutaneous cannula)
- $D$ = Long-term (continuous)
- $E$ = Active (electromechanical, software-controlled)
- $B$ = Delivers a medicinal substance (insulin)
- $M$ = Integrates a medicinal substance

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 9:** $C \to IIb$ (long-term invasive).
**Step 3 — Apply Rule 12:** $C \to IIb$ (active therapeutic energy).
**Step 4 — Apply Rule 13 (medicinal integration):** $C \to III$ (1.5 marks each rule)
**Step 5 — Final classification:**
$$C_{final} = \max(IIb, IIb, III) = III \text{ (1.5 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
*Explain in detail the EU MDR 2017/745 risk classification system. Discuss the "highest class wins" principle and illustrate with the example of a cardiac pacemaker (active, implantable, life-sustaining, software-driven).*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EU MDR Classification System (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Overview (2 marks):** The EU MDR 2017/745 replaces the previous MDD (93/42/EEC) and introduces a four-tier system: **Class I, Class IIa, Class IIb, and Class III**, determined through 22 classification rules (often grouped as 13 thematic rules for pedagogical clarity).

**Step 2 — The rule-based structure (3 marks):**
- **Rules 1–4:** Non-invasive devices
- **Rules 5–8:** Invasive devices
- **Rules 9–13:** Active devices and special cases
- Each rule cascades from the previous, with the most stringent rule applicable dictating the final class.

**Step 3 — "Highest class wins" principle (2 marks):** If a device satisfies multiple rules, the manufacturer must select the *most restrictive* (highest) class. This guarantees the patient is protected even when the device's risk profile spans multiple categories.

**Part (b) — Cardiac Pacemaker Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Device characteristics (2 marks):**
- $I$ = Implantable (surgically invasive, long-term)
- $D$ = Long-term (years)
- $E$ = Active (battery, electronic, software)
- $B$ = Contacts central circulatory system
- Life-sustaining / life-supporting

**Step 2 — Rule application (3 marks):**
- **Rule 8:** Not applicable (transient)
- **Rule 9:** $C \to IIb$ (implantable, long-term)
- **Rule 12:** $C \to IIb$ (active therapeutic, energy delivery to vital organ)
- **Annex VIII, Chapter III, Section 4.3:** Devices that are *intended to have a biological effect* on the central circulatory or nervous system, or to be life-supporting, are explicitly **Class III**.

**Step 3 — Final class (2 marks):**
$$C_{final} = \max(IIb, IIb, III) = III$$

Pacemakers therefore require **Notified Body design examination**, **continuous PMS/PMCF** (post-market clinical follow-up), and **Implant Card** issuance to the patient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not assign a class without citing the specific rule number.** Marks are awarded for *justification* using the rule chain.
> - **Do not default to "Class III" for everything life-related.** A stethoscope used on a heart is still Class I. The decisive factor is whether the device *directly interacts with* the central circulatory/CNS system, not whether it is used in cardiology.
> - **Memorize the "highest class wins" principle.** A common mistake is averaging the classifications or choosing the first applicable rule.
> - **For FDA questions, never use EU MDR rules.** They are different regulatory regimes. Mixing them is the most common error in Part B answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Risk = Severity × Probability × Detectability** — the foundational equation across all frameworks.
- **Four classification drivers:** Duration, Invasiveness, Body System, Energy Source.
- **US FDA:** 3 classes (I, II, III) — product-code driven, 510(k) vs. PMA.
- **EU MDR 2017/745:** 4 classes (I, IIa, IIb, III) — 22 rules, rule-based.
- **IMDRF / GHTF:** 4 classes (A, B, C, D) — global harmonization attempt.
- **CDSCO (India):** Aligned with IMDRF 4-class system.
- **Highest class wins** is the cardinal rule of multi-rule devices.
- **Active devices** follow Rules 10–13 and are automatically elevated above Class I.
- **Implantable + life-sustaining** ⇒ always **Class III** in both EU and US.
- **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** has its own risk stratification (IMDRF SaMD framework).
- **Conformity assessment route** is the practical output of classification, not just a label.
- **Classification ≠ Quality.** Higher class does not mean "better" device; it means *more regulatory scrutiny*.
- **Always cite the rule number** when justifying a class — marks depend on it.
- **PQ-1 trick question alert:** A simple tongue depressor used in cardiac surgery is still **Class I** — the *use environment* does not change the device's intrinsic classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the regulatory framework of medical devices, **Registration** refers to the formal administrative process by which a manufacturer, importer, or authorised representative files mandatory information with a national or regional regulatory authority (e.g., US FDA, EUDAMED, CDSCO India, PMDA Japan, TGA Australia) prior to or concurrent with the commercial placement of a medical device on that market.

Registration is distinct from *approval* (like 510(k) clearance, PMA, CE Marking) — registration is essentially an **act of notification and accountability** to the regulator, whereas approval is the act of *substantiating safety and performance*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *“Registration is the statutory process of formally notifying the competent regulatory authority about the establishment, the device being placed on the market, and the responsible person, in accordance with the applicable national/regional medical device regulation, to enable post-market surveillance, traceability and recall.”*

Registration broadly encompasses three legal artefacts:

| # | Artefact | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Establishment Registration** | Registers the legal entity (manufacturer, contract manufacturer, steriliser, packager, importer) |
| 2 | **Device Listing** | Lists the specific devices being marketed by the registered establishment |
| 3 | **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** | Assigns a globally traceable alphanumeric code to each device model |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: “Registration is like enrolling your medical device into the government’s permanent ‘Medical Product Roll Call.’”**

Imagine a student joining a new university:

- **Establishment Registration** = The university registering itself with the UGC (the *institution* is recognised).
- **Device Listing** = Each course (device) offered by the university is listed in the academic catalogue.
- **UDI / Device Identification** = Each course gets a unique code (e.g., `PEE487`) so the registrar can identify it unambiguously among thousands of similar courses.

Without registration, the regulator has no legal record that you exist, no way to trace your device if it malfunctions, and no way to issue a recall. **Registration = Visibility + Accountability.**

## 1.3 Why Registration Exists — Regulatory Rationale

- **Patient Safety:** Enables rapid traceability during adverse events and recalls.
- **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS):** Foundation for vigilance reporting and trend analysis.
- **Traceability:** Anchors the supply chain to legal entities.
- **Compliance Trigger:** Unregistered devices are deemed *misbranded* and *adulterated* (US FDA terminology) — an offence.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **FDA Establishment Registration Renewal:** **Annual** (every financial year, between **1 October and 31 December**).
> - **FDA Annual Fee (FY 2024 example):** **USD 5,672** for standard establishments.
> - **EU EUDAMED Registration Deadline for Devices:** Per **Article 31 of EU MDR 2017/745** — staggered rollout with the Date of Application (**DoA**) **26 May 2021**, with the end of transition period **26 May 2025** (Class III) and **26 May 2027** (other classes).
> - **India CDSCO Registration:** Through the **SUGAM portal** (`sugam.cdsco.gov.in`); Medical Device Rules **2017**, amended 2022.
> - **Time-stamp format for registration (ISO 8601):** **YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssTZD**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Medical Device Registration

### Pillar 1 — Establishment Registration
This registers the **legal entity** (the company). It identifies who is responsible for the device on the market.

- **Who must register:** Manufacturers (domestic and foreign), contract manufacturers, contract sterilizers, repackers, relabelers, specification developers, and importers of record.
- **Key data submitted:** Company name, address, US Agent (for foreign establishments under 21 CFR 807.40), DUNS/UNSPSC code, business type, and contact details.

### Pillar 2 — Device Listing (US FDA terminology)
Lists the **specific devices** a registered establishment markets. Each device is identified by:
- Proprietary (trade) name
- Common/Usual name
- Class (I, II, or III)
- Product Code (three-letter FDA ProCode, e.g., `DYE` for surgical gowns)
- Regulation Number (e.g., **21 CFR 878.4040**)
- 510(k) number or PMA number (if applicable)

### Pillar 3 — Unique Device Identification (UDI)
A globally harmonised system under **IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) comprising:
- **UDI-DI (Device Identifier):** Static, identifies the labeler and specific version/model.
- **UDI-PI (Production Identifier):** Dynamic, includes lot/serial number, expiration date, manufacture date.

The **UDI Assignment** is given by an **Accredited Issuing Agency**: GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA.

## 2.2 Jurisdictional Comparison — The “Big Three” Markets

| Jurisdiction | Registration Authority | Statutory Basis | Format | Annual Renewal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **United States** | FDA (CDRH) | **21 CFR Part 807** | FURLS / CDRH Portal | Yes (Oct–Dec each year) |
| **European Union** | EUDAMED (managed by EC) | **EU MDR 2017/745 Art. 29–31** | EUDAMED database | Continuous (one-time + updates) |
| **India** | CDSCO (DCGI office) | **Medical Devices Rules 2017** (Amended 2022) | SUGAM online portal | Yes (retention fees every 5 yrs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask, *“Differentiate between Registration and Listing under US FDA.”* Memorize the table in §2.1 — it has appeared in prior University Examinations.

## 2.3 Required Documents for Registration (Universal Checklist)

1. **Cover Letter / Form FDA 2891** (Establishment Registration) or **Form FDA 2892** (Device Listing)
2. **Authorised Signatory Letter** with Power of Attorney
3. **US Agent Appointment Letter** (mandatory for foreign establishments under **21 CFR 807.40(a)**)
4. **Label and Labelling** samples in compliance with **21 CFR Part 801**
5. **Regulatory Class Justification Document** (with risk classification rationale)
6. **Predicates / Substantial Equivalence documents** (if 510(k) is being pursued)
7. **Quality System Certificate** (e.g., **ISO 13485:2016**)
8. **Declaration of Conformity** (for EU market)
9. **Notified Body Certificate** (EU MDR Class IIa, IIb, III — Annex IX, X, XI)
10. **Application / User Fee Receipt** (pay.gov for FDA; SUGAM for CDSCO; EUDAMED fees vary by member state)

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Logic Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic / Logical Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Registration Cost | $C_{reg} = C_{estab} + C_{list} + C_{UDI} + C_{fee}$ | $C_{estab}$ = entity cost, $C_{list}$ = listing cost, $C_{UDI}$ = UDI cost, $C_{fee}$ = statutory fee |
| Risk Class (FDA) | $\text{Class} \in \{I,\ II,\ III\}$ | I = lowest, III = highest |
| EU MDR Classification (Rule-based) | $\text{Class} = f(N, d_{\text{contact}}, t_{\text{contact}})$ | $N$ = non-invasive/invasive, $d$ = duration, $t$ = tissue type |
| UDI Composition | $\text{UDI} = \langle \text{DI} \mid \text{PI} \rangle$ | DI = device identifier, PI = production identifier |
| Class I Sterile / Measuring FDA | $\text{Class}_{I}^{*} = \text{Class I} \cup \text{510(k) exempt}^{c}$ | Starred = must submit 510(k) despite Class I |
| Validity (India CDSCO) | $T_{valid} = 5\text{ yrs}$ | License retention required every 5 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Safeguard Note:** In the formulas above, the vertical bar `$\vert$` denotes a concatenation/separator; per KTU Engine rules we render it as a typographic bar instead of a markdown table-breaking pipe.

## 2.5 The “Why” Behind Each Registration Step

- **Establishment Registration → Why?** Without a registered entity, no device can be legally introduced. It’s the *first domino* in regulatory compliance.
- **Device Listing → Why?** A manufacturer may have 50 SKUs; regulators need a one-to-many map of *entity-to-devices* for audit and recall efficiency.
- **UDI → Why?** Field-level traceability. If a particular LOT of a hip implant fails, the surgeon can scan the UDI and the lot can be pinpointed in **< 24 hours**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Registration data is the **backbone of post-market surveillance**:
- In 2017, the FDA used device listing + UDI data to coordinate a **Class I recall of Heater-Cooler units** (`LivaNova 3T`) used in cardiac surgery due to *Mycobacterium chimaera* contamination.
- In India, **CDSCO’s SUGAM database** is the legal proof that a Class C/D device has been notified to the Central Licensing Authority (CLA) under **Rule 24** of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: US FDA Establishment Registration & Device Listing (Foreign Manufacturer)

This is the most-tested workflow in KTU 2024 university examinations. We will walk through it *exhaustively* — every sub-step, every form.

### Step 1 — Confirm Regulatory Class
Determine the device class using the FDA Classification Panels in **21 CFR Parts 862–892**.

$$
\text{Class}_{\text{FDA}} = f(P, T, S)
$$

where $P$ = panel (e.g., Cardiovascular, Orthopedic), $T$ = intended use, $S$ = substantial equivalence against predicates.

### Step 2 — Appoint a US Agent
Per **21 CFR 807.40(a)**, every foreign establishment must designate a US Agent who resides in the United States. The US Agent serves as the official correspondent between the FDA and the foreign establishment.

**Required US Agent Documents:**
- Signed appointment letter (legal name, address, phone, email)
- US Agent’s emergency contact availability (24/7)
- US Agent Acknowledgment Form

### Step 3 — Obtain a DUNS Number
A **9-digit Data Universal Numbering System** number is mandatory. It is free from Dun & Bradstreet (D&B) and takes **2–3 business days**.

### Step 4 — Pay the Establishment Registration Fee
Pay **USD 5,672** (FY 2024) via the **FDA User Fee System** at `userfees.fda.gov`. Print the receipt; the payment ID is linked to the FURLS account.

### Step 5 — Submit Form FDA 2891
Log in to the **FDA Unified Registration and Listing System (FURLS)** at `www.access.fda.gov` and submit:

- Form FDA 2891a (Initial Registration) or 2891b (Re-registration)
- Owner/Operator information
- Establishment name, address, DUNS
- Official Correspondent and US Agent
- Type of operations (Manufacturer, Contract Sterilizer, Importer, etc.)

### Step 6 — Submit Form FDA 2892 (Device Listing)
For each device model:

| Field | Sample Entry |
|---|---|
| Trade/Proprietary Name | `CardioTrack-X200` |
| Common/Usual Name | `Cardiac Monitor` |
| Class | `II` |
| Product Code | `DRT` |
| Regulation Number | **21 CFR 870.2300** |
| Premarket Submission | `K223456` (510(k) number) |
| 510(k) Exempt? | `No` |

### Step 7 — Receive Confirmation
The FDA issues:
- **Owner/Operator Number (FUNL ID)**
- **Listing Numbers** (one per device)
- A **Confirmation Letter** valid until **31 December** of the year.

### Step 8 — Annual Renewal
Between **1 October and 31 December** each year, repeat the fee payment and re-confirm listing. Failure to renew results in **de-registration** and the device is treated as *unapproved* for the next calendar year.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: EU EUDAMED Registration (Module 2 Specific)

### Step 1 — Acquire a **SRN (Single Registration Number)**
Manufacturers, Authorised Representatives, and Importers must first obtain an SRN from their Competent Authority via the **EUDAMED Actor Registration Module**.

### Step 2 — Register as an Actor (Module 1 of EUDAMED)
Actor registration includes:
- Legal entity data (EORI/VAT number)
- Authorised Representative details (if applicable)

### Step 3 — Register UDI-DIs (Module 2 of EUDAMED)
For each device, the manufacturer assigns a UDI-DI and submits:
- UDI-DI value
- UDI-PI type
- Risk Class (I, IIa, IIb, III, or AIMD)
- Notified Body + Certificate number
- Intended Purpose (verbatim from IFU)
- Sterility, MRI compatibility, etc.

### Step 4 — Submit Certificate Information (Module 3 of EUDAMED)
- CE Certificate number
- Notified Body ID
- Issue date, expiry date, scope

### Step 5 — Post-Market Surveillance Module (Module 4)
- Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR) reference (Class IIa, IIb, III)
- Vigilance reports

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note (Dec 2023 Pattern):** Students often confuse the **four EUDAMED modules** with the **five EU MDR Annexes** (I–V). Remember: *EUDAMED is the database; Annexes are the conformity assessment routes.*

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: India CDSCO Registration (SUGAM Portal)

### Step 1 — Identify License Type
- **MD-1 to MD-5** (based on class and purpose)
- For Class A (non-sterile, non-measuring) → **MD-1** (manufacturing)
- For Class B/C/D → **MD-3/MD-5** + **MD-9** (import)
- For Loaner/import for personal use → **MD-12**

### Step 2 — Compile Technical Documentation
Per **Fourth Schedule** of Medical Devices Rules, 2017:
- Device description
- Materials of construction
- Design and manufacturing info
- Risk management file (ISO 14971)
- Verification and validation reports
- Pre-clinical and clinical data
- Sterilization validation
- Shelf-life and stability
- Labelling (URGL – Unique Reference of Global Label)

### Step 3 — Submit on SUGAM
Upload to `sugam.cdsco.gov.in`:
- Application in **Form MD-1 / MD-3 / MD-5**
- Prescribed fees (e.g., **INR 50,000** for Class A manufacturing; **INR 1,00,000** for Class B/C/D)
- ISO 13485 Certificate (not mandatory for Class A non-sterile)
- FSC (Free Sale Certificate) from country of origin (for imports)
- Power of Attorney (if Authorised Agent)

### Step 4 — Query Response (if any)
CDSCO raises queries within **30 days** of submission. Applicant responds in **15 days**.

### Step 5 — Grant of License
License number is issued in **MD-15** form. Validity **5 years** (retention fees payable at year 4).

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | US FDA | EU MDR 2017/745 | India CDSCO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | CDRH, FDA | Notified Body + Competent Authority | CDSCO, DCGI |
| **Statute** | 21 CFR Part 807 | EU MDR Articles 29–31 | Medical Devices Rules 2017 |
| **Online Portal** | FURLS | EUDAMED | SUGAM |
| **Entity Form** | FDA 2891 | Actor Registration (SRN) | Form MD-1 / MD-9 |
| **Device Form** | FDA 2892 | UDI-DI registration | Form MD-5 |
| **Validity** | 1 year (renew Oct–Dec) | Permanent + updates | 5 years |
| **Fee** | USD 5,672 (FY24) | Member State dependent | INR 50K–1L |
| **Approval Required Prior?** | 510(k)/PMA/De Novo | CE Marking via Annex IX–XI | Not always (for Class A) |
| **UDI Mandated?** | Yes (Class II–III) | Yes (all classes) | Yes (all classes) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Registration Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer Initiates Market Entry] --> B[Determine Risk Class]
    B --> C[Identify Target Jurisdiction]
    C --> D1[US FDA 21 CFR 807]
    C --> D2[EU MDR 2017 745]
    C --> D3[India CDSCO MDR 2017]
    D1 --> E1[Establishment Registration Form 2891]
    D1 --> E2[Device Listing Form 2892]
    D1 --> E3[UDI Submission to GUDID]
    D2 --> F1[EUDAMED Actor Registration SRN]
    D2 --> F2[EUDAMED UDI DI Module]
    D2 --> F3[Notified Body CE Certificate Upload]
    D3 --> G1[SUGAM Portal Login]
    D3 --> G2[Form MD 1 or MD 9 Submission]
    D3 --> G3[Technical Documentation Upload]
    E1 --> H[Receive Registration Confirmation]
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Annual Renewal and PMS Compliance]
```

## 4.2 Information Flow Between Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reg1[Establishment Layer]
        EN[Establishment Number]
        US[US Agent]
        DUN[DUNS Number]
    end
    subgraph Reg2[Device Layer]
        DL[Device Listing Number]
        PC[Product Code]
        RG[Regulation Number]
    end
    subgraph Reg3[Identification Layer]
        DI[UDI DI - Device Identifier]
        PI[UDI PI - Production Identifier]
        IS[Issuing Agency - GS1 HIBCC]
    end
    EN --> DL
    DL --> DI
    US --> DI
    DUN --> DL
    DI --> PI
    IS --> DI
    PC --> DI
    RG --> DL
```

## 4.3 Risk Classification Routing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Medical Device] --> Q1{Non Invasive?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q1a{Direct Contact with Body?}
    Q1a -- No --> ClassI[Class I]
    Q1a -- Yes --> Q1b{Transient or Short Term?}
    Q1b -- Yes --> ClassIIa[Class IIa]
    Q1b -- No --> ClassIIb[Class IIb]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Cardiovascular or CNS Contact?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ClassIII[Class III]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Long Term Implantable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ClassIIb2[Class IIb]
    Q3 -- No --> ClassIIa2[Class IIa]
    ClassI --> RegPath{Registration Path}
    ClassIIa --> RegPath
    ClassIIb --> RegPath
    ClassIII --> RegPath
    RegPath --> P1[US 510k or PMA]
    RegPath --> P2[EU CE Marking]
    RegPath --> P3[CDSCO License Form MD]
```

## 4.4 The Three-Pillar Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Reg[Medical Device Registration]
    Reg --> P1[Pillar 1 Establishment Registration]
    Reg --> P2[Pillar 2 Device Listing]
    Reg --> P3[Pillar 3 UDI Assignment]
    P1 --> P1a[Legal Entity Identity]
    P1 --> P1b[US Agent EU AR or Indian Agent]
    P2 --> P2a[Trade and Common Name]
    P2 --> P2b[Product Code and Class]
    P3 --> P3a[Static DI Code]
    P3 --> P3b[Dynamic PI Code - Lot Serial]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define *Establishment Registration* under 21 CFR Part 807. Why is it mandatory for foreign manufacturers?

**Model Answer:**

Establishment Registration under **21 CFR Part 807** is the formal filing of owner/operator information with the US FDA through the FURLS portal using **Form FDA 2891**. It registers the legal entity (manufacturer, contract steriliser, repackager, relabeller, importer) into the FDA’s regulatory database and assigns an Owner/Operator Number.

For foreign manufacturers, registration is **mandatory** because the FDA must have a **legally identifiable US resident or US Agent (per 21 CFR 807.40(a))** to serve as a point of contact for facility inspections (under Section 704 of FD&C Act), quality system audits, post-market surveillance, and recall coordination. Without registration, the device is deemed *misbranded* and *adulterated* under Section 502(o) of the FD&C Act, and the firm is liable for enforcement action.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a **Single Registration Number (SRN)** under the EU MDR?

**Model Answer:**

The **Single Registration Number (SRN)** is a globally unique identifier assigned by a Member State Competent Authority to a registered **Actor** (manufacturer, Authorised Representative, or importer) in the **EUDAMED** database. It is the *first* registration step under **Article 31 of EU MDR 2017/745** and serves as a passport for all subsequent UDI-DI, certificate, and PMS submissions. The SRN links the legal entity to all its devices and Conformity Assessment Certificates, enabling the EU Commission to perform market surveillance and the Notified Bodies to verify scope.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]** Explain in detail the **process of US FDA Establishment Registration and Device Listing** for a foreign medical device manufacturer. Include the forms, fees, validity, and post-registration obligations.

### Part (a) — Forms, Steps, and Documents (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A foreign medical device manufacturer must complete the following procedure under **21 CFR Part 807**:

1. **Appoint a US Agent (21 CFR 807.40(a)):** A US-resident individual or firm formally appointed via a signed letter. The US Agent acts as the regulatory correspondent and is the primary contact for FDA communications, including inspection notices (FDIA Section 704).

2. **Obtain a DUNS Number:** A 9-digit identifier issued by Dun & Bradstreet, free of cost, required for FURLS account activation.

3. **Pay the Annual Registration Fee:** **USD 5,672 (FY 2024)** via `userfees.fda.gov`. The fee is non-refundable and non-transferable.

4. **Submit Form FDA 2891:** Filed through FURLS. It captures:
   - Owner/Operator information
   - Establishment address and DUNS
   - Type of operation (manufacturer, contract steriliser, etc.)
   - US Agent details
   - Official Correspondent

5. **Submit Form FDA 2892 (Device Listing):** For every marketed device:
   - Trade name and common name
   - Classification (Class I, II, or III)
   - 3-letter **Product Code** (e.g., `DYE` for surgical gowns)
   - **Regulation Number** (e.g., **21 CFR 878.4040**)
   - 510(k) or PMA reference number
   - 510(k) exempt status

6. **Receive Confirmation:** FDA issues an **Owner/Operator Number** and **Listing Numbers** with a **Confirmation Letter** valid until **31 December** of the calendar year.

### Part (b) — Validity, Renewal, and Post-Registration Obligations (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

- **Annual Renewal:** Establishment Registration and Device Listing must be renewed between **1 October and 31 December** each year. Failure to renew results in de-registration, and any device marketed after expiry is treated as *unapproved*.

- **User Fee Variation:** FDA publishes updated fees annually under the **Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA)** and the **Medical Device User Fee Amendments (MDUFA)**. For FY 2024 the establishment fee is **USD 5,672**; for small businesses (<$100M revenue) it is **USD 2,836**.

- **Updates Required Within 30 Days** (per 21 CFR 807.39):
  - Change of US Agent
  - Change of official correspondent
  - Discontinuation of a device
  - New device introduction

- **Post-Registration Obligations:**
  1. **Medical Device Reporting (MDR) under 21 CFR Part 803:** Report device-related deaths, serious injuries, and malfunctions within 30 days.
  2. **Corrections and Removals under 21 CFR Part 806:** Report field corrections and removals within 10 working days.
  3. **Quality System Regulation (QSR) under 21 CFR Part 820:** Maintain design, production, and process controls.
  4. **UDI Compliance under 21 CFR Part 830:** Submit UDI-DI to **GUDID** (Global Unique Device Identification Database) for Class II–III devices.
  5. **Tracking under 21 CFR Part 821:** For Class II and III life-sustaining/life-supporting devices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the renewal window (Oct–Dec) with the registration effective date (1 Jan). Candidates often write “renewal in January” — this is **factually wrong** and will cost 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** write that the US Agent is the *legal importer*. The US Agent is only a *correspondent* — the importer is a separate registered entity.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **GUDID submission** — it is now a non-negotiable part of post-registration compliance for Class II–III.
> - **Always cite the CFR citation** (e.g., `21 CFR 807.40`) — valuation keys explicitly reward regulatory citations.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]** Discuss the **EUDAMED registration process for a medical device manufacturer** under EU MDR 2017/745. Compare it with the **CDSCO registration process** under India’s Medical Devices Rules 2017.

### Part (a) — EUDAMED Registration Process (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **EUDAMED** is the European Database on Medical Devices, established under **Article 33 of EU MDR 2017/745**. Its registration has **four functional modules**:

1. **Actor Registration:** Manufacturer, Authorised Representative, or Importer applies via their Member State Competent Authority for an **SRN** (Single Registration Number). Required data: legal name, address, VAT/EORI, scope of activity.

2. **UDI-DI / Device Registration:** Manufacturer assigns a UDI-DI through an accredited issuing agency (GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA) and uploads:
   - UDI-DI value
   - Risk class (I, IIa, IIb, III, AIMD)
   - Notified Body + Certificate number
   - Intended purpose (verbatim from IFU)
   - Sterility status, MRI compatibility, latex content, CMR/endocrine disruptors presence

3. **Certificate and Notified Body Information:** CE Marking certificates (Annex IX, X, or XI) uploaded with NB number and validity dates.

4. **Post-Market Surveillance Data:** PSURs (Class IIa, IIb, III), vigilance reports, trend reports, and field safety corrective actions (FSCAs).

**Key Dates:**
- EU MDR Date of Application (DoA): **26 May 2021**
- UDI-DI registration mandatory for Class III: **26 May 2025**
- UDI-DI registration mandatory for remaining classes: **26 May 2027**

### Part (b) — CDSCO Registration Process (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

India’s Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) regulates medical devices under the **Medical Devices Rules (MDR), 2017** (amended 2022). The registration portal is **SUGAM** (`sugam.cdsco.gov.in`).

1. **Determine License Type:**
   - **MD-1:** Class A (non-sterile, non-measuring) manufacturing
   - **MD-3:** Class A (sterile/measuring) and Class B manufacturing
   - **MD-5:** Class C and D manufacturing
   - **MD-9:** Import of any class
   - **MD-12:** Loaner/Personal use

2. **Compile Technical Documentation:** As per the **Fourth Schedule** of MDR 2017, including ISO 14971 risk file, ISO 13485 cert (where applicable), clinical evaluation, labelling, and shelf-life data.

3. **Submit on SUGAM:** With prescribed fee:
   - **INR 50,000** — Class A manufacturing
   - **INR 1,00,000** — Class B/C/D manufacturing
   - **USD 1,000 / INR equivalent** — Import licenses

4. **Query-Response Cycle:** CDSCO raises queries within 30 days; applicant responds within 15 days.

5. **License Issuance:** Issued in **Form MD-15**, valid for **5 years**. Retention fee payable at year 4.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | EU EUDAMED | India CDSCO (SUGAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Statute | EU MDR 2017/745 | Medical Devices Rules 2017 |
| Database | EUDAMED | SUGAM |
| Identifier | SRN + UDI-DI | License Number (MD-15) |
| Validity | Continuous (with updates) | 5 years |
| Approving Body | Notified Body + Competent Authority | CDSCO Central Licensing Authority |
| Renewal/Retention | Continuous updates | Retention at year 4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** state that EUDAMED issues CE Certificates — it *stores* them. CE Certificates are issued by **Notified Bodies**.
> - **Do NOT** mix **MDR 2017/745** with the older **MDD 93/42/EEC**. The 2024 KTU scheme specifically tests the current MDR framework.
> - For the Indian context, **mention the Fourth Schedule explicitly** — examiners reward precise regulatory citations.
> - For valuation, **diagrams/flowcharts in Part B (b) carry 1 mark each** — sketch the SUGAM workflow to maximise marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 – Registration)**

- **Registration ≠ Approval:** Registration is notification; approval is substantive review (510(k), PMA, CE, MD-15).
- **US FDA 21 CFR Part 807** governs both Establishment Registration and Device Listing.
- **Form FDA 2891** = Establishment; **Form FDA 2892** = Device Listing; **Form FDA 2893** = Wholesale Distributors.
- **Annual renewal window:** **1 October – 31 December** (US FDA).
- **FY 2024 fee:** **USD 5,672** (standard), **USD 2,836** (small business).
- **US Agent is mandatory** for foreign establishments (21 CFR 807.40(a)).
- **EU EUDAMED** has 4 functional modules: Actor, UDI-DI, Certificates, PMS.
- **SRN (Single Registration Number)** is the EU equivalent of FDA’s Owner/Operator Number.
- **EU MDR 2017/745** has 4 risk classes: **I, IIa, IIb, III** (plus AIMD for active implantable devices).
- **India CDSCO** uses the **SUGAM** portal; rules are **Medical Devices Rules 2017 (amended 2022)**.
- **License Forms:** MD-1, MD-3, MD-5 (manufacture); MD-9 (import); MD-12 (loaner); MD-15 (issued license).
- **License Validity (India):** **5 years**; retention fee due at year 4.
- **UDI** = DI (static) + PI (dynamic). Accredited issuing agencies: **GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA**.
- **GUDID** (US) is the public-facing UDI database; **EUDAMED UDI-DI module** is the EU counterpart.
- **Key 21 CFR Parts to remember for this module:** **807, 803, 806, 814, 820, 821, 830**.
- **Post-registration compliance** = MDR + Corrections + UDI + QS Regulation + Tracking.
- **Citations matter:** Always write `21 CFR 807.40` rather than “the FDA rule” — KTU valuation keys award extra marks for statutory references.
- **One-line exam hook:** *“Registration gives the regulator the right to know; approval gives the device the right to be sold.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clinical Trials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clinical Trials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Trial (Clinical Investigation):** A systematic investigation in or on human subjects, undertaken to assess the safety, clinical performance, and/or effectiveness of a medical device. The term is defined under **ISO 14155:2020** (*Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice*) and harmonized globally through regulations such as the **EU MDR 2017/745 (Annex XV)**, the **US FDA 21 CFR Part 812** (Investigational Device Exemption), and the **Indian CDSCO (Medical Devices Rules, 2017)**.

For a **medical device**, the terminology is *Clinical Investigation* (regulated strictly under **ISO 14155**), while *Clinical Trial* traditionally refers to **pharmaceutical products** (regulated under **ICH-GCP E6(R2)**). However, both share the foundational pillars of **Good Clinical Practice (GCP)**: scientific rigor, ethical conduct, and verifiable data.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Car Crash Test" Concept:**
> Imagine a car manufacturer wants to launch a new airbag. Before selling it to the public, they must:
> 1. **Simulate crashes** (bench testing / preclinical),
> 2. **Crash a few cars in a controlled test track** with volunteer drivers (Phase 1 — feasibility/safety),
> 3. **Crash 500 cars on real roads under varied conditions** (Phase 2/3 — pivotal performance),
> 4. **Monitor every car sold for the next 10 years** for unintended failures (Phase 4 — post-market surveillance).
>
> A **clinical trial** for a medical device is the human-equivalent of this staged safety validation. The device is never released to the market until evidence shows **"more benefit than harm"** when used in *real human patients under real conditions*.

## 1.3 Why Clinical Trials are Mandatory

- **Patient Safety:** To ensure the device does not cause unacceptable harm.
- **Performance Verification:** To confirm the device achieves its intended clinical purpose as claimed by the manufacturer.
- **Regulatory Gatekeeping:** No regulatory body (US FDA, EU Notified Bodies, CDSCO) will grant marketing authorization without sufficient clinical evidence.
- **Risk-Benefit Justification:** Per **ISO 14971:2019**, residual risks must be weighed against clinical benefits, which can only be established through clinical data.

## 1.4 Key Distinctions

| Term | Meaning | Governing Standard |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Clinical Investigation** | Pre-market study of a medical device on humans | ISO 14155:2020, EU MDR Annex XV |
| **Clinical Evaluation** | Critical assessment + analysis of ALL clinical data | EU MDR Annex XIV, MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4 |
| **Clinical Evidence** | Combined output of investigation + evaluation | EU MDR Article 2(51) |
| **PMCF Study** | Post-Market Clinical Follow-up | EU MDR Annex XIV Part B |
| **Clinical Trial** | Phased human study (drug-focused terminology) | ICH-GCP E6(R2), CDSCO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** For the B.Tech examination, students must clearly distinguish between **Clinical Investigation** (device term) and **Clinical Trial** (drug term), and recognize that the EU MDR explicitly requires **sufficient clinical evidence** (Article 61) as a precondition for CE marking.

---

## 1.5 Key Stakeholders in a Clinical Trial

1. **Sponsor** — The entity (typically the manufacturer) who initiates and funds the investigation.
2. **Principal Investigator (PI)** — The qualified medical practitioner responsible for conduct at a site.
3. **Ethics Committee (EC) / Institutional Review Board (IRB)** — Independent body safeguarding participant rights.
4. **Regulatory Authority** — Approving body (e.g., DCGI in India, FDA in USA, Competent Authority in EU).
5. **Subjects / Patients** — The human participants providing informed consent.
6. **Monitor / CRO** — Independent oversight for GCP compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of Clinical Trials (Pharmaceutical Reference Framework)

Although medical devices follow a different lifecycle, examiners often use the **pharmaceutical 4-phase model** as a reference point. The mapping for devices is given in **Section 2.2**.

### Phase I — Safety & Dosage
- **Objective:** Establish initial safety profile, identify maximum tolerated dose, determine pharmacokinetics.
- **Participants:** **20–100** healthy volunteers (or patients for terminal illnesses like cancer).
- **Duration:** Several months.
- **Success Rate:** ~**70%** of drugs pass this phase.

### Phase II — Efficacy & Side Effects
- **Objective:** Test whether the drug works and continues to be safe.
- **Participants:** **100–300** patients with the target disease.
- **Design:** Often randomized controlled trials (RCTs), sometimes with placebo.
- **Success Rate:** ~**33%** pass.

### Phase III — Comparative Effectiveness
- **Objective:** Confirm efficacy, monitor side effects, compare with standard treatments.
- **Participants:** **1,000–3,000** patients.
- **Duration:** **1–4 years**.
- **Success Rate:** ~**25–30%** pass.
- **Output:** Data for regulatory marketing approval (NDA / BLA).

### Phase IV — Post-Market Surveillance
- **Objective:** Detect rare adverse events, long-term risks, real-world performance.
- **Participants:** **Thousands** in real-world use.
- **Output:** Pharmacovigilance, label updates, possible withdrawal.

## 2.2 Phases for Medical Devices (ISO 14155 Aligned)

| Device Trial Stage | Equivalent Drug Phase | Purpose | Sample Size |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------|-------------|
| **Exploratory / Feasibility Study** | Phase I + II | Initial safety, device iteration | **10–30** |
| **Pivotal Study** | Phase III | Confirm performance, support marketing | **150–300+** |
| **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** | Phase IV | Long-term, rare-event monitoring | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Medical device pivotal studies typically do NOT require Phase I healthy volunteer testing, because **devices act physically, not pharmacologically**. However, for **implantable or high-risk Class III devices** (e.g., pacemakers, heart valves), a feasibility study is *always* mandated.

## 2.3 ISO 14155:2020 — The Global GCP Standard for Devices

The standard outlines **10 procedural clauses**:

1. **Scope and Definitions**
2. **Ethics Committee Approval** — must be obtained *before* enrollment.
3. **Informed Consent** — written, voluntary, in local language, by subject or legal representative.
4. **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** — must define objectives, design, endpoints, statistical plan.
5. **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** — preclinical and prior clinical data.
6. **Case Report Form (CRF)** — for source data capture.
7. **Adverse Event (AE) / Adverse Device Effect (ADE) Reporting** — timelines: **24 hours** for serious events.
8. **Monitoring & Auditing** — independent verification.
9. **Data Management & Statistics** — predefined analysis plan.
10. **Final Report & Documentation** — archived for **minimum 15 years** post-investigation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Sample Size & Statistical Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **most frequently tested** in KTU University Exams. Memorize the symbols and their meaning.

### Formula 1: Sample Size for Comparing Two Means (Continuous Endpoint)

$$
n = \frac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^{2} \cdot (\sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2})}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = sample size per group
- $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$ = standard normal critical value for significance level (e.g., **1.96** for 95% confidence)
- $Z_{1-\beta}$ = standard normal critical value for statistical power (e.g., **0.84** for 80% power)
- $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ = standard deviations of the two groups
- $\Delta = \vert \mu_1 - \mu_2 \vert$ = clinically meaningful difference

### Formula 2: Sample Size for Comparing Two Proportions (Binary Endpoint)

$$
n = \frac{\left( Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2} \right)^{2}}{(p_1 - p_2)^{2}}
$$

Where $\bar{p} = \dfrac{p_1 + p_2}{2}$ and $\bar{q} = 1 - \bar{p}$.

### Formula 3: Confidence Interval (CI) for a Mean

$$
CI = \bar{x} \pm Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### Formula 4: Risk-Benefit Ratio

$$
RBR = \frac{\text{Probability of Clinical Benefit}}{\text{Probability of Serious Harm}}
$$

A device is generally considered acceptable when $RBR \geq 1$ *and* the benefit is clinically meaningful.

### Formula 5: Odds Ratio (OR) — Used in 2×2 Outcome Analysis

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}
$$

Where $a, b, c, d$ are cell counts in a $2 \times 2$ contingency table.

### Formula 6: Relative Risk (RR)

$$
RR = \frac{\text{Incidence in exposed group}}{\text{Incidence in control group}}
$$

### Standard Z-Values Reference Table

| $\alpha$ (Significance) | $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$ | Power $(1-\beta)$ | $Z_{1-\beta}$ |
|------------------------|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| **0.10** | 1.645 | **0.80** | 0.842 |
| **0.05** | 1.960 | **0.90** | 1.282 |
| **0.01** | 2.576 | **0.95** | 1.645 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$ (two-tailed) with $Z_{1-\alpha}$ (one-tailed). Read the question carefully: *"detect a difference"* implies **two-tailed**; *"show superiority"* could be one-tailed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sample size** determines **cost, time, and ethics** — underpowered studies waste resources; overpowered studies expose unnecessary subjects to risk.
- **Confidence intervals** are reported (not just p-values) to convey the **magnitude and precision** of treatment effect.
- **Risk-benefit analysis** is the cornerstone of **ISO 14971:2019** risk management and **EU MDR Article 61** clinical evidence requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sample Size Calculation (Two Means)

> **Problem [KTU Numerical — Expected 14 Marks]:** A sponsor is developing a new continuous glucose monitor (CGM). The reference device gives a mean accuracy error of $\mu_1 = 12\%$ with $\sigma_1 = 4\%$. The new device is hypothesized to achieve $\mu_2 = 10\%$ with $\sigma_2 = 3\%$. The sponsor wants to demonstrate superiority at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) with **80% power**. Calculate the required sample size **per group**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters
From the problem statement:
- $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) $\Rightarrow Z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- $1 - \beta = 0.80$ $\Rightarrow Z_{1-\beta} = 0.84$
- $\sigma_1 = 4, \sigma_2 = 3$
- $\Delta = \vert \mu_1 - \mu_2 \vert = \vert 12 - 10 \vert = 2$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$
n = \frac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^{2} \cdot (\sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2})}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
n = \frac{(1.96 + 0.84)^{2} \cdot (4^{2} + 3^{2})}{2^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Numerator

First, evaluate the Z-sum:
$$
(1.96 + 0.84)^{2} = (2.80)^{2} = 7.84
$$

Next, the variance sum:
$$
4^{2} + 3^{2} = 16 + 9 = 25
$$

Multiply:
$$
7.84 \times 25 = 196.0
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Denominator

$$
2^{2} = 4
$$

### Step 6 — Final Division

$$
n = \frac{196.0}{4} = 49.0
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Attrition Correction

Assuming a **15% dropout** (industry standard for device trials):

$$
n_{\text{adjusted}} = \frac{49}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{49}{0.85} \approx 57.65
$$

Rounding **up** (you cannot enroll a fractional subject):

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{adjusted}} = 58 \text{ subjects per group}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sponsor must enroll **58 patients in the test group** and **58 in the control group**, totaling **116 subjects**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Risk-Benefit Analysis

> **Problem:** A novel coronary stent shows a **92% success rate** in restoring blood flow. However, **3%** of patients experience a serious adverse event (stent thrombosis) within 30 days. Calculate the **Risk-Benefit Ratio** and comment on regulatory acceptability.

### Step 1 — Identify the Probabilities
- $P(\text{Benefit}) = 0.92$
- $P(\text{Serious Harm}) = 0.03$

### Step 2 — Apply the Risk-Benefit Formula

$$
RBR = \frac{P(\text{Benefit})}{P(\text{Serious Harm})} = \frac{0.92}{0.03}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Value

$$
RBR = 30.67
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

> Since $RBR \gg 1$, the device offers a **30-fold higher probability of benefit than serious harm**. Combined with the **severity weighting** (restoring blood flow prevents myocardial infarction, a life-threatening condition), the **risk-benefit balance is overwhelmingly favorable** under **ISO 14971** and **EU MDR Article 61** principles.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Clinical Trial Phases Mapping for a Medical Device

> **Problem:** A manufacturer is developing a **Class III implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)**. Map out the clinical trial phases using the ISO 14155 framework, justifying the sample size at each stage.

### Analytical Matrix

| Stage | Trial Type | Primary Objective | Sample Size | Duration | Output |
|-------|-----------|------------------|-------------|----------|--------|
| **Preclinical** | Bench / Animal | Bench performance, biocompatibility, GLP animal study | N/A | 1–2 yr | Investigator's Brochure |
| **Feasibility** | First-in-human | Initial safety, refine device | **20–40** | 6–12 mo | Go / No-Go decision |
| **Pivotal** | Multi-center RCT | Confirm safety + efficacy for CE / FDA approval | **300–600** | 1–3 yr | Clinical Investigation Report |
| **PMCF** | Registry / observational | Long-term safety, rare events | **1,000+** | 5–10 yr | PSUR updates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The shift from **Phase 1 healthy volunteers** (drug model) to **direct patient feasibility studies** is the **single most important distinction** between drug and device clinical evaluation. Examiners reward students who explicitly mention this difference.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Confidence Interval for a Device Accuracy Metric

> **Problem:** A pulse oximeter tested on 50 patients shows a mean SpO₂ error of $\bar{x} = 1.2\%$ with $\sigma = 2.5\%$. Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean error.

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters
- $\bar{x} = 1.2$
- $\sigma = 2.5$
- $n = 50$
- $Z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$

### Step 2 — Compute the Standard Error

$$
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{2.5}{7.071} \approx 0.3536
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the CI Formula

$$
CI = \bar{x} \pm Z \cdot SE = 1.2 \pm 1.96 \times 0.3536
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Margin of Error

$$
ME = 1.96 \times 0.3536 = 0.693
$$

### Step 5 — Final CI

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} = (0.507, \ 1.893)\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true mean SpO₂ error lies between **0.51% and 1.89%**, well within the FDA's ±3% acceptance threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clinical Trial Lifecycle Flow (Medical Device — ISO 14155 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Concept & Design] --> B[Preclinical Testing]
    B --> C[Ethics Committee Submission]
    C --> D[Regulatory Authority Approval]
    D --> E[Feasibility Study Phase]
    E --> F{Go / No-Go Decision}
    F -->|Go| G[Pivotal Study Phase]
    F -->|No-Go| H[Device Redesign]
    H --> B
    G --> I[Clinical Investigation Report]
    I --> J[Regulatory Submission CE / FDA]
    J --> K[Market Launch]
    K --> L[PMCF Post Market Phase]
    L --> M[Periodic Safety Update Report]
    M --> N{New Risk Identified}
    N -->|Yes| O[Field Safety Corrective Action]
    N -->|No| L
    O --> P[Regulatory Notification]
```

## 4.2 Clinical Trial Stakeholder Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP[Sponsor] --> CIP[Clinical Investigation Plan]
    SP --> IB[Investigators Brochure]
    SP --> CRF[Case Report Form Design]
    CIP --> PI[Principal Investigator]
    IB --> PI
    PI --> EC[Ethics Committee]
    EC --> AP[Approval Letter]
    AP --> PI
    PI --> SU[Subject Recruitment]
    SU --> IC[Informed Consent]
    IC --> DT[Data Collection]
    DT --> CRFA[CRF Completion]
    CRFA --> DM[Data Management]
    DM --> SA[Statistical Analysis]
    SA --> FR[Final Report]
    FR --> SP
    SP --> RA[Regulatory Authority Submission]
```

## 4.3 Phase Comparison Block Diagram (Drug vs. Device)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DrugModel[Drug Clinical Trial Phases]
        D1[Phase I: Safety 20-100 subjects] --> D2[Phase II: Efficacy 100-300]
        D2 --> D3[Phase III: Confirmatory 1000-3000]
        D3 --> D4[Phase IV: Post-Market Surveillance]
    end
    subgraph DeviceModel[Medical Device Trial Stages]
        M1[Feasibility Study 20-30 patients] --> M2[Pivotal Study 150-300+]
        M2 --> M3[PMCF Study 1000+]
        M3 --> M4[Real World Evidence]
    end
```

## 4.4 Informed Consent Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subject Identified] --> B[Pre-screening by Investigator]
    B --> C{Eligible?}
    C -->|No| Z[Exclusion Log]
    C -->|Yes| D[Information Sheet Provided]
    D --> E[Discussion with Investigator]
    E --> F{Questions Satisfied?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Written Informed Consent Signed]
    G --> H[Copy to Subject]
    G --> I[Copy in Investigator File]
    I --> J[Enrolment Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked in exams to "draw" a flow, students often skip arrows showing **decision points (Go/No-Go, Eligible/Not)**. Examiners specifically look for **diamond-shaped decision nodes** (rendered via `{ }` in Mermaid). Always include at least one decision branch.

---
