The current revision, **ISO 9001:2015**, specifies the generic requirements for a QMS that an organization must fulfil to demonstrate its ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements.

For the medical device industry, ISO 9001:2015 serves as the **foundational QMS standard** upon which the sector-specific standard **ISO 13485:2016 (Medical Devices — Quality Management Systems)** is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Management Principles (QMP)** are the foundational beliefs, norms, rules, and values that govern an organization's approach to achieving quality. ISO 9001:2015 codifies **seven (7) Quality Management Principles** that form the philosophical bedrock of every QMS.

### 1.2 What is a Process? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **process** is defined as a **set of interrelated or interacting activities** that transforms **inputs** into **outputs**. Every process consumes resources, follows defined activities, and delivers value to internal or external customers.

The **Process Approach** is the systematic definition and management of these processes and their interactions to achieve the organization's intended results in alignment with its quality policy and strategic direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Clause 3.4.1 of ISO 9000:2015):**
> A process is a *set of interrelated or interacting activities which transforms inputs into outputs*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Kitchen"**
> Imagine a hospital kitchen preparing food for 500 patients:
> - **Inputs:** Raw vegetables, recipes, chefs, stoves, water, electricity.
> - **Process:** Washing → Cutting → Cooking → Plating → Quality Check → Delivery.
> - **Outputs:** Hot, hygienic, well-presented meals.
> - **Customers:** Patients, dietitians, doctors.
> - **Controls:** Temperature logs, hygiene audits, feedback forms.
>
> The kitchen manager does NOT randomly cook. She defines a **process** (recipes, steps, timings, checks) and ensures every cook follows it consistently. If a patient complains about cold food, the manager *traces the process step that failed* and fixes it. **This is the Process Approach.**

In the **medical device** context, every activity — from design, sterilization, packaging, to post-market surveillance — must be defined as a process with clear inputs, outputs, controls, and owners.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **High-Level Structure (HLS) of ISO 9001:2015 = 10 Clauses**
- **Quality Management Principles = 7 Principles**
- **PDCA Cycle = 4 Phases** (Plan, Do, Check, Act)
- **Documentation Hierarchy = 4 Levels** (Manual, Procedures, Work Instructions, Records)
- **Minimum Required Procedures = 6** (Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Nonconforming Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action — though the last two are merged in 2015 revision)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven (7) Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2015

These principles are **non-negotiable philosophical foundations** of any compliant QMS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 QMPs (Memorize in Order for KTU Exams):**

**1. Customer Focus**
- The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations.
- *Why:* Without customers, no organization survives; medical devices directly affect patient safety.
- *How:* Voice of Customer (VoC) tools, CAPA, post-market surveillance, customer satisfaction surveys.

**2. Leadership**
- Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and an enabling environment.
- *Why:* Without top management commitment, QMS becomes a paper exercise.
- *How:* Management review, quality policy, defined roles/responsibilities (RACI matrix).

**3. Engagement of People**
- Competent, empowered, and engaged people are essential at all levels.
- *Why:* Quality is built by people, not by paperwork.
- *How:* Training records, competency matrix, internal communication, recognition programs.

**4. Process Approach**
- Understanding and managing interrelated processes as a coherent system yields effective and efficient results.
- *Why:* Prevents silo-thinking; ensures traceability of defects to root cause.
- *How:* Process maps, turtle diagrams, SIPOC analysis, KPIs per process.

**5. Improvement**
- Successful organizations focus on continuous improvement as a permanent objective.
- *Why:* Medical technology evolves rapidly; stagnation is regulatory and clinical risk.
- *How:* PDCA, Kaizen, CAPA, management review, statistical process control (SPC).

**6. Evidence-Based Decision Making**
- Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data and information are more likely to produce desired results.
- *Why:* Intuition in medical devices is unacceptable — patient harm may result.
- *How:* Trend analysis, Pareto charts, statistical analysis of complaints, KPIs.

**7. Relationship Management**
- For sustained success, an organization manages its relationships with interested parties (suppliers, regulators, partners).
- *Why:* A medical device supply chain failure can halt production entirely.
- *How:* Supplier audits, supplier agreements, performance reviews, regulatory liaison.

### 2.2 The Process Approach — Deep Dive

A **process-based QMS** treats every organizational activity as a process. The PDCA cycle applies to **each individual process** AND to the **overall QMS**.

**Core Elements of Every Process (KTU Must-Know):**

1. **Inputs:** What is required to start? (e.g., raw materials, design specs, customer orders)
2. **Activities:** What is done? (e.g., machining, inspection, packaging)
3. **Outputs:** What is produced? (e.g., finished device, test report, batch record)
4. **Resources:** What is needed? (people, equipment, environment, infrastructure)
5. **Controls:** What governs it? (SOPs, regulations, risk management, monitoring)
6. **Responsibilities:** Who owns it? (Process Owner)
7. **Risks & Opportunities:** What could go wrong? (ISO 9001:2015's risk-based thinking requirement)

### 2.3 PDCA Cycle (Deming/Shewhart Cycle)

The **PDCA cycle** is the operational engine of the process approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **PDCA = Plan – Do – Check – Act**

| Phase | Action in QMS Context | Medical Device Example |
|-------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, procedures, risk controls | Plan a new stent design; create Design History File (DHF) plan |
| **Do** | Execute the planned processes; provide training | Manufacture pilot batch; train operators on new SOP |
| **Check** | Monitor, measure, analyze; compare against objectives | Inspect stent dimensions; analyze tensile test data |
| **Act** | Take corrective actions; improve performance | Modify tooling; update SOP; revise design input |

### 2.4 Clause Structure of ISO 9001:2015 (Annex SL — 10 Clauses)

| Clause | Title | Focus |
|--------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Scope | Defines applicability |
| 2 | Normative References | References ISO 9000 |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Uses ISO 9000 vocabulary |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Understand organization & stakeholders (NEW in 2015) |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment, quality policy |
| 6 | Planning | Risk-based thinking, quality objectives |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning & control, product/service requirements |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Clauses 4–10 are **auditable requirements** (called "normative"). Clauses 1–3 are **introductory** and not audited.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> Although ISO 9001 is not a heavy math subject, the following formula is used to evaluate process performance in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Actual Output (Good Units)}}{\text{Planned Output (Target Units)}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Value-Adding Output}}{\text{Total Resources Consumed (Input)}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{First Pass Yield (FPY)} = \frac{\text{Units Passing Inspection on First Attempt}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Defect Rate (DPMO)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities per Unit} \times \text{Units Produced}} \times 10^{6}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU tables, use `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown syntax. For example: $\text{CL} = \overline{X} \pm 3\sigma$ — never use $\mid$ as a vertical pipe in table cells.

**Summary Comparison Table — Process Categories in a Medical Device QMS:**

| Process Type | Examples in Medical Device Context | Customer of Process |
|--------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Customer-Oriented (COP)** | Design, Production, Sterilization, Distribution | End user, patient, hospital |
| **Support Processes** | HR, IT, Training, Document Control, Calibration | Internal process owners |
| **Management Processes** | Strategic Planning, Management Review, Internal Audit | Top management, regulators |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Medical Field

The process approach is the **backbone of regulatory compliance** for medical devices worldwide. Every regulatory framework relies on it:
- **US FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)** — expects process controls.
- **EU MDR 2017/745** — requires documented QMS processes.
- **ISO 13485:2016** — the medical device-specific QMS standard is built on process-based thinking.
- **MDSAP (Medical Device Single Audit Program)** — audits process effectiveness.

For a KTU biomedical/medical device engineer, mastering the process approach is essential because it is the **language of regulatory auditors and quality professionals** in the medical device industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Building a Process-Based QMS — Step-by-Step Methodology

This is the **analytical procedure** by which any organization (including a medical device company) implements the process approach required by ISO 9001:2015.

**Step 1: Determine the Scope of the QMS**
Define the boundaries and applicability of the QMS. For a medical device company, this typically includes design, manufacture, sterilization, packaging, labeling, distribution, and post-market activities.

**Step 2: Identify Interested Parties and Their Requirements**
Identify all relevant stakeholders — patients, clinicians, regulators (FDA, EU Notified Bodies, CDSCO), suppliers, distributors — and document their requirements.

**Step 3: Define the Core, Support, and Management Processes**
Map all activities into three categories:
- **Core (Customer-Oriented) Processes:** Those that directly add value for the external customer.
- **Support Processes:** Those that enable core processes to function.
- **Management Processes:** Those that govern the system.

**Step 4: Establish the Process Map (Top-Down)**
Create a high-level process map showing the sequential and interactive flow of processes. This is often called a **Turtle Diagram** for each process.

**Step 5: Define Process Owner, Inputs, Outputs, KPIs, and Risks**
For each process, document:
- **Process Owner:** Person accountable for process performance.
- **Inputs:** Materials, information, or requirements needed.
- **Outputs:** Deliverables produced.
- **Resources:** People, equipment, infrastructure.
- **KPIs:** Measurable indicators of success.
- **Risks & Opportunities:** What could go wrong, what could improve.

**Step 6: Apply the PDCA Cycle to Each Process**
- **Plan:** Set quality objectives and process criteria.
- **Do:** Execute the process as documented.
- **Check:** Measure against KPIs; perform internal audits.
- **Act:** Implement CAPA; drive continual improvement.

**Step 7: Document Interactions and Interfaces**
Map how outputs of one process become inputs to another. This is the **process interaction matrix**.

**Step 8: Establish Documented Information**
Maintain the **Documented Information Hierarchy** as required by ISO 9001:2015 (replaces the term "documents and records" used in ISO 9001:2008):

$$
\text{Level 1: Quality Manual} \rightarrow \text{Level 2: Procedures (SOPs)} \rightarrow \text{Level 3: Work Instructions / Forms} \rightarrow \text{Level 4: Records}
$$

**Step 9: Implement Risk-Based Thinking**
For each process, identify risks (probability × severity) and opportunities. Establish controls to mitigate risks. This is the **single biggest change** introduced in ISO 9001:2015.

**Step 10: Management Review and Continual Improvement**
Top management reviews the QMS at planned intervals (typically every 6–12 months) using inputs such as:
- Audit results
- Customer feedback
- Process performance and KPIs
- Status of CAPA
- Follow-up from previous reviews
- Changes in external context (regulations, standards)
- Recommendations for improvement

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Process Effectiveness with Worked Example

**Worked Example (KTU-style numerical):**

A medical device company plans to manufacture **10,000 sterile syringes per day**. At the end of the shift, the actual output was **9,200 good units**, and **150 units** were reworked. Determine the **Process Effectiveness** and **First Pass Yield (FPY)**.

**Given:**
- Planned Output = 10,000 units
- Good Units (Actual Output) = 9,200 units
- Reworked Units = 150 units
- Total Defects = (Planned - Good) = 10,000 - 9,200 = 800 units

**Step 1: Calculate Process Effectiveness**

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Actual Good Output}}{\text{Planned Output}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{9{,}200}{10{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = 0.92 \times 100\% = 92\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate First Pass Yield (FPY)**

Total units entering inspection = 9,200 (good) + 800 (defects) = 10,000 units

Units passing first time = 9,200 units

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{9{,}200}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 92\%
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation for KTU**

> [!NOTE]
> A FPY of **92%** means **8%** of units required rework or were rejected. The process owner must trigger a CAPA investigation to bring FPY above the industry-acceptable threshold (typically **≥ 99%** for sterile medical devices).

**Step 4: Calculate Process Efficiency (if value-adding time = 6 hours of an 8-hour shift):**

$$
\text{Process Efficiency} = \frac{6}{8} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk-Based Thinking in Process Approach

For every identified process risk, the organization must apply the following logical matrix:

$$
\text{Risk Priority Number (RPN)} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity of harm (rated 1–10)
- **O** = Probability of Occurrence (rated 1–10)
- **D** = Probability of Detection failure (rated 1–10)

> **Decision Rule:**
> - If RPN ≥ 100 → **Mandatory risk control** (mitigation action required).
> - If RPN 50–99 → **Conditionally acceptable** with monitoring.
> - If RPN < 50 → **Acceptable as-is** with periodic review.

**Worked Example (KTU style):**
A hospital infusion pump has:
- S = 9 (severity of patient harm if pump fails)
- O = 5 (occurrence of motor failure)
- D = 4 (probability that final test will NOT detect the failure)

$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180
$$

Since RPN = 180 ≥ 100, **mandatory risk control** is required — a redesign of the motor controller and 100% final functional testing are both needed.

### 3.4 Mapping the Process Approach to ISO 13485 (Medical Device Bridge)

Although ISO 13485 is the medical device-specific QMS, ISO 9001:2015's process approach forms its philosophical foundation. The mapping is:

$$
\text{ISO 9001:2015 Process Approach} \longleftrightarrow \text{ISO 13485:2016 Medical Device QMS}
$$

| ISO 9001:2015 Element | Medical Device Extension in ISO 13485 |
|------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Documented Information | Device Master Record (DMR), Device History Record (DHR) |
| Risk-Based Thinking | Formal ISO 14971 Risk Management File |
| Customer Focus | Patient Safety, Clinical Evaluation |
| Design & Development | Design Controls per 21 CFR 820.30 |
| CAPA | Mandatory CAPA with regulatory reporting |
| Management Review | Includes post-market surveillance data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PDCA Cycle Applied to a Process-Based QMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PLAN: Establish Quality Objectives, Processes, KPIs, and Risk Controls"]
    node2["DO: Implement and Execute the Planned Processes with Trained Resources"]
    node3["CHECK: Monitor, Measure, and Analyze Process Performance vs Objectives"]
    node4["ACT: Take Corrective Actions, Drive Continual Improvement"]
    node5["Improved QMS Performance: Better Patient Safety, Higher FPY, Lower CAPA"]
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 -.Feedback Loop.-> node1
```

### 4.2 Process Approach — Inputs, Activities, Outputs, and Controls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["INPUTS: Customer Requirements, Raw Materials, Design Inputs, Regulations"]
    inB["ACTIVITIES: Design, Manufacture, Sterilization, Inspection, Packaging"]
    inC["OUTPUTS: Finished Medical Device, Test Reports, Labels, DHR"]
    inD["CONTROLS: SOPs, ISO 13485, ISO 14971 Risk Management, KPIs"]
    inE["CUSTOMERS: Patients, Hospitals, Clinicians, Regulators"]
    inA --> inB
    inB --> inC
    inD --> inB
    inC --> inE
    inE -.Feedback and Complaints.-> inA
```

### 4.3 The Seven Quality Management Principles — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QMSCore["ISO 9001:2015 QMS FOUNDATION"]
    QMSCore --> P1["Principle 1: Customer Focus"]
    QMSCore --> P2["Principle 2: Leadership"]
    QMSCore --> P3["Principle 3: Engagement of People"]
    QMSCore --> P4["Principle 4: Process Approach"]
    QMSCore --> P5["Principle 5: Improvement"]
    QMSCore --> P6["Principle 6: Evidence Based Decision Making"]
    QMSCore --> P7["Principle 7: Relationship Management"]
    P1 --> MDApp["Applied to Medical Devices: Patient Safety, Post Market Surveillance"]
    P2 --> MDApp
    P3 --> MDApp
    P4 --> MDApp
    P5 --> MDApp
    P6 --> MDApp
    P7 --> MDApp
```

### 4.4 Top-Down QMS Process Map for a Medical Device Company

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MGMT["Management Processes: Strategic Planning, Management Review, Internal Audit, Risk Management"]
    CORE1["Core Process 1: Design and Development"]
    CORE2["Core Process 2: Procurement and Supplier Control"]
    CORE3["Core Process 3: Production and Sterilization"]
    CORE4["Core Process 4: Distribution and Post Market Surveillance"]
    SUP1["Support Process A: Document Control and Training"]
    SUP2["Support Process B: Calibration and Maintenance"]
    SUP3["Support Process C: HR and Infrastructure"]
    CUST["External Customer: Patient, Clinician, Hospital, Regulator"]
    MGMT --> CORE1
    MGMT --> CORE2
    MGMT --> CORE3
    MGMT --> CORE4
    SUP1 --> CORE1
    SUP1 --> CORE2
    SUP1 --> CORE3
    SUP1 --> CORE4
    SUP2 --> CORE3
    SUP3 --> CORE1
    SUP3 --> CORE2
    SUP3 --> CORE3
    SUP3 --> CORE4
    CORE1 --> CUST
    CORE2 --> CORE3
    CORE3 --> CORE4
    CORE4 --> CUST
```

### 4.5 Risk-Based Thinking Flow (Integrated into Process Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Identify the Process and Its Context"]
    A1["Identify Interested Parties and Requirements"]
    A2["Identify Risks and Opportunities per Process"]
    A3["Evaluate Risk using S x O x D Matrix"]
    A4{"Is RPN greater than or equal to 100?"}
    A5["Implement Risk Control Measures: SOP, Validation, Training"]
    A6["Document as Acceptable with Periodic Monitoring"]
    A7["Verify Effectiveness through KPIs and Internal Audit"]
    A8["Update Quality Manual and Process Documentation"]
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -- YES --> A5
    A4 -- NO --> A6
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define the Process Approach as described in ISO 9001:2015. List any three Quality Management Principles.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Process Approach** is the systematic identification and management of interrelated processes and their interactions to achieve the organization's intended results, as defined in Clause 4.4 of ISO 9001:2015. It involves defining inputs, activities, outputs, controls, and resources for each process, then managing them as a coherent system using the PDCA cycle.

**Three Quality Management Principles:**
1. **Customer Focus** — Meeting customer requirements and striving to exceed expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Establishing unity of purpose and direction by top management.
3. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Basing decisions on the analysis of data and information.

*(Any three of the seven principles accepted for full marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is a Turtle Diagram? How is it used in the process approach?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Turtle Diagram** is a visual tool used to document a process by capturing six elements (drawn as a "turtle" shape):
1. **What** — The process name and scope.
2. **With What** — Inputs, resources, and materials.
3. **Who** — Process owner and people involved.
4. **How** — Procedures, work instructions, and methods.
5. **Measured by** — KPIs and monitoring methods.
6. **To Achieve** — Objectives and expected outputs.

**Use in Process Approach:**
- It is used to **define, document, and control** every process in the QMS.
- Provides auditors and process owners a single-page view of process inputs, controls, and outputs.
- Facilitates gap analysis and risk identification per process.
- Forms the basis of **internal audit checklists** in ISO 9001:2015 and ISO 13485:2016 implementations.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, internal choice between Question A and Question B is provided.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **seven (7) Quality Management Principles** of ISO 9001:2015. For each principle, give **one example from the medical device industry**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** as applied to a medical device manufacturing process. How does it differ when applied to an individual process versus the entire QMS? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The seven Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2015 are:

**1. Customer Focus**
Organizations depend on their customers; therefore, they should understand current and future customer needs, meet requirements, and exceed expectations.
*Medical Device Example:* A cardiac stent manufacturer conducts post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) studies to gather real-world feedback from interventional cardiologists and improve subsequent device generations.

**2. Leadership**
Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and an engaging environment that enables people to become fully involved in achieving the organization's quality objectives.
*Medical Device Example:* The CEO and top management sign the Quality Policy, conduct Management Reviews quarterly, and allocate budget for QMS improvements and CAPA closure.

**3. Engagement of People**
Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance the organization's ability to create and deliver value.
*Medical Device Example:* Production line operators are trained and certified on aseptic filling techniques; they are authorized to stop the line if a contamination risk is detected (stop-the-line authority).

**4. Process Approach**
Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively and efficiently when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes that function as a coherent system.
*Medical Device Example:* The Design Control process is integrated with Risk Management (ISO 14971), Verification, Validation, and Design Transfer processes through documented interfaces in the Design History File (DHF).

**5. Improvement**
Successful organizations focus on continual improvement as a permanent objective.
*Medical Device Example:* Implementing Kaizen events in an injection molding line to reduce syringe defect rate from 5% to 1% over 6 months.

**6. Evidence-Based Decision Making**
Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data and information are more likely to produce desired results.
*Medical Device Example:* Trend analysis of complaint data revealing a clustering of malfunctions in a specific device lot triggers a CAPA and possible recall.

**7. Relationship Management**
For sustained success, an organization manages its relationships with interested parties such as suppliers.
*Medical Device Example:* Conducting annual supplier audits, maintaining Approved Supplier List (ASL), and signing Quality Agreements with critical component suppliers.

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**The PDCA Cycle Explained:**

The PDCA cycle is a four-stage iterative management method used for the continuous improvement of processes and products. In the medical device industry, it is applied at two levels:

**1. PDCA Applied to an Individual Manufacturing Process (e.g., Sterilization of Surgical Gloves):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PLAN:} & \quad \text{Define sterilization parameters: temperature } 121^{\circ}\text{C}, \text{ pressure } 15\text{ psi}, \text{ time } 30 \text{ min}. \\
& \quad \text{Document in SOP. Identify risks: under-sterilization, over-exposure.} \\
\text{DO:} & \quad \text{Operate the autoclave as per SOP. Record temperature, pressure, and time on batch record.} \\
\text{CHECK:} & \quad \text{Review biological indicator (BI) test results. Compare with } F_0 \text{ value target.} \\
& \quad \text{If } F_0 \geq 8 \text{ min, sterilization is validated as effective.} \\
\text{ACT:} & \quad \text{If BI fails, place batch on quarantine, raise NCR (Non-Conformance Report),} \\
& \quad \text{investigate root cause (5-Why or Fishbone), apply CAPA, and re-validate.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. PDCA Applied to the Entire QMS (Macro Level):**

- **PLAN:** Top management establishes the **Quality Policy**, sets **Quality Objectives** (e.g., reduce field complaints by 20% YoY), and allocates resources in the annual QMS plan.
- **DO:** All departments execute their processes per documented procedures; training is delivered; CAPA is implemented.
- **CHECK:** Internal audits are conducted; Management Review evaluates audit results, customer feedback, KPI trends, and CAPA status every 6–12 months.
- **ACT:** Improvement projects are launched; the Quality Manual is revised; the QMS is continually refined.

**Key Difference Between the Two Levels:**

| Aspect | Individual Process PDCA | QMS-Wide PDCA |
|--------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Scope** | One specific process | Entire quality system |
| **Owner** | Process owner | Top management |
| **Frequency** | Per batch / per shift | Per management review cycle (6–12 months) |
| **Inputs** | Process-specific data (e.g., BI results) | Audit results, KPIs, customer feedback, regulatory changes |
| **Output** | Corrected/improved process | Revised quality policy, objectives, and resource allocation |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **clause structure of ISO 9001:2015** (Annex SL — 10 clauses). Which clauses are auditable and which are not? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A medical device company plans to manufacture **5,000 surgical masks per day**. At the end of the shift, **4,650 good units** were produced, and **200 units** were reworked. Calculate the **Process Effectiveness** and **First Pass Yield (FPY)**. Interpret the result and recommend improvements using the **PDCA cycle**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

ISO 9001:2015 follows the **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, also known as **Annex SL**, consisting of 10 clauses.

| Clause | Title | Auditable? | Key Requirement |
|--------|-------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Scope | No (Introductory) | Defines the boundaries of QMS application |
| 2 | Normative References | No (Introductory) | Refers to ISO 9000:2015 |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | No (Introductory) | Vocabulary used in the standard |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | **Yes (Normative)** | Identify internal/external issues, interested parties, QMS scope |
| 5 | Leadership | **Yes (Normative)** | Top management commitment, quality policy, roles & responsibilities |
| 6 | Planning | **Yes (Normative)** | Risk-based thinking, quality objectives, planning of changes |
| 7 | Support | **Yes (Normative)** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| 8 | Operation | **Yes (Normative)** | Operational planning, control of products/services, design & development, control of externally provided products |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | **Yes (Normative)** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| 10 | Improvement | **Yes (Normative)** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

**Auditable vs. Non-Auditable:**
- **Clauses 1, 2, 3** are **introductory** and **not auditable**.
- **Clauses 4 to 10** are **normative (auditable)** — these contain the actual requirements against which an organization is certified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the new risk-based thinking (Clause 6) with the old "Preventive Action" concept. Preventive action was **removed** in ISO 9001:2015; it is now addressed through **risk-based thinking** embedded in the entire QMS.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Planned Output = 5,000 units
- Good Units Produced = 4,650 units
- Reworked Units = 200 units
- Total Defective Units = 5,000 - 4,650 = 350 units

**Step 1: Calculate Process Effectiveness**

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{4{,}650}{5{,}000} \times 100\% = 93\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate First Pass Yield (FPY)**

Total units inspected = 5,000 units (assumed all passed through final inspection)

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Units Passing on First Attempt}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{4{,}650}{5{,}000} \times 100\% = 93\%
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

A FPY of **93%** indicates that **7%** of masks failed first-time inspection and required rework. For a Class I medical device, this is **below industry benchmark (≥ 99%)** and signals a process capability issue.

**Step 4: PDCA-Based Recommendations**

- **PLAN:** Set a target of **FPY ≥ 99%** within 3 months; review the melt-blown fabric supplier; revise the ultrasonic welding SOP.
- **DO:** Train operators on the new SOP; install in-line vision inspection; perform IQ/OQ/PQ on the welding equipment.
- **CHECK:** Monitor daily FPY on the SPC chart; conduct weekly trend analysis; raise CAPA for any FPY drop below 99%.
- **ACT:** Implement preventive maintenance of welding heads every 500 batches; conduct supplier quality audit; document lessons learned in the Quality Manual.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to convert the rework figure (200 units) into the calculation — for FPY, **only first-pass successful units** count, NOT reworked units. Including reworked units will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. Many students fail to **interpret** the result and skip the PDCA-based improvement recommendation. Marks are explicitly allocated for **interpretation** and **action plan** in KTU's analytical questions.
> 3. Avoid using the vertical pipe symbol in markdown answers; use words like "equals" or "divided by" when writing solutions in tabular form on the answer sheet.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Facts for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- **ISO 9001:2015** is the current revision; the previous version (ISO 9001:2008) is now withdrawn.
- ISO 9001 specifies **requirements** for a QMS, not a product quality standard.
- There are **7 Quality Management Principles** — memorize all seven in order: **Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management**.
- The **Process Approach** is Principle No. **4** and is implemented using the **PDCA cycle**.
- ISO 9001:2015 has **10 clauses** (Annex SL). Clauses **1–3 are introductory (not auditable)**, and clauses **4–10 are auditable (normative)**.
- **Risk-Based Thinking** is a major change introduced in 2015 — it replaces the old Preventive Action concept.
- **Documented Information** is the new term (replaces "documents and records" in the 2008 version).
- The **PDCA cycle** has 4 phases: **Plan, Do, Check, Act** — and is applied at both the process level and the QMS level.
- A **process** has: **Inputs, Activities, Outputs, Resources, Controls, Owner, KPIs, Risks**.
- For medical devices, ISO 9001 is the **foundation** for **ISO 13485:2016** (the medical device-specific QMS standard).
- **Process Effectiveness** = (Actual Good Output / Planned Output) × 100%
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** = (Units passing on first attempt / Total units inspected) × 100%
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** = Severity × Occurrence × Detection (each rated 1–10).
- Six minimum documented procedures were required in 2008; in 2015, the focus is on "documented information" with flexibility in format and media.
- **Top management commitment** (Clause 5) is the single most critical success factor for any QMS implementation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** If you remember nothing else, remember the **7 Principles** + the **4 PDCA phases** + the **10 clauses**. These three items alone account for **60–70%** of marks in any KTU question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 13485 (Deeper Understanding)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ISO 13485 (Deeper Understanding)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ISO 13485

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 13485:2016 – "Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes"** is an internationally harmonized standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that specifies the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide **medical devices and related services** that consistently meet **customer requirements** and applicable **regulatory requirements**.

The current edition is the **third edition**, published on **March 1, 2016**, and it supersedes the second edition (ISO 13485:2003). Unlike most modern ISO standards, ISO 13485:2016 deliberately retains the structure of ISO 9001:2008 rather than adopting the High-Level Structure (HLS) introduced in Annex SL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** ISO 13485 is the *de facto* regulatory QMS standard for medical devices globally. It is mandated by the **EU MDR 2017/745**, **EU IVDR 2017/746**, **Canada (MDSAP)**, **Japan (MHLW Ordinance 169)**, and forms the foundation of the **Medical Device Single Audit Program (MDSAP)**. In India, the **CDSCO** accepts ISO 13485 certification as substantial evidence of QMS compliance under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are running a **hospital pharmacy** that prepares sterile injections. A patient receiving an injection trusts that:

1. The needle is sterile (no contamination).
2. The correct drug is in the correct dose.
3. The injection will not cause unexpected harm.
4. If something goes wrong, the pharmacy will investigate and prevent recurrence.

Now scale this trust from one pharmacy to a **factory producing 10 million syringes per year**, distributed across **50 countries**, each with different regulators. How do you *systematically* guarantee this trust?

> **Answer:** You build a **documented, auditable, repeatable system** — a Quality Management System — and you follow a recognized international recipe. That recipe is **ISO 13485**.

**Real-world analogy:**

| Concept | ISO 13485 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Restaurant hygiene rules (FSSAI/HACCP) | ISO 13485 QMS for medical devices |
| Recipe standardization in a chain restaurant | Documented Work Instructions & SOPs |
| Customer complaint register in a hotel | CAPA, Feedback & Complaint Handling |
| Annual kitchen audit by health inspector | Internal Audit & Management Review |
| Supplier verification for fresh produce | Purchasing Controls & Supplier Management |

In short: **ISO 13485 is the "kitchen hygiene code" of the medical device industry** — but instead of avoiding food poisoning, it avoids patient harm.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Terminology (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Definitions (ISO 13485:2016, Clause 3):**
> - **Medical Device (3.10):** Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, implant, reagent, software, material, or other article intended by the manufacturer to be used for human beings for medical purposes.
> - **Active Medical Device (3.1):** A device whose operation depends on a source of energy other than the energy generated by the human body.
> - **Implantable Medical Device (3.4):** A device intended to be totally introduced into the human body.
> - **Sterile Medical Device (3.13):** A device that is free from viable microorganisms.
> - **Manufacturer (3.8):** Any natural or legal person with responsibility for design, manufacture, packaging, or labelling of a medical device.
> - **Quality Management System (3.10):** The set of interrelated or interacting elements that an organization uses to direct and control its activities.
> - **Risk (3.13):** The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Audit Cycle Metric:** Certified organizations undergo surveillance audits **once per year (every 12 months)** and a full re-certification audit **every 3 years**. **Non-conformities (NCs)** are categorized as *Minor* (single isolated lapse) or *Major* (systemic failure, absence, or multiple related minors). A Major NC can suspend certification.

---

## 1.4 Scope of ISO 13485

ISO 13485:2016 applies to organizations regardless of their size and type, including:

1. **Manufacturers** of medical devices
2. **Distributors** and importers
3. **Sterilization service providers** (contract sterilizers)
4. **Calibration and maintenance service providers**
5. **OEM suppliers** of components or sub-assemblies
6. **Software developers** for medical device Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)

The standard is applicable across the **entire life cycle** of a medical device: from design and development → production → installation → servicing → post-market surveillance → disposal.

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of ISO 13485 Philosophy

```
┌─────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
│  PROCESS APPROACH   │  │  RISK-BASED THINKING│  │ DOCUMENTATION &     │
│                     │  │                     │  │ EVIDENCE            │
│ "Manage activities  │  │ "Apply controls     │  │ "If it isn't written│
│  as interlinked     │  │  proportional to    │  │  down, it wasn't    │
│  processes with     │  │  the risk to the    │  │  done."             │
│  measurable inputs  │  │  patient."          │  │                     │
│  and outputs."      │  │                     │  │                     │
└─────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Answers that *only* describe ISO 13485 as a "quality standard" score low. High-scoring answers explicitly mention its **regulatory purpose** (clause title literally says so), the **process approach**, **risk-based approach**, and its **linkage to ISO 14971 (Risk Management)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8-Clause Architecture of ISO 13485:2016

ISO 13485 follows a logical top-down structure. **Clauses 1–3 are informative (no auditable requirements)**; **Clauses 4–8 are normative (auditable)**.

### Clause-by-Clause Breakdown

**Clause 1 – Scope**
Defines applicability. Does not prescribe the type of medical device.

**Clause 2 – Normative References**
References **ISO 14971:2007** (Risk management) and other essential supporting standards.

**Clause 3 – Terms and Definitions**
13 specific definitions. Where undefined, definitions from **ISO 9000:2015** apply.

**Clause 4 – Quality Management System** *(Normative)*
- 4.1 General QMS requirements (process identification, interaction mapping)
- 4.2 Documentation requirements (Quality Manual, Medical Device File, control of documents & records)
- 4.3 Medical Device File — device-specific dossier
- 4.4 Control of Work Environment (contamination control)
- 4.5 Validation of Software used in QMS (e.g., ERP, eDMS)
- 4.6 Validation of Processes for Sterilization and Sterile Barrier Systems

**Clause 5 – Management Responsibility** *(Normative)*
- 5.1 Management Commitment, Customer Focus
- 5.2 Customer Focus / Regulatory Focus
- 5.3 Quality Policy
- 5.4 Planning (Quality Objectives)
- 5.5 Responsibility, Authority, Communication
- 5.6 Management Review (at least every 12 months)

**Clause 6 – Resource Management** *(Normative)*
- 6.1 Provision of Resources
- 6.2 Human Resources — competency, training, awareness, hygiene
- 6.3 Infrastructure — buildings, workspace, utilities, process equipment
- 6.4 Work Environment — contamination control, lighting, temperature

**Clause 7 – Product Realization** *(Normative)* — *Most extensive clause*
- 7.1 Planning of Product Realization
- 7.2 Customer-Related Processes (contract review, communication, complaints)
- 7.3 Design and Development (planning → inputs → outputs → review → verification → validation → transfer → changes)
- 7.4 Purchasing (supplier selection, monitoring, re-evaluation)
- 7.5 Production and Service Provision (traceability, identification, sterilization, preservation)
- 7.6 Control of Monitoring and Measuring Equipment (calibration)

**Clause 8 – Measurement, Analysis and Improvement** *(Normative)*
- 8.1 General (planning of monitoring)
- 8.2 Monitoring and Measurement (feedback, complaint handling, regulatory reporting)
- 8.3 Control of Nonconforming Product
- 8.4 Analysis of Data
- 8.5 Improvement (CAPA, preventive actions)

---

## 2.2 The Process Approach & PDCA Cycle

ISO 13485 mandates a **process approach** — every activity is treated as a process with:
- **Inputs** → **Activities** → **Outputs** → **Feedback Loop**

The classical **PDCA (Deming/Shewhart) Cycle** is embedded throughout:

| PDCA Stage | Application in ISO 13485 |
|---|---|
| **P – Plan** | Quality Policy, Quality Objectives, Planning of Product Realization (7.1) |
| **D – Do** | Design & Development (7.3), Production (7.5), Training (6.2) |
| **C – Check** | Internal Audit (8.2.4), Monitoring & Measurement (8.2), Management Review (5.6) |
| **A – Act** | CAPA (8.5.2/8.5.3), Continual Improvement (8.5.1) |

---

## 2.3 The Core 'Why' and 'How' of Each Critical Concept

### 2.3.1 Why "Medical Device File" (MDF) is mandatory (Clause 4.2.3)

A **Quality Manual** describes *the system*. A **Medical Device File** describes *the specific device*. This includes:

- General description, intended purpose, labelling
- Specifications, procedures for manufacturing, packaging, storage, handling, sterilization
- Procedures for measuring and monitoring
- Risk management file (per ISO 14971)
- Verification and validation documents
- Clinical evaluation reports
- Post-market surveillance plan and reports

> **KTU Pearl:** A common exam question is: *"Distinguish between Quality Manual and Medical Device File."* Quality Manual = System-level. MDF = Product-level.

### 2.3.2 Why Design Controls are exhaustive (Clause 7.3)

Design and development of a medical device follows a strict waterfall:

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Inputs} \rightarrow \text{Outputs} \rightarrow \text{Review} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Validation} \rightarrow \text{Transfer}$$

| Design Sub-Clause | What it Requires | Output Document |
|---|---|---|
| 7.3.1 General | Design & development procedures | Design Control SOP |
| 7.3.2 Planning | Stages, reviews, responsibilities | Design & Development Plan |
| 7.3.3 Inputs | Functional, performance, regulatory, risk, usability | Design Input Document |
| 7.3.4 Outputs | Drawings, specs, BOM, software code, labelling | Design Output Package |
| 7.3.5 Review | Cross-functional review at each stage | Design Review Minutes |
| 7.3.6 Verification | "Did we make the device right?" | Verification Protocols & Reports |
| 7.3.7 Validation | "Did we make the right device?" — under simulated use | Validation Protocols & Reports |
| 7.3.8 Transfer | Production specifications transferred to manufacturing | Transfer Plan & Report |
| 7.3.9 Changes | Change control during and after design | Engineering Change Order (ECO) |
| 7.3.10 Files | Design History File (DHF) | DHF Index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification vs. Validation — Examiner's Favourite Distinction:**
> - **Verification (V&V):** Confirms that design outputs meet design inputs — *"Are we building the device correctly?"*
> - **Validation (V&V):** Confirms that the device meets user needs and intended use — *"Are we building the correct device?"*

---

### 2.3.3 Why CAPA is central (Clause 8.5)

**CAPA = Corrective Action + Preventive Action**

```
Defect / Complaint / Audit Finding
        |
        v
[1] Problem Identification  (8.3 Nonconforming Product / 8.2.2 Complaint)
        |
        v
[2] Root Cause Investigation  (5 Whys, Fishbone, FMEA)
        |
        v
[3] Action Plan               (Containment + Correction + CA + PA)
        |
        v
[4] Implementation            (Documented Action Records)
        |
        v
[5] Effectiveness Verification (Did the action actually fix the root cause?)
        |
        v
[6] Closure / Re-open if ineffective
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Correction** with **Corrective Action**. A *correction* is fixing the immediate problem (e.g., recalling the batch). A *corrective action* addresses the *root cause* to prevent recurrence. ISO 13485 demands both.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although ISO 13485 is a process standard, certain **quantitative metrics** are routinely used in its application. These appear frequently in numerical exam problems.

| Metric / Equation | Formula | Application | Unit / Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Validating manufacturing process; FDA guidance recommends $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Six-Sigma quality monitoring in QMS | Defects per million |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units Passed Without Rework}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | Process performance KPI | Percentage (%) |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Multi-stage process cumulative yield | Decimal (0–1) |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA scoring (S=Severity, O=Occurrence, D=Detection), each rated 1–10 | Score 1–1000 |
| **Acceptable Risk Threshold** | $\text{Risk} = S \times P$ (where P = Probability of Harm) | ISO 14971 risk acceptability | Score-based |
| **Recall Effectiveness Rate** | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Units Returned}}{\text{Units Distributed}} \times 100\%$ | Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) audit | Percentage (%) |
| **Calibration Interval Verification** | $\Delta_{drift} = \dfrac{\vert x_{post} - x_{ref} \vert}{U_{ref}}$ | Where $\Delta_{drift}$ ≤ 1 indicates acceptable | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Training Effectiveness Index** | $TEI = \dfrac{\text{Post-Test Score} - \text{Pre-Test Score}}{\text{Max Score} - \text{Pre-Test Score}} \times 100$ | Clause 6.2 competency verification | Percentage (%) |
| **Audit Finding Closure Time** | $T_{closure} = T_{close} - T_{raised}$ | CAPA closure tracking | Days |

> [!NOTE]
> The `$\vert$` notation has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` or `mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In LaTeX-rendered output, these render as proper vertical bars.

---

## 2.5 Risk-Based Approach — Linkage to ISO 14971

ISO 13485:2016 (Annex A) makes the connection explicit: every clause should be interpreted through the lens of **risk to the patient and user**, not just risk to the business.

For example:

| Activity | Risk-Based Question |
|---|---|
| Work environment control (4.4) | What contamination risk exists for the device? |
| Training (6.2) | Is competency verified for safety-critical tasks? |
| Purchasing (7.4) | How does supplier nonconformance risk propagate? |
| Process validation (7.5.6) | Is the validation effort proportional to risk? |
| Complaint handling (8.2.2) | Does the response time match severity? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** The risk-based approach allows a manufacturer of low-risk Class I bandages to have a **proportionally lighter QMS** than a manufacturer of Class III implantable pacemakers. This is the heart of the modern "right-sized" regulatory framework.

---

## 2.6 ISO 13485 vs. ISO 9001 — The Critical Distinctions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** "ISO 13485 is based on ISO 9001, so they are the same." — **WRONG.** ISO 13485 has unique medical device clauses absent in ISO 9001.

| Aspect | ISO 9001:2015 | ISO 13485:2016 |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Customer satisfaction, continual improvement | Patient safety, regulatory compliance |
| **Structure** | High-Level Structure (HLS) – 10 clauses | Legacy 8-clause structure |
| **Risk Treatment** | Risk-based thinking for opportunities and threats | Risk management per ISO 14971 |
| **Documentation** | Less prescriptive | Highly prescriptive (MDF, DHF, technical files) |
| **Preventive Action** | Removed (replaced by risk-based thinking) | Still mandatory (Clause 8.5.3) |
| **Statutory & Regulatory** | Generic | Explicitly required (Clause 1, 5.2, 7.2.1, 8.2.2) |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | Not specified | Required (Clause 8.2.1, 8.4) |
| **Design Controls** | Generic | Detailed 7.3 series with sub-clauses |
| **Scope of Application** | Any industry | Medical devices and related services |
| **Continual Improvement** | Mandatory | Required where appropriate, not a strong driver |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations and quantitative problem walkthroughs commonly tested in KTU examinations on ISO 13485 topics.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index (Cpk) for a Sterile Barrier Process

**Problem Statement (Sample KTU-type question):**
A medical device manufacturer seals a sterile barrier pouch. The seal strength specification is:

$$\mu_{target} = 2.0 \text{ N/15mm}, \quad USL = 2.8 \text{ N/15mm}, \quad LSL = 1.2 \text{ N/15mm}$$

A sample of 25 measurements yielded:

$$\bar{x} = 2.15 \text{ N/15mm}, \quad s = 0.20 \text{ N/15mm}$$

Determine the **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** and comment on whether the process is **capable** for medical device production (FDA expectation: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

The process mean is $\mu \approx \bar{x} = 2.15$ N/15mm.
The process standard deviation is $\sigma \approx s = 0.20$ N/15mm (using $s$ as $\sigma$ estimator from the sample).

The specification limits are:
- $USL = 2.8$ N/15mm
- $LSL = 1.2$ N/15mm

**Step 2 — Recall the formula for Cpk.**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( C_{pu}, C_{pl} \right)$$

where the upper and lower one-sided capability indices are:

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the upper capability expression.**

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2.8 - 2.15}{3 \times 0.20}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$2.8 - 2.15 = 0.65$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$3 \times 0.20 = 0.60$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_{pu}$.**

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.65}{0.60} \approx 1.0833$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the lower capability expression.**

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2.15 - 1.2}{3 \times 0.20}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$2.15 - 1.2 = 0.95$$

**Step 9 — Re-evaluate the denominator.**

$$3 \times 0.20 = 0.60$$

**Step 10 — Compute $C_{pl}$.**

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.60} \approx 1.5833$$

**Step 11 — Take the minimum.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.0833, 1.5833) = 1.0833$$

**Step 12 — Interpretation against the FDA / KTU criterion.**

The reference benchmark for a validated medical device process is $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (and ideally $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$ for safety-critical parameters).

$$C_{pk} = 1.0833 < 1.33$$

Therefore, **the process is NOT capable** of consistently meeting the specification. The **upper specification side** (high seal strength, which may damage the pouch film) is the limiting factor.

**Step 13 — Recommended Corrective Action (per ISO 13485 Clause 8.3 / 8.5).**

1. Identify root cause via FMEA (e.g., temperature drift in sealing element).
2. Implement CA: recalibrate the heat sealer, retrain operators.
3. Re-validate the process (Clause 7.5.6 — validation of processes for sterile barrier systems).
4. Re-measure Cpk; closure requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RPN Calculation for Risk-Based Validation Decision

**Problem:** During design FMEA of an infusion pump, three potential failure modes are identified:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM1: Over-infusion | 10 | 4 | 3 |
| FM2: Display blank | 6 | 5 | 7 |
| FM3: Battery low warning missed | 7 | 6 | 4 |

**Step 1 — Apply the RPN formula for each failure mode.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Compute for FM1.**

$$RPN_{FM1} = 10 \times 4 \times 3 = 120$$

**Step 3 — Compute for FM2.**

$$RPN_{FM2} = 6 \times 5 \times 7 = 210$$

**Step 4 — Compute for FM3.**

$$RPN_{FM3} = 7 \times 6 \times 4 = 168$$

**Step 5 — Rank and prioritize mitigation actions.**

| Priority | Failure Mode | RPN | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FM2 (Display blank) | 210 | Improve software diagnostic; add self-test at boot |
| 2 | FM3 (Battery warning missed) | 168 | Add audible alarm; redundant low-battery detection |
| 3 | FM1 (Over-infusion) | 120 | Already mitigated by hard limit in software; monitor |

**Step 6 — Document the risk-benefit analysis per ISO 14971:2019.**

The overall residual risk must be acceptable when weighed against the medical benefit. This must be filed in the **Risk Management File** (Clause 4.2.4) and linked to the **Medical Device File**.

---

| 7.5.1 Production Controls | Manufacturing SOPs | Routers, travelers, work instructions | "Walk me through the manufacturing process." |

| 8.5.2 CAPA | Improvement actions | CAPA log, root cause analysis | "Show a closed CAPA from last year." |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: CAPA Effectiveness Verification (Quantitative)

**Problem:** A Class II medical device manufacturer received a complaint that the device's labelling was missing the UDI carrier. The CAPA team implemented the following actions:

- **Day 0:** NCR raised; batch 100 quarantined (2,000 units).
- **Day 3:** Investigation complete — root cause: print head failure, no daily check.
- **Day 5:** CA implemented — daily print head check added to SOP-WI-027.
- **Day 30:** Verification check — 30 days of daily checks audited, 100% compliance.

**Step 1 — Define the metric.**

$$TEI_{verification} = \dfrac{\text{Days with check performed}}{\text{Total days audited}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$TEI = \dfrac{30}{30} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check with product-level metrics.**

Post-CAPA complaints for missing UDI in the next 6 months:

$$\text{Recurrence rate} = \dfrac{\text{Recurrence complaints}}{\text{Total complaints}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

If $TEI = 100\%$ AND zero recurrence, the CAPA is effective. If recurrence occurs despite the daily check, the CAPA is reopened and a deeper root cause investigation (e.g., 5-Whys, Ishikawa) is triggered.

---

## 3.5 Validation vs. Verification — Mathematical Decision Framework

$$\text{Verification} \iff \text{Design Outputs} \models \text{Design Inputs}$$

$$\text{Validation} \iff \text{Device} \models \text{User Needs \& Intended Use}$$

In logic terms:

- Verification = Each design output can be **traced back** to at least one design input (Bidirectional Traceability).
- Validation = User Need → Design Input → Design Output → Verified Device → Validated Use.

The **Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is therefore:

$$RTM_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if output } j \text{ verifies input } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Coverage requirement:

$$\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\sum_{i,j} RTM_{ij}}{n_{inputs} \times n_{outputs}} \geq 1.0 \text{ (every input verified)}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024]

**"Define Quality Management System as applied to medical devices. List any four requirements specified under Clause 4 of ISO 13485:2016."**

**Model Answer:**

> **Quality Management System (QMS):** A set of interrelated or interacting elements that an organization uses to direct and control its activities with the aim of ensuring medical device quality — i.e., consistent conformance to customer and regulatory requirements (ISO 13485:2016, Clause 3.11).

**Four requirements under Clause 4 (QMS):**
1. **Clause 4.1 — General Requirements:** Identify processes, their sequence, interaction, criteria, methods, records.
2. **Clause 4.2 — Documentation Requirements:** Quality Manual, control of documents and records.
3. **Clause 4.2.3 — Medical Device File (MDF):** A file for each medical device type or family containing all required documentation.
4. **Clause 4.5 — Validation of Software used in the QMS:** Validation of software used in production, storage, or distribution.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Distinguish between Verification and Validation with reference to ISO 13485 Clause 7.3. Give one example of each from a medical device context."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Confirmation that design and development outputs meet design and development inputs (7.3.6) | Confirmation that the resulting device meets user needs and intended use (7.3.7) |
| **Question Answered** | "Are we building the product correctly?" | "Are we building the correct product?" |
| **Stage** | Early / mid design | Late design / pre-launch |
| **Method** | Inspection, testing, calculations | Simulated-use, clinical evaluation, usability testing |
| **Example (Infusion Pump)** | Bench testing confirms pump delivers ±2% flow accuracy per spec | Clinical study / simulated-use test confirms nurses can operate the pump safely and effectively |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain in detail the structure of ISO 13485:2016 with reference to its 8 clauses. Highlight the significance of Clause 7 (Product Realization).** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the role of CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) in ISO 13485. With a suitable flowchart, describe the step-by-step CAPA process as mandated by Clause 8.5.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Structure of ISO 13485:2016:**

The standard is organized into **8 clauses**:

| Clause | Title | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Informative |
| 2 | Normative References | Informative |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Informative |
| 4 | Quality Management System | Normative |
| 5 | Management Responsibility | Normative |
| 6 | Resource Management | Normative |
| 7 | Product Realization | Normative |
| 8 | Measurement, Analysis & Improvement | Normative |

**Significance of Clause 7 (Product Realization):**
- It is the **most extensive and detailed clause** covering the entire device life cycle from customer requirements to delivery.
- Sub-clauses cover: planning (7.1), customer-related processes (7.2), **design & development (7.3)**, purchasing (7.4), production & service (7.5), and calibration (7.6).
- Clause 7.3 ensures the **Design History File (DHF)** is built, verified, validated, and transferred.
- It integrates **risk management (ISO 14971)** and **regulatory requirements** into every phase.

**(b) CAPA Process:**

CAPA is the **central improvement mechanism** of ISO 13485, ensuring that problems are not only fixed but also prevented from recurring.

**Step-by-step CAPA Process (per Clause 8.5.2):**
1. **Identify** nonconformity / complaint / audit finding (Clause 8.2, 8.3).
2. **Evaluate** the need for action (risk assessment).
3. **Determine root cause** (5-Whys, Fishbone, FMEA).
4. **Plan action** to eliminate the root cause.
5. **Verify** action does not adversely affect regulatory compliance.
6. **Implement** the documented action.
7. **Review effectiveness** of the action within a defined time frame.
8. **Preventive Action** (8.5.3) is initiated for potential nonconformities.

```
Complaint/NCR/Audit → Identify → Root Cause → Action Plan
   → Implement → Verify Effectiveness → Close CAPA
                                            |
                                  Preventive Action (8.5.3)
```

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Compare ISO 13485:2016 with ISO 9001:2015. State at least six points of difference.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) With reference to Clause 4.2.3 of ISO 13485, explain the concept of the "Medical Device File." What are the typical contents of an MDF, and why is it distinct from the Quality Manual?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer:

**(a) ISO 13485:2016 vs. ISO 9001:2015 — Six Points of Difference:**

| # | Aspect | ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective | Patient safety, regulatory compliance | Customer satisfaction, business performance |
| 2 | Structure | Legacy 8-clause structure | High-Level Structure (10 clauses) |
| 3 | Risk Management | Mandatory integration with ISO 14971 | Risk-based thinking (generic) |
| 4 | Preventive Action | Required (Clause 8.5.3) | Removed (replaced by risk thinking) |
| 5 | Documentation | Highly prescriptive (DHF, MDF) | Less prescriptive |
| 6 | Continual Improvement | Required where appropriate | Mandatory driver |
| 7 | Post-Market Surveillance | Explicitly required | Not specified |
| 8 | Design Controls | Detailed (7.3.1 to 7.3.10) | Generic |
| 9 | Statutory Compliance | Mandatory throughout | Optional reference |

**(b) Medical Device File (Clause 4.2.3):**

The MDF is a **device-specific dossier** maintained by the manufacturer for **each medical device type or family**. It serves as the **single source of truth** for all product-related regulatory and QMS documentation.

**Typical Contents of an MDF:**
1. **General description** of the device, intended purpose, and classification
2. **Labelling and packaging** specifications and master labels
3. **Product specifications** and performance criteria
4. **Procedures** for manufacturing, packaging, storage, handling, and sterilization
5. **Procedures for measurement and monitoring** of product
6. **Risk management file** (per ISO 14971)
7. **Design History File (DHF)** and technical documentation
8. **Verification and validation** reports
9. **Clinical evaluation** reports (per ISO 14155, MEDDEV 2.7/1)
10. **Post-market surveillance plan and reports**
11. **Regulatory approvals** (510(k), CE Mark certificates, etc.)

**MDF vs. Quality Manual — Key Distinction:**

| Quality Manual | Medical Device File |
|---|---|
| **System-level** document | **Product-level** document |
| Describes the QMS structure | Describes the specific device |
| One per organization | One per device type / family |
| Stable across product lines | Updated with design changes |
| Focus on "how the system works" | Focus on "how this device complies" |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "preventive action" with "corrective action."** Preventive action eliminates *potential* causes; corrective action eliminates *existing* root causes.
> 2. **Quality Manual ≠ Medical Device File.** A common 1–2 mark loss occurs when students describe both as "quality documents" without distinguishing system-level vs. product-level scope.
> 3. **ISO 13485 ≠ ISO 9001.** Listing them as "same" or "different versions of the same" attracts 0 marks for comparison questions.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention "regulatory purpose"** when defining ISO 13485 loses marks. The standard is explicitly titled *"Requirements for regulatory purposes."*
> 5. **Citing non-existent standards** (e.g., "ISO 13488" or "ISO 9001:2008") is an immediate red flag for the examiner.
> 6. **No mention of PDCA / process approach / risk-based thinking** = incomplete answer for any descriptive question worth >5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **ISO 13485:** QMS standard for medical devices, *for regulatory purposes*.
- **QMS (3.11):** Interrelated elements directing and controlling medical device activities.
- **Manufacturer (3.8):** Entity responsible for design, manufacture, packaging, labelling.
- **Risk (3.13):** Combination of probability of harm and severity of harm.

### 📐 Structural Pillars
- **8 Clauses**: 1–3 (Informative), 4–8 (Normative / Auditable).
- **Clause 4:** QMS, documentation, MDF, software validation, sterile process validation.
- **Clause 5:** Management responsibility, customer focus, QMR every 12 months.
- **Clause 6:** Resources, competency, infrastructure, work environment.
- **Clause 7:** Product realization — design, purchasing, production, calibration.
- **Clause 8:** Monitoring, NCR, CAPA, preventive action, data analysis.

### ⚙️ Critical Concepts
- **Process Approach:** Inputs → Activities → Outputs → Feedback.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan–Do–Check–Act embedded in every clause.
- **Risk-Based Approach:** Every clause interpreted through ISO 14971 lens.
- **Verification (V&V):** Output meets Input. **Validation (V&V):** Device meets user need.
- **MDF (4.2.3):** Product-level. **Quality Manual:** System-level.
- **DHF (7.3.10):** Design History File — complete design record.

### 🔄 Key Workflows
- **Design Controls:** Plan → Inputs → Outputs → Review → Verification → Validation → Transfer → Changes → DHF.
- **CAPA Flow:** Identify → Evaluate → Root Cause → Plan → Verify → Implement → Effectiveness Review → Close.
- **Certification:** Stage 1 Audit → Stage 2 Audit → Certificate (3 yrs) → Annual Surveillance → Re-Certification.

### 📊 Must-Know Formulas
- **Cpk:** $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$, target $\geq 1.33$.
- **RPN:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, range 1–1000.
- **RTY:** $RTY = \prod Y_i$.
- **DPMO:** $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$.
- **TEI:** $TEI = \dfrac{\text{Post} - \text{Pre}}{\text{Max} - \text{Pre}} \times 100$.

### 🌐 Regulatory Linkages
- **EU MDR 2017/745:** Mandates ISO 13485 for CE marking.
- **MDSAP:** Uses ISO 13485 as audit basis (USA, Canada, Japan, Brazil, Australia).
- **CDSCO India:** Accepts ISO 13485 certification as evidence under MDR 2017.
- **ISO 14971:** Risk management standard; mandatory companion.
- **IEC 62304:** Software lifecycle.
- **IEC 62366-1:** Usability engineering.
- **ISO 10993:** Biocompatibility.

### ⚠️ Examiner Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Conflating **Correction** with **Corrective Action**.
- ❌ Calling ISO 13485 and ISO 9001 "same" or "substitutable."
- ❌ Forgetting the **regulatory purpose** of the standard.
- ❌ Describing MDF without distinguishing from Quality Manual.
- ❌ Citing **preventive action as "removed"** (it is *still* in ISO 13485).
- ❌ Forgetting the **PDCA / risk-based thinking** pillars in descriptive answers.

### ✅ Quick Memory Mnemonic: **"Q-R-D-P"**
- **Q**uality Management System
- **R**isk-Based Thinking (ISO 14971)
- **D**ocumentation (MDF, DHF, DHR)
- **P**rocess Approach (PDCA)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ICMED 13485
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ICMED 13485

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ICMED 13485** is an *Indian Certification Scheme for Medical Devices* developed and managed jointly by the **Quality Council of India (QCI)**, the **Association of Indian Medical Device Industry (AiMeD)**, and the **National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB)**. It is the **Indian adaptation of the international standard ISO 13485:2016** — *"Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes."*

The scheme provides a **voluntary, third-party conformity assessment** certification that demonstrates a medical device manufacturer's compliance with the Quality Management System (QMS) requirements specified under ISO 13485:2016, tailored to suit Indian regulatory and industrial conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEEBT862 Module 3):**
> ICMED 13485 is positioned within Module 3 as the **Indian Quality Management System benchmark** that bridges domestic manufacturing practice with global regulatory expectations. It is **not** a regulation enforced by law — instead, it is a *certification scheme* that gives manufacturers a recognised mark of QMS conformity.

The scheme was officially launched in **November 2016** under the Indian government's *Make in India* initiative to address the chronic quality and compliance gaps in the Indian medical device sector, where, prior to 2017, India had **no dedicated medical device regulatory law** (devices were regulated as "drugs" under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to sell your grandmother's **homemade pickle** in a supermarket. The supermarket manager doesn't trust random jars — he wants proof that your pickle is **consistently safe, hygienic, and labeled correctly every single time**. He asks for a third-party certificate.

- **ISO 13485** is the *international recipe book* that tells pickle-makers worldwide how to maintain quality.
- **ICMED 13485** is the *Indian certificate* given by a recognised Indian auditor who has read that recipe book and verified that **your specific Indian pickle factory** follows those quality rules.
- **QCI** is the national body that issues this certificate, **AiMeD** represents the pickle-makers (industry voice), and **NABCB** ensures the auditors themselves are competent.

So, **ICMED 13485 = ISO 13485's Indian cousin**, designed to give credibility to Indian medical device makers both domestically and in export markets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - Launch Year: **November 2016**
> - Governing Body: **Quality Council of India (QCI)**
> - Industry Partner: **Association of Indian Medical Device Industry (AiMeD)**
> - Accreditation Body: **National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB)**
> - International Reference Standard: **ISO 13485:2016**

### 1.3 Why ICMED 13485 Was Created — The Regulatory Gap

Prior to the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (which came into effect on **1 January 2018**), India had **no specific QMS mandate** for medical device manufacturers under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940. This created a **compliance vacuum**:

| Issue | Pre-2017 Reality | Post-ICMED 13485 Impact |
|-------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Quality proof | Self-declared | Third-party verified |
| Export credibility | Indian firms rejected abroad | Globally accepted QMS benchmark |
| Domestic trust | Patients unsure of device safety | Confidence through certification mark |
| Regulatory readiness | Manufacturers unprepared for MDR 2017 | Bridge training to full compliance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Relationship Between ICMED 13485 and ISO 13485:2016

The ICMED scheme is **structurally and substantively aligned** with ISO 13485:2016 but adds **Indian-specific requirements**. Think of ICMED 13485 as a **superset layer** sitting on top of ISO 13485's foundation.

**Structural mapping:**

1. **ISO 13485:2016** = the technical QMS requirements (clauses 4 through 8).
2. **ICMED 13485** = the certification framework that verifies compliance with those ISO 13485 requirements, **plus** Indian regulatory linkages (CDSCO licensing, MDR 2017 risk classification, BIS standards, etc.).

### 2.2 Two Certification Levels Under ICMED

The ICMED scheme offers **two distinct certification grades** to accommodate the wide spectrum of Indian manufacturers — from small-scale cottage industries to large multinationals operating in India.

**Level 1 — ICMED 13485 (Basic QMS Certification)**

- Verifies the manufacturer's QMS against **core ISO 13485:2016 requirements**.
- Suitable for **Class A and Class B** (low-to-moderate risk) medical devices.
- Audit duration is shorter.
- Does **not** include product-type testing.

**Level 2 — ICMED 13485 Plus (Advanced Certification)**

- Verifies QMS **plus** product compliance through **independent product testing**.
- Mandatory for **Class C and Class D** (high-risk) medical devices.
- Includes verification against applicable Indian standards (BIS, ISO, IEC).
- Audit duration is more extensive.
- Aims to provide a **comprehensive compliance badge** that satisfies multiple regulatory touchpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Favourite Distinction:**
> If asked "*What is the difference between ICMED 13485 and ICMED 13485 Plus?*" — always mention: **product testing**. ICMED 13485 Plus = QMS + Product testing; ICMED 13485 = QMS only.

### 2.3 The ISO 13485:2016 Clause Structure (Which ICMED 13485 Audits Against)

Although ICMED is an Indian certification, the **auditing is performed against ISO 13485:2016 clauses**. KTU examiners frequently test students on the clause-level structure:

- **Clause 4 — Quality Management System**: General requirements, documentation requirements, medical device file, control of documents and records.
- **Clause 5 — Management Responsibility**: Management commitment, customer focus, quality policy, planning, responsibility/authority, management review.
- **Clause 6 — Resource Management**: Human resources, infrastructure, work environment, contamination control.
- **Clause 7 — Product Realization**: Planning, customer-related processes, design and development, purchasing, production and service provision, control of monitoring/measuring devices.
- **Clause 8 — Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement**: Monitoring and measurement, control of nonconforming product, analysis of data, improvement (CAPA).

### 2.4 The Process Approach in ICMED/ISO 13485

A core philosophical underpinning of ISO 13485 (and therefore ICMED 13485) is the **Process Approach**, combined with the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle and **Risk-Based Thinking**.

**The Process Approach means:** A QMS is not viewed as a set of isolated procedures but as a **network of interconnected processes**, each with inputs, outputs, controls, and feedback loops.

**PDCA integration:**

- **Plan**: Establish objectives and processes (Clause 4 + Clause 7).
- **Do**: Execute the processes (Clauses 5, 6, 7).
- **Check**: Monitor and measure (Clause 8).
- **Act**: Take corrective and improvement actions (Clause 8 — CAPA).

### 2.5 Key QMS Definitions (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions frequently asked in KTU exams:**

| Term | Definition (ISO 13485 / ICMED Context) |
|------|------------------------------------------|
| **Medical Device** | Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, implant, software, or related article intended by the manufacturer to be used for diagnostic and/or therapeutic purposes in humans. |
| **QMS** | Quality Management System — the set of interrelated or interacting elements that an organisation uses to direct and control activities to meet quality objectives. |
| **Validation** | Confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that the requirements for a specific intended use or application have been fulfilled. |
| **Verification** | Confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that specified requirements have been fulfilled. |
| **CAPA** | Corrective Action and Preventive Action — a structured process to eliminate causes of non-conformities. |
| **Management Review** | Formal evaluation of the QMS by top management at planned intervals to ensure continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness. |
| **Design and Development** | The systematic process of converting market needs into a medical device specification. |
| **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** | The systematic process to gather and analyse experience gained from devices that have been placed on the market. |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula / Metric | Symbol / Value | Unit / Reference |
|---------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| ISO 13485:2016 Reference Standard | ISO 13485:2016 | International QMS Standard |
| ICMED 13485 Launch Year | 2016 (Nov) | Year |
| Governing Body (Certification Issuer) | QCI | Quality Council of India |
| Accreditation Body (Auditor Oversight) | NABCB | National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies |
| Industry Body (Co-developer) | AiMeD | Association of Indian Medical Device Industry |
| Total ISO 13485 Clauses | Clauses 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 | Number |
| Sub-clauses in Clause 7 (Product Realization) | 7.1 through 7.6 | Number |
| Number of Certification Levels in ICMED | 2 (Basic and Plus) | Number |
| ICMED 13485 Plus = QMS plus | Product Testing | Combined Requirement |
| PDCA Cycle Phases | 4 (Plan, Do, Check, Act) | Number |
| Risk Class Mapping (Basic) | Class A and B | Low-Moderate |
| Risk Class Mapping (Plus) | Class C and D | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ICMED 13485 is the **on-ramp credential** for Indian medical device manufacturers to participate in global supply chains.
