Without it, an Indian OEM struggles to supply components or finished devices to MNCs, hospital procurement tenders, and export markets demanding ISO 13485 proof.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: How ICMED 13485 Certification Is Achieved

The certification is achieved through a structured, multi-stage process. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** of the certification lifecycle — required for 14-mark KTU questions.

**Stage 1 — Application and Pre-Assessment**

The manufacturer first submits an **application form** to a QCI-accredited certification body. The application must include:
- Legal identity and business registration of the company.
- List of medical devices manufactured.
- Risk classification (Class A, B, C, or D) of each device.
- Desired certification level (ICMED 13485 or ICMED 13485 Plus).
- Existing QMS documentation (quality manual, procedures, records).

The certification body reviews the application for **eligibility and scope**.

**Stage 2 — Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)**

The auditors verify that the manufacturer's QMS documentation **conforms in principle** to the requirements of ISO 13485:2016. Specifically, they check:
- Quality Manual (Clause 4.2.2).
- Required procedures (control of documents, control of records, internal audit, control of nonconforming product, CAPA).
- Medical Device File (Clause 4.2.3).
- Design and development procedures (if applicable).

If gaps are found, the auditor issues **Stage 1 findings**.

**Stage 3 — Stage 2 Audit (On-Site Certification Audit)**

This is the **main audit**. The auditor visits the manufacturer's facility and verifies **implementation** of the documented QMS. The auditor:
- Interviews personnel.
- Observes processes.
- Examines records.
- Tests traceability of product from receipt of raw materials to dispatch.
- Verifies CAPA effectiveness.

**Stage 4 — Non-Conformity Management**

Any deviations are classified:
- **Major non-conformity**: A systemic failure that raises doubt on the QMS.
- **Minor non-conformity**: An isolated incident that does not indicate a systemic failure.
- **Observation**: A potential risk that does not yet constitute a non-conformity.

The manufacturer must **close all major non-conformities** within **90 days** through root cause analysis and corrective action.

**Stage 5 — Certification Decision**

An independent **certification decision committee** (separate from the audit team) reviews the audit report and either:
- **Grants certification**, or
- **Defers / denies certification** based on the severity of findings.

**Stage 6 — Issuance of Certificate and ICMED Mark**

Upon approval, the manufacturer receives:
- An **ICMED 13485 certificate** valid for **3 years**.
- Permission to use the **ICMED mark** on packaging and promotional materials.

**Stage 7 — Surveillance Audits**

Surveillance audits are conducted **annually** (Year 1 and Year 2) to verify continued conformity.

**Stage 8 — Re-Certification**

At the end of the **3-year cycle**, a full re-certification audit is conducted.

### 3.2 Mathematical/Logical Representation of the Certification Timeline

We can express the certification validity cycle mathematically:

$$
T_{total} = T_{stage1} + T_{stage2} + T_{closure} + T_{validity}
$$

Where:
- $T_{stage1}$ = Duration of Stage 1 documentation audit (typically **1–3 days**).
- $T_{stage2}$ = Duration of Stage 2 on-site audit (typically **3–10 days**, depending on the number of employees and devices).
- $T_{closure}$ = Time allowed for non-conformity closure (typically **≤ 90 days**).
- $T_{validity}$ = Certificate validity period (typically **3 years**).

The total elapsed time from application to certificate issuance:

$$
T_{issue} = T_{stage1} + T_{stage2} + T_{closure}
$$

For a typical small-to-medium manufacturer:

$$
T_{issue} \approx (1 \text{ to } 3) + (3 \text{ to } 10) + (\leq 90) \text{ days}
$$

Which simplifies to:

$$
T_{issue} \leq 103 \text{ days}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ISO 13485 Clause-by-Clause QMS Mapping

The following matrix decomposes each ISO 13485 clause into its operational components — required for any 14-mark KTU question on *"Explain the requirements of ISO 13485 / ICMED 13485 QMS."*

**Clause 4 — Quality Management System**

The manufacturer must:
- Identify all QMS processes and their interaction (process map).
- Establish criteria and methods for monitoring each process.
- Ensure availability of resources and information.
- Monitor, measure (where applicable), and analyse processes.
- Implement actions to achieve planned results and maintain effectiveness.

**Clause 5 — Management Responsibility**

Top management must demonstrate **leadership and commitment** by:
- Communicating the importance of meeting customer and regulatory requirements.
- Establishing the quality policy.
- Ensuring that quality objectives are established.
- Conducting management reviews at **planned intervals** (typically every 6–12 months).

**Clause 6 — Resource Management**

The manufacturer must determine and provide:
- **Human resources**: Competent personnel based on education, training, skills, and experience.
- **Infrastructure**: Buildings, workspace, process equipment, and supporting services.
- **Work environment**: Conditions that affect product conformity.

**Clause 7 — Product Realization**

This is the **largest and most detailed clause**, covering the entire device lifecycle:
- **7.1 Planning of Product Realization**: Risk management linkage throughout the product lifecycle.
- **7.2 Customer-Related Processes**: Determination of requirements, review, and communication.
- **7.3 Design and Development**: A multi-stage process (planning, inputs, outputs, review, verification, validation, transfer, change control).
- **7.4 Purchasing**: Supplier selection, monitoring, and re-evaluation.
- **7.5 Production and Service Provision**: Production controls, installation, servicing, sterilization, validation of processes.
- **7.6 Control of Monitoring and Measuring Devices**: Calibration and validation of measuring equipment.

**Clause 8 — Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement**

The manufacturer must plan and implement monitoring, measurement, analysis, and improvement processes to:
- Demonstrate conformity of product.
- Ensure conformity of the QMS.
- Maintain effectiveness of the QMS.

This includes:
- **8.2 Monitoring and Measurement**: Customer satisfaction, internal audits, monitoring of processes and product.
- **8.3 Control of Nonconforming Product**: Identification, segregation, disposition.
- **8.4 Analysis of Data**: Statistical and trend analysis.
- **8.5 Improvement**: CAPA, preventive action, continual improvement.

### 3.4 Risk-Based Thinking — The Logical Linkage

A defining feature of ISO 13485:2016 (and therefore ICMED 13485) is **risk-based thinking applied throughout the QMS**, not just in product risk management.

The **risk-based decision formula** in QMS is:

$$
R_{residual} = f(R_{identified}, C_{controls})
$$

Where:
- $R_{residual}$ = Residual risk after controls.
- $R_{identified}$ = Initially identified risk.
- $C_{controls}$ = Effectiveness of implemented controls.

The goal of the QMS is to reduce $R_{residual}$ to an **acceptable level** as defined by the manufacturer's risk management plan (typically aligned with **ISO 14971**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ICMED 13485 Certification Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer Submits Application to Certification Body] --> B[Stage 1 Audit: Documentation Review]
    B --> C{Stage 1 Pass?}
    C -- No --> D[Manufacturer Corrects Gaps]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Stage 2 Audit: On-Site QMS Verification]
    E --> F{Major Non-Conformities?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Manufacturer Closes NCs within 90 Days]
    G --> H{Re-Verification}
    F -- No --> I[Certification Decision Committee Review]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Certificate Approved?}
    J -- No --> K[Certification Denied]
    J -- Yes --> L[ICMED 13485 Certificate Issued: Valid 3 Years]
    L --> M[Year 1 Surveillance Audit]
    M --> N[Year 2 Surveillance Audit]
    N --> O[Re-Certification Audit at Year 3]
    O --> L
```

### 4.2 ICMED 13485 Two-Tier Certification Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ICMED 13485 Scheme] --> B[Level 1: ICMED 13485]
    A --> C[Level 2: ICMED 13485 Plus]
    B --> D[QMS Audit Only]
    B --> E[Suitable for Class A and B Devices]
    C --> F[QMS Audit + Product Testing]
    C --> G[Suitable for Class C and D Devices]
    C --> H[Independent Lab Verification]
```

### 4.3 ISO 13485 Clause-to-QMS Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Clause 4: QMS Foundation] --> M[Clause 5: Management Responsibility]
    M --> R[Clause 6: Resource Management]
    R --> PR[Clause 7: Product Realization]
    PR --> MA[Clause 8: Measurement Analysis and Improvement]
    MA -.Feedback Loop.-> P
    MA -.Feedback Loop.-> M
    MA -.Feedback Loop.-> PR
    style P fill:#e1f5ff
    style M fill:#fff4e1
    style R fill:#e8f5e1
    style PR fill:#fce4ec
    style MA fill:#f3e5f5
```

### 4.4 PDCA Cycle Integration with ISO 13485 Clauses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PLAN[Plan: Clauses 4 and 7] --> DO[Do: Clauses 5, 6, 7]
    DO --> CHECK[Check: Clause 8 Monitoring]
    CHECK --> ACT[Act: Clause 8 CAPA and Improvement]
    ACT --> PLAN
```

### 4.5 ICMED 13485 Stakeholder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QCI[Quality Council of India - Scheme Owner] --> NABCB[NABCB - Accreditation of Certification Bodies]
    QCI --> AiMeD[AiMeD - Industry Voice]
    NABCB --> CB1[Certification Body A]
    NABCB --> CB2[Certification Body B]
    CB1 --> Manuf1[Medical Device Manufacturer]
    CB2 --> Manuf2[Medical Device Manufacturer]
    QCI -.Sets Standards.-> CB1
    QCI -.Sets Standards.-> CB2
    AiMeD -.Industry Feedback.-> QCI
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*"Define ICMED 13485. Name the bodies involved in its governance."*

**Model Answer:**
ICMED 13485 is an Indian Certification Scheme for Medical Devices, launched in November 2016, which certifies medical device manufacturers against the requirements of ISO 13485:2016.

The three governing bodies are:
1. **Quality Council of India (QCI)** — scheme owner and certification issuer.
2. **Association of Indian Medical Device Industry (AiMeD)** — industry partner representing manufacturers.
3. **National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB)** — accredits the certification bodies that perform audits.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*"Differentiate between ICMED 13485 and ICMED 13485 Plus certification levels."*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ICMED 13485 | ICMED 13485 Plus |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Scope | QMS audit only | QMS audit + product testing |
| Risk Class | Class A and B | Class C and D |
| Product Verification | Not included | Independent lab testing |
| Audit Duration | Shorter | Extensive |
| Mark of Conformity | QMS-level badge | QMS + product-level badge |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain the need for the ICMED 13485 certification scheme in the Indian medical device industry. Discuss the role of QCI, AiMeD, and NABCB in its governance. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Describe the two-tier certification structure of ICMED 13485. Which risk class of medical devices is targeted under each level, and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — The Need for ICMED 13485 and the Role of Governing Bodies (7 Marks)**

**Need for ICMED 13485:**

Before the launch of ICMED 13485 in **November 2016**, the Indian medical device industry faced several structural challenges:

1. **Absence of a dedicated QMS regulation**: Medical devices were regulated as "drugs" under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, with no specific QMS obligation.
2. **Lack of credibility in export markets**: Indian manufacturers were often rejected by foreign buyers due to the absence of internationally accepted QMS certification.
3. **No third-party quality verification**: Manufacturers self-declared conformity, leaving hospital procurement teams with no objective quality signal.
4. **Need for a bridge to MDR 2017**: The Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (effective 1 January 2018) introduced risk-based classification, requiring manufacturers to be QMS-ready.

**Role of the Three Governing Bodies:**

- **Quality Council of India (QCI)**: Established by the Government of India in 1997, QCI is the apex body for quality assurance. Under ICMED, QCI owns the scheme, sets certification rules, and grants the ICMED mark.
- **Association of Indian Medical Device Industry (AiMeD)**: Founded in 2008, AiMeD is the umbrella industry body representing Indian medical device manufacturers. It provides industry perspective during scheme design.
- **National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB)**: An arm of QCI that accredits certification bodies (auditors) ensuring they have the technical competence to audit medical device manufacturers.

**Part (b) — Two-Tier Certification Structure (7 Marks)**

The ICMED scheme operates on two certification levels:

- **ICMED 13485 (Basic)**: Confirms that the manufacturer has a QMS conforming to ISO 13485:2016. Targeted at manufacturers of **Class A (low risk)** and **Class B (low-moderate risk)** devices such as surgical gloves, examination gloves, bandages, and simple measuring instruments. The audit duration and scope are smaller.

- **ICMED 13485 Plus (Advanced)**: Confirms QMS conformity **plus** independent product testing against applicable Indian/international standards. Targeted at **Class C (moderate-high risk)** and **Class D (high risk)** devices such as stents, orthopaedic implants, cardiac devices, and ventilators. Higher risk class warrants deeper scrutiny because device failure could cause serious harm.

The two-tier approach ensures **proportionate scrutiny** — small-scale manufacturers of low-risk devices are not burdened with the same compliance cost as high-risk device manufacturers, while patient safety is preserved across all risk classes.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain the step-by-step process by which a medical device manufacturer obtains ICMED 13485 certification. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Describe the Quality Management System requirements under ISO 13485:2016 as adopted by ICMED 13485, covering all major clauses (4 to 8). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Certification Process (7 Marks)**

The certification process follows these sequential steps:

1. **Application**: The manufacturer submits the application with company details, device list, risk class, and desired certification level to a QCI-accredited certification body.
2. **Stage 1 Audit**: Auditors review the QMS documentation — quality manual, procedures, medical device file — to verify conformity in principle.
3. **Stage 2 Audit**: On-site audit to verify implementation — interviews, observation, and record verification.
4. **Non-Conformity Management**: Findings are categorised as major, minor, or observation. Major NCs must be closed within 90 days.
5. **Certification Decision**: An independent committee reviews the audit report and grants or denies the certificate.
6. **Certificate Issuance**: Certificate is valid for 3 years.
7. **Surveillance Audits**: Annual audits during years 1 and 2.
8. **Re-Certification**: A full re-certification audit at the end of year 3.

**Part (b) — QMS Requirements Across Clauses 4 to 8 (7 Marks)**

- **Clause 4 (QMS)**: Establish, document, implement, and maintain a QMS. Maintain a medical device file for each device family.
- **Clause 5 (Management Responsibility)**: Top management must demonstrate commitment, establish quality policy and objectives, define responsibilities, and conduct periodic management reviews.
- **Clause 6 (Resource Management)**: Provide competent personnel, adequate infrastructure, and a controlled work environment (including contamination control where applicable).
- **Clause 7 (Product Realization)**: Plan product realisation, determine customer requirements, control design and development through a structured process, control purchased products, control production processes (including validation of sterilisation and software), and calibrate monitoring/measuring devices.
- **Clause 8 (Measurement, Analysis, Improvement)**: Monitor customer satisfaction, conduct internal audits, control nonconforming product, analyse data, and take corrective/preventive actions. Establish a documented procedure for CAPA.

These requirements collectively ensure that the manufacturer consistently produces medical devices that meet customer and regulatory requirements.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in ICMED 13485 / ISO 13485 Answers:**
> 1. **Conflating ICMED with ISO 13485**: Students often write "ICMED 13485 is an international standard" — it is **not**. ISO 13485 is international; ICMED is the **Indian certification scheme** that audits against ISO 13485.
> 2. **Forgetting AiMeD**: Many students mention only QCI. AiMeD and NABCB are equal partners and must be cited.
> 3. **Confusing ICMED 13485 Plus with ISO 13485 Plus**: There is no ISO 13485 Plus. The "Plus" is unique to the ICMED scheme.
> 4. **Missing "voluntary"**: ICMED 13485 is a **voluntary** certification, not a mandatory CDSCO regulation.
> 5. **Skipping the 3-year validity and 90-day NC closure**: These numerical values are favourite KTU exam questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ICMED 13485** is the **Indian Certification Scheme for Medical Devices**, launched in **November 2016**.
- It is governed by **QCI (owner), AiMeD (industry), NABCB (auditor accreditation)**.
- It is the **Indian certification framework** auditing compliance with **ISO 13485:2016**.
- It is **voluntary**, not mandatory under Indian law.
- It has **two certification levels**: **ICMED 13485** (QMS only, Class A and B) and **ICMED 13485 Plus** (QMS + product testing, Class C and D).
- **Validity** of certificate: **3 years**, with **annual surveillance audits** and a full **re-certification audit** at year 3.
- **Non-conformity closure** window for major NCs: **≤ 90 days**.
- ICMED 13485 audits against the **5 ISO 13485 clauses (4, 5, 6, 7, 8)**.
- ICMED 13485 uses the **process approach** integrated with the **PDCA cycle** and **risk-based thinking**.
- ICMED 13485 is a **bridge to ISO 13485 compliance**, helping Indian manufacturers participate in global supply chains and prepare for the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.
- It supports the **Make in India** initiative by giving Indian manufacturers a credible quality mark recognised domestically and internationally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 14971 - Risk Management of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ISO 14971 - Risk Management of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ISO 14971** is the internationally recognized standard titled *"Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices."* It specifies a process for a manufacturer to identify the hazards associated with medical devices, including *in vitro* diagnostic (IVD) medical devices, to estimate and evaluate the associated risks, to control these risks, and to monitor the effectiveness of the controls throughout the product life cycle.

The current active revision is **ISO 14971:2019** (Third Edition), which harmonizes with **IEC 60601-1** (safety of medical electrical equipment), **ISO 13485** (QMS), and supports conformity with **EU MDR 2017/745** and **US FDA 21 CFR Part 820 / Part 803** (MDR/Adverse Event Reporting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ISO 14971 is a **process-based**, **lifecycle**, and **risk-based** standard. It does NOT prescribe pass/fail limits — the manufacturer must define, justify, and document risk acceptability criteria within their **Risk Management Policy**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (ISO 14971:2019 Clause 3)

| Term | Definition (Verbatim Style) |
|---|---|
| **Harm** | Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment |
| **Hazard** | Potential source of harm |
| **Hazardous Situation** | Circumstance in which people, property, or the environment are exposed to one or more hazards |
| **Risk** | Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm |
| **Risk Analysis** | Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk |
| **Risk Evaluation** | Process of comparing the estimated risk against given risk acceptability criteria |
| **Risk Control** | Process in which decisions are made and measures implemented to reduce risks to, or maintain risks within, specified levels |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after risk control measures have been taken |
| **Benefit-Risk Analysis** | Analysis of the benefit(s) of the intended use versus the residual risk |
| **Risk Management File (RMF)** | Set of records and other documents that demonstrate the manufacturer's compliance with this document |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Driving a Car on a Mountain Road"**

Imagine you are driving a car down a steep, winding mountain road. You want to reach the bottom safely.

- **Hazard Identification** = Spotting dangers (sharp curves, falling rocks, slippery patches).
- **Risk Estimation** = Judging how likely each danger is and how bad an accident would be (a small scratch vs. driving off a cliff).
- **Risk Evaluation** = Deciding which dangers you can tolerate (small scratches) and which you cannot (cliff falls).
- **Risk Control** = Installing guardrails, reducing speed, using headlights — actions that lower risk.
- **Residual Risk** = The risk that still remains even after guardrails are installed (you could still have a minor fender bender).
- **Benefit-Risk Analysis** = The road is dangerous, BUT you *need* to deliver critical medicine to the village at the bottom — so the benefit outweighs the residual risk.

In a **medical device** context, the "car" is the device (e.g., an implantable defibrillator, an MRI scanner, or even a software algorithm). The "passengers" are the **patient, clinician, and bystanders**. The "road" is the **clinical use environment**. The "guardrails" are **Risk Control Measures (RCMs)** such as alarms, design features, labeling, and training.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Goal of ISO 14971 is NOT zero risk.** Zero risk is impossible. The goal is to reduce risk **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** and to demonstrate that the **benefit** outweighs the **residual risk**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Management Process (Clause 4 of ISO 14971:2019)

ISO 14971 defines a closed-loop, iterative process with the following mandatory sub-processes. For the KTU 2024 examination, memorize the **logical sequence** of the **8 sub-clauses of Clause 4**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Process Logic

**Step 1 — Risk Management Process (Clause 4.1)**
The manufacturer shall establish, implement, document, and maintain an ongoing risk management process throughout the product life cycle.

**Step 2 — Management Responsibility (Clause 4.2)**
Top management shall provide evidence of its commitment to the risk management process by ensuring adequate resources, assigning responsibilities, and reviewing the process at planned intervals.

**Step 3 — Competence of Personnel (Clause 4.3)**
Personnel performing risk management tasks shall be competent based on education, training, skills, and experience.

**Step 4 — Risk Management Policy (Clause 4.4)**
The manufacturer shall establish a written **Risk Management Policy** that defines:
- Criteria for risk acceptability (e.g., a 3×5 risk matrix).
- The approach used to evaluate whether the overall residual risk is acceptable.
- The basis for the acceptability of individual risks.
- Criteria for accepting benefits when residual risk is non-acceptable.

**Step 5 — Risk Management Plan (Clause 4.5)**
The manufacturer shall establish a **Risk Management Plan** for each medical device. This is a **living document** that documents:
- Scope of the plan (device, life-cycle stages).
- Responsibilities and authorities.
- Criteria for risk acceptability (from Policy).
- Verification activities.
- Activities for collection and review of production and post-production information.

**Step 6 — Risk Management File (Clause 4.6)**
A **Risk Management File (RMF)** shall be maintained for each device, providing traceability for each identified hazard to:
- Risk analysis
- Risk evaluation
- Implementation and verification of risk control measures
- Assessment of residual risk acceptability

**Step 7 — Review of Risk Management Activities (Clause 4.7)**
The manufacturer shall conduct a **Review of Risk Management Activities** at defined milestones (e.g., design freeze, pre-market, post-market).

**Step 8 — Risk Management Report (Clause 4.8)**
A **Risk Management Report** shall be prepared at the conclusion of risk management activities, summarizing the risk management plan, execution, results of evaluation, and confirmation that the overall residual risk is acceptable.

## 2.2 Risk Analysis, Evaluation & Control — The Engineering Core (Clauses 5, 6, 7)

### 2.2.1 Risk Analysis (Clause 5)

Inputs:
- Intended use, reasonably foreseeable misuse, and the characteristics of the device.
- Hazards and hazardous situations (e.g., electrical shock, mechanical failure, software bug, biological incompatibility).
- Estimated severity and probability of occurrence of harm.

Techniques used in industry:
- **FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis)** — bottom-up, component-by-component.
- **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** — top-down, system-level failure decomposition.
- **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** — process deviations.
- **STPA (Systems-Theoretic Process Analysis)** — software/systems engineering.

### 2.2.2 Risk Evaluation (Clause 6)

Each estimated risk is compared to the **risk acceptability criteria** defined in the Risk Management Policy. The output is one of:
- Risk is acceptable — proceed.
- Risk is not acceptable — apply risk control.

### 2.2.3 Risk Control (Clause 7)

The hierarchy of risk control is **mandatory** and must be applied in this **strict priority order**:

1. **Inherent Safety by Design** — eliminate the hazard or reduce risk by changing the device itself (e.g., use lower voltage, add insulation).
2. **Protective Measures in the Device or Manufacturing Process** — add guards, alarms, or fail-safes (e.g., interlock switches).
3. **Information for Safety (IFU / Labeling / Training)** — warnings, contraindications, training material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If during Risk Control, the probability of harm cannot be estimated, the manufacturer shall list the possible consequences (severity) and determine risk control measures that reduce severity. This is a key 2019 update.

### 2.2.4 Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk (Clause 8)

After individual risks are controlled, the **Overall Residual Risk** must be evaluated. The manufacturer shall decide whether the overall residual risk is acceptable based on:
- The Risk Management Policy.
- Knowledge of the state of the art.
- Benefit-Risk Analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Risk Estimation Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk is not a single number — it is a combination function.** ISO 14971 allows manufacturers to define their own quantitative or qualitative risk scale.

### 2.3.1 Qualitative Risk Function

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability of Occurrence})
$$

### 2.3.2 Quantitative Risk (Semi-Quantitative Example)

$$
R = S \times P \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 5)
- $P$ = Probability (1 to 5)
- $D$ = Detectability or Exposure (1 to 5) — *often used in FMEA but NOT required by ISO 14971 itself.*

### 2.3.3 Standard 5×5 Risk Matrix (Industry Convention)

| Severity \ Probability | Negligible (P1) | Remote (P2) | Occasional (P3) | Probable (P4) | Frequent (P5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Negligible (S1)** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Minor (S2)** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Serious (S3)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | **Critical** |
| **Critical (S4)** | Medium | Medium | High | **Critical** | **Intolerable** |
| **Catastrophic (S5)** | Medium | High | **Critical** | **Intolerable** | **Intolerable** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In your KTU answer, never write "Risk = Severity × Probability" as if it is a fixed mathematical law. ISO 14971 explicitly states that the relationship between severity and probability may be non-linear and is best represented as a **risk matrix** or **risk chart**.

### 2.3.4 Severity Scale (ISO/TR 24971:2020 Guidance)

| Level | Severity | Description (Examples) |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | Negligible | Inconvenience or temporary discomfort |
| S2 | Minor | Temporary injury or impairment not requiring professional medical intervention |
| S3 | Serious | Injury or impairment requiring professional medical intervention |
| S4 | Critical | Permanent impairment or life-threatening injury |
| S5 | Catastrophic | Patient death |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ISO 14971 is used by **every** medical device manufacturer globally for:
- **Pre-market design control** — guiding design choices during R&D.
- **Regulatory submissions** — FDA 510(k), EU MDR Technical File, India CDSCO, Health Canada.
- **Post-market surveillance** — feeding complaints, MDRs (Medical Device Reports), and CAPAs back into the Risk Management File.
- **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** — applied alongside **IEC 62304** and **IEC 62366-1** (usability engineering).
- **Combination products** — bridging drug/device risk analyses (e.g., drug-eluting stents, prefilled syringes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Risk Management Workflow & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow Walkthrough (KTU Long-Answer Model)

The following is the canonical sequence a manufacturer follows when applying ISO 14971:2019. Every step below is examinable.

### Step A — Define Intended Use & Identify Hazards

The first engineering task is to define **intended use** and **reasonably foreseeable misuse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intended Use** = "what the manufacturer says the device is for" (labeling, IFU).
> **Reasonably Foreseeable Misuse** = use of the product for a purpose not intended by the manufacturer, but which can be reasonably anticipated.

**Worked Example — Home-Use Pulse Oximeter:**
- Intended use: non-invasive measurement of arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂) in adults at home.
- Foreseeable misuse: use on infants, use during MRI, use with nail polish still applied, use on a finger with poor perfusion.

**Hazard Identification Table — Example Output:**

| Hazard ID | Hazard Source | Hazardous Situation | Harm |
|---|---|---|---|
| H-01 | Electrical circuit | Insulation failure during battery replacement | Electrical shock (S4) |
| H-02 | Optical sensor | Misalignment due to motion artifact | Incorrect SpO₂ reading → delayed treatment (S3) |
| H-03 | Display software | Algorithm error in low-perfusion state | False normal reading → hypoxia (S4) |
| H-04 | Battery | Leakage of electrolyte | Skin burn (S3) |
| H-05 | Biocompatibility | Skin contact with enclosure | Skin irritation (S2) |

### Step B — Estimate Severity and Probability

For each hazard, severity is assigned based on clinical outcome. Probability is estimated using pre-clinical data, historical complaint rates, or published literature.

**Example Estimation:**

$$
\text{Risk}_1 = f(S4, P2) = \text{Medium (per the 5×5 matrix)}
$$

$$
\text{Risk}_2 = f(S3, P3) = \text{Medium}
$$

$$
\text{Risk}_3 = f(S4, P3) = \text{High}
$$

### Step C — Risk Evaluation

Each estimated risk is compared against the Risk Acceptability Criteria defined in the Risk Management Policy. Suppose the policy states: **"All risks classified as Medium or higher shall be reduced through risk control."**

Therefore, **H-01, H-02, H-03, H-04** must undergo risk control.

### Step D — Apply Risk Control Measures (Hierarchy Order)

**H-01 — Electrical Shock:**
- **Design (Priority 1):** Use a sealed, non-user-serviceable battery compartment.
- **Protective Measure (Priority 2):** Add reverse-polarity protection on the battery terminals.
- **Information (Priority 3):** Add IFU warning "Do not open battery compartment."

**H-03 — Algorithm Error (Highest Severity):**
- **Design (Priority 1):** Implement a built-in confidence indicator and signal-quality index (SQI) that suppresses readings when perfusion is low.
- **Protective Measure (Priority 2):** Add an audible low-confidence alert.
- **Information (Priority 3):** Update IFU with "If the display shows '---', reposition the device."

### Step E — Verify Risk Control Effectiveness

Each control measure must be **verified** through:
- Design Verification Testing (e.g., IEC 60601-1 insulation test).
- Software Validation (per IEC 62304).
- Usability Validation (per IEC 62366-1).

### Step F — Evaluate Residual Risk

Re-estimate risk after controls:

$$
\text{Residual Risk}_1 = f(S4, P1) = \text{Medium (downgraded by controls)}
$$

$$
\text{Residual Risk}_3 = f(S4, P1) = \text{Medium}
$$

### Step G — Benefit-Risk Analysis (If Residual Risk Remains Non-Acceptable)

For a pulse oximeter used in COPD patients at home:
- **Benefit:** Early detection of hypoxemia enables timely hospitalization, reducing mortality from respiratory failure.
- **Residual Risk:** False readings in motion, possibly leading to unnecessary or delayed intervention.
- **Conclusion:** The clinical benefit substantially outweighs the residual risk.

### Step H — Risk Management Report & Production Information

After launch, the manufacturer must:
- Monitor complaints and adverse events (post-market surveillance).
- Update the Risk Management File when new hazards emerge.
- Trigger a Risk Management Review at planned intervals or upon significant change.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Risk Control Decision Logic

| Estimated Risk | Risk Control Required? | Hierarchy Preference | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low / Negligible** | Optional | None | Routine QA |
| **Medium** | Mandatory | Design → Protective → IFU | Design V&V |
| **High** | Mandatory (Strict) | Design must be attempted first | Independent Verification + Validation |
| **Critical / Intolerable** | Mandatory + Management Approval | Must NOT be released unless Benefit-Risk justifies it | Regulatory Submission + Clinical Evaluation |

## 3.3 Common Quantitative Risk Expressions (For KTU Numerical Memory)

$$
R = S \times P \quad \text{(semi-quantitative, range 1 to 25)}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Priority Number (RPN)} = S \times P \times D \quad \text{(FMEA convention, range 1 to 125)}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Reduction Factor} = \frac{R_{\text{before}}}{R_{\text{after}}}
$$

$$
\text{Probability per Use} = \frac{\text{Number of Failures}}{\text{Number of Uses}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** If asked to "compute" risk, present a worked 5×5 matrix and clearly mark the cell. Do not invent numerical risk thresholds that are not in your Risk Management Policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 14971:2019 Risk Management Process — Master Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initiate Risk Management Process] --> B[Establish Risk Management Policy]
    B --> C[Define Risk Acceptability Criteria]
    C --> D[Create Risk Management Plan]
    D --> E[Define Intended Use and Foreseeable Misuse]
    E --> F[Identify Hazards and Hazardous Situations]
    F --> G[Estimate Risk for Each Hazard]
    G --> H{Risk Evaluation: Is Risk Acceptable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Document Residual Risk]
    H -- No --> J[Apply Risk Control Measures]
    J --> K[Priority 1: Inherent Safe Design]
    K --> L[Priority 2: Protective Measures]
    L --> M[Priority 3: Information for Safety]
    M --> N[Verify Effectiveness of Risk Control]
    N --> O{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    O -- No --> P[Conduct Benefit Risk Analysis]
    P --> Q{Overall Benefit Outweighs Risk?}
    Q -- No --> R[STOP: Device Not Acceptable]
    Q -- Yes --> I
    O -- Yes --> I
    I --> S[Evaluate Overall Residual Risk]
    S --> T[Prepare Risk Management Report]
    T --> U[Production and Post Production Activities]
    U --> V{New Hazard or Complaint Data?}
    V -- Yes --> E
    V -- No --> W[Periodic Management Review]
    W --> X[End of Process]
```

## 4.2 Risk Control Hierarchy — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified Hazard] --> B{Is the Risk Acceptable?}
    B -- Yes --> H[Document and Proceed]
    B -- No --> C[Apply Risk Control Hierarchy]
    C --> D1[Level 1: Inherent Safety by Design]
    D1 --> D2[Level 2: Protective Measures in Device or Process]
    D2 --> D3[Level 3: Information for Safety Labeling Training]
    D3 --> E[Verify Each Risk Control Measure]
    E --> F[Re Evaluate Residual Risk]
    F --> G{Acceptable?}
    G -- Yes --> H
    G -- No --> I[Benefit Risk Analysis by Management]
```

## 4.3 Risk Management File — Document Traceability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intended Use Document] --> B[Risk Management Plan]
    B --> C[Risk Analysis Worksheet]
    C --> D[Risk Evaluation Record]
    D --> E[Risk Control Implementation Record]
    E --> F[Verification and Validation Report]
    F --> G[Residual Risk Evaluation]
    G --> H[Benefit Risk Analysis]
    H --> I[Risk Management Report]
    I --> J[Production and Post Production Information]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop to Risk Analysis]
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Students:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners expect you to draw the **process flow** as a flowchart or sequence diagram. Mermaid-rendered ASCII flowcharts are acceptable in written exams if drawn clearly. Practice drawing the **hierarchy of risk control** from memory — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Define "Risk" as per ISO 14971:2019. How is it different from "Hazard"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Risk (ISO 14971:2019, Clause 3.18):** The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.

**Hazard (Clause 3.4):** A potential source of harm.

**Difference:**
| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Source / Potential | Quantitative / Qualitative measure |
| Question Answered | "What can go wrong?" | "How likely and how bad?" |
| Example | Electrical energy | Probability × Severity of shock |

**Marking Scheme:** Definition 1 mark, Hazard definition 1 mark, Tabular distinction 1 mark.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**List the three levels of the Risk Control hierarchy in the correct priority order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Inherent safety by design** (highest priority — eliminate or reduce the hazard at the design stage).
2. **Protective measures in the medical device itself or in the manufacturing process** (e.g., alarms, guards, interlocks).
3. **Information for safety** (e.g., warnings, contraindications, training material in IFU).

The manufacturer shall apply these in the order given, and if during risk control, the probability of harm cannot be estimated, the severity shall be used to determine the required controls.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per correct level in correct order.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Pattern: One Question (14 marks), Internal Choice. Answer ALL sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the Risk Management Process as per ISO 14971:2019 with a neat flowchart. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Risk Management Process specified in **Clause 4 of ISO 14971:2019** is an ongoing, iterative, lifecycle-spanning process. The key sub-clauses are:

1. **Risk Management Process (4.1)** — Establish, implement, and maintain throughout the product lifecycle.
2. **Management Responsibility (4.2)** — Top management provides resources and reviews the process.
3. **Competence of Personnel (4.3)** — Personnel must be qualified.
4. **Risk Management Policy (4.4)** — Defines risk acceptability criteria and benefit-risk approach.
5. **Risk Management Plan (4.5)** — Device-specific plan with scope, responsibilities, verification, and post-production activities.
6. **Risk Management File (4.6)** — Maintained for traceability.
7. **Review of Risk Management Activities (4.7)** — Conducted at planned intervals.
8. **Risk Management Report (4.8)** — Final document confirming overall residual risk acceptability.

**Flowchart (refer SECTION 4.1)** — The process begins with the policy → plan → intended use → hazard identification → risk estimation → evaluation → control (3-level hierarchy) → verification → residual risk evaluation → overall residual risk → risk management report → production/post-production feedback loop.

**Mark Split:** Policy/Plan 2 marks, Flowchart 3 marks, Control hierarchy & verification 2 marks.

---

**(b) For a Home-Use Glucose Meter, identify any FIVE hazards, estimate their severity and probability, and recommend risk control measures for each in the prescribed hierarchy order. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Hazard ID | Hazard | Severity | Probability | Pre-Control Risk | Risk Control (Hierarchy Order) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H-01 | Cross-contamination from reused lancet | S3 (Serious: infection) | P3 (Occasional) | Medium | **L1:** Single-use auto-retracting lancet **L2:** Cap-removal sensor **L3:** IFU warning "Do not reuse" |
| H-02 | Incorrect reading due to humidity | S3 | P2 (Remote) | Medium | **L1:** Hermetically sealed test strip vial **L2:** Humidity-expiry alert **L3:** IFU storage instruction |
| H-03 | Battery leakage | S2 (Minor burn) | P1 (Negligible) | Low | **L1:** Use sealed Li-ion cell **L2:** Reverse-polarity protection **L3:** Replace-battery warning |
| H-04 | Display failure | S4 (Critical: incorrect therapy) | P1 | Medium | **L1:** Redundant display verification routine **L2:** Audible low-battery alert **L3:** Periodic self-test prompt |
| H-05 | Software bug in dosing algorithm | S5 (Catastrophic: incorrect insulin dose) | P2 | High | **L1:** Apply IEC 62304 software lifecycle **L2:** Failsafe to manual mode **L3:** Training program for users |

**Mark Split:** 5 hazards 2.5 marks, Risk estimation 1.5 marks, Hierarchy of risk control 3 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the Risk Analysis techniques used in ISO 14971:2019. Compare any two. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

ISO 14971:2019 does not mandate a specific technique but provides examples in Annex A (informative). Common techniques include:

1. **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — Early-stage brainstorming-based analysis used during concept phase.
2. **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Bottom-up, component-level analysis that evaluates potential failure modes and their effects.
3. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — Top-down, deductive analysis starting from an undesired top event and tracing back to basic causes.
4. **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — Originally a chemical-industry technique using guidewords (e.g., NO, MORE, LESS) to identify process deviations.
5. **STPA (Systems-Theoretic Process Analysis)** — Modern technique ideal for software and complex socio-technical systems.

**Comparison: FMEA vs FTA**

| Aspect | FMEA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Output | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | Cut sets, minimal cut sets |
| Strength | Easy to apply per component | Quantifies multi-pathway failures |
| Limitation | Hard to capture system interactions | Can become complex |
| Best for | Component failures | System-level catastrophic events |

**Mark Split:** Listing techniques 2 marks, Detailed explanation 3 marks, Comparison table 2 marks.

---

**(b) Explain the concepts of Benefit-Risk Analysis and Overall Residual Risk with a suitable example. Why are these critical for regulatory submissions? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Overall Residual Risk:** The sum of all individual residual risks after all risk control measures have been implemented and verified. The manufacturer evaluates this against the Risk Management Policy and state of the art.

**Benefit-Risk Analysis:** A documented comparison of the clinical benefit(s) of the intended use against the overall residual risk. Required when individual or overall residual risk is judged non-acceptable against pre-defined criteria.

**Example — Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD):**
- **Residual Risk:** Infection at implant site (~1-3%), inappropriate shock delivery (~5%), lead fracture.
- **Benefit:** Prevention of sudden cardiac death in high-risk patients — survival benefit of 20-30% over 5 years.
- **Conclusion:** The clinical benefit substantially outweighs the residual risk; the device is acceptable.

**Regulatory Importance:**
- **US FDA:** Required in 510(k) and PMA submissions as part of the Risk Management File.
- **EU MDR 2017/745 (Annex I):** Mandates benefit-risk determination based on clinical evaluation.
- **India CDSCO:** Required under MDR 2017 rules.
- **Post-market:** Trigger for benefit-risk re-assessment when new adverse event data emerges.

**Mark Split:** Definitions 2 marks, Example 2 marks, Regulatory linkage 3 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Risk with Hazard:** Students often write "Risk is a hazard" — this will cost you 1-2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hierarchy Order:** You MUST list the three levels of risk control in priority order. Reversing the order (e.g., listing IFU before design) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping Verification:** ISO 14971 requires that every risk control measure be **verified**. Skipping this loses 1 mark in Part B.
> 4. **Missing the Feedback Loop:** The process is not linear. Production and post-production information MUST feed back into risk analysis. Drawing a one-way arrow loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Fabricating Numerical Limits:** Never invent specific probability thresholds (e.g., "<10⁻⁶ is acceptable") unless your Risk Management Policy explicitly defines them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **ISO 14971:2019** is the **third edition**, applicable to **all medical devices** including IVDs and SaMD.
- [ ] **Risk = f(Severity, Probability)** — not a fixed equation; represented via a **risk matrix** or risk chart.
- [ ] **Process has 8 sub-clauses** under Clause 4 — memorize the sequence: Policy → Plan → RMF → Review → Report.
- [ ] **Risk Control Hierarchy (mandatory order):** Design → Protective Measures → Information for Safety.
- [ ] **Goal is NOT zero risk** — it is **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) plus demonstrated **benefit-risk acceptability**.
- [ ] **Risk Management File (RMF)** is a living document — updated throughout the product lifecycle.
- [ ] **Production and Post-Production Information** MUST be reviewed and fed back into risk analysis.
- [ ] **Management Responsibility** is a top-down requirement — top management reviews at planned intervals.
- [ ] **Personnel Competence** must be documented — risk management tasks must be performed by qualified personnel.
- [ ] **Harm includes:** physical injury, damage to health, damage to property, damage to the environment.
- [ ] **Hazardous Situation** is the bridge between Hazard and Harm — never skip this in your analysis.
- [ ] **ISO 14971 harmonizes with:** ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1, IEC 62304 (software), IEC 62366-1 (usability).
- [ ] **Regulatory linkage:** EU MDR Annex I, US FDA Quality System Regulation, India CDSCO MDR 2017.
- [ ] **Severities typically graded 1-5:** Negligible, Minor, Serious, Critical, Catastrophic.
- [ ] **Probabilities typically graded 1-5:** Negligible, Remote, Occasional, Probable, Frequent.
- [ ] **Verification** is mandatory for every risk control measure.
- [ ] **Benefit-Risk Analysis** is mandatory when overall residual risk is non-acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unique Device Identification (UDI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unique Device Identification (UDI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Unique Device Identification (UDI)** is a globally harmonized system used to adequately identify medical devices throughout their lifecycle—from manufacturing and distribution to patient use. The UDI is a unique numeric or alphanumeric code that follows specific standards set by international issuing agencies and is regulated by bodies such as the **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)** under the Final Rule of **24 September 2013**, and the **European Commission** under **EU MDR 2017/745** and **EU IVDR 2017/746**.

A complete UDI is composed of two principal parts:

$$\text{UDI} = \text{UDI Device Identifier (UDI-DI)} \; + \; \text{UDI Production Identifier (UDI-PI)}$$

The **UDI-DI** identifies the labeler (manufacturer) and the specific version or model of a device, while the **UDI-PI** identifies the unit-specific production data such as lot number, serial number, expiration date, or manufacture date.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to understand the structure of UDI, the role of accredited issuing agencies (GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA), the differences between FDA UDI and EU UDI systems, and the connection between UDI and post-market surveillance databases such as GUDID and EUDAMED.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine every medicine strip has a unique barcode at the pharmacy that allows you to trace it back to the manufacturer, batch, and expiry. **UDI does exactly the same thing for medical devices.** Just as an **Aadhaar number** uniquely identifies every Indian resident across all government databases, the UDI is the medical device equivalent—a single, traceable identity that works across hospitals, regulators, manufacturers, and supply chains worldwide.

- **Think of the UDI-DI as the family surname**—it identifies the make and model (e.g., "all 4mm titanium bone screws manufactured by Company X").
- **Think of the UDI-PI as the individual Aadhaar number**—it identifies the specific piece produced in a specific batch with a specific expiry (e.g., "Lot 27, manufactured March 2024, expires March 2029").

> [!NOTE]
> **Why UDI matters to a Biomedical Engineer:** When a defective implant is reported in a hospital in Kochi, UDI enables the manufacturer, CDSCO, and the FDA to instantly trace which batch is affected, issue a recall, and protect other patients—all within hours instead of weeks.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Bodies

| Parameter | Standard / Body | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **FDA UDI Final Rule** | 21 CFR Part 801 | Mandatory US identifier system |
| **EU MDR UDI** | EU Regulation 2017/745, Article 27 | Mandatory European identifier system |
| **Issuing Agency Standards** | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA | Three FDA-accredited agencies |
| **GMDN Code** | ISO 21881 / GMDN Agency | Generic device grouping |
| **GUDID Database** | FDA Public Database | US UDI repository |
| **EUDAMED Database** | EU Commission | European UDI repository |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Standard:** **GS1** is the most widely adopted issuing agency globally, using **GTIN (Global Trade Item Number)** as the primary identifier format, but HIBCC and ICCBBA remain equally valid under FDA and EU regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the UDI System

**Step 1 — The Manufacturer (Labeler) Applies for an Issuing Agency Code**
The labeler (manufacturer) approaches one of the three FDA-accredited Issuing Agencies (**GS1, HIBCC, or ICCBBA**) to obtain a company prefix. This prefix is the foundation upon which every UDI is built.

**Step 2 — Assigning the UDI Device Identifier (UDI-DI)**
The UDI-DI is the **static, mandatory portion** of the UDI. It is assigned at the **device family / version / model** level. It must be globally unique and, once assigned, is never reused for a different device.

**Step 3 — Assigning the UDI Production Identifier (UDI-PI)**
The UDI-PI is **conditional and dynamic**. It changes for every individual unit (or batch) produced. The PI may include:
- **Lot or Batch Number**
- **Serial Number**
- **Expiration Date**
- **Date of Manufacture**
- For Human Cells, Tissues, and Cellular and Tissue-Based Products (HCT/P): a distinct identification code per donation

**Step 4 — Encoding onto the Device Label**
The UDI must appear on the device label in two formats:
- **AIDC (Automatic Identification and Data Capture):** Machine-readable form such as a barcode or RFID.
- **HRI (Human Readable Interpretation):** Plain-text numeric/alphanumeric reading for human operators.

**Step 5 — Submission to the Regulatory Database**
The UDI-DI and associated device information (make, model, GMDN code, manufacturer details) must be submitted to **GUDID** (US) or **EUDAMED** (EU). The UDI-PI is **not** submitted—only production-level data is held internally by the manufacturer.

**Step 6 — Lifecycle Traceability**
At every point of distribution and use, the UDI is scanned and recorded, enabling:
- Adverse event reporting linkage
- Targeted recall management
- Post-market surveillance analytics
- Supply chain integrity verification

### 2.2 The Three FDA-Accredited Issuing Agencies

| Issuing Agency | Identifier Format | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **GS1** | GTIN (Global Trade Item Number) + Application Identifiers | General medical devices, globally dominant |
| **HIBCC** | HIBCC Labeler Identification Code (LIC) + Product/Catalog Number | Healthcare industry, North America |
| **ICCBBA** | ISBT 128 standard | Blood, cell, tissue, and regenerative medicine products |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element | Definition | Type | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UDI-DI (Device Identifier)** | Identifies labeler and specific device model/version | Static | **Yes, mandatory** |
| **UDI-PI (Production Identifier)** | Identifies unit/batch-level data (lot, serial, expiry) | Dynamic | **Conditional** (depends on device class) |
| **Basic UDI-DI (EU only)** | Highest-level grouping key for EUDAMED database | Static | **Yes (EU MDR)** |
| **UDI Carrier** | AIDC + HRI combined on label | Mixed | **Yes, mandatory** |
| **GMDN Code** | Generic Medical Device Nomenclature term | Static | **Yes (required for database entry)** |
| **Lot Number** | Identifies production batch | Variable | Conditional |
| **Serial Number** | Unique per individual unit | Variable | Conditional |
| **Expiration Date** | Date after which device must not be used | Variable | Conditional |
| **Date of Manufacture** | Date unit was produced | Variable | Conditional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The UDI-PI is **conditional**, not optional. Whether it is required depends on the device class and type. For example, an implant requires a **serial number**, while a sterile glove only requires a **lot number**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The UDI system directly supports multiple domains of biomedical and clinical engineering practice:

- **Hospital Asset Management:** RFID-enabled UDI scanners allow hospitals to track thousands of devices in real time.
- **Implant Traceability:** Orthopedic implants (hip, knee, screws) are routinely scanned during surgery to update patient implant cards—essential for any future revision surgery.
- **Adverse Event Investigation:** When a ventilator fails, the UDI instantly traces back to the production batch.
- **Cybersecurity Vulnerability Tracking:** Software-based medical devices (SaMD) require UDI to alert affected hospitals of firmware patches.
- **Regulatory Submission Integration:** UDI-DI links a device to its 510(k), CE Mark, or CDSCO license.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of UDI Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Since UDI is a regulatory and structural concept rather than a calculus-based engineering topic, this section presents the *exhaustive analytical construction logic* of a complete UDI—the regulatory equivalent of a step-by-step derivation.

### 3.1 Construction of a Complete UDI — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Logic

We will construct a complete UDI for a hypothetical medical device: **"Sterile 4mm Titanium Bone Screw, Model TS-4, manufactured by ABC Orthopedics Pvt. Ltd."**

**Step 1 — Determine the Labeler (Company) Prefix**

The labeler approaches GS1 (in our example) and obtains a company prefix. GS1 issues ABC Orthopedics a unique **GS1 Company Prefix**.

$$\text{Company Prefix (CP)} = 8901234$$

Note: The leading **'8'** in GS1 prefixes indicates a healthcare-related prefix in many Asian contexts. In the US, GS1 prefixes typically begin with '0' through '3' depending on GS1 Member Organization.

**Step 2 — Construct the GTIN-14 (Device Identifier)**

GS1 uses the GTIN-14 standard for medical devices. It is a 14-digit numeric code:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GTIN-14} &= \text{Indicator Digit} + \text{Company Prefix} + \text{Item Reference} + \text{Check Digit} \\
&= 1 + 8901234 + 56789 + \text{Check Digit}
\end{aligned}$$

The **Indicator Digit** ('1' here) identifies the packaging level (e.g., '1' = unit package, '3' = carton). The **Item Reference** ('56789') is assigned by the manufacturer to the specific device model.

**Step 3 — Calculate the GS1 Check Digit**

The Check Digit ensures the integrity of the GTIN during scanning. Using the standard Modulo-10 algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GTIN (without check digit)} &= 1890123456789 \\
\text{Digits from right (excluding check position)} &: 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0, 9, 8, 1 \\
\text{Apply weights alternately } 3, 1, 3, 1, \ldots &: \\
9 \times 3 &= 27 \\
8 \times 1 &= 8 \\
7 \times 3 &= 21 \\
6 \times 1 &= 6 \\
5 \times 3 &= 15 \\
4 \times 1 &= 4 \\
3 \times 3 &= 9 \\
2 \times 1 &= 2 \\
1 \times 3 &= 3 \\
0 \times 1 &= 0 \\
9 \times 3 &= 27 \\
8 \times 1 &= 8 \\
1 \times 3 &= 3 \\
\text{Sum} &= 27+8+21+6+15+4+9+2+3+0+27+8+3 = 133 \\
\text{Nearest equal or higher multiple of 10} &= 140 \\
\text{Check Digit} &= 140 - 133 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the complete GTIN-14 is:

$$\text{GTIN-14} = 18901234567897$$

This GTIN-14 is the **UDI-DI** for the "Sterile 4mm Titanium Bone Screw, Model TS-4."

**Step 4 — Assign the UDI Production Identifier (UDI-PI)**

For this implant (a Class II implantable device), the UDI-PI must include the **Lot Number** and **Expiration Date**. Using GS1 Application Identifiers (AI):

- **AI (10)** = Lot Number → e.g., `10ABC0424` → represents Lot `ABC0424`
- **AI (17)** = Expiration Date → e.g., `260331` → represents 2026-03-31

The complete UDI for one unit of this device becomes:

$$\text{UDI} = \underbrace{18901234567897}_{\text{UDI-DI}} + \underbrace{10\text{ABC}0424 \; 17\,260331}_{\text{UDI-PI}}$$

**Step 5 — Encode in Both AIDC and HRI Forms**

- **AIDC form:** Encoded as a GS1-128 barcode or DataMatrix.
- **HRI form:** Printed as readable text: `(01)18901234567897 (10)ABC0424 (17)260331`.

**Step 6 — Submit the UDI-DI to GUDID**

The manufacturer submits the **UDI-DI (18901234567897)** along with associated device metadata (brand name, model, GMDN code, sterile status, etc.) to the **Global Unique Device Identification Database (GUDID)**. The UDI-PI is **not** submitted—only the manufacturer keeps lot-level records.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Note:** A common student error is to confuse **UDI** with **Basic UDI-DI**. The **Basic UDI-DI** is a separate concept used **only in EU MDR** as the highest-level grouping key in EUDAMED. It is **not** the same as the UDI-DI.

### 3.2 Summary Analytical Matrix — FDA UDI vs EU UDI

| Parameter | FDA UDI System | EU UDI System |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Regulation | 21 CFR Part 801 | EU MDR Article 27 / IVDR Article 24 |
| Issuing Agencies | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA | Same three + additional European entities |
| Database | GUDID | EUDAMED |
| Identifier Format | UDI-DI + UDI-PI | UDI-DI + UDI-PI + **Basic UDI-DI** |
| Application Date | Phase-in started 2014 | Full enforcement from May 2021 (MDR) |
| Implantable Devices | Required | Required with patient implant card |
| Class I Devices | Required from Sep 2022 (FDA extended timeline) | Required from MDR date |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UDI Composition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["UDI SYSTEM"] --> nodeB["UDI DEVICE IDENTIFIER UDI DI"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["UDI PRODUCTION IDENTIFIER UDI PI"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["LABELER COMPANY PREFIX"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["DEVICE MODEL OR VERSION"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["CHECK DIGIT"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["LOT OR BATCH NUMBER"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["SERIAL NUMBER CONDITIONAL"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["EXPIRY DATE CONDITIONAL"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["MANUFACTURE DATE CONDITIONAL"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["STATIC PER DEVICE MODEL"]
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL["DYNAMIC PER UNIT PRODUCED"]
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM["SUBMITTED TO GUDID OR EUDAMED"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["RETAINED BY MANUFACTURER ONLY"]
```

### 4.2 FDA UDI System Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["MANUFACTURER LABELER"] -->|"Applies to Issuing Agency"| node2["GS1 OR HIBCC OR ICCBBA"]
    node2 -->|"Assigns Company Prefix"| node1
    node1 -->|"Constructs UDI DI"| node3["UDI DI DEVICE IDENTIFIER"]
    node1 -->|"Constructs UDI PI"| node4["UDI PI PRODUCTION IDENTIFIER"]
    node3 -->|"Combined with"| node4
    node4 -->|"Encoded as"| node5["AIDC BARCODE OR RFID"]
    node4 -->|"Encoded as"| node6["HRI PLAIN TEXT"]
    node3 -->|"Submitted to"| node7["GUDID DATABASE FDA"]
    node7 -->|"Publicly accessible for"| node8["HOSPITALS AND CLINICIANS"]
    node7 -->|"Used by"| node9["REGULATORS FOR SURVEILLANCE"]
    node9 -->|"Enables"| node10["RECALL MANAGEMENT"]
    node9 -->|"Enables"| node11["ADVERSE EVENT TRACKING"]
```

### 4.3 EU UDI System Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["MANUFACTURER"] -->|"Assigns"| nodeB["BASIC UDI DI HIGHEST GROUPING KEY"]
    nodeA -->|"Assigns"| nodeC["UDI DI PER DEVICE MODEL"]
    nodeA -->|"Assigns"| nodeD["UDI PI PER UNIT"]
    nodeB -->|"Submitted to"| nodeE["EUDAMED DATABASE"]
    nodeC -->|"Submitted to"| nodeE
    nodeD -->|"Retained internally"| nodeF["MANUFACTURER RECORDS"]
    nodeE -->|"Supports"| nodeG["POST MARKET SURVEILLANCE"]
    nodeE -->|"Supports"| nodeH["VIGILANCE AND RECALL"]
    nodeE -->|"Supports"| nodeI["CLINICAL INVESTIGATION LINKAGE"]
    nodeC -->|"Appears on"| nodeJ["DEVICE LABEL AIDC PLUS HRI"]
    nodeD -->|"Appears on"| nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|"Scanned at"| nodeK["HOSPITAL OR POINT OF USE"]
```

### 4.4 UDI Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT"] -->|"Assign UDI DI"| node2["REGULATORY APPROVAL FDA OR CE"]
    node2 -->|"Register in GUDID OR EUDAMED"| node3["MANUFACTURING"]
    node3 -->|"Apply UDI PI per unit"| node4["LABELING AND PACKAGING"]
    node4 -->|"Distribution"| node5["HOSPITAL OR DISTRIBUTOR"]
    node5 -->|"Scanning at point of use"| node6["PATIENT IMPLANTATION OR USE"]
    node6 -->|"Adverse event if any"| node7["MDR REPORTING TO FDA OR EU"]
    node7 -->|"UDI trace"| node8["TARGETED RECALL IF NEEDED"]
    node8 -->|"UDI scan verification"| node9["AFFECTED UNITS IDENTIFIED"]
    node9 -->|"Hospital stock segregation"| node10["PATIENT PROTECTION"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Unique Device Identification (UDI). State the two main components of a UDI.*

**Model Answer:**

Unique Device Identification (UDI) is a system mandated by regulatory bodies such as the US FDA and the European Commission to provide a unique, standardized identifier for every medical device and its packaging, enabling traceability throughout its lifecycle.

The two main components of a UDI are:
1. **UDI Device Identifier (UDI-DI):** A mandatory, static code identifying the labeler and the specific version or model of a device.
2. **UDI Production Identifier (UDI-PI):** A conditional, dynamic code identifying production-level data such as lot number, serial number, expiration date, or date of manufacture.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Name the three FDA-accredited Issuing Agencies for UDI. Which one is most widely adopted globally?*

**Model Answer:**

The three FDA-accredited Issuing Agencies for UDI are:
1. **GS1** — uses the GTIN (Global Trade Item Number) standard.
2. **HIBCC (Health Industry Business Communications Council)** — uses the HIBCC Labeler Identification Code (LIC) standard.
3. **ICCBBA (International Council for Commonality in Blood Banking Automation)** — uses the ISBT 128 standard.

**GS1** is the most widely adopted issuing agency globally, particularly for general medical devices, owing to its compatibility with the broader GS1 supply chain standards.

---

### Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Path: UDI Structure Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the structure of UDI with a suitable example. Differentiate between UDI-DI and UDI-PI. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Unique Device Identification (UDI) is a unique numeric or alphanumeric code assigned to a medical device to enable traceability. It consists of two components:

**1. UDI Device Identifier (UDI-DI):**
- Mandatory and static.
- Identifies the labeler (manufacturer) and the specific version/model of the device.
- Submitted to the GUDID (US) or EUDAMED (EU) database.
- Example: GTIN-14 = `18901234567897` (for a 4mm titanium bone screw by ABC Orthopedics).

**2. UDI Production Identifier (UDI-PI):**
- Conditional and dynamic.
- Identifies production-level information: lot number, serial number, expiry date, or manufacture date.
- Not submitted to the database; held internally by the manufacturer.
- Example: `(10)ABC0424 (17)260331` (Lot ABC0424, expires 31 March 2026).

**Difference Table:**

| Parameter | UDI-DI | UDI-PI |
|---|---|---|
| Mandatory? | Yes | Conditional |
| Nature | Static | Dynamic |
| Identifies | Model/version | Unit/batch production data |
| Database submission | Yes (GUDID/EUDAMED) | No |

**Example of Complete UDI:**
$$\text{UDI} = \underbrace{18901234567897}_{\text{UDI-DI}} + \underbrace{(10)\text{ABC0424}\;(17)260331}_{\text{UDI-PI}}$$

**(b)** *Discuss the role of UDI in post-market surveillance and recall management. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

UDI plays a critical role in **post-market surveillance (PMS)** by:

1. **Adverse Event Traceability:** When a malfunction or injury is reported, the UDI-DI instantly identifies the device make/model and the UDI-PI narrows it down to the specific lot or serial number. This converts a generic complaint into an actionable investigation.

2. **Recall Management:** UDI enables **targeted recalls** instead of broad-market withdrawals. Instead of recalling an entire product line, only the affected lot/serial numbers are flagged—saving manufacturer cost and protecting hospital supply chains.

3. **Database Integration:** UDI is linked with **MAUDE** (FDA's adverse event database), **EUDAMED**, and **GUDID**, enabling regulators to run population-level safety analytics on devices.

4. **Real-World Example:** In 2018, a specific lot of implantable defibrillators was found to have premature battery depletion. The UDI allowed hospitals to identify exactly which patients had affected devices, reducing response time from months to weeks.

5. **Implant Cards (EU):** Under EU MDR, implantable devices must come with a patient implant card carrying the UDI, enabling lifelong traceability.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Path: UDI Regulatory Framework

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Compare the FDA UDI system with the EU UDI system under MDR 2017/745. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | FDA UDI System | EU UDI System (MDR 2017/745) |
|---|---|---|
| Regulation | 21 CFR Part 801 | EU MDR Article 27 / IVDR Article 24 |
| Effective Date | Phase-in began 2014 | Full enforcement from May 2021 |
| Database | GUDID | EUDAMED |
| Issuing Agencies | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA | Same three (plus European entities) |
| Basic UDI-DI | Not applicable | **Mandatory** — highest-level grouping key in EUDAMED |
| Class I Devices | Required from September 2022 | Required from MDR date |
| Implantable Devices | Required | Required with **Patient Implant Card** |
| UDI Carrier | AIDC + HRI | AIDC + HRI (mandatory on all packaging levels) |

**Key Difference:** The **Basic UDI-DI** is unique to the EU system and acts as the master grouping identifier under which multiple UDI-DIs (variants/packaging) are registered.

**(b)** *Explain the GUDID database. What information is submitted and what is not? (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Global Unique Device Identification Database (GUDID)** is the FDA-administered public database containing key device identification information submitted by manufacturers.

**Information Submitted (UDI-DI-level only):**
- Device Identifier (UDI-DI)
- Manufacturer/labeler information
- Brand name, model number, version
- GMDN (Global Medical Device Nomenclature) code
- Device classification (Class I, II, or III)
- Sterility status, single-use/reusable flag
- MRI safety status (if applicable)
- FDA premarket submission number (e.g., 510(k), PMA)
- Contact information

**Information NOT Submitted (UDI-PI-level, retained internally):**
- Lot numbers
- Serial numbers
- Expiration dates
- Date of manufacture
- Production quantities
- Patient-level distribution data

**Public Access:** GUDID data is publicly accessible via the **AccessGUDID** portal, allowing hospitals, clinicians, and patients to look up device information by scanning or entering the UDI-DI.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark Loss Warning:**
> - Students often wrongly state that the UDI-PI is also submitted to GUDID. **This is incorrect** — only the UDI-DI is submitted. Loss of **2 marks** is common for this mistake.
> - Many students confuse UDI with Basic UDI-DI. Basic UDI-DI is **EU-specific** and is not used in the US system. Loss of **1 mark** per confusion.
> - Students sometimes forget that UDI must be on **all packaging levels** (unit, carton, shipper), not just the device itself. Loss of **1 mark**.
> - Avoid mentioning specific names of issuing agencies incorrectly — ICCBBA is for blood/cell products, not general devices. Loss of **1 mark** if confused with HIBCC.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UDI = UDI-DI + UDI-PI** — the two-part structure is the single most tested concept.
- **UDI-DI is mandatory, static, and database-submitted.**
- **UDI-PI is conditional, dynamic, and manufacturer-retained.**
- **Three FDA-accredited Issuing Agencies: GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA** — GS1 is the most widely adopted.
- **GMDN code** is required for database submission but is **not** part of the UDI itself.
- **AIDC + HRI** are both mandatory on the device label.
- **GUDID (US)** and **EUDAMED (EU)** are the two key databases.
- **Basic UDI-DI is EU-MDR specific** and acts as the master grouping key.
- **Implantable devices require patient implant cards (EU) and serial numbers (US).**
- **Class III → Class II → Class I** — the FDA phase-in order.
- **UDI enables targeted recalls**, adverse event tracking, and improved patient safety.
- **The check digit** in GTIN is calculated using the **Modulo-10 algorithm** — examiners love testing this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labelling Requirements and Post Market Surveillance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Labelling Requirements and Post Market Surveillance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Medical Device Labelling — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Labelling (per ISO 13485:2016 & EU MDR Article 2(67))** is the written, printed, or graphic matter affixed to a medical device, its packaging, or accompanying documents, including the Instructions for Use (IFU), that provides information about the device's identity, intended purpose, manufacturer, safe use, and regulatory compliance.

**In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology**, labelling is *not* just the sticker on a box* — it is the **entire information package** a user (clinician, patient, or technician) interacts with before, during, and after using a medical device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Hierarchy of Labelling (highest to lowest):**
> 1. Statute / Regulation (e.g., EU MDR 2017/745, US FDA 21 CFR Part 801)
> 2. Harmonised Standards (ISO 15223-1, ISO 20417)
> 3. Common Specifications (CS) / Guidance documents
> 4. Manufacturer's own risk-management decisions

---

## 1.2 Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Market Surveillance (per EU MDR Article 2(60) and ISO 13485:2016 Clause 8.2.1)** is a systematic, proactive, and continuous process conducted by the manufacturer to collect, review, and act upon experience gained from medical devices placed on the market, with the objective of identifying and implementing any necessary corrective actions, and ensuring the continuing safety and performance of the device throughout its lifecycle.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🎯 The "Car Dashboard" Analogy for Labelling
Imagine you buy a new car. The information ecosystem you interact with is *labelling*:
- The **sticker on the windshield** (outer label) = Device name, model, manufacturer
- The **dashboard warning lights** (symbols) = ISO 15223-1 symbols
- The **owner's manual** (IFU) = Instructions for Use
- The **service booklet** (maintenance schedule) = Maintenance/service instructions
- The **emission certification** (compliance) = CE Mark / 510(k) clearance

Without these, even a perfect car is **unsafe and illegal to drive**. The same applies to medical devices — a brilliant device with poor labelling is a regulatory non-conformity and a patient-safety hazard.

### 🎯 The "Black Box Flight Recorder" Analogy for PMS
Think of PMS as the **"black box" of a medical device**:
- It **continuously records** real-world performance after launch
- It **triggers alerts** when anomalies occur (vigilance, complaints, CAPA)
- It feeds data back into **design improvements** (next-generation device)
- It is reviewed by **regulatory authorities** periodically (PSUR for Class IIa+, PMS reports for Class I)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Stat (bolded metric):** Under EU MDR, approximately **~80% of compliance work shifts to post-market activities** compared to the old MDD framework — this makes PMS one of the highest-weightage topics in KTU Module 3.

---

## 1.4 Three Pillars of the Topic

| Pillar | Regulates | Primary Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Labelling** | What information reaches the user | ISO 20417:2021, ISO 15223-1:2021, 21 CFR 801 |
| **PMS** | How performance is monitored in market | EU MDR Chapter VII, ISO 13485 §8.2.1 |
| **Vigilance** | How serious incidents are reported | EU MDR Article 87–92, MEDDEV 2.12-1, 21 CFR 803 (MDR) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Medical Device Labelling

Labelling is a **layered information architecture**, not a single label. The KTU syllabus expects you to identify the following components:

### Layer 1 — Outer Packaging Label
- Manufacturer name & address
- Authorised representative (for non-EU manufacturers)
- Device trade name, model, catalogue number
- Lot number / Serial number / UDI carrier
- Sterility status (sterile / non-sterile / single-use)
- Expiry date (if applicable)
- Storage & transport conditions
- CE Mark (EU) or 510(k) number (US)

### Layer 2 — Device Itself (On-Device Marking)
- UDI carrier (UDI-DI + UDI-PI in HRI or AIDC form)
- Symbols per ISO 15223-1
- Manufacturing date (if no expiry)
- Warnings / cautions

### Layer 3 — Instructions for Use (IFU) / Operator Manual
- Intended purpose
- Indications, contraindications
- Patient population
- Warnings, precautions
- Clinical benefits, residual risks
- Operating instructions
- Performance characteristics
- Maintenance, calibration, disposal
- Reporting procedure for serious incidents

### Layer 4 — Implant Card & Patient Information (Class III & Implantables)
- Patient identifier
- Device identifier
- Implantation date, surgeon info
- Website reference for further info (per EU MDR Article 18)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** is **mandatory under EU MDR (Article 27)** and **US FDA (21 CFR 830)**. It consists of:
> - **UDI-DI** (Device Identifier) — static, identifies the device model
> - **UDI-PI** (Production Identifier) — dynamic, identifies the unit (lot, serial, expiry)

---

## 2.2 ISO 15223-1:2021 — Symbol Library (High-Yield)

The KTU examiner frequently tests **symbol recognition**. Here is the consolidated reference:

| Symbol | Meaning | Section |
|---|---|---|
| 🛡️ | Manufacturer | 5.1.1 |
| 📅 | Date of manufacture | 5.1.3 |
| ⏰ | Use-by date | 5.1.4 |
| 🔢 | Catalogue number | 5.1.6 |
| 🆔 | Batch code / Lot number | 5.1.7 |
| 🔖 | Serial number | 5.1.8 |
| 🦠 | Sterile (generic) | 5.2.x series |
| ☠️ | Do not re-use (single-use) | 5.4.2 |
| ⚠️ | Caution | 5.4.4 |
| 🌡️ | Temperature limits | 5.3.7 |
| 📖 | Consult IFU | 5.4.3 |
| 🛡️✚ | Medical Device (under MDR) | 5.7.10 |
| 🏥 | Authorised representative in EU | 5.1.2 |
| 🌍 | Importer | 5.1.8 |
| 🗑️ | WEEE — separate disposal | 5.7.10 |

> [!NOTE]
