\text{MTBF} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ hours}}{25 \text{ failures}} = 2{,}000 \text{ hours/engine}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** MTBF tells the manufacturer, "On average, a properly maintained medical device is expected to run for **X hours** before the next inherent failure occurs." It is **not** a guarantee that the device will not fail earlier — it is a *statistical expectation* derived from reliability theory.

### Why MTBF Matters in Medical Device Engineering

| Domain | Why MTBF is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Patient Safety** | Higher MTBF → fewer unexpected failures during clinical use → reduced risk of harm |
| **Risk Management (ISO 14971)** | MTBF feeds into probability-of-failure estimates used in risk matrices |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | Field MTBF is compared with predicted MTBF to detect design drift |
| **Service Planning** | Hospitals use MTBF to schedule preventive maintenance and spare-part inventory |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | FDA, EU MDR, and CDSCO submissions often request reliability predictions |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Symbol Conventions Used in KTU Textbooks:**
> - **λ (lambda)** = constant failure rate (failures/hour)
> - **R(t)** = reliability function at time *t*
> - **f(t), F(t)** = probability density & cumulative distribution of failure
> - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair
> - **MTTF** = Mean Time To Failure
> - **A** = inherent (intrinsic) availability

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reliability Foundation — Constant Failure Rate Model

Medical device reliability is most commonly modelled using the **exponential distribution**, which assumes a *constant hazard rate* λ during the useful life phase of the **bathtub curve** (excluding infant mortality and wear-out regions). Under this assumption:

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0
$$

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The **expected value** of an exponentially distributed random variable is:

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

This expected time-to-failure, for a constant-failure-rate system, is precisely the **MTBF**.

## 2.2 Logical Derivation Chain — From Raw Data to MTBF

Step 1 — *Define the operating population.* Track **N** identical, repairable devices over a **total observed time** $T_{total}$ (sum of operating hours across the population).

Step 2 — *Count inherent failures.* Let **r** = number of observed inherent failures during $T_{total}$. Exclude failures caused by external abuse, mishandling, or out-of-spec operation.

Step 3 — *Compute the empirical failure rate.*

$$
\lambda = \frac{r}{T_{total}} \quad \text{[failures per hour]}
$$

Step 4 — *Invert to obtain MTBF.*

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{total}}{r} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

Step 5 — *Apply to system architecture.* For **series** systems, failure rates add; MTBFs combine via reciprocal addition. For **parallel/redundant** systems, the system MTBF is computed through the combined reliability function $R_s(t) = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i(t))$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTBF vs MTTR vs MTTF vs Availability — The Repairable System Quartet**
>
> For a **repairable** system, one full operational cycle consists of:
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = MTTF + MTTR
> - **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) = average up-time before failure
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) = average down-time for restoration
> - **Inherent Availability** $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Standard Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{r}$ | Empirical MTBF from observed data | IEC 60300-3-1 |
| 2 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Theoretical MTBF (constant λ) | Exponential model |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ | Reliability at time *t* | ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 |
| 4 | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Series-system failure rate | Reliability block diagrams |
| 5 | $R_s(t) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Series-system reliability | Series RBD |
| 6 | $R_s(t) = 1 - \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i(t))$ | Parallel (redundant) reliability | Parallel RBD |
| 7 | $\text{MTBF}_{series} = \left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i}\right)^{-1}$ | Series-system MTBF | Reciprocal addition |
| 8 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent availability | Maintainability theory |
| 9 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Repairable cycle identity | Definition |
| 10 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Non-repairable mean life | Exponential model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** MTBF is expressed in **hours** for medical electrical equipment. Failure rate **λ** is in **failures per hour** (or **FIT** = failures per $10^9$ device-hours in semiconductor contexts).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a regulated environment, MTBF predictions are used to:
1. **Justify design choices** during design input reviews (ISO 13485 §7.3).
2. **Quantify probability of harm** in ISO 14971 risk analyses — a failure with high severity but very low probability (high MTBF) may be deemed acceptable.
3. **Establish preventive-maintenance schedules** in the device's Instructions for Use (IFU).
4. **Support post-market surveillance** under EU MDR Annex III and FDA 21 CFR 803.
5. **Allocate reliability budgets** among subsystems (e.g., power supply, processor, sensor) during architectural design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Expected Value of the Exponential Distribution

> **Goal:** Prove that the expected time-to-failure under a constant hazard rate $\lambda$ equals $1/\lambda$.

**Step 1 — State the exponential density function.**
For a non-negative random variable *T* representing time-to-failure, the probability density function is:

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0, \; \lambda > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of expected value.**

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Use integration by parts.**
Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$, so that $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$
E[T] = \left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term.**
As $t \to \infty$, the product $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates linear growth). At $t = 0$, the term is $0$. Hence:

$$
\left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the derivation.**

$$
\boxed{E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda} \;\equiv\; \text{MTBF}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Series System MTBF

> **Problem:** A patient monitor consists of three subsystems with the following constant failure rates:
> - Power supply: $\lambda_1 = 2000$ FIT
> - Main processor board: $\lambda_2 = 5000$ FIT
> - Display module: $\lambda_3 = 3000$ FIT
>
> Compute the **system MTBF** assuming a series reliability architecture. Take 1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour.

**Step 1 — Convert FIT to failures/hour.**

$$
\lambda_1 = 2000 \times 10^{-9} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = 5000 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_3 = 3000 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the series-system failure-rate summation rule.**

$$
\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = (2 + 5 + 3) \times 10^{-6} = 10 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert to obtain system MTBF.**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 100{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years (interpretation).**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \frac{100{,}000}{24 \times 365} \approx 11.4 \text{ years}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Interpretation:** This patient monitor is statistically expected to operate for **~11.4 years** between inherent failures. However, this does *not* mean the device will never fail before then — it is a central-tendency value, and the probability of failure within the first year is $F(8760) = 1 - e^{-8760/100000} \approx 8.4\%$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Reliability at a Given Time

> **Problem:** Using the same monitor, compute the reliability at $t = 10{,}000$ hours (~14 months of continuous operation) and the probability that it fails within that period.

**Step 1 — Compute reliability.**

$$
R(10{,}000) = e^{-10{,}000 / 100{,}000} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048
$$

**Step 2 — Compute unreliability (failure probability).**

$$
F(10{,}000) = 1 - R(10{,}000) = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952 \approx 9.52\%
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Empirical MTBF | Total time, # failures | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = T_{total} / r$ |
| Theoretical MTBF | Failure rate λ | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ |
| Reliability at *t* | λ, *t* | R(t) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| Series MTBF | Individual λ's | $\text{MTBF}_{sys}$ | Reciprocal of summed λ's |
| Failure probability | MTBF, *t* | F(t) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ |
| Inherent availability | MTBF, MTTR | $A_i$ | $A_i = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ |
| FIT conversion | λ in FIT | λ in /h | $1 \text{ FIT} = 10^{-9}$ /h |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MTBF Computation in a Medical Device Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Input Phase] --> B[Allocate Reliability Budget]
    B --> C[Component Selection and Derating]
    C --> D[Subsystem MTBF Estimation]
    D --> E[Series and Parallel RBD Computation]
    E --> F[System Level MTBF Prediction]
    F --> G[Design Verification Testing]
    G --> H{Field MTBF vs Predicted?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Release to Market ISO 13485]
    H -- No --> J[Root Cause Analysis and Design Update]
    J --> D
    I --> K[Post Market Surveillance]
    K --> L[Trend Analysis per ISO 14971]
    L --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:** The lifecycle begins with a *reliability budget* at design input. Subsystem-level MTBF estimates feed into reliability block diagrams (RBDs), which yield a system-level MTBF prediction. Design verification testing validates the prediction. If field data diverges from the prediction, root-cause analysis triggers a design loop. ISO 13485 and ISO 14971 govern the gate criteria.

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — Series vs Parallel Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input] --> B1[Subsystem 1]
    B1 --> C1[Subsystem 2]
    C1 --> D1[Subsystem 3]
    D1 --> E1[Output]
    A2[Input] --> B2[Subsystem A]
    A2 --> C2[Subsystem B]
    A2 --> D2[Subsystem C]
    B2 --> E2[Output]
    C2 --> E2
    D2 --> E2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The upper path shows a **series RBD** — all three subsystems must function for the system to work. The lower path shows a **parallel (redundant) RBD** — only one of the three parallel branches needs to survive. Parallel architectures dramatically increase MTBF and are common in life-supporting medical devices (e.g., redundant ventilator blowers).

## 4.3 The Bathtub Curve — Where MTBF Applies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Region] -->|Burn In Screening| B[Useful Life Region]
    B -->|Constant Lambda Region| C[Wear Out Region]
    C -->|Preventive Replacement| D[End of Life]
    B --> E[MTBF = 1 divided by lambda is valid here]
```

**Reading the diagram:** MTBF is **only rigorously valid** in the *useful-life* region of the bathtub curve, where the failure rate λ is approximately constant. In infant-mortality and wear-out regions, the Weibull or other non-exponential models must be used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define MTBF. How is it different from MTTF?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the expected average time interval between two consecutive inherent failures of a *repairable* system, mathematically given by $\text{MTBF} = T_{total}/r = 1/\lambda$ for constant failure rate.
>
> **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is the expected time to failure of a *non-repairable* component.
>
> **Key distinction:** MTBF applies to repairable systems and includes the *up-time only*; for repairable systems, $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$. MTTF applies to single-use or non-repairable items where no restoration occurs.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**State the relationship between reliability function and MTBF for a constant failure-rate system.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> For a constant failure-rate λ, the reliability function is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Since $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$, we may substitute to obtain:
> $$ R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}} $$
> This relationship is fundamental in ISO 14971 risk estimation, where the probability of a hazardous event is derived from the failure rate, and the failure rate is the reciprocal of MTBF.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for MTBF of a repairable system under the assumption of constant failure rate. Show that the expected time-to-failure equals $1/\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An infusion pump has the following subsystem failure rates: motor $\lambda_m = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/h, control PCB $\lambda_p = 8 \times 10^{-6}$/h, sensor module $\lambda_s = 4 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Compute (i) the system MTBF assuming series architecture, and (ii) the reliability at 5,000 operating hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of MTBF = 1/λ (7 marks)**

The time-to-failure of a constant-hazard-rate system follows the exponential distribution with density $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. The expected value (mean) is computed by definition:

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

Apply integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$, yielding $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$
E[T] = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

The boundary term vanishes at both limits. The remaining integral evaluates to $1/\lambda$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda} \equiv \text{MTBF}}
$$

For a repairable system, each operating cycle has mean up-time MTTF = $1/\lambda$, and including the repair time MTTR gives MTBF = MTTF + MTTR.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**(i) Series-system MTBF:**

$$
\lambda_{sys} = 3 \times 10^{-6} + 8 \times 10^{-6} + 4 \times 10^{-6} = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^{-6}} = 66{,}666.67 \text{ hours} \approx 66{,}667 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at 5,000 hours:**

$$
R(5000) = e^{-5000 / 66{,}667} = e^{-0.075} \approx 0.9277
$$

> **Result:** The pump has ~92.77% probability of operating without failure through the first 5,000 hours (~7 months of continuous duty).

---

### Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the concept of a **reliability block diagram (RBD)** for series and parallel configurations in a medical device. Derive the MTBF expression for the series case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A defibrillator uses two parallel-connected high-voltage capacitor banks, each with $\lambda_c = 1 \times 10^{-5}$/h. Compute (i) the reliability of the parallel combination at 10,000 hours, and (ii) the effective MTBF of the combination (approximated by $1/\lambda_{eff}$ where $\lambda_{eff}$ is the effective failure rate derived from the reliability expression). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) RBD Concept and Series MTBF Derivation (7 marks)**

A **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)** is a graphical representation of how component reliabilities combine to yield system reliability. A block is "working" if the component survives; a path from input to output represents functional continuity.

**Series RBD:** All blocks must work. $R_s(t) = \prod R_i(t) = \prod e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-(\sum \lambda_i)t}$. Thus $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ and $\text{MTBF}_{series} = 1/\lambda_s = \left(\sum 1/\text{MTBF}_i\right)^{-1}$.

**Parallel RBD:** At least one block must work. $R_s(t) = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i(t))$.

For series architecture with constant failure rates:

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF}_{series} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i}\right)^{-1}}
$$

**(b) Parallel Capacitor Bank Calculation (7 marks)**

For two identical parallel components, each with reliability $R_c(t) = e^{-\lambda_c t}$:

**(i) Parallel reliability at 10,000 hours:**

$$
R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - R_c(t))^2 = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda_c t})^2
$$

Substitute $t = 10{,}000$ h and $\lambda_c = 1 \times 10^{-5}$/h:

$$
R_c(10{,}000) = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048
$$

$$
R_{parallel}(10{,}000) = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^2 = 1 - (0.0952)^2 = 1 - 0.00906 = 0.99094
$$

> **Result:** ~99.09% reliability at 10,000 hours — substantially higher than the single-component 90.48%.

**(ii) Effective MTBF:**
The effective failure rate is obtained from the small-$t$ expansion. For $N$ identical parallel components with low individual failure rates, $\lambda_{eff} \approx N \lambda_c^N \cdot t^{N-1}/(N-1)!$ is time-dependent. For $N=2$, the average time between system failures is found by integrating:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{parallel} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t)\, dt
$$

For two parallel identical components:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{parallel} = \frac{1}{\lambda_c} + \frac{1}{2\lambda_c} = \frac{3}{2\lambda_c} = \frac{3}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 150{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

(General result: $\text{MTBF}_{N\,parallel} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} 1/(k\lambda_c)$.)

> **Result:** The parallel configuration raises MTBF from 100,000 h to **150,000 h** — a 50% improvement demonstrating the value of redundancy in life-supporting equipment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Conflating FIT (failures per $10^9$ h) with failures per hour leads to 1000× errors.
> 2. **Series vs Parallel confusion:** In series, MTBFs add as *reciprocals* — NOT directly. In parallel, MTBF *increases* dramatically; never use the series formula for parallel systems.
> 3. **MTBF ≠ Lifetime:** Students often write "the device will last MTBF hours." MTBF is a *statistical mean*; actual lifetime varies. Use $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ for explicit reliability.
> 4. **Exponential-model assumption:** MTBF = $1/\lambda$ is valid only when the failure rate is constant (useful-life phase of the bathtub curve). Stating this assumption earns full credit.
> 5. **Repairable vs Non-repairable:** Forgetting to add MTTR when asked for full-cycle MTBF costs marks in risk-management contexts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MTBF = 1/λ** for constant failure rate (exponential distribution).
- MTBF applies to **repairable** medical electrical systems; MTTF to non-repairable components.
- **Series system:** failure rates add, MTBFs add as reciprocals.
- **Parallel system:** $R_s = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$; MTBF increases significantly with redundancy.
- **Reliability function:** $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$.
- **Availability:** $A_i = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- **Standards linkage:** IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60300-3-1.
- **Unit conversion:** 1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour.
- MTBF is a **statistical mean**, not a guaranteed lifetime — always quote reliability curves in regulatory submissions.
- The exponential model is valid **only** in the useful-life phase of the bathtub curve.
- Empirical MTBF is computed from field data: $\text{MTBF} = T_{total}/r$, where $T_{total}$ is total observed operating time and *r* is the number of inherent failures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FMEA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FMEA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a structured, systematic, and proactive engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes of a product, process, or service. According to **IEC 60812:2015** — *“Analysis techniques for system reliability — Procedure for failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA and FMECA)”* — FMEA is defined as:

> *“A systematic procedure for the analysis of a system to identify the potential failure modes of the system, their causes, and the effects of the failures on system performance, safety, and the mission.”*

In the context of **medical devices**, FMEA is mandated as a risk analysis tool under **ISO 14971:2019 — Application of risk management to medical devices**, and it forms the analytical backbone of design control requirements in **21 CFR Part 820.30 (Design Control)** of the US FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR) and **EU MDR 2017/745, Annex I, Section 3**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FMEA is not merely a quality tool — it is a **regulatory compliance requirement**. Under ISO 14971:2019 Clause 5.4, manufacturers are expected to implement a risk analysis process, and FMEA (or its variant FMECA) is the most cited methodology in regulatory submissions (510(k), CE Technical Files, and Design Dossiers).

---

## 1.2 Types of FMEA (Critical Classification)

| Type | Full Form | Application in Medical Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **DFMEA** | Design FMEA | Evaluates failure modes introduced by **product design** (e.g., a stent fracture, syringe plunger leak) |
| **PFMEA** | Process FMEA | Evaluates failure modes in **manufacturing/assembly** (e.g., welding defect, sterilization failure) |
| **SFMEA** | Software FMEA | Evaluates failure modes in **embedded software/firmware** (per IEC 62304) |
| **uFMEA** | Use FMEA | Evaluates **user errors** (per IEC 62366-1:2015 usability engineering) |
| **FMECA** | Failure Mode, Effects & Criticality Analysis | FMEA + criticality ranking with quantitative severity |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Pre-Flight Check"**
>
> Imagine a pilot preparing for takeoff. Before every flight, she does NOT simply start the engines and hope for the best. Instead, she runs a **structured checklist**:
> 1. *What could go wrong?* (Fuel leak? Engine failure? Bird strike?)
> 2. *How bad would it be if it happened?* (Catastrophic / Hazardous / Minor?)
> 3. *How likely is it?* (Often? Rare? Almost impossible?)
> 4. *Would I detect it before disaster strikes?* (Yes, by warning lights / No, silent failure?)
> 5. *What should I do to prevent or catch it?* (Maintenance schedule, redundant sensors, pilot training)
>
> **FMEA is exactly this checklist — applied mathematically to a medical device.**
>
> - "What could go wrong?" → **Failure Mode**
> - "How bad?" → **Severity (S)**
> - "How likely?" → **Occurrence (O)**
> - "Would I catch it?" → **Detection (D)**
> - "What to do?" → **Recommended Actions**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** *"FMEA = SOD"* — **S**everity × **O**ccurrence × **D**etection. Think of it as a doctor asking: **"How Sick (S), How Often (O), and How Hidden (D) is the failure?"**

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Origin

- **1960s:** Developed by **NASA** for the Apollo space program.
- **1974:** US Department of Defense formalized it as **MIL-STD-1629 (Military Standard)**.
- **1980s:** Adopted by the automotive industry (Ford Motor Company) for quality control.
- **2019:** Released the harmonized **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook** — adopted globally across automotive and increasingly in medical devices.
- **Current Relevance:** Cited by FDA, EU Notified Bodies, and CDSCO (India) in risk management files.

---

## 1.5 Key Performance Indicator — RPN

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is the single most important output metric of a traditional FMEA. The threshold for **medical device action is typically RPN ≥ 100**, though severity alone can override this.

$$\boxed{\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D}$$

Where each parameter is scored on a **1–10 scale**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Step FMEA Methodology (KTU Board-Standard Answer Structure)

The **IEC 60812:2015** procedure follows seven logically sequenced steps:

### Step 1 — Define the Scope and Boundaries
- Identify the **system, subsystem, or component** under analysis.
- Establish **assumptions, constraints, and resolution level**.
- For a medical device, define: device class, intended use, patient population, environment of use.

### Step 2 — Build the Block Diagram / Process Flow
- Create a visual representation of system inter-relationships.
- In DFMEA: functional block diagram. In PFMEA: process flow chart (PFD/PFMEA).

### Step 3 — Identify Potential Failure Modes
- A **failure mode** is the *manner in which a component or process could potentially fail to meet its design intent*.
- Examples: *cracks, leaks, short circuits, software deadlock, incorrect output, overheating*.

### Step 4 — Determine Effects and Severity (S)
- **Effect** = the consequence on the next higher level, system, or patient.
- **Severity** = how serious the effect is.

### Step 5 — Determine Causes and Occurrence (O)
- **Cause** = the mechanism that initiates the failure.
- **Occurrence** = how frequently the cause happens.

### Step 6 — Determine Detection and Current Controls
- **Detection** = the *probability that the existing control will detect the failure mode before it reaches the patient*.
- **Critical note:** Detection has *inverse* meaning — a **HIGH score = HARD to detect** (poor detection control).

### Step 7 — Calculate RPN, Evaluate, and Take Action
- Compute RPN. Take **action for high RPN items**. Re-evaluate after action.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Scale | Meaning | Typical Medical Device Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Severity | **S** | **1 to 10** | How bad is the harm? | 10 = Death; 9 = Permanent injury; 7 = Temporary injury requiring intervention; 4 = Minor inconvenience; 1 = No effect |
| Occurrence | **O** | **1 to 10** | How frequent? | 10 = Very high (≥1 in 2); 7 = High (1 in 100); 4 = Moderate (1 in 10,000); 1 = Remote (failure eliminated) |
| Detection | **D** | **1 to 10** | How hard to detect? | 10 = Absolute uncertainty (no control); 7 = Low detection capability; 4 = Moderate; 1 = Almost certain (100% automated inspection) |
| Risk Priority Number | **RPN** | **1 to 1000** | Composite risk | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ |
| Action Priority (AIAG-VDA 2019) | **AP** | H / M / L | Newer replacement metric | Replaces RPN for prioritization |
| Criticality (FMECA) | **C** | Continuous | Quantitative form | $C = S \times O$ (ignores D) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall — Detection Inversion:** Many students mistakenly treat Detection as a "higher-is-better" parameter. **It is the opposite.** A *high D score means the failure is hard to detect*, which is undesirable. If your company has zero inspection, D = 10. If you have 100% automated inline optical inspection catching the defect, D = 1.

---

## 2.3 RPN Interpretation Matrix (Examiner-Standard)

| RPN Range | Classification | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| **1 – 49** | **Low Risk** | Acceptable; document and monitor |
| **50 – 99** | **Moderate Risk** | Review; consider risk reduction |
| **100 – 199** | **High Risk** | **Mandatory corrective action** |
| **200 – 1000** | **Critical Risk** | **Stop production** until mitigated; senior management escalation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Severity Override Rule (per ISO 14971:2019):** Even if RPN is low, if **Severity ≥ 9**, the risk is unacceptable and **must be reduced** before market release. RPN alone is never the sole criterion.

---

## 2.4 AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP) — The Modern Replacement for RPN

The 2019 AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook introduced **Action Priority (AP)** to overcome RPN's mathematical limitations (e.g., two completely different risk profiles producing identical RPNs). AP uses a *lookup table* to assign **H (High), M (Medium), or L (Low)** based on the triplet (S, O, D).

> **Real-world utility:** Major medical device OEMs (Medtronic, Stryker, Philips) have migrated from RPN to AP since 2020. For KTU exam purposes, **know both methods** — RPN is still asked frequently, but AP is the "modern" answer.

---

## 2.5 FMEA in the ISO 14971 Risk Management Workflow

FMEA sits at the **risk analysis** stage (Clause 5.4) of the ISO 14971 workflow:

```
Risk Management Process (ISO 14971:2019)
│
├── 5.1 Risk Management Process
├── 5.2 Responsibility / Management Review
├── 5.3 Risk Management Plan
├── 5.4 RISK ANALYSIS  ← FMEA conducted here
├── 5.5 Risk Evaluation
├── 5.6 Risk Control
├── 5.7 Residual Risk Evaluation
├── 5.8 Risk Management Review
└── 5.9 Production & Post-Production
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 14-mark question asks *"Explain the role of FMEA in the ISO 14971 risk management process."* You must position FMEA as a **component-level analytical tool within the broader ISO 14971 risk management framework**, not as a standalone activity.

---

## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations of FMEA

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Proactive — identifies risks before occurrence | Time-intensive for complex devices |
| Quantitative — provides numerical priority | RPN arithmetic is misleading (8×5×2 = 80 = 10×4×2) |
| Cross-functional team engagement | Requires expertise — "garbage in, garbage out" |
| Regulatory accepted (FDA, EU MDR, ISO) | May miss system-level interactions (use Fault Tree Analysis — FTA) |
| Drives continuous improvement | Subjective scales — inter-rater variability |

> [!TIP]
> **Complementary tool — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):** FMEA is **bottom-up** (inductive). FTA is **top-down** (deductive). The FDA and EU regulators expect manufacturers to **combine both** for high-risk devices (Class III implants, ventilators, infusion pumps).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: FMEA for an Insulin Pen (Class IIb Medical Device)

> **Device:** Reusable insulin pen for diabetes management
> **Subsystem under analysis:** Cartridge holder assembly
> **Regulatory context:** ISO 14971:2019 + IEC 62304 (embedded dose counter)

We construct the FMEA worksheet using industry-standard columns:

| # | Component | Failure Mode | Effect | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D | RPN | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cartridge holder | **Cracks during injection** | Insulin leakage → underdose → hyperglycemia → hospitalization | **9** | Material fatigue at thread (Polycarbonate) | **4** | Visual inspection only | **7** | **252** | Switch to medical-grade PEEK; add 100% leak test |
| 2 | Dose counter | **Freezes at "0" units** | Patient cannot confirm dose → wrong dose delivered | **8** | Software deadlock in firmware | **3** | Watchdog timer (limited) | **6** | **144** | Implement IEC 62304 Class B redundancy; add audible click feedback |
| 3 | Needle attachment | **Needle detaches mid-injection** | Needle stick injury / underdose | **10** | Luer slip connection wear | **2** | Mechanical interlock | **5** | **100** | Add visual color-change indicator; redesign with Luer-Lok |
| 4 | Battery contact | **Intermittent power loss** | Dose counter resets → wrong setting memory | **7** | Corrosion of contacts | **3** | Hermetic seal | **4** | **84** | Gold-plated contacts; conformal coating |
| 5 | Plunger O-ring | **Sticking plunger** | Patient applies excessive force → bent needle | **6** | Silicone swelling (sterilization) | **4** | 100% functional test | **3** | **72** | Change to EPDM; cap sterilization dose |

---

## 3.2 Detailed RPN Calculation Walkthrough

Let us compute the RPN for **Failure #1: Cartridge holder cracks** step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{crack}} &= 9 \quad \text{(Permanent or serious injury — underdose leads to DKA hospitalization)} \\
O_{\text{crack}} &= 4 \quad \text{(Moderate — based on field complaint rate of 1 in 5,000)} \\
D_{\text{crack}} &= 7 \quad \text{(Low detection — visual inspection misses sub-surface cracks)} \\
\\
\text{RPN}_{\text{crack}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 9 \times 4 \times 7 \\
&= 36 \times 7 \\
&= 252
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marker:** Show all arithmetic explicitly. Do NOT write `RPN = 9×4×7 = 252` in a single line. Break down multiplication to demonstrate the intermediate product — this is a valuation key pattern.

**Severity Override Check:** $S = 9 \geq 9$ → **Action mandatory** regardless of RPN magnitude.

---

## 3.3 Post-Action RPN Verification (Closure of the FMEA Loop)

After implementing the action (*switch to PEEK + 100% leak test*):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{post}} &= 9 \quad \text{(Severity cannot be reduced for underdose — patient harm unchanged)} \\
O_{\text{post}} &= 1 \quad \text{(Failure eliminated by superior material — design out)} \\
D_{\text{post}} &= 2 \quad \text{(Now detectable by inline pressure decay leak test)} \\
\\
\text{RPN}_{\text{post}} &= 9 \times 1 \times 2 = 18
\end{aligned}$$

**RPN reduction:** $\dfrac{252 - 18}{252} \times 100\% = 92.86\%$ reduction. Documented in **Design History File (DHF)** as required by 21 CFR 820.30(g).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Why AP > RPN (Modern Justification)

Consider two failure modes with identical RPN = 100:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | True Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mode A | 10 | 5 | 2 | **100** | Catastrophic but detectable |
| Mode B | 4 | 5 | 5 | **100** | Minor and moderately detectable |

> **RPN says:** Both equal (priority = 100).
> **AP says:** Mode A = **H (High Priority)**; Mode B = **L (Low Priority)** — because Severity dominates.

**Conclusion for KTU exam:** Always discuss RPN limitations and the AIAG-VDA AP method in your conclusion — this is a **favorite 7-mark sub-question**.

---

## 3.5 Decision Tree — When to Use FMEA vs. FTA vs. HACCP

| Method | Direction | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **FMEA** | Bottom-up (inductive) | Component-level failure, manufacturing process |
| **FTA** | Top-down (deductive) | System-level single-point failures |
| **HACCP** | Hazard-driven | Sterilization, contamination control (per ISO 13408 / ISO 11137) |
| **Hazard Analysis** | Pre-design | Initial concept screening |

For high-risk medical devices, **FMEA + FTA + HACCP + Usability Engineering** are used in combination — this demonstrates **state-of-the-art** compliance per EU MDR 2017/745.

---

## 3.6 Cost-Benefit Analysis of FMEA (Industry Justification)

Industry data (ASQ, FDA reports):

$$\text{FMEA Cost} \approx \$50{,}000 \text{ for a Class II device (initial)}$$

$$\text{Cost of one field recall} \approx \$10\text{M–\$600M} \text{ (Tuohy needle, infusion pump, etc.)}$$

$$\text{ROI of FMEA} = \frac{\text{Recall Cost Avoided}}{\text{FMEA Cost}} \gg 100\times$$

This is why regulators expect FMEA in the **design input → design output → verification → validation** chain.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow Diagram (IEC 60812:2015 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define Scope and System Boundary] --> startB[Develop Block Diagram or Process Flow Chart]
    startB --> startC[Identify Failure Modes for each Component]
    startC --> startD[Determine Effects on System Patient User]
    startD --> startE[Assign Severity Score S 1 to 10]
    startE --> startF[Identify Causes for each Failure Mode]
    startF --> startG[Assign Occurrence Score O 1 to 10]
    startG --> startH[Identify Current Detection Controls]
    startH --> startI[Assign Detection Score D 1 to 10 Note Higher means Harder to Detect]
    startI --> startJ[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    startJ --> startK{RPN greater than 100 OR Severity greater than 9}
    startK -- Yes --> startL[Develop Recommended Actions Risk Control]
    startL --> startM[Implement Actions and Re-score]
    startM --> startN[Document in Risk Management File per ISO 14971]
    startK -- No --> startO[Document and Monitor as Acceptable Residual Risk]
    startO --> startP[Periodic Review during Post Market Surveillance]
    startN --> startP
```

---

## 4.2 RPN Calculation Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Failure Mode] --> inputB[Effect]
    inputB --> severityBox[Score Severity S 1 to 10]
    inputC[Cause] --> inputD[Occurrence Rate]
    inputD --> occBox[Score Occurrence O 1 to 10]
    inputE[Current Control] --> inputF[Detection Capability]
    inputF --> detBox[Score Detection D 1 to 10]
    severityBox --> rpnBox[Risk Priority Number RPN equals S times O times D]
    occBox --> rpnBox
    detBox --> rpnBox
    rpnBox --> decision{RPN greater than 100 OR Severity greater than 9}
    decision -- True --> actAction[Take Action Reduce S O or D]
    decision -- False --> acceptAction[Accept and Document]
```

---

## 4.3 FMEA Position within ISO 14971 Risk Management Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    isoStart[ISO 14971 Risk Management Process] --> plan[Risk Management Plan]
    plan --> analysisBlock[Risk Analysis Stage]
    analysisBlock --> fmeaTool[FMEA Component Level]
    analysisBlock --> ftaTool[FTA System Level]
    analysisBlock --> haccpTool[HACCP Sterilization]
    analysisBlock --> useHaz[Use FMEA Usability per IEC 62366]
    fmeaTool --> evalBlock[Risk Evaluation]
    ftaTool --> evalBlock
    haccpTool --> evalBlock
    useHaz --> evalBlock
    evalBlock --> controlBlock[Risk Control Implementation]
    controlBlock --> residBlock[Residual Risk Evaluation]
    residBlock --> benefit[Benefit Risk Analysis per ISO 14971]
    benefit --> pmcBlock[Production and Post Production Activities]
    pmcBlock --> feedback[Update FMEA from Field Data]
    feedback --> fmeaTool
```

---

## 4.4 RPN Severity vs Occurrence Heat Map (Examination Visual Aid)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title RPN Heat Map: Severity vs Occurrence with Detection Contours
    x-axis "Low Occurrence" --> "High Occurrence"
    y-axis "Low Severity" --> "High Severity"
    quadrant-1 "Critical Zone Reduce Immediately"
    quadrant-2 "High Priority Action Required"
    quadrant-3 "Acceptable Low Risk"
    quadrant-4 "Monitor and Reduce If Feasible"
    "Failure Mode A Death Rare": [0.15, 0.92]
    "Failure Mode B Hospitalization Moderate": [0.55, 0.75]
    "Failure Mode C Minor High Frequency": [0.85, 0.30]
    "Failure Mode D Hidden Catastrophe": [0.20, 0.85]
    "Failure Mode E Low Severity Moderate": [0.50, 0.35]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Quadrant:** Mode D (Hidden Catastrophe) is in Quadrant 1 — it has low occurrence BUT its **Detection score is 10 (impossible to detect)** which makes RPN explode. This is why detection must always be evaluated — it represents the *latent* risk the FMEA seeks to expose.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define FMEA. List any four applications of FMEA in the medical device industry.**

**Model Answer:**
FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a systematic, proactive engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes in a product, process, or system. It is defined under **IEC 60812:2015** as a structured procedure to analyze failure modes, their causes, and their effects on system performance and safety.

Four applications in medical device industry:
1. **Design verification** of new devices (DFMEA) — e.g., stent, pacemaker, infusion pump.
2. **Process validation** of manufacturing operations (PFMEA) — e.g., welding, sterilization.
3. **Software reliability analysis** (SFMEA per IEC 62304) — embedded firmware in diagnostic devices.
4. **Usability risk analysis** (Use FMEA per IEC 62366-1) — identifying user errors during home-use devices.
5. *(Bonus)* Post-market complaint trend analysis and CAPA triggering.

*(Examiner's Note: Any 4 valid applications earn full 3 marks. Do NOT exceed 4 — wastage of writing time in 3-mark questions.)*

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is RPN in FMEA? Explain the three components used to compute it.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a quantitative metric used in FMEA to rank the relative risk of identified failure modes. It is the product of three scoring parameters, each rated on a scale of 1 to 10.

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

The three components are:
1. **Severity (S):** The seriousness of the failure effect on the patient, user, or system. $S = 10$ represents catastrophic harm (death); $S = 1$ represents no effect.
2. **Occurrence (O):** The likelihood or frequency with which the failure cause occurs. $O = 10$ represents very high frequency; $O = 1$ represents remote probability.
3. **Detection (D):** The probability that the current control will detect the failure before it reaches the patient. **Higher D = harder to detect.**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B are from Module 4 — answer ANY ONE.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the seven-step FMEA methodology as per IEC 60812:2015. How does FMEA integrate with the ISO 14971 risk management process for medical devices? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The following failure modes were identified in an FMEA on a *patient monitor's ECG cable*:

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead wire fracture | 7 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | Connector pin corrosion | 6 | 4 | 8 |
| 3 | Shielding braid break | 5 | 3 | 7 |

Compute the RPN for each. Identify which failure mode needs corrective action and propose appropriate risk control measures. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Seven-Step FMEA Methodology + ISO 14971 Integration:**

**Step 1 – Scope Definition:** Identify the system boundary (e.g., ECG cable subassembly), resolution level (component), and intended use (continuous bedside monitoring).

**Step 2 – Block Diagram:** Construct a functional block diagram showing cable → connector → monitor input port.

**Step 3 – Failure Mode Identification:** For each component, brainstorm possible failure modes (e.g., fracture, corrosion, insulation failure, short circuit).

**Step 4 – Effect and Severity:** Determine consequence on patient monitoring (e.g., false asystole alarm → inappropriate resuscitation). Assign Severity score.

**Step 5 – Cause and Occurrence:** Identify root causes (mechanical fatigue, electrolytic corrosion, EMI). Assign Occurrence score based on field complaint data.

**Step 6 – Detection:** Identify current controls (visual inspection, continuity test, hi-pot test). Assign Detection score.

**Step 7 – RPN and Action:** Compute RPN, prioritize, implement actions, re-score.

**Integration with ISO 14971:** FMEA operates as a **risk analysis tool** within Clause 5.4 of ISO 14971:2019. It supports risk evaluation (Clause 5.5) by producing the RPN values used in the risk acceptability matrix. FMEA outputs (failure modes, causes, controls) feed directly into **risk control measures** (Clause 5.6) such as design changes, protective measures, and information for safety. After implementation, residual risks are re-evaluated through a re-scored FMEA. FMEA is updated in the **post-production phase** (Clause 5.9) using field complaint data, complaints, and CAPA — making it a **living document** within the Risk Management File (RMF).

---

**(b) RPN Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_1 &= S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1 = 7 \times 5 \times 6 = 210 \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 6 \times 4 \times 8 = 192 \\
\text{RPN}_3 &= S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3 = 5 \times 3 \times 7 = 105
\end{aligned}$$

**Prioritization (descending order):**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead wire fracture | **210** | **Critical — Immediate action** |
| 2 | Connector pin corrosion | **192** | **High — Corrective action** |
| 3 | Shielding braid break | **105** | **High — Reduce if feasible** |

**Recommended Risk Control Measures:**

1. **Lead wire fracture:** Redesign using stranded silver-plated copper with strain relief boot at both ends; add 100% flexure endurance test (≥100,000 cycles per IEC 60601-1).
2. **Connector pin corrosion:** Specify gold-plated pins (≥0.76 µm thickness); add hermetic sealing and IPX4 rating per IEC 60529.
3. **Shielding braid break:** Use spiral-wound braid (vs. braided); add automated continuity test; label with "do not sharply bend."

**Documentation:** All actions to be recorded in Design History File (DHF) per 21 CFR 820.30(j) and Risk Management Report per ISO 14971 Clause 8.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of traditional RPN-based FMEA. Explain how the **AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP)** method overcomes these limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A blood glucose meter (Class IIa) has the following three failure modes:

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Test strip misread (false low) | 9 | 4 | 5 |
| 2 | Display backlight failure | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| 3 | Battery door detachment | 4 | 6 | 3 |

Calculate RPN, classify the risk levels, and recommend a Severity-Override justification citing ISO 14971. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) RPN Limitations and AP Advantages:**

**Advantages of RPN-based FMEA:**
- Quantitative ranking enables prioritization.
- Forces cross-functional review of failure modes.
- Regulatory acceptance across FDA, EU MDR, ISO 14971.
- Triggers CAPA and continuous improvement.
- Documents traceability for audits.

**Limitations of RPN:**
1. **Equal-weighting fallacy:** $8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80$ and $4 \times 10 \times 2 = 80$ produce identical RPNs, but the latter has higher Occurrence (more frequent).
2. **Severity blindness:** RPN dilutes catastrophic failures (S=10) when O and D are low.
3. **Detection inversion confusion:** Practitioners mis-score Detection.
4. **Subjective scales:** Inter-rater variability reduces consistency.
5. **No time dimension:** RPN does not capture failure timing or operational phase.

**AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP) Method (2019):**
The AP method replaces RPN with a categorical prioritization (H/M/L) determined by a **lookup table** that considers the relative weighting of Severity, Occurrence, and Detection. Key improvements:

- **Severity-weighted:** Catastrophic failures (S ≥ 9) are automatically AP = High, regardless of O and D.
- **Detection-aware:** Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) detection gaps are highlighted.
- **No arithmetic dilution:** Two failure modes with the same AP cannot be arbitrarily distinguished by score arithmetic — they are treated by the **same risk control effort**.
- **Aligned with IATF 16949** automotive quality standard and increasingly adopted by **EU MDR-compliant medical device manufacturers**.

---

**(b) RPN Calculation and Severity Override:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_1 &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180 \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= 3 \times 5 \times 4 = 60 \\
\text{RPN}_3 &= 4 \times 6 \times 3 = 72
\end{aligned}$$

**Classification:**

| # | Failure Mode | RPN | Risk Class | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Test strip misread (false low) | **180** | **Critical** | **Immediate risk control** + Severity Override |
| 2 | Display backlight failure | 60 | Moderate | Document and monitor |
| 3 | Battery door detachment | 72 | Moderate | Reduce if feasible |

**Severity Override Justification (per ISO 14971 Clause 5.5 and 5.6):**
Even though Failure Mode 1 has the highest RPN, the **decisive factor is Severity (S = 9)**, which represents probable serious injury (patient may self-administer insulin based on falsely low reading → hypoglycemia → coma → death). Per **ISO 14971:2019**, severity of harm overrides RPN-based prioritization. The manufacturer **must implement risk control measures**:

1. **Inherent safety by design:** Add redundant electrochemical + photometric measurement algorithm (cross-check).
2. **Protective measure:** Display warning icon "Repeat test if symptomatic."
3. **Information for safety:** IFU states: *"If results inconsistent with symptoms, repeat immediately and contact physician."*

After controls: target reduction to $S = 9$, $O = 1$, $D = 2$ → $\text{RPN} = 18$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Treating D incorrectly as "higher = better":** This is the **#1 mistake**. Examiners explicitly test this. Always state "**higher Detection score = harder to detect = more dangerous**" in your answer.
> 2. **Confusing Severity Scale with Harm Probability:** Severity refers to the *consequence*, not the *likelihood*. Likelihood is captured by Occurrence.
> 3. **Skipping arithmetic steps in RPN:** Do not write "RPN = 9×4×5 = 180" in one stroke — show intermediate products ($9 \times 4 = 36$; $36 \times 5 = 180$).
> 4. **Omitting Severity Override:** Even if the question does not ask, mentioning it once signals examiner-level understanding and earns bonus marks.
> 5. **Ignoring AIAG-VDA AP method:** A 14-mark answer that *only* discusses RPN is now considered incomplete per the **2024 syllabus revision**.
> 6. **Not linking to ISO 14971:** FMEA in isolation = 50% marks. FMEA + ISO 14971 integration = full marks.
> 7. **Forgetting post-market update:** FMEA is not a one-time document. State that it must be updated during **post-market surveillance** per ISO 14971 Clause 10.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — FMEA**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Systematic, proactive technique per **IEC 60812:2015** to identify and prioritize failure modes.
>
> ✅ **Regulatory Anchor:** Required under **ISO 14971:2019 Clause 5.4**, **21 CFR 820.30**, **EU MDR Annex I §3**.
>
> ✅ **Types:** DFMEA, PFMEA, SFMEA, Use FMEA (uFMEA), FMECA.
>
> ✅ **Master Formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ — each rated **1 to 10**.
>
> ✅ **Detection Inversion:** **High D = Hard to detect** (more dangerous).
>
> ✅ **Action Threshold (Medical Devices):** RPN ≥ 100 **OR** Severity ≥ 9.
>
> ✅ **Severity Scale Anchor (Medical):** 10 = Death; 9 = Permanent injury; 7 = Temporary injury requiring intervention.
>
> ✅ **Modern Method:** **AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP)** — replaces RPN; uses H/M/L categorization.
>
> ✅ **Seven Steps:** Scope → Block Diagram → Failure Modes → Effects/Severity → Causes/Occurrence → Detection/Controls → RPN/Action.
>
> ✅ **Complementary Tools:** FTA (top-down), HACCP (sterilization), Use FMEA (usability).
>
> ✅ **Documentation:** Output stored in **Risk Management File (RMF)**, **Design History File (DHF)**, and **CAPA system**.
>
> ✅ **Living Document:** Must be updated during **post-market surveillance** (ISO 14971 Clause 10).
>
> ✅ **Severity Override Rule:** Severity = 9 or 10 mandates action **regardless of RPN**.
>
> ✅ **RPN Limitations:** Equal-weighting fallacy; arithmetic dilution; subjective scales → resolved by AP method.
>
> ✅ **Failure Mode vs. Failure Cause:** Failure Mode = *what* goes wrong; Failure Cause = *why* it goes wrong. Do not interchange.
>
> ✅ **Residual Risk:** After action, re-compute RPN and document in Risk Management Report.
>
> ✅ **Industry Origin:** NASA (1960s) → MIL-STD-1629 (1974) → Automotive (1980s) → Medical Devices (2000s onwards).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing and White Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing and White Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Software Verification & Validation (V&V) in Medical Devices

In the context of **Medical Device Regulations and Standards** (IEC 62304, FDA Guidance on Software Validation), software embedded in a medical device — whether it is firmware inside a pacemaker, an infusion pump, or a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) diagnostic app — must undergo rigorous **Verification and Validation (V&V)** before regulatory clearance.

**Verification** answers: *"Are we building the product right?"* (Did we implement the requirements correctly?)
**Validation** answers: *"Are we building the right product?"* (Does it meet the actual user/needs/regulations?)

Within this V&V framework, the two foundational dynamic testing techniques are **Black Box Testing** and **White Box Testing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **IEC 62304 Clause 5.6 (Software System Testing)** and **5.7 (Software Integration Testing)** explicitly require manufacturers to define test cases based on both **external behaviour (Black Box)** and **internal architecture (White Box)**. The two techniques are complementary — never alternatives.

---

## 1.2 Black Box Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU/IE 62304 Standard Terminology):**
> *Black Box Testing is a software testing methodology in which the internal structure, design, implementation, and code paths of the software under test (SUT) are NOT known to the tester. Test cases are derived purely from the **software requirements specification (SRS)**, **design input documents**, and **functional specifications** of the medical device. The tester interacts only with the inputs and observable outputs, treating the program as a closed "box."*

It is also called:
- **Specification-Based Testing** (preferred regulatory term in IEC 62304)
- **Behavioural Testing**
- **Functional Testing**
- **Closed-Box Testing**
- **Data-Driven Testing**

### Conceptual Analogy — The Locked MRI Cabinet

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are a **biomedical field service engineer** standing in front of a brand-new **MRI control console** that you have never opened. You don't know the circuit boards, the FPGA firmware, or the source code inside. Yet, you must certify it works safely. How do you do it?
>
> You press the "Start Scan" button → you observe whether the magnetic field ramps up correctly, whether the patient table moves to the correct position, and whether the display shows the expected image parameters. You feed in boundary inputs — extremely low SAR limits, very high coil current settings — and observe the outputs. You are not looking at the code; you are **testing the behaviour against the operator manual (the specification)**.
>
> That, in essence, is **Black Box Testing**. The MRI console is the "black box." The operator manual is your "test oracle."

---

## 1.3 White Box Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU/IE 62304 Standard Terminology):**
> *White Box Testing is a software testing methodology in which the internal structure, code logic, control flow, data flow, and architectural design of the software under test (SUT) are fully known and visible to the tester. Test cases are derived from the **source code**, **software architecture document**, **detailed design specification**, and **flow graphs**. The tester examines the program from "inside the box" to verify internal paths, branches, conditions, and statements.*

It is also called:
- **Structure-Based Testing** (preferred regulatory term in IEC 62304)
- **Glass-Box Testing**
- **Clear-Box Testing**
- **Open-Box Testing**
- **Code-Based Testing**
- **Logic-Driven Testing**

### Conceptual Analogy — The Watchmaker's Loupe

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Now imagine you are the **design engineer** who designed that MRI console. You have the full schematic diagram, the VHDL source of the FPGA, the C code of the controller, and the wiring diagram. You want to know: *Did I cover every branch of my alarm-handling state machine?* You trace the path: "If coil temperature > 60°C AND humidity > 80% → trigger alarm. ELSE IF coil temperature > 60°C AND humidity ≤ 80% → soft warning." You count: only 1 of 2 branches in the AND-OR logic was executed in the previous test. That uncovered branch is a **code coverage gap** — a regulatory defect.
>
> That, in essence, is **White Box Testing**. You are the watchmaker using a loupe to see every gear (every line, branch, and path).

---

## 1.4 Side-by-Side Quick-Reference Matrix

| Dimension | Black Box Testing | White Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | End-user / external | Developer / internal |
| **Knowledge of code** | None | Full |
| **Test basis** | SRS, use cases, risk file | Source code, design |
| **Tested by** | QA, regulatory team, end-users | Developer, integration team |
| **IEC 62304 clause** | 5.6 (System test) | 5.7 (Integration) / 5.8 (Unit) |
| **Detects** | Missing/wrong functions, UX defects | Dead code, unreachable branches, security flaws |
| **Cannot detect** | Hidden code defects | Missing requirements, UX issues |
| **Skill required** | Domain + regulatory | Programming + static analysis |
| **Coverage metric** | Requirements coverage | Code coverage (statement/branch/path) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Categories of Black Box Testing Techniques

The medical-device software engineer must choose the appropriate specification-based technique based on the **input domain characteristics** of the function being tested.

### 2.1.1 Equivalence Partitioning (EP)
The input domain is divided into **equivalence classes** — partitions of valid and invalid inputs that the system should treat identically. A single representative value from each class is tested. This drastically reduces the number of test cases while maintaining high defect-detection power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Medical context — SpO₂ Sensor):** A pulse oximeter accepts SpO₂ inputs from a sensor ADC. The valid range is 0–100%. Define classes:
> - Class 1 (Valid): 70% ≤ SpO₂ ≤ 100% (normal oxygen saturation)
> - Class 2 (Valid): 0% ≤ SpO₂ < 70% (hypoxic — alarm zone)
> - Class 3 (Invalid): SpO₂ > 100% (physically impossible → reject)
> - Class 4 (Invalid): SpO₂ < 0% (negative → reject)
>
> One representative value (e.g., 75, 60, 110, -5) tests each class.

### 2.1.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)
Defects cluster at the **edges** of equivalence classes. BVA tests values exactly on, just above, and just below each boundary. Always pair BVA with EP for medical-device firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Defibrillator Energy Limit):** If valid energy is 2 J to 360 J, test: 2, 3, 360, 359, 1, 361.

### 2.1.3 Decision Table Testing
Used when complex **Boolean combinations** of conditions determine outputs. Constructs a truth-table where each column is a complete test scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Infusion Pump — Should Pump Run?):** Conditions: Battery OK? Door Closed? Air-in-line clear? Drug loaded? — The pump runs only when all 4 are TRUE; 2⁴ = 16 combinations reduced via rule consolidation.

### 2.1.4 State Transition Testing
Validates behaviour when the device moves between **defined states** (Standby → Ready → Infusing → Alarming → Standby). Tests valid transitions, invalid transitions, and events triggering each transition.

### 2.1.5 Error Guessing
The tester's experience and prior defect history guide guesses about likely failures. In medical devices, this is often linked to **Use-Related Risk Analysis (URRA)** and **post-market surveillance data**.

### 2.1.6 Use Case Testing
Tests the device end-to-end through realistic **clinical workflows** defined in the Use Specification (IEC 62366-1).

---

## 2.2 Categories of White Box Testing Techniques

### 2.2.1 Statement Coverage (Line Coverage)
Measures the percentage of executable statements exercised by the test suite. Minimum standard for IEC 62304 Class B software is generally **100%** statement coverage.

### 2.2.2 Branch (Decision) Coverage
Measures the percentage of decision outcomes (TRUE/FALSE branches) exercised. Stronger than statement coverage — it forces both arms of every `if`, `switch`, `while` to be tested.

### 2.2.3 Condition Coverage
Each **Boolean sub-expression** must evaluate to both TRUE and FALSE independently.

### 2.2.4 Multiple Condition Coverage (MCC)
All combinations of sub-conditions in a Boolean expression are tested (most exhaustive — usually infeasible for large expressions).

### 2.2.5 Path Coverage
Every independent execution path from entry to exit is traversed. Tied to **Cyclomatic Complexity**.

### 2.2.6 Data Flow Testing
Tracks definitions and uses of variables. Detects anomalies: *def-use anomalies* like using a variable before initialization, redefining without use, etc.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following coverage and complexity metrics appear regularly in KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions for software engineering modules.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Statement Coverage** | $SC = \dfrac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | $S_{executed}$ = statements hit by tests; $S_{total}$ = total executable statements |
| 2 | **Branch Coverage** | $BC = \dfrac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | $B_{executed}$ = branches hit; $B_{total}$ = total branches (2 × # of decisions) |
| 3 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (Graph form)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | dimensionless | $E$ = edges in control-flow graph; $N$ = nodes (including entry/exit) |
| 4 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (Predicate form)** | $V(G) = P + 1$ | dimensionless | $P$ = number of predicate nodes (decisions) in flow graph |
| 5 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (Region form)** | $V(G) = R$ | dimensionless | $R$ = number of enclosed regions in the planar flow graph |
| 6 | **Independent Paths** | $IP = V(G)$ | dimensionless | Lower bound on number of test cases required for full path coverage |
| 7 | **Condition Coverage** | $CC = \dfrac{C_{executed}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | $C_{executed}$ = sub-conditions evaluated both TRUE & FALSE; $C_{total}$ = total sub-conditions × 2 |
| 8 | **Halstead Program Length** | $N = N_1 + N_2$ | tokens | $N_1$ = total operators; $N_2$ = total operands |
| 9 | **Halstead Volume** | $V = N \times \log_2(\eta)$ | bits | $\eta$ = vocabulary size ($n_1 + n_2$) |
| 10 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | defects/KLOC | Lower is better; typical medical device target: $\leq 0.5$ per KLOC |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX/Markdown Safety:** In the table above, absolute value bars and conditions are written using `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\` divisions, never the bare pipe `\vert vert` — to prevent KTU PDF rendering tools from breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Medical-Device Engineering

- **FDA General Principles of Software Validation** mandates a **defence-in-depth** testing strategy: use *both* black box (system-level behavioural) and *white box* (unit/integration) tests. Inspectors will reject a Design History File (DHF) that contains only one.
- **IEC 62304 Safety Class B & C** software require documented **structural coverage analysis** (white box) at the unit and integration levels.
- **ISO 14971 Risk Management** drives black-box test selection — high-severity hazardous situations get exhaustive specification-based tests, even when white box coverage is at 100%.
- In **Cybersecurity testing of medical devices** (FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance, IEC 81001-5-1), **fuzz testing** (a black-box technique) and **static/dynamic code analysis** (white-box) are both required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Cyclomatic Complexity of a Medical-Device Decision Module

### 3.1.1 Source Code Under Test

Consider the alarm-handling module of an **infusion pump** written in C:

```c
int evaluateAlarm(int pressure, int battery, int airDetected) {
    if (pressure > 300) {                  // Decision 1
        if (battery < 20) {                // Decision 2
            return CRITICAL_SHUTDOWN;
        } else {
            return HIGH_PRESSURE_WARN;
        }
    } else if (airDetected == 1) {         // Decision 3
        return AIR_IN_LINE_ALARM;
    } else {
        return NO_ALARM;
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the Control-Flow Graph (CFG)

We draw a directed graph where each node represents a basic block (a sequence of statements with no internal branches) and each edge represents control transfer.

| Node | Represents |
|---|---|
| **n1** | Function entry |
| **n2** | Test: `pressure > 300` (Decision 1) |
| **n3** | TRUE branch of n2 → Test `battery < 20` (Decision 2) |
| **n4** | TRUE branch of n3 → return `CRITICAL_SHUTDOWN` |
| **n5** | FALSE branch of n3 → return `HIGH_PRESSURE_WARN` |
| **n6** | FALSE branch of n2 → Test `airDetected == 1` (Decision 3) |
| **n7** | TRUE branch of n6 → return `AIR_IN_LINE_ALARM` |
| **n8** | FALSE branch of n6 → return `NO_ALARM` |
| **n9** | Function exit |

### 3.1.3 Counting Nodes and Edges

Counting carefully from the graph:

$$ N = 9 \quad \text{(n1, n2, n3, n4, n5, n6, n7, n8, n9)} $$

$$ E = 11 \quad \text{(n1→n2, n2→n3, n2→n6, n3→n4, n3→n5, n4→n9, n5→n9, n6→n7, n6→n8, n7→n9, n8→n9)} $$

### 3.1.4 Applying All Three Forms of Cyclomatic Complexity

**Method 1 — Graph Formula $V(G) = E - N + 2$:**

$$ V(G) = E - N + 2 = 11 - 9 + 2 = 4 $$

**Method 2 — Predicate Formula $V(G) = P + 1$:**

Predicate (decision) nodes in the code are: `pressure > 300`, `battery < 20`, `airDetected == 1`. So:

$$ P = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4 $$

**Method 3 — Region Formula $V(G) = R$:**

The planar flow graph encloses 4 regions:
1. Region containing path n2→n3→n4→n9
2. Region containing path n3→n5→n9
3. Region containing path n6→n7→n9
4. Region containing path n6→n8→n9

Plus the outer unbounded region. Counting the bounded + 1 outer = **5**? Let me recount carefully — in McCabe's original definition, $V(G)$ is the number of **bounded regions** of the planar flow graph, which equals the number of independent paths.

The four enclosed regions are:
- Region A: n2→n3→n4→n9 (CRITICAL path)
- Region B: n2→n3→n5→n9 (WARN path)
- Region C: n2→n6→n7→n9 (AIR path)
- Region D: n2→n6→n8→n9 (NO_ALARM path)

$$ R = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(G) = 4 \quad \checkmark $$

All three methods agree: **$V(G) = 4$**.

### 3.1.5 Minimum Number of Test Cases

The number of **independent paths** equals $V(G) = 4$. Therefore, a minimum of **4 test cases** is required to achieve **100% path coverage**:

| Path # | Input Combination | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | pressure = 350, battery = 10 | CRITICAL_SHUTDOWN |
| 2 | pressure = 350, battery = 80 | HIGH_PRESSURE_WARN |
| 3 | pressure = 100, airDetected = 1 | AIR_IN_LINE_ALARM |
| 4 | pressure = 100, airDetected = 0 | NO_ALARM |

### 3.1.6 Branch Coverage Verification

Total branches: each decision contributes 2 branches, so $B_{total} = 3 \times 2 = 6$.

Branches executed by the 4-path test suite: n2→n3, n2→n6, n3→n4, n3→n5, n6→n7, n6→n8 → all 6 hit.

$$ BC = \frac{6}{6} \times 100\% = 100\% $$

Statement coverage: All 4 return statements + the function body statements are also executed.

$$ SC = 100\% $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Coverage Calculation on Real Test Run

A Class B medical-device firmware module contains **120 executable statements** and **18 decision branches** (i.e., 9 if/switch constructs). The QA team executes a black-box test suite derived from the SRS. After execution:

- **102 statements** are traversed.
- **14 branches** are traversed (7 TRUE outcomes + 7 FALSE outcomes).
- The test report records that the function `validate_dose()` was never called, and the `else` branch of `check_kvo_rate()` was never executed.

### 3.2.1 Compute Statement Coverage

$$ SC = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{102}{120} \times 100\% = 85\% $$

### 3.2.2 Compute Branch Coverage

$$ BC = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{14}{18} \times 100\% = 77.78\% $$

### 3.2.3 Regulatory Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 62304 Class B requires 100% statement coverage and 100% branch coverage at the unit level** (after integration). Since $SC = 85\%$ and $BC = 77.78\%$, the test suite **fails** the regulatory acceptance criterion. The QA team must:
> 1. Add a positive test case that calls `validate_dose()`.
> 2. Add a test case where `check_kvo_rate()` returns the FALSE branch (e.g., KVO rate = 0.5 mL/h vs. expected ≥ 1.0 mL/h).
> 3. Re-execute and re-verify that both metrics reach 100%.
> 4. Update the **Software Test Documentation** (IEC 62304 Clause 5.6/5.7) and sign off.

This single example demonstrates why **white-box coverage metrics are non-negotiable in regulated software development.**

---

## 3.3 Coverage Strength Hierarchy

In software engineering, the standard hierarchy of structural coverage criteria is:

$$ \text{Statement Coverage} \subseteq \text{Branch Coverage} \subseteq \text{Condition/Decision Coverage} \subseteq \text{Multiple-Condition Coverage} \subseteq \text{Path Coverage} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Sketch:** Statement coverage is the weakest — it can be satisfied by following a single path that hits every line, even if other branches are dead. Branch coverage requires both arms of every decision — strictly stronger. Condition/Decision coverage adds sub-expression independence. Multiple-Condition requires all combinations. Path coverage is the strongest — every entry-to-exit path, including loops.

**Regulatory minimum** (FDA, IEC 62304):
- **Class A (No injury):** Statement coverage is generally acceptable.
- **Class B (Non-serious injury):** 100% branch coverage.
- **Class C (Death or serious injury):** 100% branch + condition coverage, with modified condition/decision coverage (MC/DC) for high-risk modules per **DO-178C**-style rigor adapted to medical devices.

---

## 3.4 Black-Box vs White-Box — Defect Detection Effectiveness Matrix

| Defect Type | Black-Box Effective? | White-Box Effective? |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| Missing requirement | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Wrong function (logic error) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Incorrect interface handling | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Partially |
| Performance / timing defect | ⚠️ Partially | ⚠️ Partially |
| Dead code | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Unreachable branch | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Memory leak | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Buffer overflow | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Security flaw (SQL injection) | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ Yes |
| UX / ergonomic error | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The matrix above is a **high-yield visual** for KTU 14-mark questions. Examiners love asking: *"Give two defects that black-box testing can detect but white-box cannot, and vice-versa."* Memorize the table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Black Box Testing Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end process** of executing a black-box (specification-based) test campaign for a medical device, from requirement extraction to defect closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Read Software Requirements Specification SRS]
    A1 --> A2[Read Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    A2 --> A3[Read Use Specification IEC 62366-1]
    A3 --> A4[Identify Input Domain and Output Domain]
    A4 --> A5[Apply Black Box Technique: EP / BVA / Decision Table / State Transition / Use Case]
    A5 --> A6[Design Test Cases: ID, Precondition, Input, Expected Output, Pass-Fail Criterion]
    A6 --> A7{Test Case Traceable to Requirement?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Reject: Redefine Test Case]
    A8 --> A6
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Configure Test Harness or Simulated Clinical Environment]
    A9 --> A10[Execute Test Case on Medical Device Under Test MDUT]
    A10 --> A11[Capture Actual Output and Logs]
    A11 --> A12{Actual Output equals Expected Output?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Mark Test Case PASS and Archive Evidence]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Log Defect in Bug Tracking System with Severity per Risk File]
    A14 --> A15[Risk Assessment: Does Defect Affect Patient Safety?]
    A15 -- Yes --> A16[Initiate CAPA Corrective and Preventive Action per ISO 13485]
    A15 -- No --> A17[Schedule Fix in Next Build]
    A17 --> A18[Developer Patches and Re-runs Regression Suite]
    A18 --> A10
    A13 --> A19{All Test Cases Executed?}
    A16 --> A19
    A19 -- No --> A6
    A19 -- Yes --> A20[Generate Test Summary Report and Traceability Matrix]
    A20 --> A21([End: Test Closure per IEC 62304 Clause 5.6])
```

---

## 4.2 White Box Testing Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **structural, code-based** testing pipeline typically executed by the development team at unit and integration levels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Start]) --> B1[Read Source Code and Detailed Design Document]
    B1 --> B2[Read Software Architecture Description IEC 62304 Clause 5.3]
    B2 --> B3[Construct Control Flow Graph CFG for Each Unit]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Cyclomatic Complexity V of G]
    B4 --> B5[Derive Independent Paths Equals V of G]
    B5 --> B6[Identify Predicate Nodes and Boolean Sub-expressions]
    B6 --> B7[Choose Coverage Criterion: Statement / Branch / Condition / MC-DC / Path]
    B7 --> B8[Author Unit Test Cases for Each Independent Path]
    B8 --> B9[Run Static Analysis Tools: MISRA C, CWE, CERT C]
    B9 --> B10[Execute Unit Tests with Coverage Instrumentation e.g., gcov, Bullseye, VectorCAST]
    B10 --> B11[Capture Coverage Report: SC percent, BC percent, CC percent]
    B11 --> B12{Coverage Meets IEC 62304 Safety Class Threshold?}
    B12 -- No --> B13[Identify Uncovered Lines and Branches]
    B13 --> B14{Defect or Insufficient Test?}
    B14 -- Defect --> B15[Fix Code and Re-test]
    B14 -- Insufficient Test --> B16[Add New Test Case to Cover Gap]
    B16 --> B17[Re-execute Coverage Suite]
    B15 --> B17
    B17 --> B10
    B12 -- Yes --> B18[Move to Integration Test Clause 5.7]
    B18 --> B19([End: Unit Test Closure per IEC 62304 Clause 5.5])
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Architecture: Where Each Technique Fits in the V-Model

The following Mermaid block diagram positions **Black Box** and **White Box** testing within the **medical device V-model** as required by IEC 62304 and recognized by FDA. Each testing level is mapped to its corresponding design phase on the left side and decomposition level on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VModel["IEC 62304 V Model of Medical Device Software Development"]
        direction LR
        L1[User Needs] --> L2[System Requirements] --> L3[Software Requirements] --> L4[Software Architecture] --> L5[Detailed Design] --> L6[Implementation Code]
        L6 --> L7[Unit Test] --> L8[Integration Test] --> L9[System Test] --> L10[Acceptance Test]
        L1 -.-> L10
        L2 -.-> L9
        L3 -.-> L8
        L4 -.-> L7
        L5 -.-> L7
    end
    BB["Black Box Testing"]:::bbStyle
    WB["White Box Testing"]:::wbStyle
    BB --> L9
    BB --> L10
    WB --> L7
    WB --> L8
    classDef bbStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef wbStyle fill:#7d2a3a,stroke:#06d6a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows from **L7 (Unit Test)** and **L8 (Integration Test)** are sourced by **White Box Testing** (you need the code). The arrows from **L9 (System Test)** and **L10 (Acceptance Test)** are sourced by **Black Box Testing** (you test against the spec, not the code). This V-model positioning is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

---

## 4.4 Decision-Tree: When to Choose Which Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0([Start: What do you want to verify?]) --> C1{Is your goal to confirm functional behaviour against the SRS or user need?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[Use Black Box: Equivalence Partitioning, BVA, Decision Table, Use Case, State Transition]
    C1 -- No --> C3{Is your goal to verify code structure, branches, paths, or to detect dead code?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Use White Box: Statement, Branch, Condition, Path, MC-DC Coverage]
    C3 -- No --> C5{Is your goal regulatory submission and audit defence?}
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Use BOTH: Black Box for system level and White Box for unit and integration level per IEC 62304 and FDA Guidance]
    C2 --> C7([End])
    C4 --> C7
    C6 --> C7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A carries 3 marks each. Answers should be precise (typically 1.5 to 2 pages of crisp writing) and may include a diagram or short table for full marks.

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEBT862]`

**Differentiate between Black Box Testing and White Box Testing. List any three specification-based (black-box) techniques used in medical-device software verification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Black Box Testing | White Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal code knowledge** | Not required | Fully required |
| **Test basis** | Software Requirements Specification (SRS), use cases, risk file | Source code, design, control-flow graph |
| **Performed by** | QA, regulatory, end-users | Developer, integration engineer |
| **Detects** | Missing/wrong requirements, UX defects | Dead code, unreachable branches, memory leaks |
| **IEC 62304 mapping** | System test (5.6), acceptance test | Unit test (5.5), integration test (5.7) |

**Three specification-based (black-box) techniques:**

1. **Equivalence Partitioning** — divides the input domain into classes of equivalent data; one representative value per class is tested.
2. **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** — tests values at, just above, and just below class boundaries, since defects cluster at edges.
3. **Decision Table Testing** — for systems with complex Boolean combinations of inputs that determine outputs, captures all rule combinations.

*(Any three of: Equivalence Partitioning, BVA, Decision Table, State Transition, Error Guessing, Use Case Testing.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEBT862]`

**Define Cyclomatic Complexity. State its three computational forms and compute the value of $V(G)$ for a module with 12 edges, 10 nodes, and 3 predicate nodes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
