Creepage deficit} = 2.0 \text{ mm, clearance deficit} = 1.7 \text{ mm. Redesign per remedies 1 and 2.}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **leakage current classification** in IEC 60601-1. List any **four types** with their **permissible limits** for **Type CF** applied parts in **normal condition (NC)** and **single fault condition (SFC)**. Justify why Type CF has the **strictest limits**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A defibrillator prototype is being designed. The manufacturer wants to classify the **applied part** and define **essential performance** as required by IEC 60601-1 (3rd Edition). With reference to **IEC 60601-2-4** (cardiac defibrillators), outline the **process of conformity assessment** the manufacturer must follow to obtain a **CE Mark** under EU MDR 2017/745. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Leakage Current Types in IEC 60601-1:** The standard defines **multiple leakage currents**, each measured in a specific test configuration (clause 8.7). The four primary types are: 1. **Earth Leakage Current ($I_{EL}$):** Current flowing through the protective earth conductor to ground. Measured from mains to PE. Limit (NC): 5 mA; (SFC): 10 mA. 2. **Enclosure (Touch) Current ($I_{TC}$):** Current flowing from the operator-accessible enclosure through a person to ground. Limit (NC): 0.1 mA; (SFC): 0.5 mA. 3. **Patient Auxiliary Current ($I_{PA}$):** Current flowing between two patient-applied parts in normal operation, not intended to produce a physiological effect. Limit (NC): 0.1 mA; (SFC): 0.5 mA. 4. **Patient Leakage Current ($I_{PL}$):** Current flowing from an applied part through the patient to ground. Limit depends on type: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Type B (NC/SFC)} &: 0.1 \text{ mA} / 0.5 \text{ mA} \\ \text{Type BF (NC/SFC)} &: 0.1 \text{ mA} / 0.5 \text{ mA} \\ \text{Type CF (NC/SFC)} &: \mathbf{0.01 \text{ mA} (10 \mu A)} / \mathbf{0.05 \text{ mA} (50 \mu A)} \end{aligned} $$ **Why Type CF has the strictest limits (10 µA NC):** The heart has a **micro-shock threshold** of approximately **10 µA** for inducing **ventricular fibrillation** when current passes directly through the myocardium. This is established in **physiological research** (e.g., the original work of Carl Wiggers, validated in IEC 60479). A typical patient with external (Type B) skin contact can tolerate 1000 µA before fibrillation risk. But for **intracardiac catheters, pacemaker leads, and direct heart contact**, every microampere matters. Hence **Type CF** is mandated for any device whose applied part may contact the heart. #### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Step 1 — Classify the Applied Part** A defibrillator's paddles/pads deliver current through the chest and may also be used in contact with the patient's torso during synchronized cardioversion. The applied part classification depends on use: - For external (trans-thoracic) use: **Type BF** (Body Floating) — patient is isolated from ground. - The defibrillator must additionally comply with **IEC 60601-2-4**, which adds the **defibrillation-proof applied part** requirement (withstand 5 kV across the part without damage to the device or the operator). **Step 2 — Define Essential Performance (per Risk Management File, ISO 14971)** The manufacturer must document, in the RMF, the performance characteristics whose loss or degradation would cause unacceptable risk. For a defibrillator, examples: - **Energy delivery accuracy:** ±15% or ±3 J (whichever is greater) at 50 Ω load. - **Synchronization accuracy:** ≤ 60 ms delay from R-peak to discharge. - **Discharge button fail-safe:** No accidental discharge. - **ECG continuity during discharge** (for synchronized cardioversion). - **Recovery time** of the ECG after discharge: < 10 s. **Step 3 — Conformity Assessment Pathway (EU MDR 2017/745)** A defibrillator is **Class IIb** (or **Class III** if implantable) per Rule 22 of Annex VIII. The manufacturer must follow: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Conformity Path} &= \text{Annex IX (Quality Management System + Technical Documentation Assessment)} \\ &\;\; \text{OR Annex X (Type Examination) + Annex XI (Production QA)} \end{aligned} $$ **Required Steps:** 1. **Establish Quality Management System** (ISO 13485) covering design, production, and post-market. 2. **Compile Technical Documentation** per Annex II: device description, design verification, RMF, clinical evaluation, labeling. 3. **Test Compliance:** - **IEC 60601-1** (parent general standard). - **IEC 60601-2-4** (defibrillators particular standard). - **IEC 60601-1-2** (EMC — critical for defibrillators due to high-energy discharge). - **IEC 60601-1-8** (alarm systems — required for the device's alerts). - **IEC 62366-1** (usability — defibrillator must be usable in crisis by stressed personnel). 4. **Notified Body Audit:** A Notified Body (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA) audits the QMS and reviews the Technical File. 5. **Issue EU Type Examination Certificate** + **ISO 13485 Certificate**. 6. **Affix CE Mark + Notified Body Number** (e.g., CE 0123) and **draw up EU Declaration of Conformity**. 7. **Register device** in EUDAMED database. 8. **Establish Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** and **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** per Article 86. 9. **Vigilance reporting** for serious incidents per Article 87. $$ \boxed{\text{Conformity assessment} = \text{RMF (ISO 14971)} + \text{Testing (60601-1 + 60601-2-4 + collaterals)} + \text{Notified Body Audit} + \text{CE Marking} + \text{PMS/Vigilance}} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in IEC 60601-1 Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing 60601-1 (Medical) with 60950-1 / 62368-1 (IT) or 60335-1 (Household).** Medical has **stricter** patient-leakage limits. Writing "Class I earth leakage 3.5 mA" (IT standard) instead of 5 mA/10 mA (medical) costs marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the "Single Fault Condition" doubling/5x principle.** Students often state NC limits only. SFC limits are **typically 5× higher** in IEC 60601-1. > > 3. **Conflating MOPP and MOOP.** Always state which you are computing. MOPPs are stricter than MOOPs (8 mm creepage vs 2.5 mm for reinforced, in many cases). > > 4. **Mixing up Type B, BF, CF symbols.** The KTU examiner expects the **symbols** (B in circle, B+F in box, C+F in box). Writing "Type B" for a cardiac catheter is a critical error. > > 5. **Omitting the IEC 60601-1-2 (EMC) and IEC 60601-1-8 (Alarms) collaterals.** Many students forget that 60601-1 alone is insufficient — collaterals are **mandatory companions**. > > 6. **Not linking to ISO 14971.** IEC 60601-1 (3rd/4th edition) is **explicitly risk-based**; a 14-mark answer without an ISO 14971 reference will lose 2–3 marks. > > 7. **Forgetting the dielectric strength formula $V_{test} = 2 \times V_{rated} + 1000$.** A high-frequency favorite for Part B calculations. > > 8. **Stating "Class III" for battery-powered medical devices.** IEC 60601-1 dropped Class III in the 3rd Edition. Such devices are simply "internally powered" and still must meet MOP requirements. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):** - ⭐ **IEC 60601-1** = the **parent general standard** for **Basic Safety + Essential Performance** of all medical electrical equipment. - ⭐ **IEC 60601-2-XX** = **particular** standards for **specific equipment types**; read in conjunction with 60601-1. - ⭐ **IEC 60601-1-XX** = **collateral** standards for **cross-cutting topics** (EMC, alarms, usability, home use). - ⭐ The **3rd Edition (2005)** introduced **Risk Management (ISO 14971)** and **Essential Performance**. - ⭐ **MOP** = Means of Protection; **MOPP** protects patient, **MOOP** protects operator. - ⭐ **2 MOPPs / 2 MOOPs** are required for **single-fault tolerance**. - ⭐ **Type B** = no patient / adequately isolated; **Type BF** = body-floating; **Type CF** = cardiac-floating (10 µA NC). - ⭐ **Class I** = basic insulation + PE; **Class II** = double/reinforced insulation, no PE. - ⭐ **Patient Leakage Current, Type CF (NC)** = **10 µA**; **(SFC)** = 50 µA. - ⭐ **Earth Leakage Current (NC)** = 5 mA; **(SFC)** = 10 mA. - ⭐ **Dielectric strength test** for reinforced insulation: $V_{test} = 2 V_{rated} + 1000 \text{ V rms}$. - ⭐ **Creepage at 250 V**: Basic 3.0 mm, Reinforced 6.0 mm (1 MOPP vs 2 MOPP). - ⭐ **Clearance at 250 V**: Basic 1.6 mm, Reinforced 3.2 mm. - ⭐ IEC 60601-1 is **risk-based** (3rd/4th ed.) — always link to **ISO 14971** in answers. - ⭐ Mandatory companions: **60601-1-2 (EMC)**, **60601-1-8 (Alarms)**, **60601-1-6 (Usability)**, **IEC 62366-1**. - ⭐ **CE Mark pathway**: QMS (ISO 13485) + Testing + Notified Body + Technical File + EUDAMED + PMS + Vigilance. - ⭐ **HBSE** (Hazard-Based Safety Engineering) is the modern safety philosophy; the **Risk Management File** is the central document. - ⭐ **Internally powered devices** (battery only) still must meet MOP requirements. - ⭐ IEC 60601-1-9 emphasizes **environmentally conscious design** (RoHS, life-cycle analysis). - ⭐ The **4th Edition amendments** (A1:2012, A2:2020) tighten usability and risk analysis — globally adopted.

topics.title: Overview of IEC 61010 and IEC 62304 Compliances: RoHS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of IEC 61010 and IEC 62304 Compliances: RoHS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 IEC 61010 – Safety Requirements for Electrical Equipment

**Formal Definition:**
IEC 61010 is an international safety standard published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** that specifies general safety requirements for electrical equipment intended for **professional, industrial, and laboratory use**, including electrical test and measurement equipment, electrical control equipment, and electrical laboratory equipment. For medical devices, the closely related derivative is **IEC 60601-1**, but IEC 61010 applies to in-vitro diagnostic (IVD) medical devices, medical laboratory equipment, and associated accessories not directly connected to patients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IEC 61010 governs the **electrical safety, mechanical safety, thermal safety, and hazard protection** of medical/laboratory equipment. It is a **horizontal standard** — meaning it applies across multiple product families — and is harmonized under the EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of IEC 61010 as the **"traffic rules for electrical equipment."** Just as traffic rules (red lights, speed limits, lane discipline) ensure every driver reaches their destination safely, IEC 61010 ensures every piece of electrical equipment in a lab or hospital operates without shocking the operator, burning the patient, or catching fire. It standardizes what "safe" means so a device made in Germany and one made in Japan can both be trusted in a Kerala pathology lab.

---

### 1.2 IEC 62304 – Medical Device Software Life Cycle Processes

**Formal Definition:**
IEC 62304 is the international standard that defines the **software development life cycle (SDLC) requirements for medical device software**, including software embedded in medical devices (embedded software) and software that is itself a medical device (stand-alone software / SaMD). It specifies a risk-based framework covering planning, development, testing, maintenance, configuration management, and risk management activities throughout the software life cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** IEC 62304:2006 + Amendment 1:2015 — *"Medical device software — Software life cycle processes."* The FDA recognizes it as a **consensus standard** (Recognition Number 13-79).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are building a **multi-storey apartment** in Kerala. You cannot just start laying bricks — you need an architect's plan, structural engineering drawings, an electrical blueprint, government approvals, inspections at each floor, and a maintenance manual for the residents. IEC 62304 plays the same role for medical software. It mandates a documented plan, a defined development process, rigorous testing at every stage, traceability of every line of code to a requirement, and a post-market maintenance plan. It ensures that the software controlling an insulin pump or MRI machine is built as carefully as a Kochi skyscraper.

---

### 1.3 RoHS – Restriction of Hazardous Substances

**Formal Definition:**
**RoHS** (Directive 2002/95/EC, recast as Directive 2011/65/EU, and further amended by Directive (EU) 2015/863) is a European Union regulatory framework that **restricts the use of specific hazardous materials** in electrical and electronic equipment (EEE). Medical devices became fully in-scope under RoHS 2 (Directive 2011/65/EU) starting **22 July 2014**, with an extended transition period to **22 July 2021** for in-vitro diagnostic medical devices and **22 July 2023** for all remaining medical devices under RoHS 3.

The **10 restricted substances** under RoHS 3 (EU 2015/863) with their **Maximum Concentration Values (MCV)** by weight in homogeneous materials are:

| # | Substance | Symbol | MCV (ppm) | MCV (%) |
|---|-----------|--------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Lead | Pb | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 2 | Mercury | Hg | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 3 | Cadmium | Cd | **100 ppm** | 0.01% |
| 4 | Hexavalent Chromium | Cr(VI) | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 5 | Polybrominated Biphenyls | PBB | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 6 | Polybrominated Diphenyl Ethers | PBDE | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 7 | Bis(2-Ethylhexyl) Phthalate | DEHP | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 8 | Butyl Benzyl Phthalate | BBP | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 9 | Dibutyl Phthalate | DBP | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |
| 10 | Diisobutyl Phthalate | DIBP | **1000 ppm** | 0.1% |

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of RoHS as the **"kitchen hygiene rules"** for electronics. Just as FSSAI in India bans certain harmful food colours and preservatives (e.g., Rhodamine-B, lead chromate) because they poison the consumer over time, RoHS bans ten toxic chemicals that leach out of circuit boards, cables, and plastic housings when devices are used, recycled, or dumped in landfills. When your hospital's old ECG machine is scrapped in a yard in Ernakulam, RoHS ensures it does not poison the soil with lead or cadmium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **RoHS** = restricts **hazardous SUBSTANCES** (materials) in the hardware
> - **IEC 62304** = governs the **software life cycle** of medical device software
> - **IEC 61010** = ensures the **electrical/mechanical safety** of the hardware
>
> These three standards are **complementary**, not interchangeable. A medical device manufacturer must comply with all three to legally affix the **CE Mark** in the European Union.

---

### 1.4 The Trinity of Medical Device Hardware-Software Compliance

For a modern medical device (e.g., a patient monitor, blood glucose meter, or MRI scanner), the compliance landscape looks like this:

- **Hardware layer** → governed by **IEC 61010** (safety) and **RoHS** (substance restrictions)
- **Software layer** → governed by **IEC 62304** (software life cycle)
- **Overall system** → governed by **ISO 13485** (QMS), **ISO 14971** (risk management), and **IEC 60601-1** (if patient-contacting)

Standard **metrics to remember**:
- IEC 62304 software safety classes: **Class A, B, C** (in increasing order of risk)
- RoHS maximum concentration threshold: **0.1% by weight** (1000 ppm) for 9 substances, **0.01%** (100 ppm) for Cadmium
- IEC 61010 measurement categories: **CAT I, CAT II, CAT III, CAT IV**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 IEC 61010 — Deep Dive

**Scope and Applicability:**
IEC 61010-1 (the parent standard) applies to electrical equipment with the following characteristics:
- Designed for measurement, control, or laboratory use
- Intended for professional, industrial, or educational use (not for household or general public)
- Supply voltage not exceeding **1000 V AC** or **1500 V DC** (for measurement circuits up to the same)
- For medical/clinical laboratory use (IVD equipment, analyzers, centrifuges, incubators)

**Key Hazard Categories Addressed (Clause-wise):**

| Clause | Hazard Type | What It Covers |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Clause 6 | Protection against electric shock | Insulation, earthing, creepage, clearance |
| Clause 7 | Protection against mechanical hazards | Sharp edges, moving parts, instability |
| Clause 8 | Resistance to mechanical stress | Drop test, impact test, vibration |
| Clause 9 | Protection against thermal hazards | Burn risk from hot surfaces, fire resistance |
| Clause 10 | Protection against radiation | Optical, acoustic, EMF radiation |
| Clause 11 | Protection against fluid pressure | Pressure vessels, fluid ingress (IP rating) |
| Clause 12 | Protection against fluid ingress | IPX1 to IPX8 ratings |

**Why and How of IEC 61010:**
- **WHY:** Because an ECG machine that leaks 230 V AC to a patient can be lethal (macroshock ≥ 100 mA or microshock ≥ 10 µA directly to the heart).
- **HOW:** By mandating **basic insulation**, **protective earth bonding**, **leakage current limits** (e.g., earth leakage ≤ 5 mA, touch current ≤ 0.5 mA for Class I equipment), **dielectric withstand tests** (e.g., 1500 V AC for 1 minute), and **mechanical robustness tests** (e.g., 1 m drop test).

**Measurement Categories (Overvoltage Categories):**
- **CAT I** — Signal-level electronic equipment (low-voltage, protected circuits)
- **CAT II** — Local-level electrical outlets, plug-in equipment (household appliances, portable tools)
- **CAT III** — Distribution-level equipment (panel boards, circuit breakers, fixed industrial equipment)
- **CAT IV** — Origin of installation, utility-grade equipment (service entrance, outdoor conductors)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**I**nside, **II**n the room, **III**n the panel, **IV** at the pole." Higher the CAT number, higher the transient voltage the equipment must withstand.

---

### 2.2 IEC 62304 — Deep Dive

**Software Safety Classification (Annex C of IEC 62304):**

| Class | Risk Level | Definition | Examples |
|-------|------------|------------|----------|
| **Class A** | No injury or damage to health is possible | Software cannot contribute to a hazardous situation | Software that displays ECG but does not control therapy; appointment scheduling |
| **Class B** | Non-serious injury is possible | Software can contribute to a non-serious injury | Software for monitoring of patient temperature, where incorrect reading may lead to delayed treatment |
| **Class C** | Death or serious injury is possible | Software can contribute to death or serious injury | Software that controls an infusion pump, ventilator, or radiation therapy device |

**Why and How of IEC 62304:**
- **WHY:** Software failures in medical devices have caused real deaths (e.g., the Therac-25 radiation overdose incidents of the 1980s, which led directly to the development of this standard).
- **HOW:** By enforcing a documented SDLC with five core processes:
  1. **Software Development Process** (planning → requirements → architecture → detailed design → unit implementation → integration → system testing)
  2. **Software Risk Management Process** (linked to ISO 14971)
  3. **Software Configuration Management Process** (version control, build management)
  4. **Software Problem Resolution Process** (bug tracking, change control)
  5. **Software Maintenance Process** (post-market updates, patches)

**Documentation Requirements by Class:**

| Document | Class A | Class B | Class C |
|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| Software Development Plan | Required | Required | Required |
| Software Requirements Specification | Not required | Required | Required |
| Software Architecture | Not required | Required | Required |
| Software Detailed Design | Not required | Not required | Required |
| Software Unit Implementation | Not required | Not required | Required |
| Software Verification | Required (limited) | Required (full) | Required (full + detailed) |
| Software System Testing | Required | Required | Required |
| Software Release | Required | Required | Required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The documentation burden **increases** with safety class. This is the **proportional-effort principle** — more risk = more documentation = more verification.

---

### 2.3 RoHS — Deep Dive

**Why and How of RoHS:**
- **WHY:** Lead in solder, mercury in displays, and cadmium in batteries leach into groundwater when devices are landfilled. Worker exposure during informal recycling in places like Moradabad (India's e-waste hub) causes neurological damage, kidney disease, and cancer. RoHS is an environmental + occupational health directive.
- **HOW:** By mandating that manufacturers (a) **declare** the presence of any restricted substance above the MCV, (b) maintain **technical documentation** proving compliance, and (c) **affix the CE Mark** only after conformity assessment.

**RoHS Compliance Process (Exemption-Based Logic):**
Some applications of restricted substances are granted **exemptions** (e.g., lead in high-melting-point solders, lead in certain medical device applications under Annex IV). The exemption list is reviewed periodically by the European Commission and is being progressively narrowed (the "substitution principle").

**Key KTU Distinction — RoHS vs. REACH vs. WEEE:**
- **RoHS** = restricts substances in **new products placed on the market**
- **REACH (EC 1907/2006)** = requires **registration and communication** of chemicals in products
- **WEEE (2012/19/EU)** = governs the **collection, recycling, and recovery** of end-of-life electronic waste

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Standard | Full Name | Scope | Risk Stratification | Mandatory Output |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **IEC 61010-1** | Safety requirements for electrical equipment for measurement, control, and laboratory use | Hardware electrical safety of lab/IVD medical equipment | CAT I to CAT IV (overvoltage) | Test reports, risk file, technical file |
| **IEC 62304** | Medical device software — Software life cycle processes | Software life cycle of any medical device software | Class A / B / C (safety) | SDLC documentation, verification records, SOUP list |
| **RoHS 3 (EU 2015/863)** | Restriction of Hazardous Substances | All electrical/electronic medical devices | MCV thresholds (0.1% / 0.01%) | Declaration of Conformity, CE Mark, test reports |

**Quick-Fire Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- RoHS: 10 restricted substances
- RoHS: MCV = 1000 ppm (0.1%) for 9 substances, 100 ppm (0.01%) for Cadmium
- IEC 62304: 3 software safety classes (A, B, C)
- IEC 61010: 4 measurement categories (CAT I–IV)
- IEC 61010: Earth leakage current limit ≤ **5 mA** for Class I portable equipment
- IEC 62304: 5 core software processes
- Medical devices fully in RoHS scope: **22 July 2014**; IVDs: **22 July 2021**; all others: **22 July 2023**

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For an embedded systems engineer in Kerala** designing a blood pressure monitor, IEC 61010 governs the choice of insulation class and creepage distances on the PCB.
- **For a software developer** writing firmware for an infusion pump, IEC 62304 dictates the documentation hierarchy and unit test coverage expected.
- **For a supply chain manager** sourcing components from Shenzhen or Coimbatore, RoHS mandates supplier declarations, XRF (X-ray fluorescence) testing, and material disclosure forms.
- **For a regulatory affairs officer** filing for CE Marking, the three standards together form the **technical file backbone** reviewed by Notified Bodies like TÜV SÜD or BSI.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Compliance Process

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Achieving IEC 61010 Compliance

**Step 1 — Identify applicable part of IEC 61010**
Determine whether your product falls under IEC 61010-1 (general) plus a specific part (e.g., IEC 61010-2-101 for IVD equipment, IEC 61010-031 for probe assemblies).

**Step 2 — Classify the equipment by overvoltage category**
Assign a CAT rating based on the mains connection point.

**Step 3 — Conduct a hazard analysis (per ISO 14971)**
Identify all potential hazards: electric shock, mechanical, thermal, chemical, radiation.

**Step 4 — Apply risk control measures**
Use the hierarchy: inherently safe design → safeguarding → protective devices → information for safety.

**Step 5 — Perform type tests on a representative unit:**
- Dielectric strength test
- Earth continuity test (≤ 0.1 Ω for Class I)
- Leakage current measurement
- IP rating verification
- Drop test (50 mm on hardwood, 3 faces, per IEC 61010-1 Clause 8)

**Step 6 — Compile the technical file and Declaration of Conformity.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic:** The compliance process follows a **V-Model** pattern. Design (top of V) → Implementation (bottom of V) → Verification & Validation (right arm of V, going up). Each design level has a corresponding test level.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Achieving IEC 62304 Compliance

**Step 1 — Classify the software safety class (A, B, or C)**
Classification must be justified and documented.

**Step 2 — Develop a Software Development Plan (SDP)**
The SDP defines scope, deliverables, tools, standards, and responsibilities.

**Step 3 — Elicit and document Software Requirements**
For Class B/C: a formal **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is required, with each requirement uniquely identified and **traceable**.

**Step 4 — Design the software architecture**
For Class B/C: define **software items** (modules), their **interfaces**, and **segregation** (separation of safety-critical from non-critical code).

**Step 5 — Implement and unit test**
For Class C: each **software unit** must be tested, and the test results documented.

**Step 6 — Integrate and perform integration testing**
Verify that interfaces between modules work as specified.

**Step 7 — Perform software system testing**
Verify that the integrated software meets the SRS in the target hardware environment.

**Step 8 — Release the software**
Document the version, configuration, and known residual anomalies.

**Step 9 — Maintain and update post-market**
Every change triggers a re-evaluation of safety class and a new verification cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Question Pattern:** Examiners often ask: *"For a Class C medical device software controlling an insulin pump, list the documentation deliverables required by IEC 62304."* Answer: All documentation rows in the table above for Class C (SRS, architecture, detailed design, unit implementation, full verification, system testing, release documentation).

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Achieving RoHS Compliance

**Step 1 — Determine scope**
Does the product contain any **homogeneous material** that might include a restricted substance? "Homogeneous material" = mechanically separable, single substance (e.g., a single plastic housing, a single solder joint).

**Step 2 — Collect material declarations from suppliers**
Each supplier must provide a **Material Declaration Form** or **Full Material Disclosure (FMD)** confirming substance concentrations.

**Step 3 — Perform analytical testing if needed**
Use **X-ray Fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy** to screen for restricted substances in homogeneous materials.

**Step 4 — Identify applicable exemptions (if any)**
Consult Annex III and IV of Directive 2011/65/EU for medical-device-specific exemptions (e.g., lead in certain imaging detector materials).

**Step 5 — Compile the Technical File**
Includes bill of materials, supplier declarations, test reports, exemption justifications.

**Step 6 — Issue the EU Declaration of Conformity and affix the CE Mark.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Standards to Device Life Cycle

$$\text{Device Life Cycle Phase} = \{ \text{Concept},\ \text{Design},\ \text{Verification},\ \text{Production},\ \text{PMS} \}$$

$$\text{Compliance Activity}_{\text{Phase}} = \begin{cases} \text{Risk Class} \rightarrow \text{ISO 14971} & \text{if Phase = Concept} \\ \text{Hardware Safety} \rightarrow \text{IEC 61010},\ \text{Substance Control} \rightarrow \text{RoHS} & \text{if Phase = Design} \\ \text{Software V\&V} \rightarrow \text{IEC 62304},\ \text{Type Testing} \rightarrow \text{IEC 61010} & \text{if Phase = Verification} \\ \text{Production Control} \rightarrow \text{ISO 13485},\ \text{RoHS Incoming Inspection} & \text{if Phase = Production} \\ \text{CAPA, Updates} \rightarrow \text{IEC 62304 Maintenance},\ \text{Substance Surveillance} & \text{if Phase = PMS} \end{cases}$$

**Key Inference:**
Each standard touches a different life cycle phase, and the **compliance evidence must be carried forward** in the **Design History File (DHF)**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: A Portable Pulse Oximeter

**Scenario:** A startup in Trivandrum is developing a fingertip pulse oximeter with Bluetooth connectivity and a mobile app.

**Step 1 — Hardware (IEC 61010)**
- Battery-powered, internally powered, Class II construction
- Leakage current limit ≤ 0.1 mA (touch current, normal condition)
- IP22 rating (finger ingress protection, as it is a body-worn device)
- Must pass 1 m drop test on hardwood

**Step 2 — Software (IEC 62304)**
- Bluetooth firmware: Class B (incorrect reading may delay treatment)
- SpO2 algorithm: Class B or C depending on whether it drives any therapy decision
- Mobile app: Class A (display only, not a therapy driver)

**Step 3 — RoHS**
- The PCB must be lead-free (RoHS-compliant solder)
- The plastic housing must be free of PBB/PBDE flame retardants
- A Declaration of Conformity must accompany the CE Mark

**Inference:** The same physical product triggers three parallel compliance streams that converge in the **Technical File** submitted to a Notified Body (if the device is Class IIa or higher in the EU MDR classification).
