## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define IEC 62304 and list its three software safety classes with one example of a medical device software for each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** IEC 62304 is an international standard titled *"Medical device software — Software life cycle processes."* It defines the requirements for the entire software life cycle of medical device software, including development, risk management, configuration management, problem resolution, and maintenance.

**Three Software Safety Classes (1.5 marks):**
- **Class A:** Software cannot contribute to a hazardous situation. *Example:* Display software in a blood pressure monitor that shows the reading but does not control therapy.
- **Class B:** Software can contribute to a non-serious injury. *Example:* Software for monitoring patient temperature where an incorrect reading may delay treatment.
- **Class C:** Software can contribute to death or serious injury. *Example:* Software controlling an insulin pump's delivery rate or a radiation therapy beam.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Classification drives the documentation burden, with Class C requiring the most exhaustive verification and design records.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State any six substances restricted under the RoHS 3 directive along with their maximum concentration values (MCV).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RoHS 3 (Directive (EU) 2015/863) restricts ten substances. Six of them are:

| # | Substance | MCV (in homogeneous material) |
|---|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Lead (Pb) | 0.1% by weight (1000 ppm) |
| 2 | Mercury (Hg) | 0.1% by weight (1000 ppm) |
| 3 | Cadmium (Cd) | 0.01% by weight (100 ppm) |
| 4 | Hexavalent Chromium (Cr VI) | 0.1% by weight (1000 ppm) |
| 5 | Polybrominated Biphenyls (PBB) | 0.1% by weight (1000 ppm) |
| 6 | Bis(2-Ethylhexyl) Phthalate (DEHP) | 0.1% by weight (1000 ppm) |

(Any six correctly identified substances with their MCVs earn full marks. Cadmium's lower 100 ppm threshold is a frequent bonus point.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Format (per KTU 2024 regulations):** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the scope of **IEC 61010-1** and describe, with reference to the relevant clauses, **any four hazard categories** that the standard addresses. Illustrate with a medical laboratory equipment example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **RoHS and WEEE** directives of the European Union. Why are medical devices in the scope of RoHS? State the date by which all medical devices became fully compliant with RoHS 3. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Scope of IEC 61010-1 and Four Hazard Categories (7 marks)**

**Scope (2 marks):** IEC 61010-1 specifies general safety requirements for electrical equipment intended for **measurement, control, and laboratory use**. It covers equipment designed to be used by trained personnel in a professional, industrial, or educational environment. For medical devices, the standard is applied to **in-vitro diagnostic (IVD) equipment** and **medical laboratory equipment** that does not directly contact patients. The supply voltage is limited to 1000 V AC or 1500 V DC.

**Four Hazard Categories (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Protection against electric shock (Clause 6):** Mandates basic insulation, protective earthing, creepage and clearance distances, and limits leakage current. *Example:* A clinical chemistry analyzer must have earth leakage current ≤ 5 mA and touch current ≤ 0.5 mA under normal conditions.

2. **Protection against mechanical hazards (Clause 7):** Covers sharp edges, moving parts (e.g., centrifuge rotors), and instability. *Example:* A centrifuge must have an interlocked lid that prevents operation when open and must withstand a 1 J impact test.

3. **Protection against thermal hazards (Clause 9):** Limits surface temperatures to prevent burns and ensures fire resistance of enclosures (e.g., 30 s glow-wire test at 850 °C). *Example:* The heating block of an incubator must not exceed 70 °C on user-accessible surfaces.

4. **Protection against fluid pressure and ingress (Clauses 11 & 12):** Mandates IP rating verification and pressure-relief mechanisms. *Example:* An automated blood analyzer must meet IP21 (protection against vertically falling drops) and must not become unsafe if internal fluid tubes leak.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** IEC 61010-1 provides a horizontal safety framework; medical laboratory and IVD equipment manufacturers use it to demonstrate electrical safety under the EU IVDR (Regulation 2017/746) or MDR (Regulation 2017/745).

---

**(b) RoHS vs. WEEE and Medical Device Scope (7 marks)**

**RoHS — Restriction of Hazardous Substances (3 marks):**
- **Legal basis:** Directive 2011/65/EU, amended by (EU) 2015/863.
- **Objective:** Restrict the use of 10 specific hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, PBB, PBDE, and 4 phthalates) in electrical and electronic equipment.
- **Mechanism:** Limits substances to MCV (0.1% or 0.01%) in homogeneous materials of **new products placed on the market**.
- **Output:** Declaration of Conformity and CE Mark.

**WEEE — Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (3 marks):**
- **Legal basis:** Directive 2012/19/EU.
- **Objective:** Govern the **collection, treatment, recycling, and recovery** of end-of-life electronic waste to reduce landfill and informality in recycling.
- **Mechanism:** Producers must finance the collection and recycling of their products at end-of-life; consumers must be informed about proper disposal.
- **Output:** WEEE marking (crossed-out wheelie bin symbol) on products; producer registration with national authorities.

**Why Medical Devices in RoHS Scope (0.5 mark):** Because medical devices contain electronic components (PCBs, displays, batteries) that contribute to environmental contamination and occupational exposure during manufacture, use, and disposal.

**Compliance Date (0.5 mark):** All medical devices (Category 8 under RoHS 2) became fully compliant with RoHS 3 by **22 July 2023** (Category 8 = medical devices other than IVDs; IVDs in Category 9 had to comply by 22 July 2021).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **five core processes** of **IEC 62304** and the **proportional documentation principle** based on software safety class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class C medical device software controls the **insulin delivery rate** of an implanted insulin pump. List the **mandatory documentation deliverables** required by IEC 62304 for this software, and justify why each is needed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Core Processes and Proportional Documentation (7 marks)**

**Five Core Processes (5 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Software Development Process (Clause 5):** Defines the activities from planning, requirements analysis, architectural design, detailed design, unit implementation, integration, and system testing to release.

2. **Software Risk Management Process (Clause 7):** Identifies software-related hazards, evaluates risks, and implements risk control measures. Must be integrated with ISO 14971.

3. **Software Configuration Management Process (Clause 8):** Establishes baselines, change control, version identification, and reproducibility of released software.

4. **Software Problem Resolution Process (Clause 9):** Defines how problems (bugs, anomalies, customer complaints) are recorded, analyzed, resolved, and tracked to closure.

5. **Software Maintenance Process (Clause 6):** Governs post-market changes, patches, and updates using a feedback loop that re-enters the development process.

**Proportional Documentation Principle (1.5 marks):**
The level of rigor and documentation increases with the software safety class:

- **Class A:** Minimum — risk management file, configuration management, problem resolution records.
- **Class B:** Adds software requirements specification, architectural design, integration testing.
- **Class C:** Adds detailed design, unit implementation and unit testing, comprehensive verification and validation.

**Flowchart (0.5 mark — to be drawn by the student):**
A typical IEC 62304 process flowchart shows the five processes arranged around the development V-model, with risk management and configuration management running as horizontal cross-cutting activities.

---

**(b) Mandatory Documentation for Class C Insulin Pump Software (7 marks)**

| # | Deliverable | Clause | Justification (0.5 mark each) |
|---|-------------|--------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Software Development Plan (SDP) | 5.1 | Defines scope, tools, standards, roles — needed to coordinate a safety-critical project. |
| 2 | Software Requirements Specification (SRS) | 5.2 | Each requirement must be uniquely identified and traceable to risk control measures. |
| 3 | Software Architecture | 5.3 | Must show segregation between safety-critical insulin delivery logic and non-safety code (e.g., UI). |
| 4 | Software Detailed Design | 5.4 | Every module's logic must be specified to enable thorough review. |
| 5 | Software Unit Implementation | 5.5 | Source code must be produced against the detailed design. |
| 6 | Software Unit Verification | 5.5 | Each unit must be tested, with results recorded. |
| 7 | Software Integration Testing | 5.6 | Verifies interfaces between units. |
| 8 | Software System Testing | 5.7 | Verifies that the integrated software meets the SRS in the target hardware. |
| 9 | Software Release Documentation | 5.8 | Version, configuration, residual anomalies, and release authorization. |
| 10 | Software Risk Management File | 7 | Documents hazard analysis, risk control measures, and verification of risk control. |
| 11 | Software Configuration Management Records | 8 | Demonstrates reproducibility of each released version. |
| 12 | Software Problem Resolution Records | 9 | Tracks all reported issues from pre-market through post-market. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For Class C software, **no documentation step can be omitted** because a software failure could lead to patient death (e.g., incorrect insulin bolus causing fatal hypoglycemia). The cumulative evidence forms a part of the **Design History File (DHF)** required by 21 CFR 820.30 and the EU MDR Technical Documentation.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Conflating IEC 61010 with IEC 60601-1:** IEC 61010 is for **laboratory and IVD equipment**; IEC 60601-1 is for **patient-contacting medical electrical equipment**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
>
> 2. **Stating 6 RoHS substances instead of 10:** RoHS 3 added **4 phthalates** (DEHP, BBP, DBP, DIBP) in 2015. If a question says "RoHS 3" or "RoHS as currently applicable," listing only the original 6 is **incomplete**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Cadmium threshold:** Cadmium's MCV is **100 ppm (0.01%)**, not 1000 ppm like the other nine. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 4. **Saying IEC 62304 is a software "standard" without specifying "life cycle":** The full name and scope must be cited for full marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing Class B and Class C medical device software examples:** A patient monitor displaying vitals is typically **Class B**; a ventilator's breath delivery control is **Class C**.
>
> 6. **Sketching IEC 62304 as a "waterfall" model:** It is best described as a **V-model** with horizontal risk management and configuration management overlays. Examiners expect V-model recognition.
>
> 7. **Omitting dates for RoHS medical device compliance:** Examiners test whether you know that medical devices became fully RoHS-compliant by **22 July 2023**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IEC 61010** = **HARDWARE electrical safety** for measurement, control, and laboratory equipment (incl. IVD medical equipment).
- **IEC 62304** = **SOFTWARE life cycle** for medical device software; classifies software into **Class A / B / C** by risk.
- **RoHS 3 (EU 2015/863)** = **restricts 10 hazardous substances** in EEE; MCV = 0.1% (1000 ppm) except Cd = 0.01% (100 ppm).
- **RoHS medical device compliance date:** All medical devices by **22 July 2023**; IVDs by **22 July 2021**.
- **IEC 61010 hazard categories:** electric shock, mechanical, thermal, radiation, fluid pressure, fluid ingress, etc.
- **IEC 61010 measurement categories:** CAT I → CAT IV (in increasing transient withstand).
- **IEC 62304 five core processes:** Development, Risk Management, Configuration Management, Problem Resolution, Maintenance.
- **Class A software:** No injury possible → minimal documentation.
- **Class B software:** Non-serious injury possible → SRS, architecture, integration test required.
- **Class C software:** Death or serious injury possible → All documentation, including detailed design and unit testing, is mandatory.
- **RoHS ≠ REACH ≠ WEEE:** RoHS restricts substances in new products; REACH is about chemical registration; WEEE governs end-of-life recycling.
- **These three standards are complementary:** they cover hardware safety, software life cycle, and material substance restrictions — together they form part of the **CE Marking technical file** for a medical device.
- **CE Mark** in the EU is the visible sign that a manufacturer has affirmed compliance with applicable directives (including RoHS and the MDR/IVDR which reference IEC 61010 and IEC 62304 as harmonized standards).
- **V-Model analogy** for IEC 62304: design descends on the left arm, verification ascends on the right arm, with risk management running horizontally.
- **For KTU exams:** Always cite the **clause number** when referencing an IEC standard requirement — it shows depth of understanding and earns higher marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: REACH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** REACH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**REACH** is the acronym for **Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals**. It is the European Union (EU) regulation **EC No 1907/2006**, which came into force on **1 June 2007**. It is one of the most comprehensive and stringent chemical management legislations in the world, addressing the production, import, placement on the market, and use of chemical substances, including those used in the manufacture of **medical devices**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *REACH is an EU regulation (EC No 1907/2006) that shifts the burden of proof from public authorities to industry (manufacturers and importers) to demonstrate that substances used or placed on the European market are safe for human health and the environment, throughout their lifecycle.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Anchor:** REACH is administered by the **European Chemicals Agency (ECHA)**, headquartered in **Helsinki, Finland**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive library containing every single chemical substance used in Europe. Before REACH, no one really knew which chemicals were on the shelves, who put them there, or whether they were safe to read (use). **REACH acts like a strict librarian system**:

- **Registration** = Every "book" (chemical) above a certain size (1 tonne/year) must be officially catalogued.
- **Evaluation** = The librarian (ECHA) checks if the cataloguing details are accurate and whether the book is genuinely safe.
- **Authorisation** = Some "books" are too dangerous to be left on open shelves. Manufacturers need special permission to use them.
- **Restriction** = Some "books" are banned outright because they are simply too harmful.

For a **medical device manufacturer**, REACH means every plasticizer, polymer, solvent, colorant, metal alloy, or biocompatible coating used in the device must be tracked, registered (if applicable), and proven safe.

## 1.3 Why REACH Matters to Medical Device Engineers

Although **medical devices** are largely regulated by **EU MDR 2017/745**, the *substances* and *materials* used to make them fall under REACH. Thus, a biocompatibility failure (e.g., presence of a carcinogenic, mutagenic, or reprotoxic — **CMR** — substance) can result in simultaneous non-compliance under both MDR and REACH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Threshold:** **1 tonne per year** is the baseline annual manufacturing or import volume that triggers mandatory registration for a substance (Articles are treated differently — see Section 2.3).

## 1.4 Key Stakeholders in REACH

| Stakeholder | Role |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturer** | Produces a substance within the EU |
| **Importer** | Brings a substance from outside the EU into the EU market |
| **Downstream User (DU)** | Uses a substance (alone or in a mixture) in industrial/professional activities |
| **Article Producer** | Produces or assembles an article |
| **ECHA** | Central coordinating agency |
| **Member State Competent Authorities** | National enforcement bodies |

## 1.5 REACH Timeline at a Glance

- **2007** — Regulation entered into force
- **2010** — First registration deadline (>1000 t/yr)
- **2013** — Second deadline (>100 t/yr)
- **2018** — Final deadline (>1 t/yr)
- **Ongoing** — SVHC additions to the **Candidate List** every ~6 months

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of REACH (Operational Breakdown)

REACH operates through four interconnected procedures. Below is the structured logic step-by-step:

### Pillar 1: **Registration** (Articles 5–24)
- **Why?** To build a public database of all chemical substances manufactured or imported in quantities of **≥ 1 tonne/year**.
- **How?**
  1. Manufacturer/Importer compiles a **Technical Dossier** (for >1 t/yr).
  2. For substances >10 t/yr, a **Chemical Safety Report (CSR)** is also required.
  3. Dossier is submitted to **ECHA** via the **REACH-IT** portal.
  4. A unique **Registration Number** is granted upon successful review.
- **Key Principle:** "**No data, no market**" — without registration, the substance cannot be placed on the EU market.

### Pillar 2: **Evaluation** (Articles 40–54)
- **Why?** To verify the quality of submitted dossiers and to assess whether a substance poses a risk.
- **How?** Two types:
  - **Dossier Evaluation:** ECHA checks compliance of technical dossiers (at least 5% randomly checked + all CSR-based dossiers).
  - **Substance Evaluation:** Member State authorities assess actual substance risk, with ECHA coordinating the **Community Rolling Action Plan (CoRAP)**.

### Pillar 3: **Authorisation** (Articles 55–66)
- **Why?** To progressively replace SVHCs with safer alternatives.
- **How?**
  1. ECHA identifies **Substances of Very High Concern (SVHCs)** and adds them to the **Candidate List**.
  2. Substances from the Candidate List are then added to **Annex XIV** (the Authorisation List).
  3. After a "sunset date," the substance cannot be used or placed on the market unless **authorisation** is granted.
  4. Applications are submitted to ECHA, demonstrating that the risk is adequately controlled OR that socio-economic benefits outweigh risks AND no alternatives exist.

### Pillar 4: **Restriction** (Articles 67–73)
- **Why?** To ban or limit the manufacture, placement on the market, or use of certain substances.
- **How?**
  1. EU Commission (often on ECHA's recommendation) prepares a restriction dossier.
  2. The dossier is reviewed by **ECHA's Committees** (RAC and SEAC).
  3. A binding **Restriction** is added to **Annex XVII** of the REACH regulation.

## 2.2 Critical Definitions (Board-Exam Must-Know)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Substance** | A chemical element and its compounds in the natural state or obtained by any manufacturing process |
| **Mixture** | A mixture or solution composed of two or more substances |
| **Article** | An object which during production is given a special shape, surface, or design which determines its function to a greater degree than its chemical composition |
| **SVHC** | Substance of Very High Concern — meets criteria of CMR Cat 1A/1B, PBT, vPvB, or has equivalent level of concern |
| **CMR** | Carcinogenic, Mutagenic, or toxic to Reproduction |
| **PBT** | Persistent, Bioaccumulative, and Toxic |
| **vPvB** | very Persistent and very Bioaccumulative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Article vs. Substance"** distinction is the single most confusing point in REACH. Once a substance becomes part of an article (e.g., a polymer chain), it is no longer "the substance." However, **SVHCs released from articles** still trigger communication obligations if concentration exceeds **0.1% w/w**.

## 2.3 REACH and Medical Devices — The Exemption Nuance

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exemption:** Substances used in **medicinal products for human or veterinary use** are largely exempt from REACH Title II (Registration), as they fall under their own sectoral legislation.
>
> **However**, REACH applies to:
> - **Substances used in the manufacturing process** of medical devices (e.g., solvents, cleaning agents)
> - **Articles** (the finished medical device itself, if it contains SVHCs > 0.1% w/w)
> - **Substances used in medical device packaging**

## 2.4 SVHC Communication Obligations for Medical Devices

If a medical device contains an **SVHC above 0.1% w/w** in any article:

1. The supplier must inform the **recipient** (downstream user/professional) within **45 days** of receipt of a request.
2. **Consumers** can also request the same information.
3. Information must enable safe use.
4. **ECHA must be notified** if SVHC > 0.1% w/w AND the substance totals >1 t/yr in all articles produced/imported (Notification under Article 7(2)).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

| Element | Key Value / Threshold |
|---|---|
| Regulation Number | **EC No 1907/2006** |
| Entry into force | **1 June 2007** |
| ECHA Location | **Helsinki, Finland** |
| Registration Trigger | **≥ 1 tonne/year** per manufacturer/importer |
| CSR Trigger | **> 10 tonnes/year** |
| SVHC Communication Trigger | **> 0.1% w/w** in an article |
| Notification to ECHA (Article 7(2)) | **SVHC > 0.1% w/w + >1 t/yr** |
| Authorisation List | **Annex XIV** |
| Restriction List | **Annex XVII** |
| Dossier evaluation random sample | **≥ 5%** |
| Communication response time | **45 days** |
| REACH Pre-registration | **Before 1 December 2008** (now closed) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Compliance Procedure & Analytical Matrix

Since REACH is a regulatory (not mathematical) topic, this section adapts the "step-by-step derivation" structure to a **step-by-step compliance procedure** and **analytical matrix**.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Determining REACH Obligations for a Medical Device Material

### Step 1: Identify the Material Form

$$\text{Is the material a Substance, Mixture, or Article?}$$

| Form | Test |
|---|---|
| Substance | Single chemical entity (e.g., pure titanium powder) |
| Mixture | Two or more substances not chemically bonded (e.g., an adhesive) |
| Article | Object with defined shape/surface function (e.g., a titanium hip stem) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Boundary Case Rule** from the European Court of Justice (Case C-106/14, FCD and FMB): An object is an "article" if its chemical composition is **less important** to its function than its **shape, surface, or design**.

### Step 2: Calculate the Annual Tonnage

$$\text{Tonnage Threshold} = \text{Annual Volume (tonnes)} \times \text{Concentration Factor}$$

- If **< 1 t/yr** → Registration may not be triggered for a substance.
- If **≥ 1 t/yr** → Registration dossier required.
- If **≥ 10 t/yr** → Chemical Safety Report (CSR) required.

### Step 3: Check for SVHC Presence

For each substance in the material, cross-reference with **ECHA's Candidate List** (currently contains **~240+ SVHCs** as of 2024 scheme).

If **SVHC present**:
1. Compute weight percentage in the article: $\text{C}_{SVHC} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of SVHC}}{\text{Total Mass of Article}} \times 100\%$
2. Check threshold: $\text{C}_{SVHC} > 0.1\% \text{ w/w}$?
3. Check tonnage: $\text{Total SVHC mass} > 1 \text{ t/yr}$?
4. If both conditions are met → **Notification obligation to ECHA under Article 7(2)**.

### Step 4: Check Authorisation and Restriction

- Is the SVHC in **Annex XIV (Authorisation List)**? → Apply for authorisation OR substitute.
- Is the substance in **Annex XVII (Restriction List)**? → Prohibited use unless specific exemption applies.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Compliance Walkthrough

**Scenario:** A manufacturer in India produces a **silicone-coated stainless steel guidewire** and exports **3 tonnes/year** to EU distributors. The silicone contains **0.15% w/w** of **decamethylcyclopentasiloxane (D5)**, which is an SVHC. Total SVHC mass = $3 \text{ t} \times 0.0015 = 0.0045 \text{ t} = 4.5 \text{ kg/year}$.

**Step-by-step analysis:**

- **Step 1:** Guidewire is an **article** (shape-dependent function).
- **Step 2:** Annual tonnage = 3 t/yr → Registration not directly triggered (article).
- **Step 3:** D5 is an SVHC at 0.15% w/w → exceeds 0.1% threshold.
- **Step 4:** Total SVHC = 4.5 kg/yr → below 1 t/yr threshold.
- **Step 5:** Conclusion: **No Article 7(2) notification to ECHA required**, BUT **downstream communication (Article 33)** is required if requested, within 45 days.

## 3.3 Authorisation Application Procedure (SVHC Lifecycle)

1. **Identification** → Substance flagged by ECHA or Member State.
2. **Inclusion in Candidate List** → Public database.
3. **Priority Listing for Authorisation** → ECHA recommendation.
4. **Inclusion in Annex XIV** → "Sunset date" set.
5. **Pre-application to ECHA** (by industry).
6. **Formal application** (latest 18 months before sunset date).
7. **ECHA opinion** (Committees RAC + SEAC).
8. **European Commission decision** → Grant / Refuse.
9. **Review** → Authorisations reviewed periodically (time-limited).

## 3.4 REACH vs. Other Key EU Chemical Regulations (Comparison Matrix)

| Aspect | REACH (EC 1907/2006) | CLP (EC 1272/2008) | RoHS (2011/65/EU) | MDR (2017/745) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Chemical safety | Classification, labelling, packaging | Hazardous substances in EEE | Medical device safety & performance |
| Scope | All chemical substances | All substances and mixtures | 10 restricted substances in EEE | Medical devices and accessories |
| Database | REACH-IT | C&L Inventory | — | EUDAMED |
| Agency | ECHA | ECHA | National authorities | Notified Bodies / Member States |
| SVHC concept | Yes (Candidate List) | No | No | References REACH SVHCs |
| Key Threshold | 1 t/yr | No tonnage trigger | Concentration limits | No tonnage trigger |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 REACH Process Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer or Importer] --> B[Identify Substance or Article]
    B --> C[Annual Tonnage Assessment]
    C --> D{Tonnage >= 1 tonne per year}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compile Technical Dossier]
    E --> F[Tonnage > 10 t]
    F -- Yes --> G[Add Chemical Safety Report CSR]
    F -- No --> H[Submit to ECHA via REACH IT]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Receive Registration Number]
    D -- No --> J[Substance Exempt from Registration]
    I --> K[Dossier Evaluation by ECHA]
    K --> L[Substance Evaluation by Member States]
    L --> M{SVHC Identified}
    M -- Yes --> N[Add to Candidate List]
    N --> O[Priority Assessment]
    O --> P{Add to Annex XIV}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Authorisation Procedure]
    P -- No --> R[Continue Evaluation]
    R --> S{Restriction Needed}
    S -- Yes --> T[Add to Annex XVII]
    S -- No --> U[Substance Cleared]
```

## 4.2 SVHC Lifecycle and Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SVHC Identification] --> B[ECHA Candidate List]
    B --> C[Downstream Communication Check]
    C --> D{SVHC greater than 0.1 percent w/w}
    D -- Yes --> E[Article 33 Communication Duty]
    E --> F[Recipient or Consumer Request]
    F --> G[Response within 45 Days]
    D -- No --> H[No Communication Triggered]
    B --> I{On Annex XIV Authorisation List}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply for Authorisation or Substitute]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Monitoring]
    B --> L{On Annex XVII Restriction List}
    L -- Yes --> M[Prohibited Use or Limited Use]
    L -- No --> K
    J --> N[ECHA RAC and SEAC Review]
    N --> O[European Commission Decision]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — REACH Compliance System for a Medical Device Manufacturer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A1[Raw Material Suppliers]
        A2[Substance SDS]
        A3[In House Formulation Data]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        B1[REACH Compliance Database]
        B2[SVHC Screening Engine]
        B3[Tonnage Aggregator]
        B4[Threshold Decision Logic]
    end
    subgraph Output
        C1[Technical Dossier to ECHA]
        C2[Article 33 Communication]
        C3[Article 7 Notification]
        C4[Substitution Roadmap]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Article vs. Substance Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Sample] --> B{Is chemical composition the PRIMARY determinant of function}
    B -- Yes --> C[Substance or Mixture]
    B -- No --> D{Is shape or surface or design the PRIMARY determinant of function}
    D -- Yes --> E[Article per REACH Article 3 3]
    D -- No --> F[Consult ECHA Guidance]
    C --> G{Annual tonnage check}
    E --> H{SVHC greater than 0.1 percent}
    G -- Above threshold --> I[Registration Required]
    G -- Below threshold --> J[Exempt from Registration]
    H -- Yes --> K[Communication and Notification Duties]
    H -- No --> L[No REACH Article Obligations]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEBT862]**
**Define REACH. List its four pillars.**

**Model Answer:**
REACH stands for **Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals**, an EU regulation numbered **EC No 1907/2006**, enforced from **1 June 2007**. It is administered by the **European Chemicals Agency (ECHA)** in Helsinki, Finland.

The four pillars are:
1. **Registration** — Submission of technical dossiers for substances ≥1 t/yr.
2. **Evaluation** — ECHA and Member States examine dossiers and substance risks.
3. **Authorisation** — Special permission required to use SVHCs listed in Annex XIV.
4. **Restriction** — Banning or limiting use of substances listed in Annex XVII.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEBT862]**
**What is an SVHC? State the communication obligation when an SVHC is present in an article above 0.1% w/w.**

**Model Answer:**
An **SVHC (Substance of Very High Concern)** is a chemical that meets one or more of the criteria: **CMR (Carcinogenic, Mutagenic, Reprotoxic) Category 1A or 1B**, **PBT (Persistent, Bioaccumulative, Toxic)**, **vPvB (very Persistent, very Bioaccumulative)**, or has an equivalent level of concern (e.g., endocrine disruptors).

**Communication obligation under Article 33 of REACH:**
- Any supplier of an article containing an SVHC at **>0.1% w/w** must provide information to the **recipient** (professional) and on request to **consumers**.
- Information must enable safe use.
- Response must be given **within 45 days** of the request.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Marks Long Answer (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Registration** procedure under REACH. State the role of ECHA and the tonnage thresholds for technical dossier and Chemical Safety Report (CSR).

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **Authorisation** procedure for SVHCs. Explain the roles of the Candidate List and Annex XIV.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Registration Procedure:**

1. **Identification of the substance** by the manufacturer or importer.
2. **Pre-registration** (closed in 2008 for non-phase-in substances; only legacy substances benefited).
3. **Data compilation** — Physicochemical, toxicological, and ecotoxicological data.
4. **Technical Dossier** — Compulsory for substances ≥1 t/yr.
5. **Chemical Safety Report (CSR)** — Required when tonnage **>10 t/yr**, containing:
   - Hazard assessment
   - Exposure scenarios
   - Risk characterisation
6. **Submission to ECHA** through the **REACH-IT** portal.
7. **ECHA checks** the dossier for completeness and assigns a **Registration Number**.
8. **Joint submission principle**: Same substance → one dossier (unless opt-out justified).

ECHA's role: Central registry, scientific and technical support, and the coordinating body for evaluations and authorisation decisions.

**(b) Authorisation Procedure:**

1. ECHA (or Member State) **identifies SVHCs** based on intrinsic properties.
2. SVHCs are placed on the **Candidate List** (public).
3. From the Candidate List, substances are prioritised and recommended to the European Commission.
4. Substances are added to **Annex XIV** (Authorisation List) with a **sunset date**.
5. After the sunset date, use of the substance is prohibited **unless an authorisation is granted**.
6. Companies apply to ECHA, supported by:
   - Chemical Safety Report
   - Analysis of Alternatives
   - Substitution Plan (if applicable)
   - Socio-economic Analysis (if risk cannot be adequately controlled)
7. **ECHA Committees (RAC and SEAC)** provide opinions.
8. **European Commission** issues the final decision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Restriction** and **Authorisation** under REACH. Which Annexes govern each?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medical device manufacturer uses a polymer containing **0.3% w/w of an SVHC**. Annual production is **5 tonnes**. Explain the REACH obligations triggered. Comment on the role of ECHA and the article-vs-substance distinction.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Restriction vs. Authorisation:**

| Feature | Authorisation | Restriction |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Manage SVHC risk via risk control / substitution | Ban or limit specific uses of any substance |
| Trigger | SVHC intrinsic property | Risk to human health/environment |
| Process | Company must apply | EU Commission acts on ECHA recommendation |
| Annex | **Annex XIV** | **Annex XVII** |
| Outcome | Permission to use (time-limited) | Mandatory prohibition/limitation |
| Decision-maker | European Commission on ECHA advice | European Commission after RAC/SEAC opinions |

**(b) SVHC Obligations:**

Given: **0.3% w/w SVHC** (> 0.1% threshold) and **5 t/yr** total production → **SVHC mass = 0.015 t/yr = 15 kg/yr**.

- **Article vs. Substance:** A polymer medical device is an **article** (shape determines function).
- **Communication duty (Article 33):** Any professional recipient or consumer can request info → response within **45 days**.
- **Notification duty (Article 7(2)):** Triggered only if **SVHC mass > 1 t/yr**. Here 15 kg/yr → **NOT triggered**.
- **ECHA role:** Maintains the Candidate List; receives notifications; supports enforcement.

**Conclusion:** Only the Article 33 communication duty applies; Article 7(2) notification is not required in this case.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing Article 7(2) notification with Article 33 communication** — many students conflate the two. Notification is *to ECHA* (mass >1 t/yr threshold); Communication is *to recipients/consumers* (threshold 0.1% w/w).
> 2. **Tonnage trigger confusion** — For substances, registration starts at 1 t/yr. For articles containing SVHCs, the threshold for *notification* (not registration) is 1 t/yr of the SVHC mass.
> 3. **Candidate List ≠ Annex XIV ≠ Annex XVII** — these are three distinct lists. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting the "1 June 2007" date** — examiners reward correct dates. Memorise them.
> 5. **Confusing REACH with CLP or RoHS** — REACH manages chemicals; CLP classifies/labels; RoHS restricts substances in Electrical & Electronic Equipment.
> 6. **Article vs. Substance failure** — the FCD/FMB court ruling (Case C-106/14) is a board-favourite: shape > chemistry = article.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **REACH =** Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, Restriction of Chemicals
- **Regulation No.:** EC No **1907/2006**
- **Date of force:** **1 June 2007**
- **Agency:** **ECHA**, Helsinki, Finland
- **Core principle:** **"No data, no market"**
- **Registration trigger:** **≥ 1 t/yr** (technical dossier); CSR for **> 10 t/yr**
- **SVHC threshold (article):** **> 0.1% w/w**
- **Notification trigger (Article 7(2)):** SVHC **> 0.1% w/w** AND total mass **> 1 t/yr**
- **Communication deadline:** **45 days**
- **Authorisation List:** **Annex XIV**
- **Restriction List:** **Annex XVII**
- **Medical device relevance:** REACH covers materials/substances used in device manufacturing; MDR covers the device itself
- **CMR Cat 1A/1B, PBT, vPvB** = SVHC criteria
- **Candidate List** is the entry point; Annex XIV follows
- **REACH-IT** is the official portal
- **Joint submission principle** for same-substance registration
- **FCD/FMB case (C-106/14):** shape > chemistry = article
- **Medical products exemption** does not extend to device manufacturing substances

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: National Level Safety Testing for Ionized Radiations (for Devices and Hospital Installations)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT862 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** National Level Safety Testing for Ionized Radiations (for Devices and Hospital Installations)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ionizing Radiation** refers to any electromagnetic or particulate radiation capable of producing ions directly or indirectly during its passage through matter by displacing orbital electrons from atoms, thereby altering the chemical and biological integrity of the absorbing medium. In the context of medical device regulation, safety testing for ionizing radiation constitutes the systematic evaluation of medical equipment emitting such radiation (X-ray, CT, fluoroscopy, linear accelerators, brachytherapy units, PET, gamma cameras) and of hospital installations housing these devices, against the **national radiation safety standards** prescribed by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** under the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962** and the **Radiation Protection Rules (RPR), 2004**.

National Level Safety Testing is the conformity assessment procedure carried out to verify that a radiation-emitting device or its installation does not expose the **occupational worker, the patient, or the general public** to radiation doses exceeding the **prescribed annual dose limits** specified by AERB, and that the device performs in conformance with the relevant **AERB Safety Code (SC)** and **AERB Safety Standard (SS)** documents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In India, ionizing radiation regulation is *NOT* governed by CDSCO/MDR-2017 alone. The **AERB (constituted under the Atomic Energy Act, 1962)** is the sole national authority for radiation safety. Every medical radiation device requires a **License (Type Approval + Site/Installation Approval + Operating License)** before clinical use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (the X-ray tube) routed into a hospital. Just as a municipality does **not** allow installation of high-pressure pipes without: (a) certifying the pipe's burst-pressure rating (Type Approval), (b) inspecting the room's wall thickness and valve layout (Installation Approval), and (c) issuing a periodic fitness certificate (Operational License) — the AERB performs exactly these three layers of safety assessment for radiation.

The **worker at the switchboard** is the *occupational worker*, the **patient lying under the beam** is the *patient being deliberately exposed* (medically justified), and the **person standing in the corridor** is the *member of the public*. All three must be protected by **shielding (the wall)**, **distance (the corridor length)**, and **time (limiting exposure duration)** — the three cardinal principles of the AERB protection philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — The Three AERB Protection Principles (ALARA Triad):**
> 1. **Justification** — No practice involving radiation shall be adopted unless it produces sufficient benefit to offset the radiation harm.
> 2. **Optimization (ALARA)** — All radiation exposures shall be kept **As Low As Reasonably Achievable**, economic and social factors being taken into account.
> 3. **Dose Limitation** — The dose received by any individual shall not exceed the prescribed **annual dose limits**.

### 1.3 Key National Bodies and Their Jurisdiction

| Authority | Statutory Basis | Jurisdiction |
|---|---|---|
| **AERB** (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board) | Atomic Energy Act, 1962 | All ionizing radiation safety in India |
| **RP&AD (BARC)** | AERB delegation | Radiological Physics & Advisory Division — does the type testing |
| **RPCs** (Regional Paving Centers) | AERB | Personnel monitoring, QA at regional level |
| **CSSC** (Committee for Safety Standard Committees) | AERB | Drafts safety codes |
| **ACPSR** (Advisory Committee on Nuclear Safety) | AERB | Top-level advisory |
| **CDSO / DCGI** | Drugs & Cosmetics Act | Concurrent regulation of medical device aspect |
| **BIS** | BIS Act 2016 | Voluntary standards (IS series) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prescribed Annual Dose Limits (AERB Safety Code AERB/SC/Med-1 and AERB Safety Directive No. 01/2011):**
> - **Occupational worker:** **20 mSv/year** (averaged over 5 consecutive years) and **30 mSv in any single year**
> - **Lens of the eye (occupational):** **20 mSv/year** (averaged) — revised in 2011
> - **Skin, hands, feet (occupational):** **500 mSv/year**
> - **Apprentice / Trainee (16–18 yrs):** **6 mSv/year**
> - **Member of the public:** **1 mSv/year**
> - **Pregnant occupational worker (abdomen surface):** **2 mSv** for the declared term of pregnancy
> - **Patient:** No dose limit, but every exposure must be *justified* and *optimized*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier National Safety Testing Framework

The AERB's safety testing framework operates as a **three-tier, sequential** conformity assessment chain. Each tier must be cleared before proceeding to the next.

**Tier 1 — Type Approval of the Equipment (Design-level Test)**
Conducted by the manufacturer at an **AERB-accredited testing laboratory** (typically RP&AD, BARC, Mumbai). The objective is to verify that the *design* of the equipment, when new, complies with the relevant AERB Safety Code. The applicant submits a **Type Approval Application** with technical documents, test reports, and an actual prototype. After successful type testing, AERB issues a **Type Approval Certificate** valid for the model.

**Tier 2 — Site / Installation Approval (Hospital Layout Test)**
Conducted at the proposed hospital site *before* the device is installed. AERB inspects the **room layout drawings, shielding design, occupancy of adjacent areas, ventilation, electrical safety, and warning signage** of the proposed installation. The hospital must obtain a **Layout Approval** followed by a **Site Approval** before commissioning.

**Tier 3 — Acceptance Testing and Operational License**
Conducted by the **Radiological Safety Officer (RSO)** of the institution, in presence of an AERB-approved QA service provider, *after* installation. Tests include radiation output calibration, beam quality, leakage radiation, beam collimation, accuracy of indicators, and interlock function. AERB then issues the **Operating License** (typically valid for 3–5 years) which authorizes clinical use.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Hospital Installation Safety Assessment

- **Step 1:** Classify the installation area as **Controlled Area** (where occupancy could deliver > 1.5 mGy/h, e.g., primary beam path, operator position) or **Supervised Area** (where occupancy delivers 0.5–1.5 mGy/h).
- **Step 2:** Determine the **design dose limit (P)** at the barrier: P ≤ **0.1 mGy/week** for controlled areas; P ≤ **0.02 mGy/week** for supervised areas.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **Workload (W)** in Gy·m²/week from the device's anticipated use.
- **Step 4:** Assign the **Use Factor (U)** — fraction of time the primary beam strikes the barrier (U = 1 for floor of therapy room; U = 1/4 for walls; U = 1/16 for ceiling).
- **Step 5:** Assign the **Occupancy Factor (T)** — T = 1 for full-occupancy areas (offices, labs, shops); T = 1/4 for corridors, restrooms; T = 1/16 for waiting rooms, stairs.
- **Step 6:** Compute the **unshielded dose equivalent rate** at the barrier using the inverse square law.
- **Step 7:** Look up the required **lead or concrete thickness** from AERB barrier-calculation charts (or use HVL/TVL table).
- **Step 8:** Verify the **primary barrier** (against primary beam) and **secondary barrier** (against leakage + scatter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Barriers?** The *primary barrier* stops the useful beam; the *secondary barrier* stops leakage radiation and patient scatter. Both are evaluated independently, and the larger of the two thicknesses is adopted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are essential for solving numerical problems on this topic in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$ | Inverse Square Law — intensity falls with square of distance | $I$ = intensity (cGy/min) |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$ | HVL attenuation: $n$ = number of HVLs | dimensionless |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-n}$ | TVL attenuation: $n$ = number of TVLs | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot U \cdot T}{d^2}$ | Unshielded primary barrier workload relationship | Gy·m²/week, m |
| 5 | $K = \dfrac{P \cdot d^2}{W \cdot U \cdot T}$ | Transmission factor $K$ to look up in barrier chart | dimensionless |
| 6 | $H_{TVL} = n \cdot TVL + (TVL - HVL)$ | HVL+TVL composite shielding formula | cm of lead / cm of concrete |
| 7 | $X = X_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Linear attenuation (Lambert-Beer) | $\mu$ in cm⁻¹, $x$ in cm |
| 8 | $\dot{H} = \dfrac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^2}$ | Gamma constant formula for sealed sources ($\Gamma$ = specific gamma ray constant) | $\Gamma$ in R·m²/(Ci·h) or Gy·m²/(GBq·h) |
| 9 | $A_{eff} = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Decay correction (radiopharmaceutical) | Bq |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant | s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\text{Dose Limit} = 20 \text{ mSv/yr (occ.)}$ | Occupational effective dose | mSv/year |
| 12 | $\text{Dose Limit} = 1 \text{ mSv/yr (public)}$ | Public effective dose | mSv/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HVL Reference Values (AERB Shielding Manual):**
> - Diagnostic X-ray (100 kVp): HVL ≈ 2.5 mm Al (inherent), 3.0–3.5 mm Al (with added filter)
> - CT (120 kVp): HVL ≈ 5.0 mm Al
> - Co-60 γ-ray (1.25 MeV): HVL ≈ 1.1 cm lead; **TVL ≈ 4.0 cm lead**
> - 6 MV Linac X-ray: HVL ≈ 1.5 cm lead; **TVL ≈ 5.5 cm lead**
> - 18 MV Linac X-ray: HVL ≈ 1.5 cm tungsten

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This regulatory framework directly governs the **mechanical engineering design of the X-ray gantry, the structural design of the treatment room walls, the architecture of the maze entry, the design of the door interlock, and the layout of the underground floor plan** of a hospital radiology/radiotherapy department. The same formulas drive the **stent design** of leaded glass viewing windows, the **thickness of the BGO scintillator** in PET detectors, and the **dose calibration protocols** of brachytherapy HDR units. Practically, the entire **structural engineering plan of a hospital radiation suite** is dictated by these calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverse Square Law from the Point-Source Geometry

Consider a point source emitting radiation isotropically. The radiation passes through a sphere of radius $d_0$ with intensity $I_0$, and through a sphere of radius $d$ with intensity $I$.

By conservation of total power (no absorption in vacuum between):

$$\dot{N} = 4\pi d_0^{\,2} \cdot I_0 = 4\pi d^2 \cdot I$$

Divide both sides by $4\pi d_0^{\,2} I_0$ and rearrange:

$$I = I_0 \cdot \frac{4\pi d_0^{\,2}}{4\pi d^2} = I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Engineering significance:** Doubling the distance reduces the intensity to **25 %** of the original; tripling reduces it to **11.1 %**. This is the basis of *distance-based protection* in AERB-designated controlled areas.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — HVL and TVL Relationship from Exponential Attenuation

The fundamental radiation attenuation follows **Lambert–Beer law**:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (cm⁻¹) of the absorber material at the given photon energy.

The **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** is defined as the absorber thickness that reduces the intensity to **half** of its incident value. Setting $I = \dfrac{I_0}{2}$:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot HVL}$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\mu \cdot HVL$$

$$-\ln 2 = -\mu \cdot HVL$$

$$HVL = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

Similarly, the **Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)** is the thickness that reduces intensity to **1/10**:

$$TVL = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}$$

**Critical relationship between HVL and TVL:**

$$\frac{TVL}{HVL} = \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.303}{0.693} \approx 3.32$$

So **TVL ≈ 3.32 × HVL**. This constant is universally applied in AERB barrier-design tables and is a high-frequency KTU exam value.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Composite HVL + TVL Barrier Thickness Formula

For a barrier requiring transmission factor $K$ (very small, e.g., $10^{-4}$), the AERB standard practice is to use one **TVL** plus an additional number of **HVLs** to reach the precise thickness. This is preferred over using fractional TVLs because HVL data is more accurately tabulated.

**Setup:** Let total transmission required = $K$. One TVL gives transmission $\dfrac{1}{10}$. We need an additional $n$ HVLs to further reduce by a factor of $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^n$.

$$K = \frac{1}{10} \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$$

Solve for $n$:

$$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 10 \cdot K$$

Take log base 2:

$$n = -\log_2 (10K) = -\frac{\log_{10}(10K)}{\log_{10} 2}$$

Total required thickness:

$$x_{total} = TVL + n \cdot HVL$$

Substitute $n = -\log_2(10K)$:

$$x_{total} = TVL - HVL \cdot \log_2(10K)$$

Using $\log_2 x = \dfrac{\log_{10} x}{\log_{10} 2} = \dfrac{\log_{10} x}{0.301}$:

$$x_{total} = TVL - \frac{HVL}{0.301} \cdot \log_{10}(10K)$$

This is the AERB-preferred formulation. It appears directly in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Type Problem)

**Problem:** A 6 MV linear accelerator operates with primary beam workload $W = 1500$ Gy/week at 1 m. The primary barrier is to be designed at distance $d = 4$ m from the target. Design dose limit at the barrier is $P = 0.1$ Gy/week. Use factor $U = 1$ (primary beam always strikes the floor). Occupancy factor $T = 1$ (full-time occupied office beyond the wall). For 6 MV X-ray, the HVL of lead is **1.5 cm** and TVL is **5.5 cm**. Calculate the required lead thickness.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the transmission factor $K$ required at the barrier.

$$K = \frac{P \cdot d^2}{W \cdot U \cdot T}$$

$$K = \frac{0.1 \times 4^2}{1500 \times 1 \times 1} = \frac{0.1 \times 16}{1500} = \frac{1.6}{1500}$$

$$K = 1.067 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2:** Express $K$ in terms of TVL + HVLs.

One TVL gives transmission $10^{-1}$. We need additional $n$ HVLs:

$$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 10 \cdot K = 10 \times 1.067 \times 10^{-3} = 1.067 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3:** Take log base 2.

$$n = -\log_2(1.067 \times 10^{-2}) = -\frac{\log_{10}(1.067 \times 10^{-2})}{\log_{10} 2}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.067 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(1.067) - 2 = 0.0281 - 2 = -1.9719$$

$$n = -\frac{-1.9719}{0.3010} = 6.55$$

**Step 4:** Total lead thickness.

$$x = TVL + n \cdot HVL = 5.5 + 6.55 \times 1.5$$

$$x = 5.5 + 9.825 = 15.325 \text{ cm of lead}$$

**Step 5:** Add a safety margin of typically **0.5 HVL** as per AERB:

$$x_{design} = 15.325 + 0.5 \times 1.5 = 16.075 \text{ cm of lead}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an *engineering practical* value. In reality, designers convert this to a **concrete equivalent** (since lead is expensive and structurally infeasible) using the equivalence: 1 cm lead ≈ 6.5 cm ordinary concrete for 6 MV photons, giving approximately **104 cm of concrete**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Categorization of Radiation Installations

| Installation Class | Example | Governing AERB Code | Inspection Body | License Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class A — Diagnostic | X-ray, CT, Mammography, Dental IOPA, OPG, CBCT, C-arm | AERB/SC/MED-1 (Rev. 2014); AERB/RF-Med/SC-1 | AERB / RSO | 5 years |
| Class B — Nuclear Medicine | Gamma Camera, SPECT, PET | AERB/SC/MED-4 (Nuclear Medicine) | AERB / RSO | 3 years |
| Class C — Teletherapy | Co-60 unit, Linac (6/15 MV) | AERB/SC/MED-3 (Teletherapy) | AERB + ACPSR | 3 years |
| Class D — Brachytherapy | HDR Ir-192, LDR I-125 seeds, PDR | AERB/SC/MED-4 (Brachytherapy) | AERB + RSO | 2 years |
| Class E — Industrial Radiography | (Out of KTU scope) | AERB/SC/IR-1 | AERB | 3 years |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — AERB Three-Tier National Safety Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Manufacturer Initiates Type Approval Application to AERB]
    nodeB{AERB Design Review by RPAD and CSSC}
    nodeC[Prototype Tested at AERB Accredited Lab - Type Test Report]
    nodeD{Type Test Result - Pass or Fail}
    nodeE[Type Approval Certificate Issued to Manufacturer - Valid for Model]
    nodeF[Hospital Submits Layout Drawing to AERB for Site Approval]
    nodeG[Shielding Calculation by RSO - Lead and Concrete Thickness]
    nodeH[Adjacent Area Survey - Controlled and Supervised Area Marking]
    nodeI[Warning Signs - Radiation Symbol - Caution Placards]
    nodeJ[Site Approval Issued - Construction of Room Authorized]
    nodeK[Equipment Installed in Approved Room]
    nodeL[Acceptance Test by RSO and AERB Approved QA Service]
    nodeM[Beam Output Calibration - Leakage Test - Interlock Test]
    nodeN{Acceptance Test Pass}
    nodeO[Operating License Issued - Clinical Use Authorized]
    nodeP[Periodic QA Tests - Annual - Performance Verification]
    nodeQ{License Renewal Due Every 3 to 5 Years}
    nodeR[Decommissioning - Source Disposal - AERB Notification]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|Pass| nodeE
    nodeD -->|Fail| nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN -->|Pass| nodeO
    nodeN -->|Fail| nodeK
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ -->|Renew| nodeL
    nodeQ -->|Retire| nodeR
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Hospital Radiation Suite Zoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZone1[UNCONTROLLED ZONE - Dose Less than 0.5 mGy per h - Public Access]
    nodeZone2[SUPERVISED ZONE - Dose 0.5 to 1.5 mGy per h - RSO Access]
    nodeZone3[CONTROLLED ZONE - Dose Above 1.5 mGy per h - Staff Only with TLD Badge]

    nodeZone1 -->|Step Up Boundary| nodeZone2
    nodeZone2 -->|Step Up Boundary with Warning Sign and Interlock| nodeZone3

    nodeZone3 --> nodePrimary[Primary Barrier - Walls Facing Primary Beam - Higher Lead Equivalent]
    nodeZone3 --> nodeSecondary[Secondary Barrier - Walls Facing Leakage and Scatter - Lower Lead Equivalent]
    nodeZone3 --> nodeMaze[Maze Entry - Reduces Door Shielding Need]
    nodeZone3 --> nodeDoor[Lead Lined Door - Interlocked with Beam On]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Shielding Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Identify Radiation Source - X-ray - Co-60 - Linac]
    nodeEnergy[Determine Photon Energy and Corresponding HVL and TVL from AERB Table]
    nodeWorkload[Compute Workload W in Gy per week]
    nodeUse[Assign Use Factor U from AERB Table]
    nodeOcc[Assign Occupancy Factor T from AERB Table]
    nodeDist[Measure Distance d from Source to Barrier]
    nodeDose[Set Design Dose P - 0.1 Gy per week for Controlled]
    nodeK[Compute K equals P times d squared divided by W U T]
    nodeTVL[Use 1 TVL plus n HVLs to Find Thickness]
    nodeMaterial[Convert to Concrete Equivalent Using Density and AERB Table]
    nodeVerify[Apply 0.5 HVL Safety Margin]
    nodeFinal[Final Barrier Specification - Documented and Approved by RSO]

    nodeStart --> nodeEnergy
    nodeEnergy --> nodeWorkload
    nodeWorkload --> nodeUse
    nodeUse --> nodeOcc
    nodeOcc --> nodeDist
    nodeDist --> nodeDose
    nodeDose --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeTVL
    nodeTVL --> nodeMaterial
    nodeMaterial --> nodeVerify
    nodeVerify --> nodeFinal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State and briefly explain the three principles of radiation protection as laid down by the AERB.**

**Model Answer:**
The AERB, in its safety code, prescribes three cardinal principles of radiation protection, collectively known as the **ALARA triad**:

1. **Justification:** No practice involving exposure to ionizing radiation shall be adopted unless it produces a net positive benefit to the exposed individual or to society that is sufficient to offset the radiation detriment it causes. *Example:* A chest X-ray is justified because the diagnostic benefit outweighs the small stochastic risk.

2. **Optimization of Protection (ALARA):** All radiation doses must be kept **As Low As Reasonably Achievable**, taking into account economic and social factors. ALARA is implemented through the three engineering controls of **shielding, distance, and time**.

3. **Dose Limitation:** The dose to any occupational worker shall not exceed **20 mSv/year** (averaged over 5 years), and the dose to any member of the public shall not exceed **1 mSv/year**. These are absolute legal limits, not to be exceeded under any routine operational circumstance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Half-Value Layer (HVL) and Tenth-Value Layer (TVL). Mention the numerical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | Tenth-Value Layer (TVL) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Thickness of an absorber that reduces the radiation intensity to **one-half** of its incident value | Thickness of an absorber that reduces the radiation intensity to **one-tenth** of its incident value |
| Use | Used for **first few HVLs** in a composite barrier design (more accurate) | Used for **first-order estimate** and rough sizing of barriers |
| Math | $I = I_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$ | $I = I_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)^{n}$ |

**Numerical relationship:** From the exponential attenuation law $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$:

$$TVL = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = 3.32 \times HVL$$

So **TVL ≈ 3.32 HVL** for any given photon energy and absorber material. The AERB-preferred composite barrier formula uses **1 TVL + n HVLs** to give an accurate thickness.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, every Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. A choice within a sub-part is *not* permitted.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]**

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the national-level regulatory framework of AERB for the safety testing of medical devices emitting ionizing radiation in India. In your answer, describe the role of the Atomic Energy Act 1962, the Radiation Protection Rules 2004, and the three-tier licensing process.**

**Model Answer (Stepwise):**

**1. Statutory Foundation — Atomic Energy Act, 1962**
The Act, enacted by the Government of India, vests the central government with exclusive power to regulate the production, use, and disposal of radioactive materials and radiation-generating equipment. Section 17 empowers the central government to make rules, and Section 3 authorizes the constitution of a regulatory board. The Act is the **parent statute** under which the AERB was constituted.

**2. Radiation Protection Rules (RPR), 2004**
Framed under Section 17 of the Atomic Energy Act, the RPR 2004 prescribe the **dose limits, working conditions, and licensing procedures** for every radiation installation. They mandate the appointment of a **Radiological Safety Officer (RSO)** for every institution operating a radiation device, define controlled and supervised areas, and specify penalties for non-compliance. The RPR 2004 applies to all medical, industrial, and research radiation installations.

**3. AERB Safety Codes and Safety Standards**
The AERB, through its standing committees (CSSC), issues binding **Safety Codes (SC)** and **Safety Standards (SS)**. Key medical codes include:
- AERB/SC/MED-1: Diagnostic X-ray
- AERB/SC/MED-2: Nuclear Medicine
- AERB/SC/MED-3: Teletherapy
- AERB/SC/MED-4: Brachytherapy
- AERB/RF-Med/SC-1: Medical cyclotron / PET

**4. The Three-Tier Licensing Process**

- **Tier 1 — Type Approval:** Manufacturer submits a device to an AERB-accredited test lab (typically RP&AD, BARC). Once the prototype passes, AERB issues a **Type Approval Certificate** for the model, valid indefinitely (subject to design change notification).
- **Tier 2 — Site Approval:** Hospital submits floor plan, shielding design, occupancy, and workload to AERB. AERB's Safety Review Committee inspects the design. On acceptance, a **Site Approval** is issued, authorizing construction.
- **Tier 3 — Operating License:** After installation, the RSO and an AERB-approved QA service perform acceptance tests. AERB issues an **Operating License**, valid typically 3–5 years, renewable after re-inspection.

**5. Enforcement and Inspection**
AERB conducts surprise inspections, can revoke a license for non-compliance, and is supported by the RP&AD, Regional Calibration Centers, and the National Radiation Monitoring Network.

**6. Conclusion**
The framework is *prescriptive* (not performance-based): every installation must demonstrate compliance with specific numerical criteria (dose limits, HVLs, barrier thicknesses), making the regulatory pathway highly deterministic and engineering-friendly.

**(b) [7 marks] A diagnostic X-ray room is to be commissioned with the following parameters: weekly workload W = 200 Gy·cm²/week at 1 m, use factor U = 1/4 (wall), occupancy factor T = 1 (full-time office), distance d = 3 m, design dose P = 0.02 Gy/week. For the given X-ray unit, HVL = 2.5 mm of lead, TVL = 8.3 mm of lead. Calculate the required lead barrier thickness using the composite HVL + TVL method. Add a 0.5 HVL safety margin as per AERB practice.**

**Model Answer (Stepwise):**

**Step 1:** Compute the required transmission factor $K$ at the barrier.

$$K = \frac{P \cdot d^2}{W \cdot U \cdot T}$$

$$K = \frac{0.02 \times 3^2}{200 \times (1/4) \times 1} = \frac{0.02 \times 9}{50} = \frac{0.18}{50} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2:** Use the composite TVL + n·HVL formula.

One TVL gives transmission $10^{-1}$. Additional HVLs must give transmission $10 \cdot K$:

$$10 \cdot K = 10 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 3.6 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for n (number of additional HVLs).

$$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 3.6 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$n = \frac{\log_{10}(1/3.6 \times 10^{-2})}{\log_{10} 2} = \frac{\log_{10}(27.78)}{0.3010}$$

$$n = \frac{1.4437}{0.3010} = 4.80$$

**Step 4:** Total barrier thickness before safety margin.

$$x = TVL + n \cdot HVL = 8.3 + 4.80 \times 2.5 = 8.3 + 12.0 = 20.3 \text{ mm of lead}$$

**Step 5:** Apply 0.5 HVL safety margin.

$$x_{design} = 20.3 + 0.5 \times 2.5 = 20.3 + 1.25 = 21.55 \text{ mm of lead}$$

**Step 6:** Round up to the commercially available standard — typically **22 mm of lead** (or its concrete equivalent of approximately 22 cm ordinary concrete).

**Answer:** The required primary barrier is **21.55 mm Pb** (use **22 mm Pb** in practice).

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style alternative]**

**(a) [7 marks] Describe in detail the AERB-prescribed acceptance testing procedure for a newly installed Cobalt-60 teletherapy unit. List the parameters measured, the instruments used, and the safety limits.**

**Model Answer (Stepwise):**

The AERB, in its Safety Code **AERB/SC/MED-3** and Safety Standard **AERB/SS/MED-1**, mandates the following acceptance tests for a Co-60 teletherapy unit before clinical release:

**1. Mechanical Tests**
- Isocenter accuracy (target cross-hair, jaw cross-hair, light field): tolerance **± 2 mm** at the isocenter
- Optical distance indicator (ODI) accuracy: **± 2 mm** at nominal SSD
- Field size indicators: accuracy **± 2 mm** at isocenter
- Gantry, collimator, and couch rotation isocenter: rotational accuracy **± 0.5°** (coincidence)
- Couch vertical and lateral movement: tolerance **± 2 mm**

**2. Radiation Output Calibration**
- Measured with a **Farmer-type ionization chamber (0.6 cc)** and **electrometer** calibrated to **BARC Secondary Standard Dosimetry Laboratory (SSDL)**
- Output is measured at **SAD = 80 cm** (or SSD = 80 cm) in a **30 × 30 cm²** field at **5 cm depth in water phantom**
- Reference output: **1 cGy/MU** (or 0.80 Gy·m²/min for Co-60, per IAEA TRS-398 protocol)
- Output reproducibility (10 measurements): ± **0.5 %**

**3. Beam Quality Verification**
- **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** of the 1.25 MeV Co-60 γ-ray: must be **1.05–1.15 cm of lead**

**4. Beam Profile and Flatness**
- Scanned with a **diode detector or small ionization chamber** in a water phantom
- **Flatness:** ± 3 % over 80 % of field width
- **Symmetry:** ± 2 %

**5. Leakage Radiation Test**
- Measured with a survey meter (Victoreen 450P or equivalent)
- Maximum permissible leakage: **0.1 % of useful beam** at 1 m, averaged over a 100 cm² area, none exceeding **0.05 %** in any single 100 cm²

**6. Safety Interlocks and Indicators**
- Door interlock test, treatment room warning lights, audio warning, emergency-off, and timer accuracy (within 0.01 min)
- Radiation-on indicator and console display check

**7. Penumbra and Wedge Filters**
- Wedge factor measurement and verification

**Approval:** The RSO signs a report, sends it to AERB, and the operating license is granted only after **AERB's independent verification** by RP&AD.

**(b) [7 marks] Compute the required thickness of a primary concrete barrier (density 2.35 g/cm³) for a 6 MV linear accelerator, given: W = 1000 Gy/week, U = 1, T = 1, d = 5 m, P = 0.1 Gy/week. For 6 MV photons, lead HVL = 1.5 cm, lead TVL = 5.5 cm. The lead-to-concrete equivalence is: 1 cm Pb ≈ 6.5 cm concrete at 6 MV.**

**Model Answer (Stepwise):**

**Step 1:** Compute transmission factor $K$.

$$K = \frac{P \cdot d^2}{W \cdot U \cdot T} = \frac{0.1 \times 5^2}{1000 \times 1 \times 1} = \frac{0.1 \times 25}{1000} = \frac{2.5}{1000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2:** Apply composite TVL + n·HVL formula.

$$10 \cdot K = 2.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$n = \frac{\log_{10}(1 / 2.5 \times 10^{-2})}{\log_{10} 2} = \frac{\log_{10}(40)}{0.3010} = \frac{1.6021}{0.3010} = 5.32$$

**Step 3:** Lead thickness.

$$x_{Pb} = TVL + n \cdot HVL = 5.5 + 5.32 \times 1.5 = 5.5 + 7.98 = 13.48 \text{ cm of lead}$$

**Step 4:** Add 0.5 HVL safety margin.

$$x_{Pb, design} = 13.48 + 0.5 \times 1.5 = 13.48 + 0.75 = 14.23 \text{ cm of lead}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to concrete equivalent using 1 cm Pb ≈ 6.5 cm concrete.

$$x_{concrete} = 14.23 \times 6.5 = 92.5 \text{ cm of concrete}$$

**Step 6:** Round up to engineering practice and add a minimum wall of 1 m — final specification: **1.0 m of ordinary concrete (2.35 g/cm³)** as the primary barrier.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — AERB Numerical Problems (KTU Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Units mismatch in $W$:** The workload $W$ must be expressed in **Gy·m²/week**, *not* Gy/week. For diagnostic X-ray, $W$ is often in Gy·cm²/week — students forget to convert cm² to m². **1 Gy·cm² = 10⁻⁴ Gy·m².**
> 2. **Confusing P for the design dose limit vs. the actual unshielded dose:** $P$ is the **dose limit at the barrier** (0.1 Gy/week controlled, 0.02 Gy/week supervised) — *not* the dose at the source.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.5 HVL safety margin:** AERB mandates this margin. Marks are *specifically awarded* for explicitly mentioning and adding it.
> 4. **Using the wrong TVL/HVL table value:** 6 MV ≠ Co-60; 18 MV ≠ 6 MV. Always quote the value with the photon energy and the source.
> 5. **Treating U and T as both 1 in every problem:** A common error. $U$ depends on the barrier's geometric relation to the primary beam; $T$ depends on the occupancy of the adjacent room.
> 6. **Not classifying the barrier as primary or secondary:** Leads to a 1- to 2-mark deduction if missed in the answer.
> 7. **Ignoring the statutory citation:** AERB codes (e.g., AERB/SC/MED-3) and dose limits (20 mSv/yr, 1 mSv/yr) are **commonly asked as 1-mark sub-questions** — students lose marks by not quoting them precisely.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Regulator:** AERB (under Atomic Energy Act, 1962). RPR-2004 is the operational rulebook.
- **Three Protection Principles:** Justification, Optimization (ALARA), Dose Limitation.
- **Three-Tier Licensing:** Type Approval → Site/Installation Approval → Operating License.
- **Dose Limits (must memorize):** Occupational = **20 mSv/yr** (avg 5 yrs), Public = **1 mSv/yr**, Lens = **20 mSv/yr**, Embryo/fetus = **2 mSv** for term of pregnancy.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $I = I_0 \cdot (d_0/d)^2$ — distance is the most effective and cheapest protection.
- **HVL–TVL relationship:** TVL ≈ **3.32 × HVL** (universal constant).
- **Composite Barrier:** $x = TVL + n \cdot HVL + (0.5 \, HVL \text{ safety margin})$.
- **Standard AERB HVLs:** 100 kVp diagnostic ≈ 2.5 mm Al; Co-60 ≈ 1.1 cm Pb; 6 MV ≈ 1.5 cm Pb.
- **Primary barrier** (U = 1) needs thicker shielding than **secondary barrier** (U = 1/4 to 1/16).
- **Use Factor U** for floor = 1, wall = 1/4, ceiling = 1/16.
- **Occupancy Factor T** for offices/labs = 1, corridors = 1/4, stairs/waiting = 1/16, parking = 1/32.
- **Categorization of areas:** Controlled (>1.5 mGy/h), Supervised (0.5–1.5 mGy/h), Uncontrolled (<0.5 mGy/h).
- **AERB Safety Codes for medicine:** MED-1 (Diagnostic X-ray), MED-2 (Nuclear Medicine), MED-3 (Teletherapy), MED-4 (Brachytherapy), and RF-Med/SC-1 (PET/Cyclotron).
- **Acceptance tests include:** Mechanical isocenter, output calibration, beam profile, HVL verification, leakage, interlocks — all under AERB observation.
- **Personnel monitoring:** TLD badge (whole body), TLD ring badge (extremity) — issued by **BARC** accredited labs, processed quarterly.
- **AERB is *not* CDSCO:** Ionizing medical devices are *exclusively* under AERB; CDSCO handles non-ionizing medical devices (most others under MDR-2017).
- **Maze entry** is preferred over lead-lined doors in teletherapy rooms — it eliminates the need for a heavy door and allows easier egress.
- **Decay constant** $\lambda = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$ is essential for nuclear medicine shielding problems.
